fibrous tissue is radiolucent. Consequently, the presence of extracellular
matrix may be visible on routine radiographs, but even if it is radiolucent, its presence may be correlated with the histologic findings.
The patterns of histologic alterations in usual bone structure
provide important clues to the diagnosis. Even though there are many
18

more histologic variations than can be discussed in the scope of this
introductory chapter, it is worthwhile summarizing the most common
departures from the normal in which osseous microanatomy is preserved and how the pattern of departure influences the diagnostic
approach.

Normal Bone Anatomy
The most common pattern alteration seen in small biopsies is
marrow replacement by fibrous tissue with the retention of cancellous
bone structure. This basic departure from normal structure is seen in
a multiplicity of diseases with diverse etiologies but has intertrabecular
fibrosis as its common unifying feature. When this pattern is seen in a
biopsy, the most important thing to sort out is the clinical setting that
led to the biopsy and whether the biopsy was done to sample a lesion
with localizing features radiographically or if the bone and marrow are
being sampled in an attempt to diagnose a generalized process. If there
is a localized imaging abnormality, the diagnostic possibilities are narrowed to include space-occupying lesions sufficient to produce changes
visible in imaging. These may include tumors and tumor-like processes, but they could also include localized infection and even metabolic diseases that have foci of greater severity sufficient to cause bone
defects. If there are no localized imaging abnormalities, the diagnostic
possibilities are much wider—but also may include tumors. The histologic features should be considered but only in the context of the
history and after the clinical imaging has been reviewed (there is a
great temptation to examine the histology before the clinical review).
In addition, there is often some degree of pressure from the treating
physician to give an answer based on incomplete clinical data.
There are three major elements to consider in a bone biopsy from
a marrow-bearing site. The first element is the overall architecture, that
is, the relationship of bone to marrow. This is best evaluated if there is
little crush and good sampling in a bone biopsy, although the quality
of the biopsy varies with biopsy site, operator skill, and quality of the
biopsy equipment, as well as proper fixation, decalcification,

and specimen processing. The second element is the marrow itself.
Depending on the site of biopsy and the age of the patient, the proportion of hematopoietic marrow to adipocytes varies. In axial sites such
as the iliac crest or vertebral bodies, hematopoietic marrow is expected
in some abundance at any age. In appendicular sites, hematopoietic
marrow is expected only in childhood and adolescence. On average,
the volume of adipocytes and hematopoietic cells should be about
equal in a hematopoietic site; younger individuals usually have a higher
proportion of marrow, and older individuals have a somewhat higher
proportion of adipocytes (Fig. 1-38). The third element is the bone
itself. If the biopsy is from a small needle, the bone trabeculae are
discontinuous even if normal because of the small diameter of the
needle and the thinness of the section (see Fig. 1-23). The individual
trabeculae should be fairly uniform in diameter, and even if one cannot
measure them, qualitatively they should average two to three adipocyte
diameters in thickness. Although a small degree of osteoclasis is
normal, no more than 3% to 4% of the bone surfaces should be covered
by resorption pits (Howship lacunae), and there should be fewer than
1 osteoclast per 25 mm of trabecular surface (without histomorphometric equipment, this is a difficult parameter to measure). Finally, the
trabeculae must be examined qualitatively for their collagen fiber
pattern (Fig. 1-39). If all the trabeculae in a biopsy showing intertrabecular fibrosis are woven, it is logical to assume that the same process
that caused the fibrous tissue has produced them (Fig. 1-40). Should
all of the trabeculae be lamellar, it is logical to assume that the fibrous
tissue is the pathologic process and that the mature bone was there
prior to that process (Fig. 1-41). Using these parameters, differential

2
Patterns in Radiology
D. Lee Bennett, MD, and Georges Y. El-Khoury, MD

Bone Tumors 23
Lytic Bone Lesions 23
Sclerotic Bone Lesions 28
Pathognomonic Imaging Signs 29
Soft Tissue and Joint Masses 32
Magnetic Resonance Evaluation 32

Solitary bone lesions and soft tissue masses are commonly encountered
in general orthopedic practice. Emphasis in this chapter will be on the
radiographic appearance of solitary bone lesions. However, advanced
imaging patterns will also be discussed, especially in the setting in
which radiographic imaging contributes little to the evaluation of such
lesions. With lytic and blastic bone lesions, radiographs often provide
a discrete differential diagnosis. In those instances, advanced medical
imaging is used primarily for staging and treatment planning. We will
be focusing our discussion chiefly on the initial evaluation of these
lesions and will avoid discussing follow-up imaging after treatment.

Bone Tumors
This section discusses the characteristic imaging findings of lytic and
sclerotic bone lesions. We will also discuss the general approach to
evaluating bone lesions. In the imaging section we will describe those
tumors that can be confidently diagnosed by radiography, computed
tomography (CT), and/or magnetic resonance imaging (MRI).

Lytic Bone Lesions
One of the primary functions of radiography is to detect lytic lesions
in patients with nonspecific symptoms. It is important to be aware of
normal variants that can mimic lytic bone lesions in order to avoid
unnecessary biopsies. One of the most common variants that simulates
a lytic lesion is the pseudocyst. A pseudocyst is an area of focal trabecular rarefaction within the bone at a location of low stress.1 When
the area of rarefaction is visually compared to the surrounding bone
that contains more prominent trabeculae, an apparent lytic lesion (or

so-called pseudocyst) stands out. Fortunately, these areas of low stress
that cause trabecular rarefaction are well described in the literature,
and radiologists are familiar with these normal variants. Pseudocysts
most commonly occur in the greater tuberosity of the humerus, the
anterior aspect of the calcaneus, and the radial tuberosity (Fig. 2-1). If
there is any confusion as to whether there is a superimposed lytic mass
over an area of a pseudocyst, an MRI can be performed. When only a
pseudocyst is present, the MRI will show normal bone marrow signal
in the area of concern.
After a true lytic lesion is identified, several imaging patterns or
characteristics should be evaluated. These patterns are evaluated to
determine if the lesion represents an aggressive or nonaggressive
process and to narrow the differential diagnosis of the lesion. These
patterns include the following: a periosteal reaction (evaluates aggressiveness), a zone of transition (evaluates aggressiveness), the presence
of matrix mineralization (aids in differential diagnosis), and the location within the bone (aids in differential diagnosis). Finally, and probably most importantly, the patient’s age should be considered along
with these findings.2,3 The patient’s age can be one of the most effective
clinical tools in narrowing a large differential to only a few possibilities
when considering nonspecific lytic lesions.
Periosteal Reaction
If a solid, uninterrupted periosteal reaction is present, then there is no
destruction or transgression of the periosteum and the lesion can be
regarded as nonaggressive (Fig. 2-2). However, if a lesion does invade
through the periosteum, this interrupted periosteal reaction suggests
that the lesion is aggressive. Examples of an interrupted periosteal
reaction would include a lamellated (known as onion-skinning) or
perpendicular (sunburst) appearance, as well as Codman (tri)angle
(Fig. 2-3). An aggressive lytic lesion with an interrupted periosteal
reaction represents typically an aggressive neoplasm or an aggressive
pyogenic infection.
In the absence of a periosteal reaction, other imaging patterns must
be used to determine if a lytic lesion is aggressive. If no periostitis is
seen, then the lesion is either nonaggressive or it may be so aggressive
that the bone has had little time to form a periosteal reaction (Fig. 2-4).
The most common nonaggressive lytic lesions that do not incite periostitis are fibrous dysplasia, enchondroma, nonossifying fibroma, and
23

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
simple bone cyst.4,5 If any of these four lesions show periostitis, then
the cause is usually a subtle pathologic fracture.
Zone of Transition
In medical imaging, the term zone of transition refers to the spatial
relationship between diseased bone and normal bone at the border of
a lytic lesion on radiographs or CT—but not MRI. A narrow zone of
transition implies that there is normal-appearing bone immediately
adjacent to the lytic lesion and that the precise border of the lesion can
be readily seen on the radiograph. Such an appearance is said to be

geographic (Fig. 2-5). The differential diagnosis for common geographic lytic lesions is fairly small and includes chondromyxoid
fibroma, nonossifying fibroma, and enchondroma. A narrow zone of
transition indicates that the lesion is nonaggressive. A wide zone of
transition implies that the border of the lesion is not as well defined
and that there is a fair amount of bone destruction by the lesion along
with tumor infiltration of the adjacent marrow. This type of bone
destruction is described as moth-eaten (Fig. 2-6) or permeative (Fig.
2-7). Moth-eaten and permeative lesions are aggressive, with motheaten lesions tending to be less aggressive than permeative lesions.

Figure 2-1. A pseudocyst is demonstrated in the greater tuberosity (arrows). This
should not be confused with a true lytic lesion.

Figure 2-2. Example of solid, uninterrupted periosteal reaction (white arrow). This is
a nonaggressive reaction from a stress fracture in the distal aspect of the right tibia.

Figure 2-3. Osteosarcoma of the left femur. Anteroposterior
(A) and lateral (B) views of the left femur showing an interrupted periosteal reaction. A Codman triangle can be seen
(black arrows). On the lateral view (B), a sunburst or spiculated
pattern of interrupted periostitis can be seen (white arrow).

A
24

B

Patterns in Radiology
Differentiating the moth-eaten from the permeative pattern is not
always easy, and the importance of differentiating these two patterns
may be only “academic.”6,7
There are other nuances to classifying the zone of transition using
the method described by Lodwick. Using this method, the zone of
transition is graded based on the aggressiveness of the lesion, with
grade I lesions being primarily geographic, grade II lesions being

moth-eaten, and grade III lesions being permeative. Grade I lesions are
further subclassified into grades IA, IB, and IC. The subclassification
of grade I lesions is beyond the scope of this chapter with the exception
of grade IA lesions, which have less than a 2% likelihood of being
malignant. Grade IA lesions are geographic, have a thick sclerotic rim,
have no cortical penetration, and have no significant expansion (<1 cm
of expansion in any plane) (Fig. 2-8). If a grade IA lesion is asymptomatic and not characteristic for a definite tumor, it can be followed with
serial radiographs.
Finally, the zone of transition in long bones and most flat bones can
be readily evaluated on conventional radiographs. However, the zone
of transition can sometimes be grossly misinterpreted on radiographs
of anatomically complex osseous structures such as the scapula, ilium,
ischium, or pubic bone. In such cases, the zone of transition is best
evaluated by CT.8

2

Summary of Aggressiveness
A lytic lesion can be considered clinically nonaggressive if it is painless,
has a grade IA zone of transition, and has an absence of an interrupted
periosteal reaction.2,3 All other lesions may be aggressive or exhibit
aggressive clinical behavior. One should remember that a lesion that
does not appear radiologically aggressive may still represent a malignant neoplasm and that an aggressive-appearing lesion may still be
benign (e.g., osteomyelitis).

Figure 2-4. Metastatic renal cell carcinoma involving the right shoulder. Anteroposterior view of the right shoulder shows an aggressive lesion that has nearly completely
destroyed the proximal humerus (arrow). This tumor is growing so quickly that there
has been no significant periosteal reaction to the tumor.

A

Visible Matrix Mineralization
When mineralization within a lytic lesion is detected on radiographs,
it indicates the presence of one of two types of tumor matrix. If the
mineralization is arclike, ringlike, punctate, or popcorn-like, the lesion
contains chondroid matrix (Fig. 2-9).9 If the mineralization is wispy,
cloudlike, or opaque, the lesion contains osteoid matrix (Fig. 2-10).10
If the mineralization is too faint to characterize on radiographs, it can
be better evaluated by CT.
Examples of tumors that may contain mineralization indicating a
chondroid matrix include enchondroma, chondrosarcoma, and

(arrows). Therefore, this would be categorized as a geographic lytic lesion.

The distal portion of the lytic lesion consists of multiple superimposed holes characteristic of a moth-eaten appearance (arrows). This is an example of an aggressive lesion with a
wide zone of transition.

Figure 2-7. Metastatic prostate cancer to the left tibia. There is a permeative appearance (multiple small lytic areas) involving the proximal aspect of the left tibia on this
lateral radiograph (arrows). This is a sign of an aggressive neoplastic process.

rim of this geographic lesion (arrows). No significant expansion is present; therefore,
this would be considered a grade IA lytic lesion.

chondroblastoma. Some examples of tumors that may contain mineralization indicating an osteoid matrix include osteosarcoma and
osteoblastoma.
Finally, a single dystrophic calcification located in the center of a
lytic bone lesion is nearly pathognomonic for an intraosseous lipoma.
Intraosseous lipomas are most commonly located in the anterior aspect

of the calcaneus and in the long bones of the lower extremity
(Fig. 2-11).
Location
The location of a bone lesion is important in narrowing the differential
diagnosis. The most common nonaggressive-appearing lytic lesions

Patterns in Radiology

femur shows punctate and popcorn-like (arrows) calcifications within the tumor indicating the presence of a chondroid matrix.

A

Figure 2-10. Osteosarcoma of the right tibia. Wispy, cloudlike calcification can be
seen in the soft tissue component of this tumor (arrows) on this anteroposterior radiograph. This is a classic appearance of osteoid matrix on a radiograph.

Figure 2-11. Intraosseous lipoma of the right femur. The grade IA lytic lesion within the right femur contains a central dystrophic calcification (arrows) indicative of an intraosseous

occurring at the end of a long bone are subchondral cysts (if there is
evidence of osteoarthritis), chondroblastoma (in patients younger than
30 years), giant cell tumor (if the physis is closed), metastasis (in
patients older than 40 years or who have a known primary malignancy), multiple myeloma (in patients older than 40 years), and infection (if the physis is open).
The presence of multiple lytic lesions in different bones should raise
the possibility of metastasis, multiple myeloma, infection, brown

tumors (hyperparathyroidism), enchondromatosis (Ollier disease), or
polyostotic fibrous dysplasia. The differential diagnosis can be further
narrowed based on the age of the patient. For example, metastasis and
multiple myeloma are the most common polyostotic lytic processes in
patients older than 40 years; however, they are rare in adolescents and
young adults.
The vast majority of geographic lytic lesions in the ribs are either
fibrous dysplasia, metastasis, multiple myeloma, aneurysmal bone cyst,
27

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
or Langerhans cell histiocytosis (eosinophilic granuloma). Again, this
differential diagnosis can be further narrowed based on the age of the
patient.
Lesions that are nearly always eccentric are nonossifying fibroma
and giant cell tumor. This holds true to the extent that if a lesion is
located centrally within a long bone, then nonossifying fibroma and
giant cell tumor should not be highly considered. Conversely, a simple
bone cyst is nearly always centrally located within a long bone (typically the proximal humerus and proximal femur); therefore, if a lytic
lesion is eccentrically located within a long bone, a simple bone cyst
should not be seriously considered.
Some tumors that present as lytic lesions have a predilection for
specific bones. For example, approximately 85% of adamantinomas
occur in the anterior cortex of the tibial diaphysis,11 whereas approximately 85% of chordomas occur in the sacrum or skull base.12
Magnetic Resonance Imaging
MRI is primarily used for staging and treatment planning of malignant
bone lesions. Depending on the pulse sequence used in a particular
instrument, images are reported typically as being either bright or dark
compared with isodense tissue in the same field. Typically, if a lesion
is bright on a T1-weighted image, it represents protein-rich fluid such
as blood or fat and occasionally may be highlighting calcifications.
Alternatively, if the lesion is dark on a T1-weighted image but bright
on a T2-weighted image, it signifies increased water content such as is
present with edema, hemorrhage, or inflammation. If a lesion is dark
on a T2-weighted image, it represents fibrous tissue, hemosiderin, or
melanin. Occasionally MRI can contribute to the differential diagnosis.
An example of this would be fluid–fluid levels within a lesion, which
should raise the possibility of an aneurysmal bone cyst or, if there are
aggressive features, a telangiectatic osteosarcoma (Fig. 2-12). Additional information such as the presence or absence of intraosseous
(tissue) edema may be very helpful as would be the presence or absence
of a soft tissue mass. For example, a mass would be much more likely
to be present in a malignant bone tumor.

should be based on that diagnosis. If an asymptomatic grade IA lytic
lesion has a nonspecific imaging appearance, then it can be followed
with serial imaging. All other nonspecific lytic lesions should be biopsied to exclude the possibility of malignancy. A biopsy should be carefully planned and performed so that uninvolved compartments are not
needlessly seeded, leading to treatment difficulties such as the necessity
for an extended resection.

Sclerotic Bone Lesions
Medullary Cavity
When a solitary sclerotic bone lesion is identified within the medullary
cavity on a radiograph, then the most likely diagnosis is a bone island;
by far, this is the most common solitary sclerotic bone lesion. There
are two radiographic hallmarks of a bone island: (1) a bone island
within a long bone is typically oblong with its long axis oriented along
the shaft of the bone and (2) the margins of a bone island are spiculated, with the small spicules extending into and blending with the
adjacent normal trabecular bone (Fig. 2-13). Occasionally, CT may be
necessary to evaluate these findings at the margin. On MRI, a bone
island will have low signal intensity on both T1- and T2- weighted
sequences (Fig. 2-14).13
If a sclerotic bone lesion cannot be confirmed to be a bone island
by imaging, then further evaluation by biopsy is indicated to confirm
or exclude the presence of a malignancy. The majority of malignant
sclerotic bone lesions represent metastatic disease from prostate cancer
or breast cancer.14
If there are multifocal sclerotic bone lesions, then metastatic disease
must be radiographically differentiated from other benign processes
such as osteopoikilosis and melorheostosis. Osteopoikilosis is characterized by multiple, small sclerotic lesions that mimic bone islands but
typically cluster around joints (Fig. 2-15). Melorheostosis is characterized by dense hyperostosis “flowing” along the cortex of the bone in a
manner resembling dripping candle wax. Endosteal sclerosis at the
same level can also be present. The sclerotic lesions in melorheostosis

In summary, if one can confidently diagnose a lytic bone tumor using
the imaging patterns described previously, then workup and treatment

bone diseases should be readily identifiable radiographically.
CT plays an important role in evaluating sclerotic bone lesions
within the medullary cavity, especially in anatomically complex bones
where thorough radiographic evaluation can be limited. MRI plays
little role in the diagnostic evaluation of sclerotic lesions; however, MRI
can be useful in staging and treatment planning.
Cortical Lesions
Focal cortical sclerosis or thickening has a limited differential diagnosis. Most lesions represent osteoid osteoma, osteomyelitis, or stress
fracture. When nonspecific focal sclerotic thickening of the cortex is
observed radiographically, CT can be helpful in differentiating among
these entities. CT will demonstrate a spherical nidus within the focal

Figure 2-16. Right tibia/fibula anteroposterior radiograph of melorheostosis. The dripping candle wax appearance of melorheostosis can be seen on the right fibula (arrows).
This disease should be distributed along sclerotome divisions.

cortical thickening in osteoid osteoma, an area of longitudinal lucency
in the cortex often represents osteomyelitis, and a fracture line would
be typical of a stress fracture (see Fig. 2-2; Fig. 2-17).

Pathognomonic Imaging Signs
Several imaging signs are diagnostic of specific bone lesions. It is
important to be familiar with these signs because their presence makes
it easy to arrive at a quick and accurate diagnosis.
29

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

C

B
Figure 2-17. Osteoid osteoma versus cortical osteomyelitis. A lateral radiograph of the left tibia/fibula (A) shows an area of focal cortical thickening on the left tibial posterior
cortex (arrows). A transverse computed tomography image (B) through the area of cortical thickening demonstrates a round nidus (arrow) diagnostic of an osteoid osteoma. An
anteroposterior radiograph of the right femur (C) in a different patient has a longitudinally oriented lucency within the cortex representing cortical osteomyelitis (white arrows). A
sequestrum can also be seen within the lytic cavity (black arrow).

Enlarged Frame Sign
An enlarged vertebral body with thickened cortex and coarsened trabeculae is diagnostic of the sclerotic phase of Paget disease (Fig. 2-18).
Bone involved with Paget disease is also enlarged relative to its neighboring bone. In long bones, the pathologic changes typically extend to
the end of the affected bone, involving the epiphysis.
Fallen Fragment Sign
In a skeletally immature patient with a centrally located lytic lesion,
the fallen fragment sign is pathognomonic of a simple (unicameral)
bone cyst (Fig. 2-19). It represents fractured cortical bone that has been
displaced into the dependent portion of the cyst. The fallen fragment
appearance is best demonstrated with the patient in an upright position, which allows the fracture fragment to settle into the bottom of
the cyst.
30

Corduroy Sign
The corduroy sign, which is characterized by linear thickenings due to
coarsened bony trabeculae, indicates the presence of a hemangioma.
It is best appreciated in a vertebral body (Fig. 2-20). This sign can be
seen on both radiographic and CT images.
Rind Sign
A thick, sclerotic margin around a lytic lesion occurring in the proximal femur of young individuals is diagnostic of fibrous dysplasia (Fig.
2-21). In elderly patients, this same pattern would be indicative of
liposclerosing myxofibrous tumor (Fig. 2-22).
Cockade Sign
Dystrophic calcification (cockade sign) within a nonaggressive lytic
lesion in the lower extremity is nearly pathognomonic for an

left hemipelvis. All of these finding are classic for Paget disease.

a simple bone cyst. There is a linear density seen in the dependent portion of this
geographic, lytic lesion (arrows).

intraosseous lipoma (see Fig. 2-11). This finding is most common in
lipomas within the calcaneus.
Fluid–Fluid Levels
Fluid–fluid levels are best visualized on MRI but can sometimes be
seen on CT studies. When MRI demonstrates a fluid–fluid level
throughout the majority of a geographic lytic lesion, then the lesion

oriented (arrows) within the lesion.

thick rind sign (arrows) about the geographic focus of fibrous dysplasia in the right

most likely represents an aneurysmal bone cyst. However, if the lesion
appears aggressive on the radiographs or if the fluid–fluid levels make
up only a small portion of the lesion, then other tumors, such as

Fig. 2-23).

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Figure 2-22. Thickened sclerotic border (arrows) about a lytic lesion on this lateral view
of the right hip. This appearance in an elderly patient is consistent with a liposclerosing
myxofibrous tumor.

Figure 2-23. Magnetic resonance image of a giant cell tumor of the left hip. A transverse
fat-saturated T2-weighted image of the pelvis shows multiple fluid–fluid levels (arrows)
within a giant cell tumor involving the greater trochanter of the left hip.

Gas within a Lesion
A geographic lytic lesion close to a joint containing air most likely
represents either a ganglion or a subchondral (degenerative) cyst. A
subchondral cyst is more likely if there is associated osteoarthritis in
the adjacent joint (Fig. 2-24).
Spiculated Margin
A focal sclerotic lesion with a spiculated margin that blends into the
surrounding normal trabecular bone is most likely a bone island. Additional clues are an oblong shape and orientation along the long axis of
the bone (see Fig. 2-13).
Periarticular “Bone Islands”
When multiple sclerotic bone lesions are clustered near joints and have
an appearance of multiple bone islands, then the findings represent
osteopoikilosis (see Fig. 2-15). This entity should not be confused with
blastic metastatic disease.
Cortical and Medullary Continuity of an Osseous Protuberance
An osseous protuberance that demonstrates cortical and medullary
continuity represents an osteochondroma (Fig. 2-25). Osteochondro32

mas can be sessile or pedunculated. When pedunculated and close to
a joint, they will point or grow away from the joint.

Soft Tissue and Joint Masses
The primary imaging modality for evaluating palpable soft tissue
masses is MRI. The major exception to this would be if the palpable
mass is believed to be most likely a ganglion or synovial cyst. In this
setting, ultrasound would be the imaging modality of choice.15 The
appearance of a mass is considered pathognomonic for a benign
cyst if it is anechoic, has a “through transmission,” and has nearly
imperceptible thin walls. Ganglion cysts can contain multiple, thin
septations.16

Magnetic Resonance Evaluation
The evaluation of a soft tissue mass starts with assessing whether its
appearance is diagnostic of a particular tumor. Unfortunately, there is
no set systematic approach for determining whether the MRI appearance of a soft tissue mass is pathognomonic for a particular tumor. The
MRI characteristics should be combined with clinical data (e.g., age of
the patient, location of the tumor, type of surrounding normal tissue).

Patterns in Radiology

An anteroposterior (A) radiograph of the right hip shows
a lytic lesion just superior to the hip (black arrow) joint
as well as joint space narrowing (white arrow) of the right
hip joint representing degenerative joint disease. A
coronal computed tomography (CT) image (B) shows the
joint space narrowing (arrow). A separate coronal CT
image (C) demonstrates the lytic lesion (black arrow)
containing a focus of gas (white arrow). These findings
are characteristic of a subchondral cyst/geode.

If the MRI appearance of the tumor is not pathognomonic, then
further evaluation of the tumor is warranted based on the clinical
history and findings. Most indeterminate soft tissue masses require a
core needle biopsy under CT or ultrasound guidance to arrive at a
specific diagnosis. A discussion of specific soft tissue lesions is beyond
the scope of this review, and interested readers are referred to the many
excellent texts on this topic.17–19

3
Clinical Patterns of Orthopedic Disease
Kevin B. Jones, MD, and Joseph A. Buckwalter, MD

Definitions 35
Musculoskeletal Deformity 35
Musculoskeletal Pain 35
Time 36
Location 36
Patterned Relationships 36
Time versus Pain 36
Time versus Deformity 37
Location versus Pain 40
Location versus Deformity 40
Deformity versus Pain 42
Location, Time, Pain, and Deformity
Conclusions

44

45

It is often said—only partly in jest—that orthopedic surgeons need to
distinguish only two disease processes: fracture and degenerative arthritis. Although it is true that much of clinical orthopedic practice focuses
more on the technical details of exactly what anatomy is directly involved
in a given fracture or degenerative joint, as well as how best surgically to
manage it, there is a broad complexity of other diagnoses that enter the
purview of orthopedic diagnostic exercises, even if the incidence of each
is rare. It is awareness of these other diagnoses, and the necessity to distinguish them from their more common counterparts, that drives the
orthopedic surgeon’s diagnostic efforts.
Although we here argue that orthopedic clinical practice involves
the diagnosis of more than two disease processes, we can settle on
essentially two symptoms behind the presentation of any patient to the
attention of an orthopedic surgeon. Mesenchymal tissues, no matter
how pathologically perturbed, simply cannot on their own generate
nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, urinary incontinence, palpitations, rhinorrhea, cough, photophobia, alopecia, or any of the host of other symptoms considered on the typically thorough review of systems. No,
musculoskeletal diseases can cause only two symptoms: pain and
deformity. When broadly defined, pain and deformity encompass the
entire range of chief complaints that bring patients to see or be referred
to an orthopedic surgeon.

Beyond specifically deformed or painful parts, patients often
present with loss of musculoskeletal function. For the purpose of discussion, lost function will be categorized here as pain when it is driven
by pain preventing some function or as deformity when that lost function is loss in range of motion of a given joint or body part. As musculoskeletal anatomy is defined by the motion it permits, changes in
this motion can be considered as anatomic aberrations in and of themselves. Although some myopathies and purely motor neuropathies,
such as Guillain-Barré syndrome, similarly cause loss of musculoskeletal function, people with these conditions rarely, if ever, present to
orthopedic surgeons for initial diagnostic evaluation.
With these few caveats, musculoskeletal disease is readily encapsulated by the symptoms of pain and deformity. The relationships of pain
and deformity with the other critical variables—time and location—
can define the breadth and depth of the clinical patterns of orthopedic
disease.

Definitions
Musculoskeletal Deformity
The very term orthopedics derives origin from the Greek, meaning
“straighten the child.” However, let us broaden the definition of deformity beyond growth-related deformities of bones to include all musculoskeletal anatomic aberrations, whether perceived by the patient or
by advanced imaging modalities. An anatomic aberration, or deformity, is the focus of most surgical evaluations and treatments, because
anatomy is one thing a surgeon can physically change. But the patterned relationships of deformity with pain, time, and location guide
more than the technical aspects of surgical intervention—they help
render diagnoses.

Musculoskeletal Pain
Pain related to the musculoskeletal system may assume many characteristics, but the language with which each pain type is described in
textbooks and clinical discussions only minimally resembles what
patients communicate. A patient goes to, or is referred to, a surgeon
because he or she hurts somewhere. Whether this is the “burning” pain
of a lumbar radiculopathy or the “aching” pain of arthritis probably
has more to do with the clinician’s editing or “leading” of the history
35

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Time
Time figures directly into three of the eight descriptive features considered helpful in the consideration of any symptom in medical history
taking, as defined by the long-used acronym “OLD CARTS” (onset,
location, duration, character, aggravating factors, relieving factors,
timing, and severity). However, in addition to Onset, Duration, and
Timing, time figures indirectly into at least five, if not six of the eight
features when Aggravating factors and Relieving factors, as well as possibly Character, are seen in the light of what factors are coincident in
time with the symptom or relief from the symptom. Patterns of modulation in musculoskeletal pain and/or deformity by time are absolutely
critical to the differential diagnosis considered in the evaluation of the
patient’s complaint.

Location
Although the precise anatomic location of each pain and deformity is
what broadens the few common orthopedic diseases into the vast
technical field of orthopedic surgery, it also contributes by a few patterns to diagnostic thinking. First, the relationship between pain and
deformity according to location is critical to the workup of any musculoskeletal pain complaint. Second, categorizing skeletal deformities
or lesions as diaphyseal, metaphyseal, epiphyseal, or intra-articular can
guide the differential diagnosis. A further designation of where a lesion
is in terms of depth in bone is also helpful, such as intraosseous, surface,
or combination intraosseous and surface locations. These location patterns as modulators of pain and deformity will often direct not only
the steps taken to achieve diagnosis but also the final diagnosis itself.

Patterned Relationships
Time versus Pain
Large, generalized groups of pathologic diagnoses can be divided into
a few patterns according to the pace of disease (Fig. 3-1). First, there
is variability in the way the patient comes to the attention of the clinician. The range of diagnoses pertinent to the emergency department,
the inpatient consultation, the self-referred outpatient consultation, the
primary care–referred outpatient consultation, and the tertiary outpatient consultation referred from another orthopedic surgeon are quite
different.
The types of diagnoses that bring a patient to an emergency department and prompt an orthopedic consult are typically related to acute
mechanical failure of some portion of musculoskeletal anatomy. Even
if a disease is present underlying the acute injury, it is nonetheless
punctuated by the injury—even if small in energy—that adds urgency
to the patient’s complaint. Bacterial infections in musculoskeletal
regions of the body also present to the emergency department, but
there are very few diagnoses not punctuated by either acute bacterial
infection or mechanical failure of some limb or the spine that prompt
an emergency orthopedic consultation. To the emergency department
come diagnoses that evolve over seconds, minutes, and hours.
The next step down in the acuity of presentation is an inpatient
consultation from another medical service. This level of timing can still
include acute mechanical failures. However, these failures usually
occur only if a new fall has taken place in the hospital or a prior fall
has been suddenly discovered after admission for another diagnosis
that compromised mentation or the ability to render a history, such as
36

A

C

B

Pain and/or Deformity

after seeing some imaging study than it does with the patient’s actual
undirected complaint. Pain has more variation between the individuals
experiencing it than it ever could between the pathologies causing it.
Therefore, for the purposes of pattern discussion, we will consider pain
as pain in all its forms, modulated much more critically by time and
location than by any honestly discernible character.

D

E
Time
Figure 3-1. Many patterns of disease are quite identifiable simply by the relationship
between time and pain and/or deformity. Line A represents an acute injury; line B, a
rapidly progressive process such as an infection; line C, a slowly progressive process
punctuated by injury, such as a pathologic fracture following antecedent pain; line D,
a slow process such as a tumor growing over weeks and months or a degenerative
joint developing over years; and line E, an inflammatory process with waxing and
waning symptoms but an often self-limited overall course.

a syncopal episode or head injury in an elderly person. Much more
common to the realm of inpatient consultations are acute or subacute
bacterial infections or impending pathologic fractures, both frequently
over the background of ongoing nonmusculoskeletal cancer treatments or other causes of immunocompromise. These are diseases
evolving over hours, days, and occasionally weeks (in the setting of
long-term cancer-related inpatient stints).
Due to increased demand for and access to specialist services
without the requirement for physician referral, many patients will
present themselves to a community orthopedic surgeon without prior
screening of their symptoms under the care of a primary care physician. Although the range of diagnoses that can present via this mechanism remains broad, the frequency of self-limited processes vastly
outweighs entities that require more thorough diagnostic investigation.
In fact, convincing patients to wait a couple of weeks before pursuit of
complex diagnostics will prevent—rather than just postpone—the
pursuit altogether for a large number of patients. Whether it is muscular low back pain that resolves or dermatomal pain that turns out to
be the prerash stage of a herpes zoster flare, the diagnosis may become
clear without any more diagnostic effort than a follow-up in 2 weeks.
Many musculoskeletal afflictions that evolve over days will also resolve
over a few more days.
There can be a slight difference in the time patterns between
primary care–referred outpatient consultations and orthopedicreferred tertiary consultations, but either usually passes the 2-week
rule for waiting. Outpatient consultation presentations will tend to be
secondary to pain and/or deformity that has lasted at least days to
weeks, with the tertiary referrals from other orthopedic surgeons more
commonly from symptoms lasting weeks to months. The process of
care in accomplishing referrals simply requires a disease process that
is progressing at a subacute rate. Deformity or pain progressing steadily
over weeks and months removes the focus from self-limited pain and
acute infections and injuries, directing it toward chronic infections and
both benign-aggressive and malignant neoplastic diseases (Fig. 3-2).
Other benign neoplasms and hamartomas tend to develop instead over
months to years, as do degenerative and developmental diagnoses.1
Another aspect of the pain-time relationship is the coincidence in
time of pain with activity or inactivity. Musculoskeletal diseases tend

Clinical Patterns of Orthopedic Disease

B

A

Figure 3-2. This chondrosarcoma in the periacetabular innominate bone
caused such a mild ache in the groin that the patient chose to ignore it for 4
years. When over a few weeks, he began having mechanical pain with hip motion
as well as increasing night pain, he sought medical attention, thinking he must
have a labral tear. On pathologic evaluation, most of the lesion appeared to be
low-grade, but the area growing into the hip joint and another small area
penetrating the cortex and forming a small soft tissue mass demonstrated
intermediate-grade histopathology. The clinical pace of the bulk of the lesion fit
with its low-grade character, but the pace of what finally prompted evaluation
better fit a more aggressive lesion, consistent with the areas of more aggressive
pathologic changes. Anteroposterior radiograph of the left hip (A) and coronal
(B) and axial (C) plane computed tomographies demonstrate the bone destructive lesion (arrows), making cartilaginous matrix.

3

C

to fall into three patterns of interaction between pain and time as it
relates to activity. Pain most noticeably coincident in time with activity
tends to be caused by mechanical failure or impending failure of the
skeleton or degeneration-related mechanical pain in a joint or muscletendon unit. The discernment of this pattern in the workup of a
destructive lesion in the bone is critical not only for management but
also for diagnosis. If all the pain associated with a lesion correlates with
activity, the disease is unlikely to be generating direct pain from its
interaction with the bone. Mechanical pain purely coincident in time
with activity often favors a biologically less aggressive diagnosis. For
example, a simple bone cyst, fibrous dysplasia, or a nonossifying
fibroma can generate pain coincident in time with activity if by location
and size it has weakened the structural integrity of the surrounding
bone (Fig. 3-3).
More aggressive processes, such as malignancies primary or metastatic to a bone, will typically have at least some component of pain
unrelated in time to activity. A lesion of either infectious or neoplastic
character that is growing rapidly within a bone will cause this pattern
of pain; this pattern is not only unrelated to activity but also usually
more prominent at rest, especially when the patient is not actively
engaged in something. Such night pain or pain at rest is considered a
somewhat ominous sign when appreciated in the evaluation of a lesion
in the bone (Fig. 3-4). Bone destruction by lesional growth is not the
only mechanism by which this pattern of pain can be produced.
Lesions that elute nociceptive chemicals or factors can also cause this
pattern of rest or night pain. Examples of these would be osteoid
osteoma and chondroblastoma (Fig. 3-5). Again, the connection
between these and more destructively aggressive lesions in terms of
pain pattern is that pain most noticeable outside of activity, and even
blunted by activity, arises from the biology of the lesion, rather than
impending structural failure of the surrounding bone.
The third pattern of the pain-time relationship as it relates to activity is pain most prominent postactivity or during the initiation of an
activity. This periactivity pain usually exposes an inflammatory component to whatever disease process is underlying. This pain pattern is
usually expressed by patients as “paying for it later” after doing more
than they should. Inflammatory muscle afflictions or inflamed arthritic
joints might not generate much pain in the midst of activity, but the

initiation of activity and the hours postactivity are usually cumbersomely painful.

Time versus Deformity
Musculoskeletal deformity, again broadly defined as an anatomic aberration from normal, also varies in its relationship with time according
a few recognizable patterns.

Figure 3-3. The thick rind of reactive bone surrounding the superior lateral aspect of
this focus of fibrous dysplasia in the femoral neck argues that it has been present for
a long time. Groin pain coincident only with activity brought the 15-year-old boy with
this lesion (arrow) to his primary care physician. Rather than aggressive biology of the
lesion, impending fracture fit the clinical pattern and informed management decision
making.
37

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

D

Figure 3-4. This melanoma metastasis to the femur diaphysis fits the pattern of pain related to an aggressive biological process rather than an immediately impending fracture
from severely compromised structural integrity. This patient had no pain coincident in time to activity, even with activities including skiing, snowboarding, and mountain bicycling.
Instead, he found it difficult to concentrate on his work as an electrician, secondary to nagging thigh pain when he was still and otherwise inactive. Anteroposterior radiographs
of the distal (A) and proximal (B) femur as well as coronal (C) and axial (D) T2-weighted MRI slices demonstrate the destructive lesion (arrows).

Figure 3-5. The lytic lesion in the talus (arrow) of this 20-year-old woman is surrounded by dense reactive bone, suggesting latent behavior or, at most, very slow bone
destruction. Nonetheless, the patient presented with a deep ache in the hind foot,
coincident in time with inactivity and night time, specifically. Because the clinical pattern
fits pain related to the biology of the lesion, but the imaging made an aggressively
destructive lesion unlikely, chondroblastoma was immediately at the top of the differential diagnosis.

38

Diseases of disrupted structural integrity generate deformity that
evolves over seconds or even shorter times. Whether this is the sudden
instability of a fracture, the recurrently sudden instability of a disrupted anterior cruciate ligament, or sudden inability to extend the
knee from a quadriceps rupture, all generate momentary deformity.
Even a chronically generated rotator cuff tear will cause momentary
deformity in the form of the failure of arm elevation, primarily only
noticeable during the immediate attempt.
Deformity evolving over weeks and months is more concerning for
a growing process, either neoplastic or infectious. The pace can be
instructive as to the disease aggressiveness. Masses that develop rapidly
over hours and days are very rarely neoplastic but commonly infectious
in character. Extremely aggressive, usually malignant, neoplasms can
demonstrate a pattern of growth over weeks. Less aggressive neoplasms
can cause a mass to develop over months or even nearly imperceptibly
over years. Gathering from patients which pattern a noticed mass
deformity fits into can guide diagnostic considerations.
Two patterns of the time–deformity relationship characteristically
evolve over years. The distinction between the two is also determined
by time—but by time during life rather than by pace of deformity. The
pattern of deformity progression over years coincident to periods of
skeletal growth encompasses a large number of developmental and
heritable genetic diagnoses. These range from idiopathic scoliosis, to
Blount disease (tibia vara), to osteochondromas, and to Ollier disease.
The deformities inherent to each of these stabilize on attainment of
skeletal maturity unless they have surpassed a threshold of deformity
that leads to progressive disease (possible in each of these diagnoses,
from different intrinsic biologies at work). Managing diseases with this
pattern of deformity changing over years during growth is complex. It
involves precise measurements and careful record-keeping as surveillance of the pace of progression and confirmation of cessation of progression on attainment of skeletal maturity (Fig. 3-6). Departures from
this steady pattern tend to elevate concern and frequently prompt

Clinical Patterns of Orthopedic Disease

Figure 3-6. The deformities present in this patient with multiple enchondromatosis,
or Ollier disease, were followed closely since the completion of skeletal growth to
confirm that they maintain the pattern of growth only during skeletal growth and not
afterward, which might suggest malignant change of one of the enchondromas to
secondary chondrosarcoma. The white arrows indicate regions of bone with endosteal
scalloping from the lesion but little change to overall bone shape. The black arrow
demonstrates a region of bone expansion from direct involvement of the cortex.

A

intervention or even further diagnostic evaluation, such as a biopsy for
Ollier deformity or an osteochondroma progressive after skeletal
maturity.
The other deformity pattern that evolves over years is coincident
with advancing age rather than with skeletal growth. End-stage osteoarthritis frequently generates varus deformity not entirely dissimilar to
that of Blount disease, but obviously the age range is easily distinguishable by this pattern (Fig. 3-7). An analogous example comes from
degenerative scoliosis, which can generate similar deformities at a
similar pace to adolescent idiopathic scoliosis, but does so much later
in life and by an entirely distinct disease process.
A distinct pattern of the deformity-time relationship is that of
waxing and waning deformity over time. Into this pattern fit primarily
benign and often inflammatory diagnoses. Inflammatory arthropathies
will typically cause relapsing and remitting thickening of the synovium
and joint effusions that come and go. These can be readily distinguished by clinical pattern from the progressive, relentless growth of a
neoplastic disorder of the same tissues, such as pigmented villonodular
synovitis (Fig. 3-8).
Specific discussion of the subtle deformities identifiable only with
advanced imaging is very important to consideration of musculoskeletal pathology. These necessarily lead to an initially indiscernible relationship between deformity and time. When a lesion in bone is
discovered by imaging, either incidentally or in the investigation of
musculoskeletal pain, only a snapshot in time is available. This makes
the pace of disease impossible to ascertain with any precision. Nonetheless, the pace of the deformity, or anatomic aberration, is perhaps
the most important variable in the determination of appropriate management. It is arguable that the progression of a cartilaginous lesion in
a bone over time may be more instructive than even pathologic evaluation of a biopsy from the same lesion.2 The challenge derives from the
initially indiscernible relationship with time. Much of diagnostic
radiographic evaluation of bone lesions comes from assessment of

pace of disease but easily distinguished by the age of the patient alone.

Figure 3-8. Pigmented villonodular synovitis, a neoplastic
synovial thickening in a joint, follows a pattern of relentless
and progressive growth over time, rather than waxing and
waning swelling, which is more indicative of an inflammatory
monoathropathy. T1-weighted (A) and T2-weighted (B) MR
images in the sagittal plane demonstrate a mass lesion in
the anterior and posterior ankle joint space that has low

B

C

snapshot-apparent clues to this time-deformity relationship. Detailed
discussion of the estimations of the time-deformity relationship from
radiographs of bone lesions will be deferred to the chapter focused on
radiology. Suffice it to say here that an estimation is made from the
available snapshot in time, and a judgment is made as to how aggressively and urgently diagnosis must be pursued.3 For many lesions suspected by initial imaging to be latent, conservative management in the
form of interval radiographs to establish the pace of deformity change
over time (or lack thereof) provides the most definitive diagnostic
directive (Fig. 3-9).

Location versus Pain
Although the character of pain as described by patients without leading
questions is not easily categorized into terribly helpful patterns, the
location of pain can fall into very useful patterns. In fact, it is often the
location-distribution of pain that initially directs the physician to begin
probing with questions aimed at eliciting the textbook character of
certain pain generators.
One pattern of pain location is pain coincident with a noted deformity. This pattern contends that the disease itself is causing pain by its
biology or by the immediate effects it has on the structural integrity of
the surrounding tissues.
A variety of patterns of pain location arise from pain not coincident
with a noted deformity. First, there are regions of the body with specific
referred pain patterns. Pathology in the shoulder, proximal humerus, or
subacromial space is frequently felt in the anterolateral midproximal
brachium, near the insertion of the deltoid muscle. Hip joint pathology
is most frequently felt in the groin but occasionally in the buttocks. Less
frequently, hip pathologies in younger patients will be felt primarily in
the medial distal thigh, anatomically remote from the hip itself.4 Knowledge of these patterns of regionally referred pain is critical to avoiding
evaluations that may entirely neglect the real location of pathology.
Another pattern of pain location is radicular pain. While a few
patients will happen upon the textbook definitions of the character of
pain, including mention of tingling or burning, most can describe
where they hurt, which can often fit a dermatomal location. Pain in a
given dermatome or dermatomes can lead to investigation of the
40

appropriate location near the spine, but it also narrows the differential
diagnosis to lesions that can physically impinge on nerve roots.5 L4
dermatomal pain indicates that something is pushing on the L4 nerve
root. It may be a herniated fragment of the adjacent intervertebral disk,
a synovial cyst from the adjacent facet joint, or a soft tissue sarcoma
in the retroperitoneum, but something must be deforming or otherwise irritating that nerve root to generate pain in that specific location
(Fig. 3-10).
The third pattern of pain located distant from pathology is that of
pain in the distribution of a peripheral nerve. Although this pattern
can be difficult to distinguish from a radicular pattern of pain, the
distinction is not impossible. Pain in a peripheral nerve distribution
renders a longer region of potentially involved anatomy but a somewhat narrower group of disease processes. Spinal nerve roots tend to
be fairly confined by their anatomic surroundings and can be irritated
by very small mass lesions, whereas peripheral nerves tend to be surrounded by highly compliant soft tissues except in very specific regions
such as the carpal or tarsal tunnel or the sciatic notch (Fig. 3-11).
Therefore, other than ganglia or small mass lesions specific to these
constrained locales, a mass lesion must either be very large, very
rapidly progressive, or directly involving the nerve itself to cause symptoms in its sensory distribution.

Location versus Deformity
The patterned relationship between deformity and location is quite
instructive in generating a differential diagnosis. Often, determination
of the location-deformity relationship depends on imaging, but it is
nonetheless part of the clinical assessment of any symptom. Lesions
can be subcategorized as those common in the diaphyses, metaphyses,
or epiphyses of bone. Intra-articular lesions represent a distinct pattern
of their own. Finally, the depth location of a lesion within a bone
follows other patterns, such as central, eccentric, or surface lesions, as
well as lesions that are both central and include surface extension into
the soft tissues.
Most bone pathologies can present in the metaphyses of bones. This
makes a lesion’s location in the epiphysis or diaphysis of bone especially
instructive to the differential diagnosis. Purely diaphyseal lesions tend

Clinical Patterns of Orthopedic Disease

3

A

C

B

D

Figure 3-9. Although the anteroposterior radiograph of this likely enchondroma (arrows in each panel) at presentation (D) offers only a brief snapshot in the time-deformity
relationship, prior radiographs had been obtained from earlier years for unrelated bilateral knee pain. As the radiographs 13 (A), 8 (B), and 3 (C) years prior to presentation show
the same very subtle lytic lesion but demonstrate that it has progressively calcified and matured over the time interval, major concerns about biological aggressiveness are readily
alleviated.

to be infection, eosinophilic granuloma, or Ewing sarcoma. Other possibilities exist, but these few tend to be the primary diagnostic exclusions necessary. The rare lesions that are purely epiphyseal in location
can also include infections, but these commonly lead to a diagnosis of
osteonecrosis, osteochondritis dissecans, or chondroblastoma in the
skeletally immature. Giant cell tumor of bone follows a frequent
pattern of both metaphyseal involvement and epiphyseal extension; it
is more common in skeletally mature young adults.
The range of entities that cause a mass lesion in or associated with
a joint is fairly narrow and is pared down further with respect to pace

of disease. For the pace of disease that develops over weeks and months
to even years, synovial chondromatosis and pigmented villonodular
synovitis are two of the only diagnoses not related to what would be
otherwise obvious systemic disease, such as an inflammatory arthropathy or hemophilia. A further designation of deformity location in joints
is which joints and how many joints are affected by a similar swelling
or synovial thickening. Patterns of small versus large joints, as well as
symmetric bilateral or asymmetric pauciarticular involvement, can
guide clinical diagnosis toward or away from the varied degenerative
and inflammatory arthropathies.
41

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

Figure 3-10. A 43-year-old man presented with persistent and worsening pain along his chest wall, beneath his axilla. When the pain was especially bad, he would even feel it
extending around to the front of his chest on that side, as well as down his posterior brachium and even into the ulnar side of his forearm. This clearly radicular location of pain,
once recognized, led to rapid identification of this osteoblastoma (arrows) in the posterior elements of the second (A) and third (B) thoracic vertebrae.

A

B

C

Figure 3-11. As is common with pelvic neoplasms, this patient failed to notice even
the large buttocks soft tissue mass associated with this nonosteogenic spindle sarcoma
of bone in the ilium. He presented to medical attention with the clinical pattern of pain
preceding the recognition of deformity, indicative of an aggressive process in the bone.
In addition to having an ache coincident in location to the tumor, he also had pain in
the sciatic nerve sensory distribution whenever he sat very long, due to the tumor
mechanically impinging on his sciatic nerve as it exited the sciatic notch. Anteroposterior
pelvis radiograph (A) and axial (B) and coronal (C) T2-weighted MR images demonstrate the large bone destructive tumor (arrows).

The depth of a lesion in the bone also follows specific patterns.
Central lesions that fill the entire intramedullary canal or are centered
away from the surface of bone include specific possibilities, such as
unicameral bone cysts and fibrous dysplasia. As for differentiating
central from eccentric lesions, consider the comparison of
42

osteochondritis dissecans to chondroblastoma. Both can arise in the
skeletally immature. Both can cause pain, although the osteochondritis
dissecans will cause more activity-related pain and the chondroblastoma more rest pain. Both have a similar appearance on imaging, often
including a sclerotic rim of bone around the lesional tissue. However,
the two are readily distinguished in almost all cases because the osteochondritis dissecans lesion is eccentric in the bone, at the surface of
the joint, rather than deeply embedded in the epiphyseal bone, where
chondroblastoma is more commonly found.
Bone lesions with a palpable extension into the soft-tissue fall into
two narrowly defined categories: true surface lesions and central
lesions with soft tissue extension. Most surface-only lesions of bone are
either benign or low-grade malignant neoplasms. These include osteochondromas, periosteal chondromas, benign parosteal osteochondromatous proliferations (Nora lesion), and parosteal osteosarcomas.
Deformities that extend both into the bone and into the soft tissues
include a more ominous list of aggressive malignancies such as osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma/primitive neuroectodermal tumor family of
tumors, lymphoma of bone, and chondrosarcoma (Fig. 3-12).
Based more on imaging than on history, the precise definition of
the location of any deformity can influence the differential diagnosis
and subsequent management.

Deformity versus Pain
Pain can arise in the complete absence of a discernible deformity, even
when deformity is broadly defined as any anatomic aberration from
normal. Well-recognized patterns of pain without appreciable deformity can occasionally truncate what would otherwise be an exhaustive
workup, including a variety of expensive imaging modalities. Such
recognized patterns of pain without anatomic abnormality are anterior
knee pain,6 low back pain,7 and chronic regional pain syndrome. Obviously, there are anatomic aberrations that can cause similar complaints,
and these must be ruled out in each case, but these syndromes of pain
can exist in the complete absence of a discernible anatomic
aberration.
More commonly, the onus lies with the clinician to be certain that
rather than pain without deformity, the real situation is not pain with

Clinical Patterns of Orthopedic Disease

3

A

B

C

D

Figure 3-12. The young man with this distal femur chondrosarcoma revealed when questioned only that he had noticed a very mild ache. Instead, he presented for a soft tissue
lump in his distal thigh. That the lump was firmly attached to bone fit the pattern of either a benign surface lesion of bone or an aggressive intraosseous neoplasm, the latter of
which was discovered on imaging. Anteroposterior (A) and lateral (B) radiographs and T1-weighted (C) and T2-weighted (D) MR images of the distal femur demonstrate the large,
aggressive tumor.

deformity that is only discernible by specific, not-yet-attempted investigations8 (Fig. 3-13). One common example is the earliest stage of
osteonecrosis of the femoral head. To the previously standard staging
systems had to be added a new “predecessor” stage when magnetic
resonance imaging (MRI) became available and demonstrated that
some patients have osteonecrosis only discernible by MRI and not by
the previously standard conventional radiographs.9
Conversely, deformity frequently arises in the complete absence of
pain. Pediatric orthopedic clinics are full of patients with painless
deformities such as scoliosis, club foot, and developmental dysplasia of
the hip. In these situations, although the deformity is painless, the
sequelae of the deformity later in life can include disabling pain, the

desire for prevention of which often prompts early treatment. Other
painless deformities include painless bone lesions, which are usually
also biologically latent. A bigger challenge is that many frequently
painful deformities are not always painful.10 For example asymptomatic rotator cuff tears are very common in the elderly. Not all degenerative meniscal tears cause significant painful symptoms.
The fact of these asymptomatic degenerative deformities can especially challenge the diagnostic workup of some lesions when multiple
unrelated deformities exist in the setting of regional pain. As bone
pathologies most commonly arise in the metaphyses of bone and joint
pain is common from a variety of less concerning etiologies, it can
become necessary but difficult to determine if a given lesion in the
43

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C

D

E

F

Figure 3-13. The anteroposterior (A) pelvis and lateral (B) proximal femur radiographs, obtained 9 months into an extensive evaluation of proximal thigh pain, which also included
a normal lumbar spine magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), failed to demonstrate the lesion that was only detectable at the imaged area by bone scan or MRI. Either complete
femur radiographs (C and D) or a femur MRI (E and F) might have prevented the patient’s pathologic fracture (arrows) 1 month later, which prompted medical evaluation and
diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma. The clinical pattern of this case was pain prior to noted deformity but not pain in the absence of deformity as was suspected for many months by the
managing physician.

bone is causing pain beyond or to the exclusion of pain from a degenerative pathology in the nearby joint. Because early changes of osteoarthritis and degenerative meniscal tears are extremely common,
discerning whether knee pain arises from one of these or from a lesion
in the distal femur can be quite difficult (Fig. 3-14). Discerning whether
a proximal humerus lesion is generating pain and merits further investigation may be difficult due to the presence of a rotator cuff tear on
the same imaging that showed the lesion. This challenge adds importance to the orthopedic training background needed for sarcoma surgeons; they must be able to judge the contribution of background
degenerative conditions to the symptoms that bring a bone lesion to
clinical attention.

Location, Time, Pain, and Deformity
When pain and deformity are coincident in location but differ in time
of onset, the order of onset for each is important. Whether a deformity
44

is a fracture, or just the imaging-driven recognition of an abnormality
in the bone, determining whether pain came before or after recognition of the bone deformity defines a crucial pattern. When a lesion in
a bone has been incidentally noted or has caused a fracture, driving its
recognition, it may very well become painful after recognition of the
deformity. Not only is a fracture clearly painful, but even new found
awareness of an incidentally noted bone lesion can often cause patients
to convince themselves that it must then be generating pain since it is
there. This pattern of pain following deformity in time is importantly
distinct from the pattern of a bone lesion whose discovery through
fracture or simple imaging investigation was preceded by the recognition of pain in the same location (Fig. 3-15). When the imaging-driven
or fracture-driven recognition of a bone lesion deformity is preceded
by a recognized pain in the bone, concern is raised significantly that
the biology of the lesion is suggestive of a malignant or aggressively
benign lesion (Fig. 3-16).

Clinical Patterns of Orthopedic Disease

3

A

B

C

D

E

Figure 3-14. This 58-year-old man returned to medical attention because his total knee replacement 4 years prior continued to provide no relief from his knee pain. A bone scan
(E), presumably ordered to assess for the possibility of loose implants, brought increased attention to a destructive lesion in his femur (arrows), but the lesion was also apparent
on radiographs (A and B). Anteroposterior (C) and lateral (D) plane reconstructions from a CT scan also demonstrate the lesion and the bone reaction surrounding it. Upon further
questioning, the patient had had an infected femur fracture as an 8-year-old boy in the Pacific islands. The lesion turned out to be reactivated chronic osteomyelitis; treatment with
aggressive débridement and intravenous antibiotics made the knee pain resolve entirely. Most likely, the total knee arthroplasty was an unnecessary step along this route to resolution of symptoms, undertaken by failure to appropriately discern the location of the pain generator in the bone rather than in the joint.

A

B

Figure 3-15. These two pathologic fractures through distal femur cartilaginous lesions (arrows) differed in clinical pattern in that the case in panel A had a mild antecedent pain
prior to the injury. This mild pain had awoken the patient at night intermittently over the weeks prior to injury. This patient was diagnosed with dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma
in an otherwise low-grade cartilaginous lesion. The patient in panel B had a benign, likely latent, enchondroma. The pattern of clinical presentation differentiated the two nearly
as well as the tissue diagnosis.

The opposite patterns in the pain-time-deformity relationship are
noteworthy in joint conditions. There, the pattern of pain preceding
the recognition of deformity is usually indicative of benign character.
An aggressive neoplastic process such as pigmented villonodular synovitis or synovial chondromatosis tends to cause pain late, long after the
mass is present, whereas inflammatory or post-traumatic causes of
synovial thickening tend to follow pain in time.

Conclusions
Detailed clinical analysis of these four critical variables of pain, deformity, time, and location can establish the pattern of most musculo­
skeletal diseases. With the simple addition of noninvasive imaging, the
differential diagnosis remaining for a given clinical pattern can usually
be deciphered. Only a few patterns of disease, usually those with either
45

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C

Figure 3-16. Although the patient with this lymphoma of bone in the distal femur presented to medical attention after a fracture that by mechanism was obviously pathologic
(gardening in the sitting position), the history also indicated that prior to the sudden onset of severe pain and deformity, the patient had noticed a nagging ache in the thigh for
a few weeks. Pain antecedent to fracture is a concerning clinical pattern, suggestive of an aggressive biological process as opposed to simply a mechanical weakening of the host
bone. A plain radiograph (A), T1-weighted (B) and T2-weighted (C) coronal plane MR images demonstrate the pathologic fracture (arrows) through what turned out to be a lymphoma of bone.

a pathologic fracture or a pace of pain and/or deformity progression
over weeks and months, will prompt the procurement of tissue for
histopathologic diagnostic assessment.
Even the cases that fall into a pattern for which pathologic diagnosis
will be necessary have specific patterns in which pathologic questions
are asked and patterns in which the diagnostic information rendered
will be used. One such pattern arises when a pathologic specimen is
sent, but no modulation of future management is intended. For
example, resected osteochondromas are often sent for pathologic
assessment, but the diagnosis is rarely in question prior to the excisional surgery.
Another pattern is confirmatory pathology in the midst of an
ongoing surgery, such as frozen section diagnosis immediately prior to
but under the same anaesthetic as pathologic fracture fixation. When
the clinical pattern of the disease has suggested either nonbiologically
aggressive disease with mechanical failure only, the information provided may be used immediately to determine to proceed with fixation.
Similarly, with pathologic fractures that share a history of metastatic
carcinoma or melanoma, the decision to proceed on confirmatory
frozen section is likely. Another confirmatory pathology situation is
the use of frozen sectioning to determine polymorphonuclear leukocyte counts per high-power field during revision total knee arthroplasty. If the clinical picture has otherwise reduced the chance for
infection, the surgeon will use pathology as a final confirmation that
it is safe to proceed.
A third pattern of confirmatory pathology is in the treatment of
benign aggressive or benign latent bone lesions with impending pathologic fractures. There also, the frozen section diagnosis will be used to
proceed with a larger, intentionally definitive operation under the same
anaesthetic. These interpretations should be rendered with caution. As
the interventions that await the frozen section confirmation of diagnosis will very likely have drastic implications for any future oncologic
46

procedures that would be necessary if the permanent section diagnosis
differs from the frozen section diagnosis, it is preferable to err on the
side of caution. Although a second anaesthetic has some inherent
additional risk, it is much less than the risks to life and limb of a major
surgery to stabilize what is felt to be a benign lesion, only to have the
diagnosis changed to a sarcoma after permanent sections are
available.
Finally, there is the standard pattern of formal pathologic evaluation of a biopsy specimen from which decisions will be made either to
ignore, excise, radiate and resect, or treat with chemotherapy and
resect a tumor or lesion in the bone. For this specific pattern of pathology involvement, it is best to engage in a multidisciplinary review of
the case with at least the surgeon, pathologist, and radiologist present.
The most important conclusion a pathologist can draw from the
relatively simple and highly instructive clinical patterns of musculo­
skeletal disease is that the information needed to understand the clinical pattern for a given patient must be shared in communication
between the pathologist, radiologist, and surgeon. Usually the patient’s
age and a one-sentence summary of the timing and location of any
pain and deformity can efficiently guide analysis of any tissues submitted. The clinical pattern generates the question that only the pathologist can definitively answer. Failure to identify and communicate this
question can lead not only to wasted effort but to faulty conclusions
as well.
