B

Figure 5-12. Osteoid osteoma. This bone scan of a femoral neck osteoid osteoma
shows small area of intense isotope uptake. A less positive and diffuse uptake halo
known as the “double density sign” can sometimes be seen.
Figure 5-13. Osteoid osteoma. This gross photograph demonstrates a reddish hypervascular nidus buried within the reactive new bone surrounding it.

Figure 5-14. Osteoid osteoma. This low-power view demonstrates a circumscribed
bone-forming lesion that abruptly stops at the endosteal cortex. The bone is micro­
trabecular and corresponds to the area of mineralization on computed tomography.
72

Figure 5-15. Osteoid osteoma. At higher power, the bony trabeculae are lined by
active osteoblasts, and there is a fibrovascular stroma containing multinucleated
osteoclasts.

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology
Differential Diagnosis
Osteoid osteoma should be differentiated from osteomyelitis, Brodie
abscess, bone island, osteoblastoma, and intracortical osteosarcoma.
The absence of pain, together with a negative radionuclide bone scan,
can differentiate intramedullary osteoid osteoma from a bone island.
However, the same does not apply to osteomyelitis, a bone abscess, and
intracortical osteosarcoma, for these conditions show avid radionuclide uptake as well. Furthermore, a small intracortical sequestrum can
be mistaken for the nidus of an osteoid osteoma on conventional
radiographs and CT scans. Biopsy of the lesion is in most cases
diagnostic, provided thorough clinical and radiologic correlation
is performed.
Osteoblastoma shows identical histologic features with osteoid
osteoma, being different only in its unrestricted growth potential
and subsequent increased local aggressiveness as well as its pre­
dilection for the axial skeleton. Correlation with radiographic
appearance is paramount in differentiating osteoid osteoma from
osteoblastoma.1,10,11,19
Treatment and Prognosis
The natural history of osteoid osteoma is difficult to assess. Most
patients seek medical attention primarily because of increasingly
severe pain. The limited growth potential of this lesion, however, was
reported in several small case series, with an average of 2 years’ duration of symptoms followed by clinical and even radiologic remission.31,32 Several treatment modalities are currently available.
NSAID therapy is very efficient in alleviating the symptoms of
osteoid osteoma, and it is recommended in those rare cases in which
the pain is not severe. However, long-term NSAID use, with its significant side effects as well as the development of disuse muscle atrophy
or limb length discrepancy, usually warrants a more aggressive treatment approach.29,33
There are several forms of surgical treatment:

• Curettage and bone grafting. This was one of the first surgical
•

•

methods of treatment to offer patients relief from pain. However, a
recurrence rate of up to 17% has been reported with this method,
which is currently reserved for those cases where other types of
treatment cannot be used.
Intralesional resection under direct visualization of the nidus. This
was advocated by Campanacci and associates in a study of 100
consecutive osteoid osteoma patients, 89 of which received said
procedure, with 100% primary cure at a mean follow-up interval of
6 years. It has the advantage of smaller recovery times and less
postoperative morbidity with similar long-term results when compared to en bloc resection.34
En bloc resection. This offers the advantage of permanent cure. The
method has a definite risk of postoperative fracture, which sometimes requires the use of extensive reconstructive procedures.
Depending on tumor location, this may or may not be feasible.1,35

Percutaneous CT-guided radiofrequency thermal ablation and laser
photocoagulation are the newest additions to the treatment arsenal
of osteoid osteoma. The methods offer excellent clinical outcome.
However, they also pose two main problems: lack of sufficient histologic material for diagnostic confirmation and restricted use in the
axial skeleton due to potential risk of injury of the neuraxis.36,37
Even without treatment, the prognosis of osteoid osteoma is excellent due to its limited growth potential. Symptom remission is expected
in approximately 3 years for most lesions.6 Also, rare cases of recurrent
osteoid osteoma with subsequent transformation into osteoblastoma
have been reported in the literature. To date, no malignant transformation of an osteoid osteoma has been described.

Osteoblastoma
Definition and Synonyms
Osteoblastoma or giant osteoid osteoma is a rare, benign, boneforming neoplasm that produces woven bone spicules bordered by
prominent osteoblasts.16

5

Brief Historical Overview
In 1932, Jaffe and Mayer described what would later be recognized as
the first documented case of osteoblastoma: an “osteoblastic osteoid
tissue-forming tumor” arising in the fourth metacarpal bone of a
15-year-old girl. The authors noted its benign nature. A few years later,
in 1951, in his proposed classification system of primary bone tumors,
Lichtenstein included the already described and accepted osteoid
osteoma together with “other osteoid tissue-forming tumors,” which
he described as tumors characterized by “formation of abundant
osteoid tissue, much of it uncalcified, within a background of osteoblastic connective tissue.” Although he described these neoplasms as
having the potential to become quite large, he, too, recognized their
benign nature and lack of metastatic potential.
In 1954, Dahlin and Johnson described 11 cases with histologic
features similar to osteoid osteoma. Although they were much larger,
the researchers termed the condition “giant osteoid osteoma.”
The term “benign osteoblastoma” was independently coined by
Jaffe and Lichtenstein in 1956. They used it to describe a benign boneforming and osteoid-forming neoplasm with the potential to be locally
destructive and with a predilection for the vertebral column. Both
authors noted that the neoplasm lacked cellular pleomorphism, abnormal mitotic activity, and tumor cartilage, features that distinguish it
from osteosarcoma.38–42
Incidence and Demographics
Osteoblastoma is a rare benign bone tumor, approximately five times
less common than osteoid osteoma. The lesion accounts for fewer than
1% of all primary osseous neoplasms. Like osteoid osteoma, it is commonly seen in adolescents and young adults with a peak incidence in
patients between 10 and 30 years of age. Nevertheless, tumors arising
in infants and the elderly have been occasionally reported. Like osteoid
osteoma, osteoblastoma shows a male predominance with a male-tofemale ratio of about 2 to 3 : 1.1,6,19,43
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Any bone in the skeleton can be affected, although the tumor has a
clear predilection for the axial skeleton. More than 40% of cases arise
in the vertebral column, with the lumbosacral spine being the overall
preferred site. In this location, the posterior arch is favored over the
vertebral body. In fact, primary vertebral body involvement is exceptionally rare. The long bones of the appendicular skeleton, pelvis, and
the bones of the foot are involved in decreasing order. As opposed to
osteoid osteoma, osteoblastoma is not uncommon in the craniofacial
bones, with the jaw bones being reported in some series as the second
most frequent site.19,44,45 The mandible is involved more frequently than
the maxilla, the tumor arising usually in the tooth-bearing region of
the bone, with the premolar area being the most commonly affected.10
In long bones, the metaphysis is the usual location, although extension
into the epiphysis is also possible. When arising in a long bone, osteoblastoma usually involves the medullary cavity and, less commonly, the
cortex. Although not as common as its intramedullary counterpart,
surface osteoblastoma is well described in the literature. Exceptionally,
rare cases of multifocal osteoblastoma have also been reported.43,46–48
The clinical manifestations of osteoblastoma are similar to those of
osteoid osteoma, although the pain is not as constant and typical and
73

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
is less readily relieved by aspirin or NSAIDs. Muscle spasm and contracture scoliosis are seen quite often in tumors arising in the posterior
elements or the thoracic and lumbar spine, whereas local swelling,
warmth, tenderness, and gait problems are seen in cases involving the
appendicular skeleton. Scoliosis, reported in cases involving the ribs,
is secondary to muscle spasm and is promptly relieved by tumor resection. Due to its unlimited growth potential, osteoblastoma arising in
the vertebral column can cause spine compression and subsequent
neural deficit. Exceptional cases of osteoblastoma associated with
oncogenic osteomalacia and systemic toxicity syndromes have been
described.1,6,13,43
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Unlike osteoid osteoma, osteoblastoma does not have a typical radiographic appearance; the images are more easily misinterpreted as other,
more ominous primary bone neoplasms. Conventional radiographs
show an irregular, mostly radiolucent lesion with various degrees of
patchy intratumoral mineralization due to the osteoblastic nature of
the neoplasm. However, purely osteolytic or purely osteoblastic lesions
are not uncommon. The size varies from 1.5 to 2 cm up to 10 cm, with
most tumors measuring around 5 cm at the time of diagnosis. Tumors
less than 1.5 cm are by convention classified as osteoid osteoma. When
osteoblastoma arises in a long bone, it is often surrounded by a rim of
osteosclerosis and can be centrally or eccentrically located in the metadiaphysis, with rare subperiosteal tumors described. The osteosclerosis
surrounding the tumor is frequently incomplete, and only rarely does
it reach the degree seen in osteoid osteoma, perhaps due to its predilection within the medulla rather than cortex and consequent lack of
periosteal irritation. The cortex can be expanded and focally destroyed,
especially in those tumors with secondary aneurysmal bone cyst
changes, in which case periosteal new bone formation is also seen. This
particular appearance in conjunction with the presence of a small
soft tissue mass may lead to a radiologic misdiagnosis of malignancy
(Fig. 5-16).

CT is the best method to highlight intratumoral matrix as well as
true tumor extension. Significant contrast agent uptake is noted due to
the rich tumor vascularization (Figs. 5-17 and 5-18). MRI is not superior to CT in diagnosing osteoblastoma; because of marked signal
intensity due to inflammation of the surrounding tissue, MRI may be
misleading (Fig. 5-19). Bone scintigraphy shows avid radionuclide
uptake.
Grossly, the tumor has a similar appearance to osteoid osteoma.
Like osteoid osteoma, it is highly vascularized, and therefore red/
brown with grittiness due to osteoid and woven bone production. A
rather sharp demarcation between the tumor and surrounding normal
bone is identified in most cases. The cortex is expanded or even interrupted, and a fibrous pseudocapsule being may be the only element
separating the tumor from the surrounding soft tissue (Fig. 5-20).1,10
Histopathology
The hallmark of osteoblastoma is an interlacing network of osteoid and
woven bone haphazardly distributed in a fibrovascular stroma. The
seams of osteoid and woven bone are rimmed by a single row of benign

Figure 5-17. Osteoblastoma. A computed tomography scan of the chest demonstrates
an expansile, radiodense lesion of the second left rib.

Figure 5-16. Osteoblastoma. This anteroposterior radiograph of the knee shows an
aggressive-appearing osteoblastoma located in the medial femoral metaphysis. The
internal circumscription and cortical continuity appear gone. There is a nonossifying
fibroma located medially that is not part of the lesion.
74

Figure 5-18. Osteoblastoma. A three-dimensional computed tomography scan demonstrates that the rib lesion is circumscribed but expansile (despite the lack of secondary
aneurysmal bone cyst).

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology

5

Figure 5-21. Osteoblastoma. This photomicrograph at 200× demonstrates osteoblasts forming microtrabeculae in a fibrovascular background; a few osteoclasts are
also seen.

Figure 5-19. Osteoblastoma. The coronal fat-saturation (FS) magnetic resonance
image (MRI) of the same case as shown in Figure 5-16 revealing extension of the tumor
through the cortex. There is clearly aggressive cortical destruction with an extraosseous
mass, which may be indicative of malignancy.

Figure 5-22. Osteoblastoma. This photomicrograph at 200× demonstrates prominent
cellular activity with osteoblasts ranging from typical to epithelioid in morphology and
scattered large osteoclasts.
Figure 5-20. Osteoblastoma. This gross image of rib osteoblastoma resection specimen demonstrates a gritty, hypervascular, and expansile intramedullary lesion. The
cortex is obscured but not obliterated.

osteoblasts, a small percentage of which may have epithelioid morphology (Fig. 5-21). When the plump epithelioid osteoblasts predominate,
occupying most of the space between the osteoid trabeculae, the tumor
is referred to as “epithelioid osteoblastoma.” This may have more
aggressive local behavior than its conventional counterpart (Fig. 5-22).
When the tumor extends outside the confines of the bone into the soft
tissue, a thin shell of reactive bone is consistently identified at the
interface between the tumor and soft tissue. Mitotic activity is present
in both histologic variants, but atypical mitoses are not seen. The
background fibrovascular stroma contains prominent capillaries and
osteoclast-like giant cells. However, the stroma is somewhat scanty in
epithelioid osteoblastoma because the spaces between the osteoid and
woven bone trabeculae are filled with sheets of plump osteoblasts.
Cartilage is not typical. However, several authors have reported the
presence of benign foci of hyaline cartilage or chondroid matrix in

otherwise typical osteoblastomas.48–51Permeative growth is not a histologic feature usually seen in osteoblastoma, although in a series of 55
cases, Rocca and Huvos50 described this particular growth pattern in
8 cases. Secondary aneurysmal bone cyst changes, with prominent
intratumoral blood-filled spaces can be seen, especially in large tumors.
Histologic Variants and Grading
Aggressive or epithelioid osteoblastoma is a histologic variant that,
since first described, continues to be riddled with controversy. Some
studies have attributed a slightly more aggressive local behavior to
epithelioid osteoblastoma, whereas others have either refuted the idea
of increased local aggressiveness altogether or, at the other extreme,
suggested the possibility that this neoplasm is, in fact, a low-grade
osteosarcoma.19,52,53 Designations such as “malignant osteoblastoma,”
“low-grade malignant osteoblastoma,” “pseudomalignant osteoblastoma,” and “osteoblastoma-like osteosarcoma” further add to the
general confusion and controversy.50,51,54,55 Nevertheless, most authors
agree that although the behavior of aggressive osteoblastoma is locally
more destructive than its conventional counterpart, it still is a benign
neoplasm that ultimately lacks metastatic potential. Furthermore,
75

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
benign osteoblastoma can undergo transformation into epithelioid
osteoblastoma with repeated local recurrences. This particular aspect,
in conjunction with the fact that the two types of osteoblastoma are
identical in incidence, sex, and site predilection as well as the fact that
complete resection is equally curative, suggest that these two entities
are no more than variants of the same benign neoplastic process. Conversely, cases of osteoblastoma-like osteosarcoma, which are well recognized and reported in the literature, appear to be malignant from
the outset, although initially misdiagnosed as benign.54-56
Histologically, aggressive osteoblastoma is characterized by the
presence of sheets of epithelioid osteoblasts filling the space between
seams of woven bone and osteoid, mostly replacing the typical fibrovascular stroma. The seams of osteoid and woven bone are architecturally more irregular than those of osteoid osteoma, and the presence of
“blue bone” and “lacelike osteoid” can be prominent. The epithelioid
osteoblasts are about twice the size of a normal osteoblast, have large
nuclei with prominent nucleoli, abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm, and
show conspicuous but not abnormal mitotic activity. Nevertheless,
frank cellular pleomorphism and permeative growth around native
bone trabeculae are features of osteosarcoma and should not be seen
in osteoblastoma (Figs. 5-23 and 5-24).57

Differential Diagnosis
Osteoid osteoma enters the differential diagnosis in cases of small
osteoblastoma. Clinical and radiographic features must be considered because, histologically, these two entities show significant
overlap.
Giant cell tumor of bone differs from osteoblastoma in that it
almost exclusively involves the epiphysis of long bones, whereas
primary epiphyseal presentation in osteoblastoma is quite rare. Furthermore, although osteoclast-like giant cells may be seen in osteoblastoma, they are never as numerous and evenly distributed as in giant
cell tumor of bone, and the background stroma is totally different in
these two neoplasms. Also, bone or osteoid formation is not a typical
feature of giant cell tumor, a purely radiolucent lesion on conventional
radiographs.
Secondary aneurysmal bone cyst changes can be superimposed on
osteoblastoma, usually in large tumors, whereas typical osteoblastoma
can appear, at least on conventional radiographs, aneurysmal bone
cyst–like. These features make both the radiologic and histologic diagnoses difficult. However, in cases of osteoblastoma with secondary
aneurysmal bone cyst changes, extensive sampling of the tumor often
results in identification of typical osteoblastoma foci.
Genetics
No consistent chromosomal aberrations have been described in osteoblastoma. However, several authors reported chromosomal rearrangements with hypodiploid, pseudodiploid, and hyperdiploid karyotypes,
especially in aggressive osteoblastoma.52

Figure 5-23. Osteoblastoma. This epithelioid osteoblastoma photomicrograph at
400× shows trabecular bone formed by large, epithelioid osteoblasts.

Treatment and Prognosis
Curettage and packing with cancellous bone or polymethyl methacrylate cement is the first-line treatment for osteoblastoma. The recurrence rate of tumors treated with this method is fairly high, being
reported at about 10%. Aggressive curettage and use of adjuvants
such as phenol, argon beam laser, or high-speed burr yield better
long-term results with lower recurrence rates. En bloc resection followed by reconstruction and internal fixation, when feasible, is the
treatment of choice for osteoblastoma, be it in its classic or aggressive
form. Nevertheless, when resection or aggressive curettage is not
feasible or indicated, cryosurgery and chemotherapy are also valid
treatment options. Radiotherapy, although used in the past, increases
the risk of postradiation sarcoma, and the local results are fair at
best.1,6,35,56,58

Osteopetrosis
Definition and Synonyms
Osteopetrosis is a group of inherited bone dysplasias characterized
by failure of resorption and remodeling of immature spongiosa
by osteoclasts.

Figure 5-24. Osteoblastoma. The same case as shown in Figure 5-23 demonstrates
sheets of osteoblasts with epithelioid morphology and basically no bone formation.
76

Brief Historical Overview
In 1904, German radiologist Albers-Schonberg first described osteopetrosis. Later it was determined that he had described the autosomal
dominant form of the disease. In an article published in 1948, Thomson
mentions McPeak and Harnapp, who in 1936 and 1937, respectively,
divided osteopetrosis in two groups: one including severe, malignant
cases and another including mild or benign cases. The same author
mentions Nussey, who in 1938 demonstrated that the severe form of
disease was associated with parents’ consanguineous marriage, whereas
the benign form was not, and suggested an autosomal recessive mode
of transmission for the malignant form and an autosomal dominant
mode for the benign form.59,60

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology
Incidence and Demographics
Autosomal recessive osteopetrosis is a rare disease with an estimated
incidence of approximately 1 in 200,000 births. Higher incidence
rates are reported in countries such as Costa Rica, where consanguinity
is common. The autosomal dominant form of osteopetrosis is
more common that its recessive counterpart; the reported prevalence
in the general population of 5.5/100,000 is most likely an underestimate because many patients are asymptomatic and remain
undiagnosed.61,62

appearance radiographically later in the disease course. The vertebral
bodies are also densely sclerotic with a prominent anterior vas­
cular notch and marked thickening of the end plates, giving the
typical “rugger jersey” appearance on conventional radiographs
(Figs. 5-25 to 5-27).
MRI shows shifting of marrow elements from the epiphyses toward
the diaphysis of long bone (Fig. 5-28). The skull also shows increased

5

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The entire skeleton is affected, although the degree of disease severity
varies with the type of osteopetrosis.

• “Precocious type,” also called lethal, malignant, or osteopetrosis

•

•

•

congenita. This is autosomal recessive, rare, and, as its name implies,
usually lethal in utero or shortly after birth, although unusual cases
of patients surviving into adulthood have been described. Despite
increased bone density, the disease manifests clinically with bone
fragility and painless pathologic fractures of the long bones.
However, the main and most severe symptoms are related to bone
marrow failure secondary to bone overgrowth. These include failure
to thrive, abnormal bleeding, and anemia. Compensatory extramedullary hematopoiesis usually ensues with massive secondary
hepatosplenomegaly. Visual impairment and other cranial nerve
palsies secondary to bone encroachment are frequent, and recurrent infections and osteomyelitis of the jaw bones also occur. Macrocephaly and frontal bossing are also common in affected
children.10,63
“Delayed type,” also called benign or osteopetrosis tarda. This is the
more common, autosomal dominant disease variant and is the type
originally described by Albers-Schonberg. Clinically, patients with
delayed osteopetrosis may remain asymptomatic, especially during
childhood. During adulthood, however, different disease manifestations may present; most patients experience some degree of bone
fragility and subsequent pathologic fractures. Facial palsy or deafness secondary to cranial nerve compression as well as optic and
trigeminal nerve involvement are late complications. Osteomyelitis
and anemia are also possible manifestations.10,64
“Intermediate recessive type,” a milder autosomal recessive form of
osteopetrosis. This was first described by Beighton and associates.65
Clinically, these patients show symptoms between the precocious
and delayed osteopetrosis; anemia, hepatomegaly, increased
risk of pathologic fractures, and short stature are the main
manifestations.
“Tubular acidosis type,” also called “marble brain” or Sly disease.
This is an autosomal recessive form first reported in 1972 and represented by a combination of osteopetrosis, renal tubular acidosis,
and cerebral calcifications. Failure to thrive, muscle weakness,
hypotonia, and mental retardation are common features of this
form of osteopetrosis. Although the bone disease is rather mild, the
life span of these patients may be reduced due to consequences of
renal tubular acidosis.10,63,66,67

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Osteopetrosis congenita is characterized radiographically by generalized osteosclerosis with loss of differentiation between the cortex and
medullary space in tubular bones. Longitudinal and transverse striations can sometimes be seen, the latter giving the “bone within bone”
radiologic appearance, which, although rare, is typical of this condition. Fractures are commonly seen, as are signs of periostitis. The skull
is always involved, showing a thick calvarium and a hair-on-end

Figure 5-25. This radiograph of adult autosomal dominant osteopetrosis shows selective radiodensity of spine endplates (“rugger jersey” spine) and of sacral wings and
innominate bones.

Figure 5-26. Osteopetrosis. A lateral chest radiograph of the same patient shown in
Figure 5-25 demonstrates increased density of ribs in addition to spinous processes.
77

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 5-27. Anteroposterior radiograph of pelvis in a patient with the intermediate
form of autosomal recessive osteopetrosis. Although the bones are very dense, they are
extremely brittle, as demonstrated by the many femoral fractures in different stages of
healing.

Figure 5-28. Osteopetrosis (magnetic resonance image). Coronal short tau inversion
recovery (STIR) sequence of the femur in the same patient shown in Figure 5-27
demonstrates loss of marrow signal throughout the entire medullary cavity of the
bone.

marrow activity, whereas the brain may display delayed myelination,
basal ganglia calcifications, optic nerve atrophy, and hydrocephalus.
Osteopetrosis tarda shows fewer severe radiographic findings than
its precocious counterpart, although the bones are still diffusely sclerotic. There are two phenotypes of autosomal dominant osteopetrosis,
each with a different distribution of bone sclerosis; type I shows
uniform sclerosis of the long bones, skull, and spine, whereas type II
shows a more prominent “bone within bone” appearance and base of
the skull sclerosis.10,66,67
Histopathology
The histopathologic changes of osteopetrosis are more prominent in
the metaphysis of long bones and are similar regardless of disease type.
A pattern of bone sclerosis with massive and irregular bone trabeculae
is invariably seen. Persistent cartilage matrix surrounded by woven
bone is also a common histologic feature of osteopetrosis. Although
osteoclasts are present, they are usually abnormally located around
capillaries not in close contact with the bone and appear inactive, with
poorly formed Howship lacunae. There is poor or no evidence of bone
resorption with subsequent reduction of marrow space (Figs. 5-29 to
5-31). Electron microscopy studies show the osteoclasts lacking a ruffle
border.13,68
Osteopetrosis, a group of diseases, is genetically as well as phenotypically heterogeneous. Two major types of genetic transmission have
been identified: autosomal recessive and autosomal dominant.
The autosomal dominant form of osteopetrosis is caused by mutations in the chloride channel 7 (ClCN7) gene at chromosome 16p13.3.
ClCN7 provides chloride conductance for efficient proton pumping in
the osteoclast ruffled border.61,69The autosomal recessive (malignant)
form of osteopetrosis results from mutations in the ATP6i gene, located
at chromosome 11q13.4-q13.5, in approximately 50% of cases. The
ATP6i gene encodes the osteoclast-specific a3 subunit of vacuolar-type
translocating ATP-ase (V-ATPase), which is located in the ruffled
78

Figure 5-29. Osteopetrosis. Typical histology of cancellous bone demonstrates retention of primary spongiosa with extensive calcified cartilage (light pink) as well as cancellous bone of both primary and secondary spongiosa with virtually no bone marrow
spaces.

border of osteoclasts, and is instrumental in bone resorption by acidifying the environment and thereby facilitating hydroxyapatite solubilization.70 Other gene mutations have been demonstrated in patients
with malignant osteopetrosis: ClCN7, receptor activator of nuclear
factor-KB ligand (RANKL), and gray-lethal (GL) gene mutations have
all been reported.68,69,71,72
Carbonic anhydrase II mutation is thought to be the cause of
tubular acidosis in the “marble brain” type of osteopetrosis.
Regardless of mutation, the phenotypic expression is somewhat
heterogeneous, and patients with similar mutations show, for reasons
not entirely clear, different clinical manifestations.70

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology
Brief Historical Overview
In 1905, Stiede first recognized osteopoikilosis, and in 1915,
Albers-Schonberg and Ledoux-Lebard fully described it. In 1928,
Buschke and Ollendorff described the disease in association with
a skin condition, which they called “dermatofibrosis lenticularis
disseminata.”73

5

Incidence and Demographics
The incidence of osteopoikilosis is estimated at 1 in 50,000 and can be
observed at any age, although it is rather uncommon before the age of
3 years. Men and women are equally affected; however, rare case report
series mention a slight male predilection.74 There is no difference
between races.10,75

Figure 5-30. Osteopetrosis. A microscopic area of incipient marrow formation shows
ineffective remodeling of primary spongiosa. Osteoclast-like giant cells are present, but
there are almost no resorption bays.

Figure 5-31. Osteopetrosis. In this field, derived from an adult patient, there has been
some active bone resorption with Howship lacunae visible and an incipient marrow
cavity. However, the number of osteoclast-like giant cells is disproportionate to the
amount of resorption.

Treatment and Prognosis
Malignant osteopetrosis is a severe disease that is usually lethal in the
first 2 years of life, primarily due to recurrent infections secondary to
bone marrow insufficiency.
Medical treatment with recombinant interferon-γ or high-dose calcitriol may be attempted in patients who are not transfusion-dependent
and are alive at 2 years of age. Bone marrow transplantation is the only
treatment that can alter the course of the disease. The success of bone
marrow transplantation largely depends on the degree of match
between the donor and recipient, with excellent results (79% 5-year
disease-free survival) in patients receiving bone marrow from human
leukocyte antigen–identical sibling donors.63

Osteopoikilosis
Definition and Synonyms
Osteopoikilosis is a rare autosomal dominant condition in which the
bones show multiple discrete or clustered foci of radiodensity, giving
them a spotted appearance. The disease is also called “osteopathia
condensans disseminata” or “spotted bones.” Hereditary, sporadic cases
of osteopoikilosis have also been reported.10

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Osteopoikilosis is a generalized and symmetrical bone dysplasia with
a predilection for the epiphyses and metaphyses of long tubular bones
as well as the pelvis, scapulae, carpal, and tarsal bones. Small tubular
bones of hands and feet are also commonly involved, whereas the
spine, skull, ribs and clavicles are less frequently affected.
The disease is usually clinically silent, although 15% to 20% of the
patients report mild articular pain with or without joint effusion. In
25% of cases, the condition is associated with multiple cutaneous
plaques known as “dermatofibrosis lenticularis disseminata” or
Buschke-Ollendorff syndrome. A predilection for keloid formation has
also been reported in these patients.
An association between osteopoikilosis, melorheostosis, and osteopathia striata is known under the common designation “mixed sclerosing bone dystrophies.”8,76
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The radiographic appearance is highly diagnostic. Multiple, small (2 to
10 mm), round or ovoid radiodense foci are seen in the medullary
cavity of long bones, mainly clustered within the epiphysis and
metaphysis. These foci of osteosclerosis can vary in size with serial
radiographs, especially in young, actively growing patients (Fig. 5-32).
On radioisotope bone scan, the lesions are nonenhancing.8,10,76
Histopathology
The histologic appearance of osteopoikilosis lesions is identical to that
of bone islands, being composed of compact lamellar bone containing
Haversian systems. Osteoclasts, osteocytes, and osteoblasts are present
within the compact bone, although they do not show evidence of
increased activity. Cartilage is not identified, which excludes the
hypothesis that the foci of osteosclerosis are formed through endochondral ossification of residual intramedullary cartilage rests.8,10
Differential Diagnosis
Osteoblastic metastatic disease, usually associated with prostate or
breast cancer, is the most important entity in the radiologic differential
diagnosis of osteopoikilosis. Furthermore, in patients with osteopoikilosis, metastases can be obscured, thereby delaying proper diagnosis
and treatment.77 A clinical history of a primary malignancy is useful
in separating the two entities. Also, a radionuclide bone scan shows
avid radiotracer uptake in metastatic disease while remaining normal
in osteopoikilosis.10
Genetics
Osteopoikilosis is an autosomal dominant hereditary bone dysplasia,
although sporadic forms of the disease have also been described. The
exact genetic defect is not known. A heterozygous deletion at chromosome 12q14 is associated with a phenotype including osteopoikilosis,
79

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 5-32. Osteopoikilosis. Radiograph of left shoulder demonstrates multiple bone
islands of varying size visible in the humeral head, acromion, and coracoid process.

short stature, and mental retardation,78 whereas Buschke-Ollendorff
syndrome (osteopoikilosis and dermatofibrosis lenticularis disseminata) is associated with loss-of-function mutations in the LEMD3
(MAN I) gene.79–81

Melorheostosis
Definition and Synonyms
Melorheostosis is a rare, nonhereditary, sclerosing bone dysplasia characterized by irregular hyperostotic changes of the cortex resembling
melting candle wax on conventional radiography. It usually involves
one side of the bone with secondary involvement of adjacent soft
tissues.
Brief Historical Overview
The disease, also known as Leri syndrome, was first described by Leri
and Joanny in 1922. They called it “hyperostose en coulée,” meaning
“flowing hyperostosis” or “melorheostosis” from the Greek words
“melos” (limb) and “rhein” (flow).82
Incidence and Demographics
The disease affects both sexes equally and is usually diagnosed in
late childhood, although occasionally the disease may manifest in
mid-adulthood. By 20 years of age, about 50% of the cases are
diagnosed.10
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Depending on the degree of skeletal involvement, melorheostosis can
be classified as a monostotic form or “forme fruste,” in which a single
bone is involved; a monomelic form, in which an entire limb is affected;
or a polyostotic form, in which multiple bones are involved. Due to its
typical distribution along sclerotomes, several authors have postulated
that melorheostosis represents bone scarring secondary to injury of a
sensory nerve.83,84
80

Figure 5-33. Melorheostosis. Radiographs of the right arm and forearm showing
cortical radiodensity in a “melted candle wax” distribution mainly involving one side
of the humerus and ulna but skipping the radius. The distribution of the process is
sclerotomal and corresponds to dermatomes.

The disease most commonly affects the appendicular skeleton,
the lower limb being approximately three times more often involved
than the upper. Occasionally, the bones of the axial skeleton can be
affected.85
In children, the disease is usually asymptomatic. Some children,
however, experience periarticular contractures resulting in joint deformity and limb length discrepancies. The soft tissue manifestations may
precede the radiographic appearance of bone sclerosis.
In adults, the disease causes joint pain with limited range of motion
and joint deformity and has a chronic course with periods of disease
exacerbation and arrest. Heterotopic bone formation and soft tissue
calcification contribute to joint ankylosis. The skin overlying the
affected bone is sometimes erythematous and shiny.8,83
Cases of melorheostosis associated with vascular anomalies, linear
scleroderma, fibromatosis, and lipomas have been reported. A case of
osteosarcoma arising in a patient with combined melorheostosis and
osteopathia striata has also been described in the literature.86,87
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The radiographic features of melorheostosis are highly characteristic.
Usually, the diagnosis is evident on conventional radiographs,
although additional bone scintigraphy studies can be helpful in
atypical cases.
Cortical hyperostosis resembling melted candle wax is seen in one
or multiple bones of a limb, producing an irregular bone contour
sometimes extending into adjacent joint spaces (Fig. 5-33). Endosteal
hyperostosis with subsequent obliteration of the medullary cavity can
also be seen. In the carpal and tarsal bones, the osteosclerosis presents
as round foci resembling osteopoikilosis. Soft tissue ossifications are
frequently seen, especially in the periarticular areas, contributing to
one of the main disease symptoms, joint ankylosis (Fig. 5-34).
Cases showing radiologic features of melorheostosis as well as
osteopoikilosis and osteopathia striata have been described in the
literature under the common designation of “mixed sclerotic bone

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology

5

Figure 5-34. Melorheostosis (radiograph). The right foot of this patient demonstrates
medial involvement of tibia and medial forefoot with ossification crossing tarsal bones
and involving soft tissues of the foot.

dystrophies.”10,76 These patients exhibit radiographically different patterns of bone sclerosis in different areas of the skeleton and, although
not perfectly matching the radiographic appearance of each disease
entity, they display some degree of similarity to osteopoikilosis,
melorheostosis, and osteopathia striata (Fig. 5-35).
Histopathology
Microscopic examination of bone in melorheostosis shows nonspecific
periosteal bone formation, with thickened bony trabeculae containing
unremarkable Haversian systems. The new bone is woven or lamellar,
depending on the degree of maturation. The medullary cavity is
fibrotic. Islands of hyaline cartilage showing endochondral bone
formation and fibrous tissue with evidence of intramembranous
bone formation are seen occasionally. No cellular atypia is identified
(Fig. 5-36).8,10,13,83,85,88
The polyostotic form of melorheostosis does not pose significant
problems in differential diagnosis due to its characteristic radiographic
appearance. The monostotic or “forme fruste” form of the disease
needs to be differentiated from parosteal osteoma, myositis ossificans,
and, most importantly, parosteal osteosarcoma.
Bone scintigraphy is sometimes the only diagnostic method needed
to differentiate melorheostosis from other bone lesions. Moderate cortical radiotracer uptake is typical for melorheostosis, whereas no
uptake is seen in either mature myositis ossificans or parosteal osteoma.

Figure 5-35. Melorheostosis. The scout computed tomography study demonstrates
left-sided involvement of the lateral femoral cortex by the sclerosing process with focal
proximal medullary involvement, a process resembling osteopathia striata of the proximal right femur and multiple bone islands of both proximal femora.

Conversely, osteosarcoma shows avid radiotracer uptake both in the
cortex and medullary cavity.83
Treatment and Prognosis
Treatment of melorheostosis is directed at correcting the joint deformities and limb length discrepancies. Tendon lengthening, fasciotomy,
capsulotomy, corrective osteotomies, and resection of soft tissue ossifications are used in an attempt to improve patient’s quality of life.83

images and their corresponding legends.

irregularly assembled, reflecting slow remodeling.

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid

Anthony Montag, MD

Conventional osteosarcoma is a high-grade intraosseous sarcoma with
osteoblastic, chondroblastic, or fibroblastic differentiation. Tumors frequently display more than one histologic lineage, but in all tumors,
some osteoid matrix is produced by malignant cells.

radiologic, and pathologic criteria for the diagnosis of osteosarcoma.5
Interestingly, the majority of specimens submitted to the registry were
found to be metastatic carcinoma. The collection subsequently became
part of the Armed Forces Institute of Pathology. Cade reported on 133
cases of osteosarcoma in 1955, with an emphasis on the efficacy of
radiation therapy and documentation of treatment effect.6 He also
noted that the majority of patients developed metastases after amputation and postulated microscopic systemic spread at the time of
diagnosis.
The current classification of conventional osteosarcoma into osteoblastic, chondroblastic, and fibroblastic subtypes is based on the 1957
series of Coventry and Dahlin,7 expanded to 600 cases over a 55-year
period in their 1967 update.8 Dahlin excluded from the series fibrosarcoma and chondrosarcoma, as well as surface osteosarcoma and
tumors arising in special sites such as the jaw. Although many variants
have since been described, this report is still the basis of modern
classification.
In his original series of patients with osteitis deformans, Paget
recognized that three of eight cases were complicated by the development of sarcoma.9 Subsequent series found that sarcoma associated
with Paget disease is rare in individuals younger than 50 years of age
but that the majority of osteosarcoma cases in those over 60 are associated with Paget disease.10,11

Historical Overview
Although the term “sarcoma,” designating a fleshy tumor, was introduced by Galen, and tumors of the limbs were described by Hippocrates, osteosarcoma as a bone-forming primary malignancy was
first recognized in 1805, both by Boyer in Philadelphia and Dupuytren
in Paris.1–3 Both based their description on gross observation. The
earliest focused review of osteosarcoma was presented in 1879 by
Samuel Gross, who reported 28 cases of bone sarcomas, of which 16
produced either bony or cartilaginous matrix.4 Gross also recognized
that the tumors were most common in the long bones of the lower
extremity and had a tendency to metastasize to the lung. A “bone
tumor registry,” the first tumor registry of any kind, was established in
1920 by Earnest Codman, James Ewing, and Joseph Bloodgood (representing Boston, New York, and Baltimore, respectively). Codman
published observations on the collection in 1926, outlining 25 clinical,

Incidence and Demographics
Osteosarcoma is the most common primary bone sarcoma. It constitutes 20% of malignant bone tumors and is twice as common as chondrosarcoma and nearly three times as common as Ewing sarcoma.
Nevertheless, the condition is uncommon, with fewer than 1000 new
cases occurring each year in the United States. Osteosarcoma has a
bimodal age distribution; most cases occur in childhood to young adult
years (4.4 cases per million in patients younger than 24 years of age)
and older patients (4.2 cases per million in patients older than 60 years
of age).12,13 Osteosarcoma is rare in patients younger than 5 years of
age.14 There is a male-to-female ratio of approximately 1.3 to 1 in both
demographic groups. However, in the youngest patients (younger than
10 years of age), the reverse is true, and on average the onset of osteosarcoma is earlier in females, possibly because of the earlier age of
pubertal growth.15 Osteosarcoma is about 1.2 to 1.4 times more

Conventional Osteosarcoma 85
Telangiectatic Osteosarcoma 93
Small Cell Osteosarcoma 96
Low-Grade Central Osteosarcoma 98

Osteosarcoma (also known as osteogenic sarcoma) includes lesions
ranging from low grade and indolent to high grade and aggressive, but
in all cases osteoid matrix is directly produced by neoplastic cells.
Histologically diverse, it includes tumors that mimic giant cell tumor,
Ewing sarcoma, aneurysmal bone cyst, osteoblastoma, chondrosarcoma, and fibrous dysplasia. It is the most common primary bone
sarcoma.

Conventional Osteosarcoma

85

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
common in African-American and Hispanic populations than in
non-Hispanic Caucasian populations.13,16,17 A multi-institutional study
found that osteosarcoma patients were significantly taller than controls, whereas no difference was found for Ewing sarcoma patients
compared to controls.15 A similar observation has been made in
osteosarcoma in dogs, where the incidence is much great in large
breeds.18
Tumors in the older incidence group are frequently secondary,19
with 20% of cases in patients older than 40 years and 50% of cases in
patients older than 60 years arising in Paget disease. The risk of developing a secondary sarcoma is about 1% for all patients with Paget
disease, as high as 10% for those with polyostotic disease, a 1000-fold
greater risk than in the general population.20 Secondary osteosarcoma
may also be associated with enchondromatosis, fibrous dysplasia,
chronic osteomyelitis, and bone infarcts.
Tumors associated with previous radiation exposure, most commonly therapeutic for a prior malignancy, account for 3% of osteosarcomas. The mean interval from radiation exposure to diagnosis is 12
to 14 years, with a range from 4 to more than 40 years.20 Pediatric
patients receiving radiation or alkylating agents have an increased risk
of sarcoma that is dose dependent.21 Ewing sarcoma, which is treated
with alkylating agents and higher doses of radiation, has a risk of secondary osteosarcoma that reaches 22% at 20 years post-therapy.22
Ingestion or administration of α- or β-particle emitting radionucleotides, such as cesium, radium, or thorium, is associated with osteosarcoma because many of these isotopes are concentrated in bone.
Thorium-containing radiologic contrast media, theraepeutic radium
injections, radium-containing elixirs, and glow-in-the-dark radium
paint have all been associated with osteosarcoma.23,24 Several cases of
sarcoma have occurred in the long-term follow-up of patients exposed
to radiation from the Chernobyl nuclear reactor, including one case of
osteosarcoma.25 There is little evidence that background environmental
radiation increases the risk of osteosarcoma, although one study from
Cornwall found levels of radon gas were significantly higher in the
homes of osteosarcoma patients than in controls.26
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Although virtually any bone may be affected by osteosarcoma, the most
common sites are the long bones of the extremities, with the femur,
tibia, and humerus accounting for more than 50% of cases.8 The small
bones of the hand and feet are rarely involved. In cases of osteosarcoma
arising in Paget disease, tumors of the pelvis, skull, and humerus are
common. Most lesions are metaphyseal (90%) or diaphyseal (9%), with
epiphyseal lesions very uncommon.
Patients typically present with pain and a palpable mass, with
pathologic fracture occurring in fewer than 10%. Skin changes, venous
dilation, and bruits may be associated with more vascular lesions.
Paget disease may affect any bone but occurs frequently in the
pelvis, spine, and skull, and it accounts for a significant share of osteosarcomas arising in these sites.27,28 Patients typically have a long history
of Paget disease, with deformation of the involved bone and not infrequently present without new symptoms, and a new lytic and soft tissue
mass are found radiographically. Pathologic fracture at presentation is
uncommon.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographic appearance is variable, depending on the rapidity of
growth and the contribution of osteoblastic, chondroblastic, and fibroblastic elements. Most tumors show a mixture of lytic and sclerotic
areas, with an indistinct interface with the surrounding bone (Fig. 6-1).
Cortical destruction is frequent, and more than 80% of cases have

86

extraosseous extension, which in 90% of cases exhibits mineralization,
often producing a “hair on end” or sunburst pattern perpendicular to
the periosteum. At the interface of extraosseous tumor and normal
bone, it is common to have a triangular area of periosteal elevation
with matrix formation; the Codman triangle represents reactive periosteum. Computed tomographic (CT) imaging is more sensitive for
the detection of small amounts of mineralized matrix and evaluating
the status of the cortical bone. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is
vital for delineation of normal anatomic structures, soft tissue extension, joint space involvement, and evaluation of the marrow space for
skip lesions and extent of tumor.29
Paget disease is radiographically lytic, becoming irregularly mixed
lytic and sclerotic with thickened trabeculae, cortical thickening, and
bone expansion and deformity. Osteosarcoma complicating Paget
disease usually presents with a soft tissue mass and periosteal reaction
(see Fig. 6-1F),28 although both may also be seen in Paget disease not
complicated by malignancy.30
Grossly, conventional osteosarcoma has a primary medullary component with erosion of the cortex and a large soft tissue mass (Fig. 6-2).
Depending on the proportion of osteoblastic, chondroblastic, and
fibroblastic components, areas of the tumor may range in consistency
from firm and sclerotic to gray and glistening to soft and fish-flesh–
like. Mineralization tends to be greater centrally, with the periphery
containing the least matrix. In sclerotic osteosarcoma, the tumor may
be dense and ivory-like. Periosteal reaction at the periphery of the
tumor may be prominent, corresponding to the radiographic Codman
triangle.
In Paget disease–associated osteosarcoma, there is marked preexisting deformity with cortical thickening and lytic and sclerotic pagetoid
bone, depending on the status of the disease. Destruction of the cortex
and a soft tissue mass are often present, varying in consistency depending on the degree of matrix production.
Histopathology
On low power, an infiltrative growth pattern is present (Fig. 6-3) within
the medullary canal and cortex; tumor infiltrates preexisting bony
trabeculae and Haversian canals, surrounding and trapping the native
elements. Tumor usually extends through the periosteum and infiltrates skeletal muscle and soft tissue. Most tumors are predominantly
osteoblastic, whereas chondroblastic and fibroblastic predominance is
seen in about 10% of cases each (Fig. 6-4).31 In all cases, osteoid matrix
is produced by tumor cells. Osteoblastic tumors typically exhibit
marked cytologic atypia, frequent, occasionally atypical, mitoses, and
netlike osteoid matrix separating individual tumor cells. In hypocellular sclerotic lesions, or sclerosing osteosarcoma, broad areas of dense
woven bone infiltrate and trap preexisiting native bone (Fig. 6-5).
Malignant osteoblastic cells entrapped in the matrix may display
minimal pleomorphism, “normalizing” their cytologic appearance. A
diagnosis of malignancy can be made based on the infiltrative pattern
and radiographic features.32 Chondroblastic osteosarcoma has broad
zones of occasionally myxoid cartilaginous matrix, simulating highgrade chondrosarcoma, but also produces osteoid matrix. Secondary
ossification of cartilaginous matrix is not sufficient for the diagnosis of
osteosarcoma. Fibroblastic osteosarcoma simulates an intermediateto high-grade fibroblastic or myofibroblastic tumor and may have
only sparse osteoid, occasionally present in broader trabeculae of
woven bone.
Eighty percent of sarcomas arising in Paget disease are osteosarcoma, and the majority are osteoblastic (71%) or fibroblastic (20%),
with chondroblastic (8%) occurring less frequently.28 Other histologies
occur only rarely. Although the osteoblastic stage of Paget disease may

Differential Diagnosis
Osteosarcoma has an extremely variable histologic appearance
depending on the dominant cell type and matrix, and the differential
diagnosis is therefore broad. Clinical presentation and radiologic
appearance are integral parts of assessing the lesion, and usually the
index of suspicion is high even before the biopsy is performed. For
osteoblastic lesions, the histologic differential diagnosis includes
osteoid osteoma and osteoblastoma. Osteoma is small, cortically based
and has a characteristic targetoid radiographic appearance, wellformed trabeculae with osteoblast rimming, and lacks cytologic atypia.
Except for the rare intracortical osteosarcoma, location and
88

radiographic appearance separate the entities. Osteoblastoma may
have a more aggressive radiographic appearance with a less welldefined interface with normal bone. Histologically, the presence of
trabeculae of woven bone with osteoblast rimming, vascular intervening stroma, and minimal atypia discriminate osteoblastoma from
osteosarcoma. The rare osteoblastoma-like osteosarcoma displays
increased atypia and fine lacy osteoid in the stroma between woven
bone trabeculae33 (Fig. 6-7).
An important differential diagnosis for chondroblastic osteosarcoma is chondrosarcoma. Histologically, the two can be indistinguishable except for the production of osteoid. For most patients, the young
age and radiographic appearance of osseous matrix make the diagnosis
of chondrosarcoma unlikely. If an initial biopsy on a patient younger
than 20 years of age reveals only a malignant cartilaginous tumor, it is

Figure 6-5. Sclerosing osteosarcoma. A, Computed tomography examination shows the dense ossification of the tumor. B, Biopsy shows a dense low cellularity matrix surrounding

better to render a descriptive diagnosis and indicate that, taking into
account the radiograph and clinical presentation, osteosarcoma is a
much more likely diagnosis.
Two rare variants of osteosarcoma produce cartilaginous matrix but
simulate benign lesions. Chondromyxoid fibroma (CMF)–like osteosarcoma lacks the radiographic sclerotic border usually seen in CMF;
however this is also lacking in 10% of CMF patients (Fig. 6-8). CMFlike osteosarcoma arises centrally, usually disrupts the cortex, and has
soft tissue extension, whereas CMF is cortically based and rarely has a
soft tissue component. Histologically, CMF-like osteosarcoma mimics
the zonal appearance of CMF, with myxoid matrix containing stellate
cells alternating with more cellular areas. Pleomorphism, mitotic activity, and the production of osteoid differentiate CMF-like osteosarcoma
from CMF.34,35
Chondroblastoma-like osteosarcoma is an extremely rare variant
that occurs preferentially in the bones of the hands and feet at an
average age of 40 years, as compared to chondroblastoma, which has
peak incidence before closure of the epiphyses.36 Radiographically,
most lesions are diaphyseal and have soft tissue extension, both
unusual for chondroblastoma. Histologically, the tumor is composed
of polygonal cells that simulate benign chondroblasts, scattered giant
cells, and a sparse cartilaginous matrix that may display “chicken
90

wire” calcification. Pleomorphism may be subtle, particularly because
chondroblasts have a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio and frequently
are dark and smudgy cytologically. An infiltrative growth pattern at
the margin, soft tissue extension, and osteoid production all suggest
osteosarcoma.
The differential diagnosis of fibroblastic osteosarcoma includes
pleomorphic sarcoma (malignant fibrous histiocytoma) of bone,
nonossifying fibroma, fibrous histiocytoma, and fibrous dysplasia.
Although sometimes sparse, fibroblastic osteosarcoma produces
osteoid matrix, in contrast to malignant fibrous histiocytoma. The
radiograph of fibroblastic osteosarcoma is indicative of an aggressive
lesion, and pleomorphism is not difficult to find.
Perhaps the most difficult differential in evaluating biopsies of
osteosarcoma is fracture callus. The history of fracture is frequently not
provided, the first biopsy specimen received often is of periosteum, and
callus displays immature osteogenic and chondrogenic tissue that
simulates malignancy.37 Fracture repair displays all the stages of normal
wound healing, with the addition of the osteocartilaginous callus (Fig.
6-9). The early changes involve granulation tissue and fibroblastic proliferation, followed by differentiation of periosteal and endosteal pluripotential cells into osteoblasts and chondroblasts. Histologically, the
early callus simulates disorganized fetal cartilage and bone and can

cement lines, indicating accelerated remodeling. B, Secondary osteosarcoma. A trabecula of pagetoid bone is partially entrapped by an osteoblastic osteosarcoma, producing
abundant osteoid.

and nucleoli, when present, are not prominent. B, Osteoblastoma-like osteosarcoma. Transition from an osteoblastoma appearance in the upper right to a cellular tumor with irregular
lacework of osteoid surrounding atypical osteoblasts with prominent nucleoli.

and condensations of stellate to spindled cells with a zonal pattern. Other areas of the lesion were overtly malignant.

Figure 6-9. Fracture repair. A, Early changes include edematous stroma, prominent
capillaries, and wispy aggregates of woven bone intermixed with osteoblasts (4 to 7
days). B, Better defined aggregates of woven bone with osteoblast rimming, superimposed on reactive myofibroblasts and edema (7 to 10 days). C, Immature cellular
cartilage and granulation tissue. Cellularity and nuclear features mimic chondrosarcoma (10 to 14 days). D, Zones of immature cartilage, interlaced trabeculae of woven
bone with osteoblast rimming, and granulation tissue (more than 2 weeks). E, Maturing fracture with arches of woven bone with osteoblast rimming (more than 2 weeks).

mimic the histology of conventional osteosarcoma or chondrosarcoma.38 Because injury is often ongoing, zones of more mature changes
may alternate with more recent injury. Acute changes of hemorrhage
and fibrin deposition are followed by neutrophils (less than 24 hours)
and macrophages (2 to 3 days). Granulation tissue and proliferating
myofibroblasts predominate until 7 days, when early osteoid formation
is seen in the stroma. Woven bone and cartilage matrix are present by
10 to 14 days, followed by well-formed trabeculae of woven bone with
osteoblast rimming by 2 to 3 weeks (see Fig. 6-6). Mitotic activity and
92

cellularity are often high, and cytologic atypia in cartilage may be striking. Atypical mitoses and frank pleomorphism are not present in
fracture callus.
Several histologic features help differentiate fracture callus from
osteosarcoma. Callus has a zonal character, with areas of immature
cartilage merging with woven bone and myofibroblasts, whereas osteosarcoma tends to be more diffuse. Maturing woven bone in fractures
forms trabeculae with prominent osteoblast rimming, maturing to an
“arches on arches” pattern of broad trabeculae of woven bone.

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Aggressive Radiology Located within Bone
Osteosarcoma produces lacelike osteoid between tumor cells and
usually lacks well-formed trabeculae and osteoblast rimming. The
matrix between trabeculae in a fracture is a mixture of vascular and
reactive fibroblastic tissues.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Immunohistochemistry has limited utility in the diagnosis of osteosarcoma; the immunoprofile reflects the broad range of histologic appearance and includes S-100 protein, smooth muscle actin, CD99,
osteonectin, and osteocalcin. Aberrant expression of cytokeratin has
been reported, especially in tumors with epithelioid differentiation.39
Osteocalcin appears to have some utility in establishing bone lineage,
with 70% sensitivity and 100% specificity.40 Immunohistochemistry for
IDH1 is useful in the differentiation of chondrosarcoma from chondroblastic osteosarcoma.41
Cytogenetic analysis is of limited use because there are no frequently recurring abnormalities and most osteosarcomas have complex
and highly aneuploid karyotypes, including multiple chromosomal
abnormalities.42 Amplification of 6p12-21, corresponding to the region
containing the osteoblast differentiation gene RUNX2,43 and of 8q2124, which includes MYC, are each found in approximately 50% of
cases.44
Several syndromes confer a hereditary predisposition to the development of osteosarcoma and mirror mutations found in sporadic
cases. Hereditary retinoblastoma patients, with a germline mutation in
the cell cycle control gene Rb, have an increased risk of developing
osteosarcoma as a secondary malignancy that ranges from 75-fold in
patients treated by surgery alone to greater than 300-fold in those
receiving radiation therapy—or a cumulative lifetime risk of 6% to 14%
for all hereditary retinoblastoma patients.45,46 There is loss of heterozygosity of the Rb gene in 39% of sporadic osteosarcoma cases, apparently without an effect on prognosis.47
Li-Fraumeni syndrome, an autosomal, dominantly inherited susceptibility to a variety of tumors, including sarcoma, is associated with
mutation in p53, a cell cycle and apoptosis control gene, in 70% of
cases.20 Approximately 3% of osteosarcomas are associated with the
syndrome, and the risk of developing sarcoma is 500 times that of the
general population.48 Approximately 40% of sporadic cases have a loss
of heterozygosity of p53.
The RECQ DNA helicase gene family is involved in DNA repair
and telomere maintenance. Mutations in three members of this group
are associated with heritable genetic disorders that carry an increased
risk of osteosarcoma.49,50 Seventy percent of patients with Rothmund
Thomson syndrome (small stature, poikiloderma, and juvenile cataracts) have a mutation in the RECQL4 DNA helicase gene, and they
have an associated 10% to 30%51 risk of developing osteosarcoma.20
Patients with this syndrome are highly sensitive to ionizing radiation
and DNA intercalating drugs such as doxorubicin.52 Sporadic osteosarcoma is not associated with mutations of RECQL4.53
Bloom syndrome (short stature, micrognathism, photosensitivity,
immune deficiency, and increased risk of multiple tumors) and Werner
syndrome (progeria; cataracts; and increased risk of soft tissue sarcoma,
melanoma, thyroid cancer, and osteosarcoma) are autosomal recessive
syndromes caused by mutation in the BLM gene (RECQL3 helicase)
and WRN gene (RECQL2 helicase), respectively. Both syndromes have
marked genomic instability, with frequent chromosomal breaks, translocations, and telomere shortening. Lymphoma and leukemia are the
most common malignancies in early life in Bloom syndrome, with
carcinomas predominating in patients in their twenties and thirties.
Osteosarcoma constitutes 2% and 5% of tumors associated with Bloom
and Werner syndromes, respectively.54,55 As with RECQL4, mutations
in the RECQL2 or RECQ3 genes are not found in sporadic

osteosarcoma. The syndromes suggest that the marked chromosomal
abnormalities seen in sporadic osteosarcoma are related to faulty DNA
repair, not to mutation of the RECQ family of helicase genes. Other
studies have found increased RECQL4 gene expression in sporadic
osteosarcoma53 and an association of RECQL5 polymorphisms with
increased risk of osteosarcoma.56
Fifteen to twenty percent of patients with Paget disease have a firstdegree relative who also suffers from the disease, with an autosomal
dominant pattern of inheritance.11 Up to 50% of familial cases and up
to 15% of sporadic cases carry a mutation in the SQSTM1/p62 gene
(5q35), which is known to indirectly affect receptor activator for
nuclear factor (RANK) ligand signaling.57 Mice harboring a mutation
in a ubiquitin-associated domain of SQSTM1 also develop Paget
disease.58 Genome-wide studies have implicated seven other genes,
including RANK and macrophage colony-stimulating factor.59 Osteosarcoma associated with sporadic Paget disease had a mutation of
SQSTM1 in three of five cases in one study.60

6

Therapy and Prognosis
Therapy in the prechemotherapy era was surgical, usually by amputation. Although local control was usually attained, 80% of patients succumbed to metastases.61 Adjuvant chemotherapy was introduced in the
1970s, and a randomized multidrug, multi-institutional study in 1986
reported a nearly 70% relapse-free survival compared to 17% for controls. Early trials of limb-sparing surgery were accompanied by a high
local recurrence rate, but with the introduction of preoperative neoadjuvant chemotherapy in the 1970s and 1980s, limb-sparing surgery
became the norm.62,63
One of the most important indicators of prognosis is the response
to neoadjuvant chemotherapy, or the pathologic assessment of percentage of tumor necrosis. The area of affected bone has usually been
excised with soft tissue margins over areas of extraosseous extension.
Following inking and sampling of soft tissue margins, the bone is
skinned of soft tissue, split in the long axis, and made into a 3-mm
thick parallel section. After fixation and decalcification, one complete
face of the tumor is mapped and blocked in. Microscopic assessment
of residual viable tumor is made, and cases with greater than 90%
necrosis are considered to be responders and have an improved survival.64 Patients with nonresponding tumors usually receive a modified
chemotherapy regimen.65 Patients with metastases at presentation and
with predominantly chondroblastic histology are less responsive to
chemotherapy. In spite of advances in chemotherapy, osteosarcoma
arising in Paget disease has a dismal prognosis, with a 5-year survival
of 10%.28

Telangiectatic Osteosarcoma
Telangiectatic osteosarcoma is a high-grade, frequently pleomorphic,
sarcoma that typically elaborates only small amounts of osteoid and
has characteristic blood-filled spaces usually separated by thin septa.
Historical Overview
Hemorrhagic cystic tumors of bone were recognized in 1876 by Sir
James Paget as representing a neoplasm of bone with a “blood cyst”66
forming as a secondary feature, and Gaylord67 in 1903 reported three
cases of “so-called bone aneurisms” that had sarcomatous linings.
However, Ewing was the first to use the term “telangiectatic osteosarcoma” when in 1922 he recognized these tumors as a variant of osteosarcoma.68 Geschickter and Copeland reported their experience with
88 cases of “osteolytic osteosarcoma,” malignant lesions that produced
little matrix, in 1936. Although the series appears to include fibroblastic osteosarcoma and malignant fibrous histiocytoma of bone, several
93

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
illustrations, including a low-power micrograph of blood spaces, are
clearly telangiectatic osteosarcoma.69 The Mayo clinic experience70 in
1976 of 24 patients and the Memorial experience71 in 1982 of 124 cases
are the two largest series.
Incidence and Demographics
Telangiectatic osteosarcoma is an uncommon subtype, constituting 4%
to 10% of all osteosarcoma cases.70,71 It occurs most frequently in the
second decade of life but otherwise has a similar demographic profile
to conventional osteosarcoma.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Like conventional osteosarcoma, the tumor is usually metaphyseal
with extension into the epiphysis. The most common sites are the distal
femur (42%), proximal tibia (17%), proximal humerus (9%), and proximal femur (7%).72 Pain and soft tissue mass are common presenting
signs. Pathologic fracture is present in more than 25% of cases, significantly more frequently than in conventional osteosarcoma.71 Occasionally, a vascular bruit may be heard over the tumor.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
On radiography, more than 90% of cases have an aggressive lytic
appearance, with an ill-defined transition zone (Fig. 6-10).73 Expansile
growth with reactive bone remodeling is present in 75% of patients and
may mimic aneurysmal bone cysts (ABCs). Periosteal reaction is
present in 75% of cases and may be extensive. Fracture associated with
the lesion is seen radiographically in 61% of affected individuals, more
than twice as frequently as clinically diagnosed fracture. Mineralization is subtle and present in only 58% of cases, usually in the periphery
or adjacent to aneurysmal areas. MRI is valuable to assess the extent
of soft tissue involvement; to identify high signal intensity areas that
correspond to hemorrhage; and to identify cysts with fluid-fluid levels,
present in 75% of patients, which represent layering of blood. Grossly,

A

B

the tumor is medullary, expands the contour of the bone, destroys the
cortex, and has soft tissue extension. It is multicystic, often with 90%
of the volume composed of blood- and fluid-filled spaces. Although
solid fleshy areas may be present, more typically only thin weblike
septa separate the cysts.
Histopathology
On low power, blood-filled spaces are separated by thin fibrous septa
composed of stromal cells varying from spindled to polygonal; the
appearance can be deceptively similar to ABC (Fig. 6-11). Intermixture
of benign osteoblast-like giant cells can add to the deceptively bland
appearance at low power. Matrix production is sparse, and reactive
osteoid at the periphery dominates. It is not unusual to review a
number of slides before finding neoplastic-appearing osteoid. Cytologic atypia is always present but is variable, and thorough sampling
may be necessary. Necrosis in the absence of significant fracture is
common in telangiectatic osteosarcoma and rare in ABCs.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes other cystic lesions of bone, including primary and secondary ABCs. Unicameral bone cyst lacks cellular
septa and is not considered in the differential.
The differentiation of ABC from telangiectatic osteosarcoma on
biopsy can be difficult, particularly if assessed on a frozen section, and
carries the risk of misdirecting the surgeon to do a primary curetting
of a malignant lesion, contaminating the surgical site with tumor and
compromising the limb-sparing surgery options. Local recurrence
rates after curettage of an osteosarcoma are about 45%, and the ultimate limb salvage rate is only 69%.74
Both ABC and telangiectatic osteosarcoma may present as an
expansile, purely lytic, cystic mass, frequently with fluid-fluid levels
on CT or MRI examination, indicating layers of fresh and old
blood in a cystic space. Grossly, the biopsy material may appear

C

lesion showing intramedullary extent and fluid-fluid levels. C, Multiple cystic spaces simulate an aneurysmal bone cyst. The dense white area is cement filling the previous biopsy
cavity.
