Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Aggressive Radiology Located within Bone

6

A

B

C

D

Figure 6-11. Telangiectatic osteosarcoma. A, Cystic spaces separated by webs of cellular tissue. B, Cellular trabeculae are composed of polygonal stromal cells and scattered
giant cells. Pleomorphism is evident in this field but may be subtle. C, Marked nuclear atypia and pleomorphic giant cells. Osteoid matrix is usually sparse. D, Less pleomorphic
focus with wisps of woven bone with osteoblast rimming. Reactive bone may be seen in both telangiectatic osteosarcoma and aneurysmal bone cyst.

similar—hemorrhagic, fleshy, with cystic spaces and little bone matrix.
Microscopically, both ABC and telangiectatic osteosarcoma have cystlike spaces without an endothelial lining. The septa are generally more
cellular in telangiectatic osteosarcoma and are frequently broader.
Osteoclast-like giant cells are present in both. Frank pleomorphism
and frequent and atypical mitotic figures are seen in most cases of
telangiectatic osteosarcoma, whereas the stromal cells of ABC are
uniform and display occasional mitoses. Osteoid production is difficult
to find in most cases of telangiectatic osteosarcoma, but when present,
it is typically a fine meshwork of woven bone. Osteoid may be present
in ABC but tends to appear as broader seams of woven bone with
histologically benign osteocytes and osteoblasts. In the absence of fracture, the presence of necrosis in a cystic lesion of bone is an ominous
sign and is highly suggestive of telangiectatic osteosarcoma.75
Somewhat analogous to telangiectatic osteosarcoma, giant cell–rich
osteosarcoma produces little matrix and has abundant benign
osteoclast-like giant cells, simulating giant cell tumor of bone. First
described in 1986, it constitutes 3% of osteosarcomas.76 Most occur in
the vicinity of the knee, and two thirds involve both the metaphysis
and epiphysis, radiologically overlapping giant cell tumor (Fig. 6-12).
The tumor is predominantly lytic, has an ill-defined margin, and lacks

a soft tissue mass.77 Histologically, the benign osteoclast-like giant cells
obscure the atypia of the underlying stromal cells, and osteoid production is usually scant. One third of cases are initially diagnosed as giant
cell tumor. An infiltrating interface with native bone and pleomorphism allow the diagnosis of malignancy, but extensive sampling may
be necessary.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
No immunohistochemical studies are useful in the diagnosis of telangiectatic osteosarcoma. In the differential with primary ABC, the latter
has a translocation involving the USP6 locus on chromosome 17,
which can be probed by fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH).78
Cytogenetically, telangiectatic osteosarcoma has a complex karyotype
similar to other osteosarcomas.79,80
Therapy and Prognosis
Patients receive neoadjuvant chemotherapy and, when possible, limbsparing surgery similar to other histologic subtypes of osteosarcoma.
In early reports, telangiectatic osteosarcoma was thought to have a
poorer prognosis than other types.70 However, in the neoadjuvant and
limb salvage therapy era, the histologic response to therapy and
95

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

C

B

D

Figure 6-12. Giant cell osteosarcoma. A, Radiograph showing lytic lesion of vertebral body with large soft tissue mass. B, Destruction of vertebral body and pedicle with soft
tissue mass. C, Low-power appearance shows numerous, evenly distributed osteoclast-like giant cells. D, Pleomorphic stromal cells with atypical mitoses. Atypical mitotic figures
are frequent.

disease-free survival appear to be equal to or better for telangiectatic
osteosarcoma.81

Small Cell Osteosarcoma
Small cell osteosarcoma is a high-grade osteosarcoma. It is composed
of cells frequently indistinguishable from Ewing sarcoma or other
small blue cell tumors that produce only focal osteoid.
Historical Overview
In 1953, Johnson postulated that all bone tumors arise from a primitive precursor cell and described a primitive round cell sarcoma with
osteoid production.82 In 1966, Hutter and colleagues reported a series
of primitive sarcomas of bone that were “multipotential,” or able to
differentiate into more than one lineage.83 Eighteen of the cases produced osteoid matrix, and one is clearly a small cell osteosarcoma.
Jacobson similarly included primitive tumors that produced cartilaginous or osteoid matrix under the diagnosis of “polyhistioma.”84 A
series of 24 patients with “osteosarcoma with small cells simulating
Ewing tumor” was reported by the Mayo clinic in 1979.85 Many of the
cases had previously been diagnosed as Ewing sarcoma, but on review
they were intermediate in differentiation between Ewing sarcoma and
96

osteosarcoma. The largest series is the 1997 Mayo Clinic experience of
72 patients.86
Incidence and Demographics
Small cell osteosarcoma is rare, comprising 1% to 2% of osteosarcomas.
Some series have shown a slight female predominance, although the
largest series has a slight male predominance.86–88 The mean age of 25
years old (range 5 to 71 years) is older than in conventional osteosarcoma, with 50% of patients presenting in the second and third decades
of life.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Two thirds of lesions occur in the long bones and one third occur
in the femur but without the predilection for the distal femur and
proximal tibia seen in conventional osteosarcoma. Most are metaphyseal or metaphyseal/epiphyseal, although 15% are purely diaphyseal.
Pain, swelling, and palpable mass are the most common presenting
symptoms.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographically, the appearance varies from that of conventional
osteosarcoma to cases that overlap Ewing sarcoma or other marrow

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Aggressive Radiology Located within Bone

6

A

C

B

D

E

Figure 6-13. Small cell osteosarcoma. A, Diaphyseal marrow space lesion with periosteal reaction. The appearance simulates Ewing sarcoma. B, Magnetic resonance image shows
extensive soft tissue extension. C, Post-therapy excision specimen. Cortical scalloping and periosteal thickening are present. D, Small blue cell tumor appearance with no osteoid
production, indistinguishable from Ewing sarcoma. E, Hemangiopericytoma-like vascular pattern with small blue cells. Osteoid is present at the periphery.

space processes (Fig. 6-13). All tumors have a lytic component,
and intramedullary sclerosis is present in 50% of these. The radiographic margin of the tumor is indistinct, and all cases have disruption
of the cortex. Sixty-three percent of cases have a soft tissue mass
on CT, 50% of which have mineralization to suggest osteosarcoma.
In approximately 40% of cases, the radiographs are purely lytic
with no mineralization in the soft tissue and are not suggestive of
osteosarcoma.
Grossly, the majority of cases have a large soft tissue component
and obvious disruption of the cortex. Depending on the extent of
matrix production, the specimen can be firm like a conventional osteosarcoma or yielding like a marrow space process.
Histopathology
Most tumors are composed of small round cells with relatively
uniform nuclei, more pleomorphism than usually seen in Ewing

sarcoma, and inconspicuous nucleoli. Less than 10% of cases have
a short blue spindle cell phenotype, sometimes accompanied by a
hemangiopericytomatous architectural pattern.86 Lacelike osteoid is
always present, but is scant in 50% of cases. Fibrosis and myxoid
change may be present.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes all tumors on the small blue cell
tumor differential, although the clinical and radiologic appearance
frequently narrows the differential. Ewing sarcoma is more frequent
than small cell osteosarcoma and is a serious consideration in lesions
that lack recognizable matrix on radiography. Although reactive bone
may be present in Ewing sarcoma, the presence of even focal lacelike
osteoid indicates osteosarcoma. Although CD99 staining may be
positive in small cell osteosarcoma, FLI1 is not expressed and is a reliable discriminant.89 Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma focally elaborates
97

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
cartilaginous matrix and also lacks FLI1 expression. Malignant lymphoma of bone is rare in the age group most at risk for osteosarcoma
and can be ruled out with appropriate workup for leukocyte-related
antigens. Leukemia, myeloma, metastatic small cell carcinoma, and
neuroblastoma can be similarly excluded using immunohistochemical
panels.90
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Small cell osteosarcoma is similar to osteoblastic osteosarcoma in
expressing osteocalcin and osteonectin but has no specific marker.
Tumors may occasionally express smooth muscle antigen, desmin,
S-100 protein, CD34, caveolin, cytokeratin, and CD99.91 Galectin-1 is
usually expressed in small cell and osteoblastic osteosarcomas but is
absent in Ewing sarcoma.92 Cytogenetic, FISH, or molecular analysis
for the t(11;22) or other Ewing-associated translocations is a more
definitive way to exclude Ewing sarcoma.
Small cell osteosarcoma has a complex karyotype, similar to conventional osteosarcoma. There is an early report of small cell osteosarcoma harboring the t(11;22) translocation found in Ewing sarcoma93;
however, the illustration of osteoid in the report is not convincing.
Recently, an EWSR1-CREB3L1 translocation was identified in a small
cell osteosarcoma.94
Therapy and Prognosis
Reports of patient outcome in the modern chemotherapeutic era are
small, but small cell osteosarcoma is usually treated with neoadjuvant
chemotherapy, like conventional osteosarcoma.86,87 The 5-year survival
is slightly worse than for conventional high-grade osteosarcoma.

Low-Grade Central Osteosarcoma
This often deceptively bland fibroblastic tumor of the medullary cavity
produces well-formed trabeculae of bone. It has a limited capacity to
metastasize.
Historical Overview
A series of 27 cases of low-grade intramedullary osteosarcomas was
published by Unni in 1977,95 and this was expanded to 80 cases in a
1990 report.96 Many cases had been previously diagnosed as fibrous
dysplasia, nonossifying fibroma, or desmoplastic fibroma.
Incidence and Demographics
Low-grade central osteosarcoma constitutes 1% to 2% of osteosarcoma
cases. Its peak incidence occurs in the third decade, when more than
50% of cases occur. There is no gender predilection.

98

MRI examination identifies cortical disruption and soft tissue extension in the majority of cases.
Grossly, the tumor is centered in the medullary cavity and has a
firm and gritty consistency depending on the relative production of
matrix. There is indistinct demarcation from the native bone at the
margin. Cortical disruption and soft tissue extension are evident in
most cases.
Histopathology
The tumor is composed of low to moderately cellular sheets of fibroblastic cells arranged in loose bundles of fascicles and producing variable amounts of well-formed spicules of mixed woven and lamellar
bone (see Fig. 6-14). The fibroblasts are plump and uniform in appearance, with minimal atypia. Mitoses are infrequent, and atypical mitoses
are rare. Depending on the amount of bone produced, the tumor may
simulate a fibromatosis, fibrous dysplasia, or parosteal osteosarcoma.
Permeation and trapping of native bone at the periphery and soft tissue
extension are reliable indications of malignancy. Foci of high-grade
osteosarcoma, indicating dedifferentiation, were not reported in early
series but may be present at presentation in up to a 30% of cases in
more recent reviews.96,98 Local recurrences have an increased risk of
dedifferentiation.
Differential Diagnosis
High-grade fibroblastic osteosarcoma enters the differential but has
increased cellularity and pleomorphism as compared to low-grade
central osteosarcoma. It does not produce well-formed trabeculae of
osteoid. Fibrous dysplasia is the most common differential diagnostic
problem. The tumors occur in the same age range, involve the long
bones, may have a low cellularity fibroblastic stroma, and may share
a radiographic lytic appearance. The presence of cortical erosion,
involvement of the epiphysis, irregular borders, and coarse trabeculation all favor low-grade central osteosarcoma. A clinical history of pain
is also suggestive, because most cases of fibrous dysplasia present with
deformity without chronic pain. Histologically, soft tissue extension
and an invasive growth pattern at the margin are consistent with osteosarcoma, and trabeculae of mixed lamellar and woven bone favor
osteosarcoma; the trabeculae in fibrous dysplasia are typically composed of woven bone. Although atypia is subtle in low-grade central
osteosarcoma, extensive sampling usually yields areas with recognizable atypia.75

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Most cases occur in the long bones of the lower extremity, with 50%
occurring in the femur and 67% in the femur or tibia.97 These tumors
are metaphyseal or metaphyseal/diaphyseal in location and rarely
involve flat bones or the bones of the hands and feet. Pain, often existing for months or years, is the most frequent presenting symptom.
Swelling or a palpable mass is less common.

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
As with parosteal osteosarcoma, low-grade central osteosarcoma is
associated with amplification of 12q13-q15, which includes the genes
for CDK4 and MDM2.80 Using molecular and FISH techniques,
89% of low-grade central osteosarcoma cases had amplification,
as opposed to no cases of fibrous dysplasia, osteofibrous dysplasia,
ABC, nonossifying fibroma, and fibro-osseous lesions of the jaw.99
Positive immunohistochemical staining for either CDK4 or MDM2 is
present in 100% of low-grade osteosarcomatous lesions.100 Abnormalities of p53 and retinoblastoma genes are rare in low-grade central
osteosarcoma.101

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The majority of tumors have a lytic component, and 25% are predominantly lytic. Matrix production ranges from coarse trabeculation to
sclerotic (Fig. 6-14). Twenty-five percent of cases have disruption of
the cortex, and 50% have a periosteal reaction, less than seen in conventional osteosarcoma.97 Codman triangle is usually not present, and
33% of cases do not have radiographically aggressive features. CT and

Therapy and Prognosis
Surgical excision with a wide margin, usually radical limb salvage or
amputation, is the treatment of choice. Marginal excisions have a high
recurrence rate and increase the risk of dedifferentiation into a highgrade tumor. Five-year and 10-year survival rates are 90% and 85%,
respectively.102 Chemotherapy is usually reserved for tumors with a
high-grade component or metastases.

Osteochondromas and Hereditary
Multiple Osteochondroma
Definition and Synonyms
Osteochondroma, also known as exostosis or osteocartilaginous exostosis, is characterized by a cartilage-capped bony outgrowth containing
a marrow cavity with continuity to the underlying bone.
Historical Background
Osteochondromas are the most common benign bone tumors. The
tumors occur exclusively in bones of endochondral origin and thus
predominantly arise from the metaphysis of long bones, especially
those around the knee and in the proximal humerus. They commonly
develop during childhood or adolescence, with a peak incidence in the
second decade of life. Approximately 85% of osteochondromas are
solitary. The remainder are seen as part of hereditary multiple osteochondroma (HMO), an autosomal dominant disease thought to be
caused principally by germline loss-of-function mutations in either the
EXT1 or EXT2 gene.1
Incidence and Demographics
The reported incidence of osteochondromas in benign bone tumors is
33.4% and in chondrogenic tumors 32.8%, and osteochondromas
represent 10.1% of all bone tumors.2 However, it is thought that the
true incidence is much higher because many osteochondromas are
small in size and asymptomatic and thus are not clinically detected or
surgically removed. Most cases present in the first three decades of life
(approximately 50% in the second decade), with a slight male
predominance.

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Osteochondromas develop exclusively in bones of endochondral origin
and arise from the metaphyseal region near the growth plate of long,
tubular bones, most commonly the distal femur, proximal tibia and
fibula, and proximal humerus. The tumors occasionally develop in
bones of ribs, scapula, and pelvis. They rarely involve the short, tubular
bones of hands and feet. Many osteochondromas are asymptomatic
and are found incidentally. Long-standing lesions may produce a large,
hard mass; thus, patients may present with pain due to mechanical
irritation, although not all lesions are painful. Common secondary
complications include limitation of motion; impingement on nerves,
vessels, or tendons; bursa formation; arthritis; and stalk fracture.
Increasing pain and/or rapidly growing masses may indicate malignant
transformation to secondary chondrosarcomas.1,2
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologically, osteochondromas present as sessile, exophytic, or
pedunculated bony masses with cartilage caps (Figs. 7-1 and 7-2).
Conventional radiographs are usually sufficient for diagnosis of these
lesions. The cortex and medullary cavity of the lesions are continuous
with the cortex and medulla of the adjacent native bone, respectively,
which is typically appreciated by computed tomography (CT) (Fig.
7-3). Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is helpful in determining the
relationship of the lesions to adjacent structures and the thickness of
the cartilage cap (Fig. 7-4).
A bursa may develop over an osteochondroma (bursa exostotica)
and is usually attached to the perichondrium of the cap. The bursa may
become inflamed, with accumulation of a large amount of fluid (bursitis), thus resulting in gradually increasing pain. A thick cartilage cap
with infiltration into surrounding soft tissues may raise the question
of malignant transformation.1,2
The gross pathologic features usually recapitulate the radiologic
findings. A surgically resected specimen is typically a bony mass with
a smooth, glistening, bluish gray cartilage cap (Fig. 7-5). On cross
section, a rim of cortical bone and central medullary bone can be
appreciated, similar to that seen in normal bone. The cartilage cap is
usually thin, and its thickness decreases with age. A thick and irregular
cap (>2 cm) may be indicative of malignancy but can be seen in benign
lesions in skeletally immature individuals.1–4
103

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 7-1. A conventional radiograph shows typical radiologic features of an osteochondroma in the distal femur.

Figure 7-2. A conventional radiograph shows multiple sessile osteochondromas in a
patient with hereditary multiple osteochondroma.

Histopathology
On low-power view, osteochondromas typically have three layers: perichondrium, cartilage, and bone (Fig. 7-6). The surface perichondrium
(not seen over the normal articular cartilage) is continuous with the
periosteum of the parental bone. The underlying hyaline cartilage,
similar to normal articular cartilage, undergoes endochondral ossification simulating the growth plate, thus creating trabecular bone that is
surrounded by marrow adipose tissue and rare hematopoietic
104

Figure 7-3. A sagittal computed tomography image shows a large pedunculated
osteochondroma arising from the left proximal femur in a patient with hereditary
multiple osteochondroma. Note the continuity of marrow between the tumor and the
underlying skeleton.

Figure 7-4. A sagittal T1 magnetic resonance image of the osteochondroma shown
in Figure 7-3. A large adventitial bursa is associated with the tumor. Again, the continuity of marrow between the tumor and the underlying bone can be appreciated.

elements. This is continuous with the medullary canal of the underlying bone (Fig. 7-7).1–3
Compared with enchondromas of long bones, the cartilage of
osteochondromas typically shows increased cellularity and a greater
number of binucleated chondrocytes. Other secondary changes, such
as irregular calcification and myxoid degeneration, may be present,

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology Located on Bone Surface

7

Figure 7-5. Gross pathologic features of a surgically resected sessile osteochondroma
with a broad base. The lesion demonstrates a prominent cartilage cap with a smooth,
glistening, and bluish cut surface. Beneath the cap is normal-appearing cortical and
medullary bone.

Figure 7-6. This low-power view of an osteochondroma in cross section shows the
three layers: perichondrium, cartilage, and bone.

especially in larger, long-standing lesions. Significant nuclear atypia,
mitotic figures, and necrosis are typically not seen.
Differential Diagnosis
Markedly increased cellularity, along with significant nuclear atypia,
discernible mitotic activity, and tumor necrosis, should raise concern
for secondary chondrosarcoma. Rarely, dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma may develop in an osteochondroma. However, distinguishing
osteochondroma from low-grade secondary chondrosarcoma is difficult based on purely histologic grounds and should be made by a
multidisciplinary approach.
Genetics
Cytogenetic alterations involving 8q22-24.1, where the EXT1 gene is
located, are found in approximately 56% to 78% of HMO cases and up
to 80% of sporadic osteochondromas, whereas cytogenetic abnormalities involving the EXT2 gene (11p12-p11) are less frequent and are only
seen in HMO cases (21% to 44%).5,6 HMO is inherited in an autosomal

Figure 7-7. The surface of the osteochondroma is represented by hyaline cartilage
resembling an epiphyseal or growth plate. Underlying the cartilage cap is cancellous
bone, with marrow spaces filled by fatty or fibrous tissue and a few hematopoietic
elements.

dominant manner, with an estimated penetrance of 96%. About 10% of
affected individuals develop HMO as the result of a de novo mutation.
The cell origin in osteochondroma is thought to be the proliferating
chondrocyte of the growth plate. The EXT gene products, exostosin-1
and exostosin-2, are transmembrane glycoproteins involved in biosynthesis of heparan sulfate proteoglycans, which are the key modulators
of endochondral ossification. A mixture of wild-type and heparan
sulfate–mutated chondrocytes are present in the cartilage cap of osteochondromas.7 Most mutations in the EXT1 or EXT2 genes in HMO
patients are thought to result in a truncated or nonfunctional protein.8
Loss of heparan sulfate may give the chondrocyte a proliferative advantage, thus resulting in the loss of polarity and leading to altered hedgehog signaling.9 The double-hit mutation model was proposed for the
formation of osteochondromas.10,11 Based on this model, the chondrocytes harboring mutations causing disturbed hedgehog signaling lose
polarity and grow out through a defective bony collar.12
The molecular genetic testing for EXT genes includes fluorescence
in situ hybridization (FISH; for larger deletions) and sequence analysis
(for small intragenic deletions/insertions and missense, nonsense, and
splice site mutations).
Treatment and Prognosis
Osteochondromas usually stop growing at the time of growth plate
closure. Treatment is generally unnecessary for asymptomatic lesions.
If the tumor is causing pain or neurologic symptoms due to compression, a complete excision at the base is curative.
The risk of malignant transformation in solitary osteochondromas
increases as the number and size of the osteochondromas increases.
Sessile lesions are more likely to transform into sarcoma than pedunculated tumors. The estimated risk of malignant transformation to a
secondary chondrosarcoma is about 1% for solitary osteochondromas
and 5% to 30% in patients with HMO.

Bizarre Parosteal Osteochondromatous
Proliferation
Definition and Synonyms
Bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation (BPOP) is characterized by a rapidly growing bony mass involving the surface of bone,
105

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
usually affecting the small, tubular bones of the hands and feet. It was
first described in 1983 by Nora and colleagues13; thus, it is also known
as Nora lesion.
Historical Background
BPOP is typically small but has aggressive features on imaging studies
and worrisome findings histologically. It was originally thought to be
a reactive process; however, recent cytogenetic studies have identified
a nonrandom molecular signature t(1 : 17)(q32;q21), thus supporting
its neoplastic nature.14
Incidence and Demographics
BPOP can occur in individuals of all ages but is more commonly seen
in younger adults, with a peak incidence in the third and fourth
decades of life. Men and women are equally affected.15

(<2 cm) and often nodular, and it is composed of cartilage overlapping
and intercalating with a mostly bony mass.14–16
Histopathology
Histologically, the lesion consists of a heterogeneous mixture of cellular
cartilaginous tissue, bone, and proliferative fibroblasts that are arranged
in a haphazard manner (Fig. 7-9). The periphery of the lesion is typically well-circumscribed, lobulated cellular cartilage, and there may be
an overlying fibrous pseudocapsule (Fig. 7-10). Unlike the “columnation” of the cartilage seen in osteochondromas, the endochondral ossification in BPOP is less organized and irregular, with random and
patchy areas of ossification. The calcified matrix has a distinct blue tint
(referred to as “blue bone”), a characteristic feature of BPOP (Fig.
7-11). The cartilage is often hypercellular and the chondrocytes are
enlarged (“bizarre”), whereas the proliferative spindled fibroblasts and

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
BPOP most commonly involves the proximal and middle phalanges,
metacarpals, and metatarsals, and it rarely occurs in the long bones,
skull, and jaw. Swelling or mass with or without pain is the most
common clinical presentation. The mass seems to grow rapidly over
weeks or months. A history of trauma is typically absent.15
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
On conventional radiographs, a well-defined bony mass is seen
attached to the underlying cortex and may project into the soft tissue
(Fig. 7-8). CT is most helpful to distinguish BPOP from osteochondroma because neither does it show cortical or medullary continuity
with the adjacent bone nor a well-formed cartilage cap characteristic
of osteochondroma. On gross examination, the lesion is typically small

Figure 7-9. A low-power view of the lesion in bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous
proliferation shows a heterogeneous mixture of cellular cartilage, bone, and fibrous
tissue arranged in a disorganized fashion.

Figure 7-8. A conventional radiograph in bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation shows a mature osseous excrescence projecting from the distal portion of the
distal phalanx of a toe. The tumor appears to be an “add on” lesion, with no cortical
or medullary continuity with the parental bone. (From Wei S, Siegal GP. Atlas of Bone
Pathology. New York: Springer; 2013:391–396. Image courtesy of Dr. Michael Pitt.)
106

Figure 7-10. The periphery of the lesion in bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous
proliferation (BPOP) is typically well-circumscribed, lobulated, cellular cartilage. There
may be a fibrous pseudocapsule overlying the lesion. Unlike osteochondromas, the
endochondral ossification in BPOP is disorganized and irregular, with random and
patchy areas of ossification.

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology Located on Bone Surface
Genetics
Molecular cytogenetic studies have shown that most BPOP cases are
characterized by a t(1;17)(q32;q21) or its variants.17,18 The gene(s)
directly involved in the pathogenesis of BPOP have not been identified.
A number of other rare cytogenetic alterations have also been reported.14

7

Treatment and Prognosis
Surgical excision is the treatment of choice. Local recurrence may
occur in nearly 50% of cases. Given the high local recurrence rate and
the lack of adjuvant therapeutic options, long-term follow-up is
recommended.

Subungual Exostosis

Figure 7-11. The irregularly calcified matrix in bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous
proliferation (BPOP) has a distinct blue tint (so-called “blue bone”), a characteristic
feature of BPOP. Intermixed with disorganized bone and cartilage is fibrovascular connective tissue with variable cellularity.

Definition and Synonyms
Subungual exostosis is a benign osteochondromatous proliferation
arising from the dorsal surface of the distal phalanx, beneath or adjacent
to the nail bed. The tumor is also known as Dupuytren exostosis.14
Historical Background
First described by Miller-Breslow and Dorfman, subungual exostosis
was initially regarded as a reactive growth of cellular fibrous tissue and
metaplastic cartilage, which undergoes endochondral ossification.19
The exact pathogenesis of this condition is unclear, but it has been
thought to reflect reactive changes resulting from microtrauma.
However, recent cytogenetic findings have revealed clonal abnormalities and suggest that at least some of these heterotopic ossifications
could be neoplastic in nature, instead of being a purely reactive process
or exuberant growth in response to trauma.20
Incidence and Demographics
Subungual exostosis most commonly occurs in young individuals, with
a peak incidence in the second and third decades of life. The condition
has a male predominance.19
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The great toe is the most common location. The lesions may also occur
under other toes and on the fingers. Local swelling and pain are the
typical clinical presentations.

Figure 7-12. The cartilage is often hypercellular in bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation and shows enlarged, bizarre chondrocytes with maturation into bone,
whereas the proliferative spindled fibroblasts show minimal cytologic atypia.

the osteoblasts seen on the bony trabeculae show minimal cytologic
atypia (Fig. 7-12).14–16
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis of BPOP essentially includes most of surface
bony lesions such as osteochondroma as previously discussed, as well
as subungual exostosis (see subsequent text), periosteal chondroma,
and parosteal osteosarcoma. Periosteal chondroma (juxtacortical
chondroma) is more commonly seen in long bones, although it can
rarely involve the phalanges of the hands and feet. The tumor typically
has a thin cortical shell and/or internal calcifications characteristic of
cartilage on radiographs. It is histologically composed of lobulated
cellular cartilage with or without endochondral ossification at the
periphery. Parosteal osteosarcoma also typically arises on the surface
of long bones and shows a heavily ossified mass on imaging. The tumor
is histologically characterized by trabeculae of neoplastic bone, usually
arranged in a parallel fashion in the background of spindled fibroblastic cells with low-grade nuclei.

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Imaging studies typically reveal a calcifying projection connecting to
the underlying bone (Fig. 7-13). Like BPOP, but in contrast to osteochondroma, the lesion lacks clear continuity with both the cortex and
medullary cavity. Grossly, the lesion is usually composed of a cartilage
cap and a bony stalk.
Histopathology
Histologically, subungual exostoses typically show trabecular bone
covered by a hyaline cartilage cap, as in osteochondromas. However,
the resected specimen may not have a well-preserved architecture. It
shows fragmented mature fibrocartilaginous tissue and bone; thus, it
may resemble a callus (Fig. 7-14).14,21 Therefore, correlation with radiographic findings is critical.
Differential Diagnosis
The histologic differential diagnosis essentially includes osteochondroma and BPOP, as previously described.
Genetics
Molecular genetic analysis has delineated a tumor-specific translocation t(X;6)(q24-q26;q15-21) in subungual exostosis, thus strongly
107

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Florid Reactive Periostitis
Definition and Synonyms
Florid reactive periostitis (FRP)is a relatively rare benign bone lesion
that most commonly develops in the tubular bones of hands and feet
and is histologically characterized by a mixture of osteoid, bone, cartilage, and fibrous tissue. A number of names have been given to this
lesion, including florid reactive periostitis ossificans, benign fibroosseous pseudotumor, fasciitis ossificans, parosteal fasciitis, and pseudomalignant osseous tumor of soft tissue.24
Historical Background
Spjut and Dorfman were the first to coin the term florid reactive periostitis in 1981.25 The etiology of FRP is unknown but is thought to be
a reactive process rather than a true neoplasm; nearly 50% of patients
with FRP report a history of trauma to the area.24,25
Incidence and Demographics
The actual incidence of FRP is difficult to estimate. Patient age ranges
from 5 to 70 years, but most cases occur in the second and third
decades of life. Women are more frequently affected than men.25

Figure 7-13. A conventional radiograph in subungual exostosis demonstrates a bony
projection extending distally from the ulnar aspect of the tip of the tuft of the index
finger distal phalanx, continuous with the underlying bone.

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
FRP predominantly involves the small tubular bones of hands and feet.
It is most commonly seen in the proximal phalanx of the hand, followed by the middle phalanx, the metacarpals, and the distal phalanx.
Other sites such as long bones are rarely affected, but there have been
reports of such involvement. The lesion typically presents as a gradually progressive swelling with pain or a painless mass.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Soft tissue swelling along the surface of the bone and a marked periosteal reaction are characteristic features on conventional radiographs
in the early phase.26 The lesion may become ossified in the later stage.
The underlying cortex is usually intact, whereas cortical erosion may
also be present but is not an indicator of aggressiveness.27

Figure 7-14. Histologically, subungual exostosis typically shows an osteochondromalike architecture, with gradual transition from spindle cell proliferation to hyaline cartilage to trabecular bone. However, the resected specimen may not have a well-preserved
architecture.

suggesting its neoplastic nature. The breakpoint regions involved in
this translocation harbor the collagen genes COL12A1 and COL4A5 in
chromosome bands 6q13-14 and Xq22, respectively.22 This rearrangement results in increased expression of the IRS4 gene located in Xq22.23
Although the exact role of IRS4 remains poorly understood, the
members of the IRS family of proteins are known to act as mediators
of signaling from a number of growth factor receptors, including the
insulin and insulin-like growth factor I receptors. Thus, IRS4 may play
an important role in regulating cell growth and survival.
Treatment and Prognosis
Surgical excision is usually curative. Recurrence is rare.
108

Histopathology
Histologically, the lesion consists of a mixture of osteoid, cartilage, and
fibrous tissue of variable proportions in a loosely cellular and myxoid
stroma (Fig. 7-15).25 Maturation of osteoid into lamellar bone tends to
occur at the periphery of the lesion, similar to that seen in myositis
ossificans.28 Distinct cartilaginous tissue undergoing endochondral
ossification may also be seen. Spindled myofibroblasts/fibroblasts lack
pleomorphism. Mitotic figures may be discernible, but no atypical
forms are present. Other nonspecific findings include variable amounts
of mixed inflammatory cells; however, they are insufficient in number
to be confused with an infectious process.
Differential Diagnosis
The histologic differential diagnosis includes BPOP and, less likely,
osteosarcoma. The histologic findings of BPOP and FRP frequently
overlap. Therefore, some authorities originally considered these to represent a spectrum of the same lesion in different stages, but molecular
genetics do not support this proposition. BPOP differs from FRP in
that it typically has (1) a dominant cartilaginous component, which
forms a cap and undergoes endochondral ossification, and (2) fibrous
tissue, which is present between the bone and the cartilage. In addition,
the cartilage is hypercellular, contains large chondrocytes (“bizarre”),
and shows characteristic “blue bone.”
Parosteal osteosarcoma of the hand is exceedingly rare.29 The tumor
is typically composed of spindle cells with minimal atypia and

Lesions Characterized by Osteoid Deposition and Nonaggressive Radiology Located on Bone Surface

7

Figure 7-15. Histologically, florid reactive periostitis consists of a mixture of immature
and mature bone, cartilage, and fibrous tissue in variable proportions in a loosely cellular
and myxoid stroma. Maturation of osteoid into lamellar bone tends to occur at the
periphery of the lesion. Cartilaginous tissue undergoing endochondral ossification may
also be seen, as illustrated in this image. The stroma ranges from myxoid to fibrous,
with variable cellularity, but lacks significant cytologic atypia.

Figure 7-16. In fracture callus, bone matrices are formed as soon as 2 weeks after
injury. This image shows endochondral ossification within a fracture callus, which is
organized into microtrabeculae within a vascular-rich, loosely cellular stroma.

well-formed bony trabeculae arranged in a parallel manner. The woven
bone typically lacks osteoblastic rimming in contrast to FRP, in which
the trabeculae of osteoid and new bone are rimmed by plumped
osteoblasts.
Treatment and Prognosis
The clinical course of FRP appears benign. Spontaneous resolution has
been documented.25 Complete surgical excision has been proved to be
curative in the majority of cases, although local recurrence has been
reported.

Fracture Callus
Definition and Synonyms
Fracture callus is unorganized woven bone formed at the site of a
fracture which is ultimately replaced by mature, lamellar bone. Thus,
the formation of fracture callus is part of the natural healing process.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Reflecting its pathophysiologic process, the radiologic features of bone
healing are a continuum of progression, with considerable overlap.
These features largely include fracture gap widening, periosteal reaction, bridging, and remodeling. The calcified callus is typically seen 2
to 4 weeks after injury. The remodeling is typically well underway by
weeks 4 to 8.
Histopathology
The healing of a fracture is initiated by hemorrhage and hematoma
formation followed by granulation tissue formation, which provides
mechanical stability to the fracture site and induces osteoblast and
chondrocyte precursors to produce matrix.30,31 This is the so-called
reactive phase. Cartilage callus formation with a lacelike and/or sheetlike bone matrix may be seen as early as 2 weeks after injury; some of
this may be organized into microtrabeculae (Fig. 7-16). Both intramembranous and endochondral ossification may occur at the fracture
site. Mineralization of the callus results in bony callus. The progression
from cartilage to bone is orderly and nonpathologic and sometimes

Figure 7-17. In fracture callus, mineralization of the callus results in a bony callus.
Note the residual cartilage and active bone remodeling.

misidentified under the term chondro-osseous metaplasia (Fig. 7-17).32
Late in the reparative reaction, the cartilage disappears, and the bone
is organized into an arrangement of trabeculae with a cellular fibrous
stroma (reparative phase; Fig. 7-18).30,31 Subsequently, the newly
formed mesh of woven bone is replaced by lamellar bone, which is
organized parallel to the axis of the bone. Eventually, the remodeling
process substitutes the trabecular bone for compact bone, thus restoring normal cortical structure that duplicates the bone’s original shape
and strength. The remodeling phase is an ongoing process that lasts for
a number of years depending on the patient’s age and other general
conditions.
A nonunion refers to the healing of a fracture site with collagen-rich
soft tissue instead of bone.
Differential Diagnosis
Histologically, the lacelike bone matrices formed in early callus typically show hypercellular stroma and reactive atypia, which may closely
resemble an osteosarcoma. Infected nonunion typically shows reactive
bone and marrow fibrosis along with chronic inflammation, thus is
109

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 7-18. In facture callus, late in the reparative phase, the bone is organized into
a trabecular network within a cellular fibrous stroma. The cartilage disappears.

histologically indistinguishable from chronic osteomyelitis. Therefore,
clinical and radiologic correlation is crucial.

Juxtacortical Lesions
Anthony Montag, MD

High-Grade Surface Osteosarcoma 121

Juxtacortical lesions of bone are a group of entities with differing histogenesis and pathogenesis that arise in association with the periosteum. Periosteal stromal cells participate in fracture repair, and as such,
they are able to differentiate into fibroblastic, osteoblastic, and chondroblastic lineages. Therefore, lesions of the periosteum present a
variety of tissue components, particularly in those entities considered
to be reactive, proliferative lesions. In many cases, the differentiation
of reactive, benign, and malignant entities relies on clinical presentation, location, and radiographic findings as much as on histologic
appearance.

Florid Reactive Periostitis
Florid reactive periostitis (FRP) presents as a periosteal-based fibroblastic proliferation with intermixed fibro-osseous elements that can
histologically mimic osteosarcoma. It has histologic similarities to
nodular fasciitis, subungual exostosis, and bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation (BPOP).
Historical Overview
Reactive fibro-osseous lesions of the digits were described in 1933 by
Mallory in a review of “metaplastic and neoplastic bone and cartilage
containing tumors of soft parts.”1 Recognizing the clinical and histologic similarity to nodular fasciitis, the term “parosteal fasciitis” was
commonly used until the 1980s. Clinicopathologic and radiologic

characterization of FRP was described in 12 cases by Spujt and
Dorfman in 1981.1
Incidence and Demographics
FRP is uncommon. In a survey of 203 benign and reactive lesions of
the hands and feet, Ostrowski and Spujt found 16 cases of FRP, slightly
greater than the number of cases of BPOP.2 Most patients are between
20 and 40 years of age (range, first to fifth decade). The condition
occurs equally in both sexes.1 Although a history of trauma is usually
not elicited, the favored etiology is traumatic.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
More than 75% of FRP cases occur in the bones of the hand, followed
by the bones of the foot; the most common site is the proximal or
middle phalanx. It has occasionally been described in the long bones
of the upper and lower extremities.3 Presenting symptoms are usually
pain and swelling and, when erythema is present, an infectious etiology
is frequently favored clinically. When FRP occurs in the foot, discomfort is typically related to wearing shoes, and the clinical differential of
Morton neuroma may arise.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
In an early stage lesion, radiographs show a periosteal-based soft tissue
swelling, often circumferential, that with maturation becomes partially
ossified. There is adjacent subperiosteal new bone formation, sometimes laminated, and rarely scalloping of the underlying cortex
(Fig. 8-1). More mature lesions may assume an osteochondromatous
appearance but lack continuity with the marrow space as seen in conventional osteochondroma.4,5
Histopathology
Depending on the age of the lesion, there are varying degrees of fibroblastic and chondro-osseous differentiation. Early lesions are fibroblastic with variable cellularity, often with myxoid regions similar to
nodular fasciitis. As in nodular fasciitis, mitotic figures may be frequent, albeit normal.6 Early bone formation appears as wisps of osteoid
that coalesce into trabeculae of woven bone with osteoblast rimming
(Fig. 8-2). One third of cases have immature chondroid elements,
similar to those seen in fracture callus. Maturing lesions develop a
111

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
zonal appearance, with more organized arcades of woven and lamellar
bone at the periphery and immature woven bone centrally. Although
hypercellularity and reactive changes (open chromatin, nucleoli) may
be present in the cartilaginous component and elicit concern for chondrosarcoma, osteoblastic areas lack pleomorphism, and the appearance
is more reminiscent of fracture repair or BPOP.
Differential Diagnosis
Malignant entities in the differential diagnosis include high-grade juxtacortical osteosarcoma, parosteal osteosarcoma, and surface involvement by a medullary osteosarcoma. Radiologic correlation is helpful;
lack of cortical destruction and medullary involvement argues against
a primary medullary osteosarcoma as does the low incidence of
primary medullary osteosarcoma in the hands and feet. Parosteal

osteosarcomas are broad based, often nearly circumferential, and
heavily ossified. In contrast, FRP is periosteal based, has less ossification, and lacks a lobulated appearance. Histologically, parosteal osteosarcoma has well-developed, long bony trabeculae with an intervening
fibroblastic stroma with low cellularity and lacks the nodular fasciitis–
like areas and small trabeculae of woven bone as seen in FRP. Highgrade juxtacortical osteosarcoma displays obvious pleomorphism, a
high nucleus-to-cytoplasm ratio, and atypical mitotic figures. Osteoid
formation in high-grade tumors is variable and fishnet-like or lacelike,
instead of the trabecular pattern seen with osteoblast rimming as in
FRP, fracture, or other reactive lesions.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Much of the literature on FRP consists of case reports in radiologic
or clinical journals, with little published immunohistochemical or
molecular information. One study has found MDM2 and CDK4
immunostaining to be useful in differentiating low-grade osteosarcoma, including parosteal osteosarcoma, from a number of benign
and reactive conditions, including FRP.7 FRP has not been shown to
have a chromosomal abnormality as found in BPOP [t(1;17)] and
subungual exostosis [t(X;6)(q13-14;q22)],8,9 although they share histologic similarities.

Bizarre Parosteal Osteochondromatous
Proliferation
Bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation (BPOP), or Nora
lesion, is a benign, self-limited chondro-osseous proliferative lesion,
believed to be associated with trauma that has histologic features
similar to fracture repair.
Historical Overview
Nora and colleagues described 35 cases of a distinctive osteochondromatous proliferation of the extremities in 1983.10 Most cases
involved bones of the hand, although involvement of the small bones
of the foot was also seen. More recently, the acronym BPOP has been
favored over the eponymous designation.

Figure 8-1. Florid reactive periostitis. A periosteal reaction and ossifying soft tissue
mass involving the fourth metatarsal.

A

Incidence and Demographics
The largest single institution experience, an update of the original
series, was published in 1993, reporting 65 cases. Patients ranged in

B

Figure 8-2. Florid reactive periostitis. A, Intermixture of osseous and fibroblastic components with vague zonation. B, A seam of woven bone coalescing in an osteoblastic area.
112

Juxtacortical Lesions

8

Figure 8-4. Bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation. A vaguely zonal
pattern of osteoid and cartilage is present, lacking the well-defined progression seen
in osteochondroma. Note the areas of bluish-tinted osteoid matrix.

Figure 8-3. Bizarre parosteal osteochondromatous proliferation. A well-defined ossifying periosteal and soft tissue lesion of the second phalanx.

age from 8 to 73 years (mean, 34 years), and the study included 35
lesions in the hand, 10 in the feet, and 17 in the long bones.11 In both
series, there was no predilection by gender and the local recurrence
rate was 55%. In a survey of 203 benign and reactive lesions of the
hands and feet, Ostrowski and Spujt found nine cases of BPOP.2
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
BPOP usually presents as a painless palpable mass adjacent to the shaft
of one of the phalanges or metacarpals. About 30% of patients give a
history of trauma.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographically, BPOP is a well-marginated surface lesion with variable periostitis, edema, and tumefaction, depending on the duration
of the lesion (Fig. 8-3). In contrast to a conventional osteochondroma,
ossification and calcification are present in a disorderly pattern. Also,
the lesion is not attached to the cortex by a pedicle and does not communicate with the medullary cavity. Grossly, the lesion is usually less
than 2 cm and is composed of intermixed bone and cartilage, without
the orderly maturation seen in osteochondroma.
Histopathology
Microscopically, BPOP has variable amounts of three components:
cartilage, bone, and spindle cells. Thus, it resembles a fracture callus
(Fig. 8-4). The usually predominant cartilage component is hypercellular with large immature chondrocytes, raising concerns for malignancy. The osteoid component includes trabeculae of woven bone
intermixed with the fibroblastic element and areas of ossification of the
lesional cartilage matrix. Osteoblast rimming is present, and the reactive bone frequently has a distinctive bluish tint, similar to that seen
in osteoid osteomas. The fibroblastic component, although mitotically
active and reactive in appearance, lacks pleomorphism. Occasionally,
a pseudocartilage cap without a perichondrial layer is present, causing
confusion with an osteochondroma, further emphasizing the need for
radiologic correlation.

Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis of BPOP includes conventional osteochondroma; juxtacortical osteosarcoma (parosteal or periosteal); and if
cartilage predominates, surface chondroma and chondrosarcoma. As
described above, BPOP lacks the orderly maturation from cartilage
cap to mature bone and marrow space seen in an osteochondroma.
Both parosteal and periosteal osteosarcoma are extremely rare in the
hands and feet, with fewer than 10 cases in the literature.12 However,
25% of cases of BPOP occur in the long bones, where the lesions are
frequently larger and the differential diagnosis of juxtacortical osteosarcoma is of real concern. Parosteal osteosarcoma has a broad,
sessile attachment to the cortex, and microscopically has long, wellformed trabeculae of mixed woven and lamellar bone separated by a
relatively bland fibroblastic proliferation. In contrast, the trabeculae
in BPOP are short, often have a distinctive blue tint, and are intermixed with fibroblastic and chondroid elements. Malignant chondroid lesions, whether on the surface or in soft tissue, are extremely
rare in the hands and feet. In the long bones, they would lack the
intermixture of cartilaginous, bony, and fibroblastic elements seen
in BPOP.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
A recent cytogenetic and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH)
study found evidence of a t(1;17)(q32;q21) translocation in all of five
cases of BPOP examined, suggesting that BPOP is a benign locally
recurrent neoplasm as opposed to a reactive proliferative process.13
The genes involved in the translocation have not as yet been
identified.
Treatment and Prognosis
Conservative excision is followed by recurrence in 50% of cases. Thus,
one or more recurrences should not necessarily suggest the lesion is
actually a missed juxtacortical osteosarcoma.

Subungual Exostosis
Subungual exostosis is a benign, self-limited proliferative lesion that
occurs in the distal phalanx. It is believed to be secondary to trauma,
and is histologically similar to BPOP.
113

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Historical Overview
Subungual exostosis was first described by Dupuytren in 1817 and was
followed by his series of five cases in 1847.14 It shares common clinical
and histologic features with BPOP.15
Incidence and Demographics
Its peak incidence is in the second and third decades. The lesion occurs
more frequently in men than in women.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The lesion occurs only in the distal phalanx, usually below the nail bed.
The great toe is involved in 80% of cases. Symptoms of swelling and
pain are common, the latter sometimes quite intense due to its association with the nail bed. There is usually a history of trauma.16
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographically, subungual exostosis evolves from a soft tissue periosteal density to a calcifying mass with trabeculated bone extending
distally from beneath the nail bed (Fig. 8-5). In contrast to an osteochondroma, it is not continuous with the marrow space of the underlying bone.
Histopathology
Histologically, early lesions may have a reactive fibroblastic appearance
similar to a fasciitis or periostitis, but as the lesion matures it displays
a mixture of immature cartilage and bone similar to BPOP (Fig. 8-6).
Differential Diagnosis
The histologic differential diagnosis is similar to the discussion above
for BPOP. However, the distinctive location and radiologic appearance
should signal the high likelihood of subungual exostosis.

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Subungual exostosis has a pathognomic t(X;6)(q22;q13-14) translocation,17 which has recently been shown to involve a collagen gene,
COL12A1, and an insulin receptor substrate gene, IRS4.18 Translocations involving collagen genes have been described in other mesenchymal tumors, including dermatofibrosarcoma protuberans, lipoblastoma,
aneurysmal bone cyst, and chondromyxoid fibroma. In the latter
example, the COL12A1 gene is also involved. Subungual exostosis, like
BPOP, is a histologically reactive process that genetically appears to be
a benign neoplasm.
Treatment and Prognosis
Simple excision affords symptomatic relief. Recurrences are
uncommon.

Periosteal Chondroma
Periosteal chondroma is a periosteal-based benign cartilaginous tumor
that is histologically similar to enchondroma (see Chapter 9).
Historical Overview
Because of clinical and histologic overlap with similar benign cartilaginous and osteocartilaginous lesions, a clearly defined periosteal chondroma did not appear in the literature until a review of tumors of the
hand by Mason in 1937 included a case involving the proximal
phalanx.19 Two cases, involving a middle phalanx and a metatarsal,
were reported in 1952 by Lichtenstein and Hall, with a description of
distinctive radiographic and histologic features.20 A series of 23 lesions
published in 198221 and the 1985 Mayo clinic series of 46 patients22 are
the largest experiences.
Incidence and Demographics
Periosteal chondroma is uncommon, accounting for less than 2% of
chondromas of bone.23 It occurs in all ages but is most common in the
second and third decade (average approximately 25 years). There is a 3 : 2
male predominance. In the Mayo clinic series, periosteal chondroma
was three times more common than periosteal chondrosarcoma.22
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Periosteal chondroma involves the metaphysis or diaphysis of the long
bones, particularly the proximal humerus and bones of the hand and

Figure 8-5. Subungual exostosis. Radiograph shows a osteocartilaginous subungual
mass of the great toe that displaces the nail. The lesion is broad based, lacking the
stalk of an osteochondroma.
114

Figure 8-6. Subungual exostosis. Well-formed cartilage cap with secondary ossification. There is no continuity with the underlying medullary cavity.

Juxtacortical Lesions

8

A

B

Figure 8-7. Periosteal chondroma. A, A distal tibial surface lesion with a well-defined sclerotic cortex at the lesion’s edge. A wispy pattern of mineralization is consistent with a
cartilaginous tumor. B, The computed tomography image shows the classic “saucerization” appearance with raised edges of cortical bone. The translucent gap corresponds to
nonmineralized cartilaginous matrix, whereas the more superficial aspect of the tumor has areas of secondary ossification.

of the lower extremity.21 In a review of 203 benign tumors of the hand,
12 were periosteal chondromas.2 Patients usually present with a palpable mass, sometimes with pain, although about 20% are incidentally
found. As with most bone and soft tissue tumors, a history of injury is
elicited in about 25% of cases and is probably not related to the development of the lesion. Multifocal lesions have rarely been reported.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologically, the lesion arises in the metaphyseal or diaphyseal periosteum and produces a scalloping or “saucerization” of the underlying
cortex buttressed by circumferential reactive bone, described by Lichtenstein and Hall as “a comparatively small, peculiar trough-like hollow
or declivity in the cortex.”20 The matrix may be translucent or display
focal mineralization (Fig. 8-7). Most lesions are 2 to 3 cm in diameter,
although lesions may be as large as 6.5 cm. The tumor frequently shells
out of the underlying bone easily. Grossly, the tumor is whitish blue,
translucent, rubbery, circumscribed, and lobulated, with an overlying
periosteum. The radiologic appearance is virtually identical to that
described for juxtacortical chondromyxoid fibroma (see Chapter 9),
although mineralization in the latter may be extensive.
Histopathology
When removed en bloc, there is a sharply demarcated border with a
sclerotic rim, with no infiltration of surrounding bony trabeculae (Fig.
8-8). Although typically the appearance is similar to an enchondroma,
many cases display hypercellularity and hyperchromasia greater than
that seen in nondigital enchondromas, which may lead to a consideration of chondrosarcoma. The circumscription seen radiographically
and at low power is important in recognition of the correct
diagnosis.
Differential Diagnosis
Other benign juxtacortical cartilaginous tumors can be excluded on
radiographic or clinical grounds. Osteochondroma has a dense bony

Figure 8-8. Periosteal chondroma. Periosteum delineates the border of the lowcellularity cartilage tumor, with no soft tissue infiltration. Secondary ossification is
present.

component, a distinctive stalk, and continuity of the marrow space of
the lesion with the underlying medullary space. Both BPOP and subungual exostosis have areas of osteoid and lack the characteristic scalloping or saucerization of the underlying cortex. Histologically, BPOP
and subungual exostosis have variable mixtures of osteoid, cartilage,
and fibroblastic components as opposed to a lobulated cartilage matrix.
Periosteal chondrosarcoma rarely involves the tubular bones of the
hands and feet, and most tumors are more than 5 cm in diameter.
Furthermore, there is usually radiographic evidence of cortical destruction and a soft tissue mass. However, small periosteal chondrosarcomas may be radiographically indistinct from periosteal chondromas.
Histologically, periosteal chondrosarcoma displays soft tissue invasion,
115

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
less commonly with bone invasion, and it typically shows greater
hypercellularity and atypia than periosteal chondroma.22
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Mutations of mitochondrial isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 (IDH1) are
common in enchondroma, in central chondrosarcoma, and in periosteal chondroma and chondrosarcoma. They are not present in other
cartilaginous tumors such as chondroblastic osteosarcoma, chondroblastoma, osteochondroma, and mesenchymal chondrosarcoma.24
Cytogenetics of periosteal chondroma reveals complex karyotypes
but with recurring rearrangements involving 11q13-15, 2q37, and
4q21-24.25,26
Therapy and Prognosis
Treatment is by en bloc resection in most cases. However, lesser surgery
is not associated with a significant recurrence rate.21

Periosteal Chondrosarcoma
Periosteal chondrosarcoma is a malignant periosteal-based tumor histologically similar to central chondrosarcoma.
Historical Overview
In 1955, a single case of periosteal chondrosarcoma of the proximal
humerus was described by Lichtenstein.27 In 1958, Jaffe identified juxtacortical chondrosarcoma as an entity.28 A 1982 report from the Instituto Rizzoli established that periosteal chondrosarcoma and periosteal
osteosarcoma are distinct clinical and histologic entities.29
Incidence and Demographics
Periosteal chondrosarcoma is rare, constituting less than 0.7% of chondrosarcomas according to 1973 to 2003 Surveillance Epidemiology and
End Results (SEER) data.30 Tumors occur most frequently in the
second to fourth decade, on average about 10 years later than in periosteal chondroma; only one third of patients are younger than 30 years
of age.22 There is a slight male predominance.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The most common locations are the metaphyses of the femur and
humerus, with lesions only rarely arising in the hands and feet.22 Most
patients present with pain, with or without a palpable mass, and symptoms may persist for years before presentation.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The tumor presents as a metaphyseal, well-marginated, periostealbased soft tissue mass with peripheral or spotty calcification characteristic of cartilage neoplasms (Fig. 8-9).31 The majority of the lesions
are larger than 5 cm, ranging up to 20 cm.22
Although a saucer-like cortical depression with peripheral buttressing may be present as in periosteal chondroma, cortical destruction is
more frequent, albeit without involvement of the medullary cavity.
Grossly, the tumor is gray, lobulated, shiny, and speckled with flecks of
calcification (Fig. 8-10). Erosion of the cortex may be grossly visible.
Histopathology
Lobules of cartilage with low to moderate cellularity are present, which
may overlap with the histologic appearance of a periosteal chondroma
(Fig. 8-11). Metaplastic reactive bone may be present at the margin,
but no osteoid is directly produced by the tumor. Cytologic atypia is
greater than in periosteal chondroma. Extension of tumor through the
periosteal cap into soft tissue is a frequent finding, whereas involvement of the marrow space is uncommon.
116

Differential Diagnosis
Differentiation from periosteal chondroma is based on size, soft tissue
infiltration, and cytologic atypia. The older average age and the rarity
of periosteal chondrosarcoma in the small bones of the hand and feet
are additional clues to the diagnosis. Differentiation from periosteal
osteosarcoma can be made on both radiologic and histologic grounds.
Periosteal chondrosarcoma is metaphyseal, fairly sessile, and not circumferential, with popcorn-like or stippled calcification but not with
trabeculae of bone.29 Periosteal osteosarcoma, by contrast, presents as
a diaphyseal fusiform elevation of the periosteum with faint trabeculae of bone running perpendicular to the periosteum and a vague
starburst appearance. Elevation of the periosteum at the periphery
may form a Codman triangle. Grossly, periosteal chondrosarcoma
is more lobulated and tends to be composed purely of hyaline cartilage, whereas periosteal osteosarcoma typically has areas of fibroblastic and osteoblastic differentiation.31 Periosteal chondrosarcoma
harbors mutations in IDH1, which are not found in periosteal
osteosarcoma.24
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
As with periosteal chondroma, mutations of mitochondrial IDH1 are
present, similar to enchondroma and central chondrosarcoma, but in
contrast to chondroblastic osteosarcoma, chondroblastoma, osteochondroma, and mesenchymal chondrosarcoma.24 There are little data
on cytogenetic abnormalities in periosteal chondrosarcoma, although
they seem distinct from central chondrosarcoma.32
Therapy and Prognosis
Treatment is surgical, usually by en bloc resection. Local recurrence
occurs in roughly 25% of cases, usually from inadequate excision.
Incomplete or marginal excision is followed by local recurrence in 75%
of cases. Larger series have found metastases in 25% of cases.33

Periosteal Osteosarcoma
Periosteal osteosarcoma is an intermediate-grade chondroblastic
osteosarcoma arising on the surface of bone beneath the periosteum.
Historical Overview
Periosteal osteosarcoma was described by Unni and associates in
197634 as a lesion clinically and histologically distinct from parosteal
osteosarcoma. In 1982, in a report of 27 cases,29 Bertoni and coworkers
clarified periosteal chondrosarcoma and periosteal osteosarcoma as
distinct entities.
Incidence and Demographics
Periosteal osteosarcoma accounts for less than 2% of all the osteosarcomas and is about one third as common as parosteal osteosarcoma.33,35
The peak incidence is in the second and third decades of life, although
in larger series, about 10% of cases occur in patients older than 50
years. In most series, there is a slight male predominance, although the
largest interinstitutional series shows a slight female predominance.36,37
Occasional multifocal cases and a familial association have been
reported.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
In contrast to the metaphyseal location of parosteal osteosarcoma,
periosteal osteosarcoma has a distinct diaphyseal or diaphyseal/
metaphyseal predilection. It usually presents as a broad, sessile anterior
medial lesion that may wrap nearly around the circumference of the
bone.38 The distal femur and proximal tibia account for nearly 80%
of cases, followed by the humerus, fibula, ulna, and pelvis.36,37 Less

soft tissue extension. C, A magnetic resonance image shows thinning of the cortex
below the lesion but no involvement of the marrow space.

Figure 8-10. Periosteal chondrosarcoma. A bisected gross lesion shows a shiny
