Figure 8-12. Periosteal osteosarcoma. A, A broad-based femoral diaphyseal surface lesion with fluffy mineralization and soft tissue extension. B, A magnetic resonance image
better shows the nearly circumferential tumor and the extent of soft tissue involvement.

frequent sites include the clavicle, ribs, cranium, and the jaw.35 One
case of bilateral metachronous lesions has also been reported.
Limb swelling, mass, and/or pain are the most common presenting
symptoms, usually for less than 1 year’s duration and, in 50% of cases,
for less than 6 months.33
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographically, the tumor is a soft tissue mass broadly based on the
cortex, usually without involvement of the medullary canal (Fig. 8-12).
Cortical thickening and extrinsic cortical scalloping are present in 82%
and 92% of cases, respectively.38,39 A periosteal reaction perpendicular
to the bone’s long axis and extending into the tumor mass, often with
a sunburst or “hair on end” pattern, is present in 95% of cases, whereas
the classic Codman triangle is seen less frequently. In the majority of
cases, other foci of mineralization are seen in the soft tissue component. Both computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance
imaging (MRI) are useful for defining margins and the relationship of
the tumor to major vessels and peripheral nerves.38
Grossly, the tumor arises from the bone surface, is broad based, and
may involve nearly the entire circumference (Fig. 8-13).39 Cortical
thickening is conspicuous, and secondary scalloping of the thickened
or native cortex is seen in most cases. Extension of tumor into the
medullary canal has been reported but is rare. The gross appearance
in dominated by glistening gray cartilaginous matrix; however, the
base of the tumor may be heavily ossified. Calcified spicules extend
perpendicularly from the cortex into the overlying mass, gradually
tapering into a cartilaginous matrix. The advancing outer margin is
generally well delineated by a pseudocapsule derived from the thickened periosteum and fibrous reaction.
Histopathology
Initial needle or open biopsy, usually taken from the area beneath the
pseudocapsule, will show a predominance of atypical cartilage, occasionally with a myxoid cartilaginous matrix (Fig. 8-14). The differential
of a moderately differentiated juxtacortical chondrosarcoma or central
chondroblastic osteosarcoma with soft tissue extension may arise, and
correlation with clinical and radiographic data, essential in evaluating
all bone lesions, is particularly important for juxtacortical tumors.
Osteoid-producing areas have the appearance of an intermediate-grade
osteosarcoma intermixed with cartilaginous elements. Alternatively,
118

Figure 8-13. Periosteal osteosarcoma. A specimen bisected in the long axis to illustrate the relationship to the cortex and periosteum. Tumor erodes the cortical bone but
does not reach the marrow space. Grossly, the tumor appears largely cartilaginous.

occasional fibroblastic areas at the tumor periphery may simulate an
intermediate-grade fibroblastic osteosarcoma.35
Differential Diagnosis
The radiology is sufficiently distinctive that other cartilaginous neoplasms, such as periosteal chondroma and chondrosarcoma, can
usually be ruled out. Although the cellularity and atypia of periosteal
chondrosarcoma and periosteal osteosarcoma may be similar, the
former lacks neoplastic osteoid and the latter does not harbor mutations in IDH1.24 Immunohistochemistry for IDH1 is therefore useful
in differentiating periosteal chondrosarcoma from periosteal osteosarcoma. Parosteal osteosarcoma, although similarly juxtacortical and

Juxtacortical Lesions
cases.48 In 1984, a Mayo clinic series described dedifferentiation to a
high-grade osteosarcomatous component.49

8

Incidence and Demographics
Accounting for only 4% of osteosarcoma cases, parosteal osteosarcoma
is nevertheless the most frequent juxtacortical malignancy and is three
times more common than periosteal osteosarcoma. Patients are on
average 10 years older than those with conventional osteosarcoma,
most commonly between the ages of 20 and 40, with a third of cases
occurring in the third decade. There is a slight female predominance,
up to two thirds in some series.48 About 15% to 20% of cases have areas
of dedifferentiation, either in the primary lesion or in recurrences.48
Patients with dedifferentiation are on average 10 years older than
conventional parosteal osteosarcoma patients, suggesting that the
tumor may be present for a long interval before undergoing
dedifferentiation.
Figure 8-14. Periosteal osteosarcoma. A highly atypical cartilaginous component with
transition to osteoid production by tumor cells.

broad-based, is metaphyseal in location and densely sclerotic while
periosteal osteosarcoma tends to be diaphyseal and display less osteoid
matrix. Histologically, periosteal osteosarcoma lacks the broad trabeculae and intervening low cellularity fibroblastic stroma that is
typical of parosteal osteosarcoma, although one half of the latter may
display areas of cartilaginous differentiation. Areas of conventional
high-grade osteoblastic or fibroblastic osteosarcoma histology should
raise the possibility of a high-grade juxtacortical osteosarcoma or
dedifferentiation of a parosteal osteosarcoma.33
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Immunostaining for IDH1 is useful in the differentiation of periosteal
osteosarcoma from periosteal chondrosarcoma, being consistently
positive in the latter.24 The genetic alterations observed in this subtype
have been largely inconsistent40 and include examples of separate cases
with trisomy 1741 and 20q12-q13.242; gains of 2q, 5p, 8q, portions of
12p and 12q, and chromosomes 14 and 21; as well as losses of chromosomes 6, 8p, and 13.43 Complex chromosomal alterations have been
reported by others.44,45 Point mutations in p53 are seen in 40% of periosteal osteosarcomas, similar to the frequency in high-grade central
osteosarcoma.
Therapy and Prognosis
Periosteal osteosarcoma has a relatively good prognosis, with an overall
5- and 10-year survival of 89% and 83%, respectively. Approximately
15% of patients develop metastases.36,37,46 Surgical excision is the
primary therapeutic modality, with most cases amenable to limb
salvage. Marginal or intralesional excision is associated with an
increased risk of local recurrence, varying from 14% to 67%. Published
series have found that chemotherapy provides no survival benefit, and
postchemotherapy necrosis in the excision specimen was nonpredictive.37,46 Age, size, and adequacy of margins are not predictive of longterm survival. However, local recurrence is highly associated with an
increased risk of metastasis.36

Parosteal Osteosarcoma
Historical Overview
First described in 1951 by Geschickter as a low-grade malignant
surface osteoma,47 parosteal osteosarcoma is an uncommon low-grade
juxtacortical tumor. The larger series have reported 12548 and 226

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
More than 67% of cases occur in the distal femur, with the proximal
tibial and humerus the next most common sites. Uncommonly, the
tumor involves the small bones of the hands and feet, and the flat bones
are rarely involved. Patients present with a several-year history of a
painless mass that may impede flexion or rotation of the joint. Pain,
when present, is described as chronic and dull.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologically, the appearance is characteristic, and primary surgery is
often performed based on the radiologic appearance alone (Fig. 8-15).
The metaphyseal, broadly periosteal-based tumor wraps around the
shaft, sometimes nearly circumferentially. A periosteal reaction extends
into the tumor mass with a “hair on end” appearance, but a Codman
triangle is seldom seen at the edge of the lesion. The underlying cortical
bone is thickened and frequently scalloped. But it is relatively spared
as evidenced by an intervening thin radiolucent line of periosteum that
is seen in 67% of cases.50 The underlying cortical bone displays thickening and scalloping in some cases. Marrow space involvement is present
in 25% to 50% of cases, better seen with CT or MRI imaging.51,52 The
base and central part of the tumor are densely mineralized, with less
mineralization peripherally.
Grossly, the tumor is lobulated, highly ossified, and broadly based
on the periosteum (Fig. 8-16). A cartilaginous component may be
present and occasionally mimics the cartilage cap of an osteochondroma. Involvement of the medullary cavity may be present but
appears to have no prognostic significance.
Dedifferentiation in parosteal osteosarcoma is usually accompanied
by a nonossified intralesional soft tissue component.53 This is better
seen on CT and MRI studies.54
On gross examination, nonossified, fish flesh–like areas should be
sampled extensively to rule out dedifferentiation. Occasionally, a
hemorrhagic cystic component is present, suggesting telangiectatic
osteosarcoma.
Histopathology
Parosteal osteosarcoma is a low to moderately cellular fibroblastic
tumor with mild atypia and low mitotic activity separated by long
parallel bony trabeculae of woven and lamellar bone (Fig. 8-17). Osteoblast rimming may be present.52 Approximately 50% of cases may have
a cartilaginous component, more commonly near the periphery and
lacking the orderly maturation to bone seen in an osteochondroma.
Generally, little osteoid is present in the intervening fibroblastic zones.
Extension into surrounding soft tissue and the medullary cavity is
present in 46% and 22% of cases, respectively.51
119

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

Figure 8-15. Parosteal osteosarcoma. A, A large femoral posterior metaphyseal mass, nearly circumferential, with dense ossification—a classic radiographic appearance.
B, A magnetic resonance image confirms the circumferential nature of the tumor.

A

B

Figure 8-16. Parosteal osteosarcoma. A, An excision specimen. Note the lobulated surface appearance and the extension into the intercondylar notch. B, A specimen bisected
down the long axis. Note the intact cortex and sparing of the medullary space.

Although most parosteal osteosarcomas are low-grade fibroblastic
lesions, about 20% will contain high-grade elements at presentation,
indicating dedifferentiation (see Fig. 8-17).55,56 High-grade osteoblastic
or chondroblastic osteosarcoma are the most common malignant
elements, although giant cell–rich and telangiectatic histologies also
occur.57
Differential Diagnosis
Although the radiographic appearance of parosteal osteosarcoma is
highly characteristic, osteochondromas may simulate the sessile broadbased appearance. Even when a cartilage cap is present, parosteal
osteosarcomas lack the orderly maturation from cartilage to bone and
the continuity with the underlying bone marrow and displays cytologic
atypia not typical for osteochondroma. Osteochondromas lack the
low-grade fibroblastic component of parosteal osteosarcoma.
120

The radiographic appearance helps separate parosteal osteosarcoma from periosteal osteosarcoma. The former is metaphyseal and
densely mineralized, whereas the latter is diaphyseal and has the
peripheral calcification characteristic of cartilage tumors. Although a
cartilaginous component may be present in parosteal osteosarcoma,
periosteal osteosarcoma lacks the fibroblastic component. Differentiation from high-grade juxtacortical osteosarcoma may be more problematic, particularly for a parosteal osteosarcoma with extensive
dedifferentiation. The presence of a low-grade fibroblastic component
argues for dedifferentiation as opposed to a de novo high-grade
tumor. As with low-grade central osteosarcoma, parosteal osteosarcoma overexpresses MDM2 and CDK4, even in the high-grade components.58 Decalcification decreases reactivity of antibodies for CDK4
and MDM2 with the corresponding antigen, so actual staining may be
variable.

Juxtacortical Lesions

8

A

B

Figure 8-17. Parosteal osteosarcoma. A, Long, well-formed spicules of mixed woven and lamellar bone are present. The intervening low cellularity fibroblastic component has
minimal atypia and mitotic activity. B, Dedifferentiation in parosteal osteosarcoma. Between the broad, bony trabeculae are high-grade osteoblastic cells. At recurrence, the tumor
was predominantly high-grade osteoblastic osteosarcoma.

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Cytogenetically, parosteal osteosarcoma has a fairly simple karyotype,
with supernumerary ring chromosomes containing amplified segments from the 12q13-15 region. CDK4 and MDM2 genes are amplified in more than 85% of cases.59,60
Although high-grade osteosarcomas have been reported with
similar ring chromosomes, these may be examples of dedifferentiation
from low-grade tumors.58 In general, whether arising centrally or in
the periosteum, or if a high-grade osteosarcoma displays immunoreactivity for CDK4 and MDM2, a careful search should be made for a
low-grade component.

Incidence and Demographics
High-grade surface osteosarcoma is rare, constituting less than 5% of
juxtacortical osteosarcomas. Most patients are in the second and third
decades of life (range 10 to 66 years, mean 21 years), and there is a 2 : 1
male predominance.63

Therapy and Prognosis
Parosteal osteosarcoma is treated by complete excision with margins,
which frequently involves a limb-sparing excision of the metaphysis
and epiphysis with hardware or bone graft. Incomplete excision greatly
increases the risk of local recurrence, which was as high as 88% in one
report.52 Metastasis is rare in cases of low-grade parosteal osteosarcoma, whereas dedifferentiation is associated with a greatly increased
risk of metastasis. In a review of the Mayo clinic experience, 10 of 11
parosteal osteosarcoma patients dying of metastatic disease had dedifferentiation in the primary tumor.51 Although there is no role for
chemotherapy in the management of low-grade parosteal osteosarcoma, a high-grade component warrants chemotherapy, preoperatively
if the component is identified by biopsy.

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Tumors are diaphyseal in 67% of cases and metaphyseal in the remaining 33%. The lesions arise from the periosteum, ranging from 4.5 to
22 cm in largest dimension (Fig. 8-18). About 50% are circumferential.63 The cortex is frequently disrupted, and 50% of cases have marrow
space involvement on CT or MRI studies. Mineralization is frequently
dense and poorly organized. A Codman triangle is frequently present,
and the periosteal stripe that separates parosteal osteosarcoma from
the cortex is not seen.
Grossly, the tumor is periosteal based with soft tissue extension and
frequent erosion of the cortex. Although marrow space involvement is
also frequent, the dominant mass is extraosseous. Color and texture
vary depending on the proportion of osteoblastic, fibroblastic, or chondroblastic elements.

High-Grade Surface Osteosarcoma
High-grade surface (or juxtacortical) osteosarcoma is a primary periosteal malignancy that exhibits histology similar to high-grade central
osteosarcoma. It is not associated with a low-grade component.61
Historical Overview
A series of nine cases was reported by Wold and colleagues in 1984,
who noted that many lesions were radiographically similar to periosteal osteosarcoma but histologically indistinguishable from conventional high-grade osteosarcoma. Seven of the nine patients died of
metastatic disease.62 The largest series is Okada and coworkers’ 1999
update of the Mayo clinic experience, reporting 46 cases.61

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Seventy percent of cases occur in the long bones of the lower extremity,
50% in the femur, and 10% in the humerus. Lesions are typically
diaphyseal. Nearly all patients present with a mass and two thirds with
pain, of an average of 11 months’ duration.63

Histopathology
Eighty percent of cases are osteoblastic, and most others are chondroblastic. In terms of cellularity and atypia, the histology resembles
central high-grade osteosarcoma (Fig. 8-19). Low-grade fibroblastic
elements, long well-formed trabeculae of bone, or a low-grade cartilaginous component should raise the possibility of dedifferentiation of
a parosteal or periosteal osteosarcoma.
Differential Diagnosis
Periosteal and soft tissue extension of a central high-grade osteosarcoma can be ruled out on radiology, particularly CT and MRI imaging.
High-grade surface osteosarcoma involves the marrow space in 50%
121

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

Figure 8-18. High-grade surface osteosarcoma. A, A periosteum-based tibial lesion with a soft tissue mass. Note the mineralization. B, A magnetic resonance image better shows
soft tissue extension and confirms that the cortex and marrow space are free of involvement.

Therapy and Prognosis
Therapy is similar to that of high-grade central osteosarcoma: neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by a limb-sparing excision. The most
recent series that includes therapy in the modern era reported a 5-year
survival of 82%.63

of cases, but the bulk of the tumor involvement is extraosseous. Parosteal osteosarcoma has amplification of CDK4 and MDM2 in most

There is little information on the cytogenetics and biology of highgrade surface osteosarcoma. Amplification of the sarcoma-amplified

reported in a case of high-grade surface osteosarcoma. However, it was
also present in parosteal osteosarcoma.64

Chondroblastoma
Definition and Synonyms
Chondroblastoma is a benign cartilage-forming neoplasm occurring
in the epiphyses of immature long bones.
Brief Historical Overview
Chondroblastoma was originally called a “calcifying giant cell tumor”
by Ewing1 and “epiphyseal chondromatous giant-cell tumor” by
Codman.2 In 1942, Jaffe and Lichtenstein recognized this entity as
distinct from the so-called giant cell tumor variants under the name
“benign chondroblastoma.”2a
Incidence and Demographics
Chondroblastoma is relatively uncommon; it accounts for less than 1%
of all bone tumors and is approximately one fifth as common as a giant
cell tumors. The peak incidence is between 10 and 25 years of age. The
male-to-female ratio varies depending on the study (1.4 : 1 to 2 : 1).3
Cases involving the skull or temporal bone are more common in
patients in their 40s and 50s.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Typically, chondroblastoma occurs in the epiphyses of the long bones
in skeletally immature patients. Although it typically arises at the end
of the major tubular bones, chondroblastoma can also originate at a
secondary ossification center, such as the greater trochanter. More than
75% of cases develop at the epiphyseal and epimetaphyseal region of
the distal and proximal femur, proximal tibia, and proximal humerus.4,5
Chondroblastoma can also occur at epiphyseal equivalent sites in flat

bone, such as the acetabulum and iliac crest. Rarely, chondroblastoma
may arise in the patella, calcaneus, or other tarsal bones.6,7 Cases
involving the craniofacial bone can be found in the base of the skull
and temporal bone.8–11
Occasional cases of multifocal synchronous chondroblastoma have
been reported.12 Chondroblastoma has almost never been found in the
spine.
The typical clinical presentation is localized pain that may occur as
a single incident or as prolonged episodes lasting several months or
years. In addition, patients may also suffer from swelling, gait disturbances, and joint stiffness. Tumors of the knee joint may present with
effusion. In the temporal bone, tumoral invasion can cause difficulty
hearing, tinnitus, or vertigo.5,8
On physical examination, patients have tenderness in the affected
area along with limitation of motion and muscular atrophy.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Chondroblastoma is usually located in the medullary portion of bone
and arises either at the epiphysis or apophysis of long tubular bones.
Extension to the metaphysis can be seen at times. Size is usually less
than 5 to 6 cm. Conventional radiographs typically demonstrate welldefined, geographic bone destruction with either a spherical or an oval
shape (Fig. 9-1A). There are amorphous calcific foci within the lesion.
In rare cases, extensive calcifications can be seen. The lesion has a
marginal sclerotic rim on plain radiography, and it shows expansion
of the cortex with a periosteal reaction in cases of extension into the
cortex. However, pathologic fracture is uncommon. In a typical case,
the contour of the involved bone is not usually changed.
On magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), chondroblastoma shows
low signal intensity on T1-weighted images and variable signal intensity on T2-weighted images (see Fig. 9-1B and C). Sometimes, the
lesion shows perilesional high signal intensity on T2-weighted images,
indicating inflammatory or edematous changes.
Grossly, chondroblastoma appears as fragments of soft pink to gray
tissue with occasional zones of hemorrhage and calcification. Small
cystic spaces are common; occasionally, the cystic change is so prominent that aneurysmal bone cyst is suspected. On rare occasions, the
boundary of the resected specimen is well delineated and a sclerotic
rim is observed.
127

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C
Histopathology
The predominant cell is the chondroblast, which is a uniform, round
to polygonal cell with a well-defined cytoplasmic border. The cytoplasm ranges from pink to focally clear (Fig. 9-2). The nucleus is
located in the central portion of the cytoplasm and is oval to round
with one to two inconspicuous nucleoli. A longitudinal groove is
typical, accounting for the so-called “coffee bean” appearance.
The chondroblasts are usually packed in pseudolobulated sheets
with a random distribution of multinucleated giant cells with 5 to 40
nuclei (Fig. 9-3). Another diagnostic clue is immature chondroid
matrix that appears as variably sized nodules composed of lightly staining, amorphous, bluish to eosinophilic material surrounded by chondroblasts5,13 (Fig. 9-4). Mature hyaline cartilage is very rarely present.
Ossification can occur; however, if there is abundant ossification, a
128

Figure 9-1. A, Chondroblastoma: radiographic features. An anteroposterior radiograph of distal humerus of 25-year-old man with elbow pain.
An osteolytic bone lesion is seen at the medial epicondyle with some
trabeculation. B, A T1-weighted coronal image of the elbow shows a low
signal lesion at the medial epicondyle of humerus with sharp margin.
C, A T2-weighted sagittal image of the elbow shows a low signal lesion
at the medial epicondyle of humerus with some cystic changes at the
posterior portion of lesion.

diagnosis of osteoblastoma should be considered. Prominent ossification is more commonly observed in talus and calcaneus lesions. Fine
networks of pericellular calcification, often called “chicken wire calcification,” are found around the degenerating tumor cells (Fig. 9-5).
Mitotic activity is usually minimal, and atypical figures are rare. Frequently, individual chondroblasts can show considerable cytologic
atypia such as enlarged, irregular, and hyperchromatic nuclei. This
finding is not indicative of aggressive behavior and has no impact on
patient prognosis.14,15
Focal spindle cell change can be seen in chondroblastoma and
expands the differential diagnosis to include lesions with divergent
pathways of differentiation or, more often, chondromyxoid fibroma.
Brown to yellow granular pigment that is positive on iron stain is
frequently observed in chondroblastoma of the skull and can be very

Lesions Characterized by Cartilage Deposition and Nonaggressive/Intermediate Radiology

9

Figure 9-2. At high magnification, the chondroblasts show typical uniform, grooved
nuclei, with a well-defined cytoplasmic border.

Figure 9-5. Fine networks of pericellular calcification, so-called “chicken wire calcification,” are present.

helpful in arriving at the correct diagnosis. Secondary aneurysmal
bone cystic change is present in about 15% of chondroblastoma cases.
Usually, these cystic changes are appreciated as microscopic cystic
changes; however, occasionally, this secondary cystic change is very
pronounced and the chondroblastoma itself is only present as a mural
nodule.
On electron microscopic examination, chondroblasts show deep
indentation of the nuclear membrane, abundant rough endoplasmic
reticulum and long cytoplasmic processes. These findings are quite
characteristic for fetal chondroblasts.16

Figure 9-3. At low magnification, the admixture of chondroblasts and numerous giant
cells with oval shaped chondroblasts can be appreciated.

Figure 9-4. Chondroblastomas contain zones of eosinophilic immature chondroid
matrix.

Differential Diagnosis
The major differential diagnoses are giant cell tumor of bone, chondromyxoid fibroma, clear cell chondrosarcoma, and osteosarcoma.
Although both chondroblastoma and giant cell tumor of bone are
epiphyseal lesions, most giant cell tumors develop in skeletally mature
patients, in contrast to chondroblastoma. Furthermore, radiographic
analysis of chondroblastoma shows a more distinct delineation than
giant cell tumor of bone, which typically lacks a reactive sclerotic
margin. Histologically, the mononuclear cells in giant cell tumor of
bone lack the characteristic nuclear groove seen in chondroblastoma.
“Chicken wire calcifications” and chondroid matrix are also lacking in
giant cell tumor of bone.
Chondromyxoid fibroma develops in a similar age group as chondroblastoma; however, chondroblastoma occurs mostly at the epiphysis of long bones, whereas chondromyxoid fibroma develops in the
metaphysis of long bones. Histologically, the lesional cells in chondromyxoid fibroma tend to have a more spindled to stellate appearance in
contrast to the round to polygonal cells of chondroblastoma. The characteristic calcification pattern in chondroblastoma is not observed in
chondromyxoid fibroma.
In older patients, clear cell chondrosarcoma enters the radiologic
differential diagnosis because it is typically a well-circumscribed lytic
lesion with sclerotic margins at the end of a long bone. Histologic
evaluation shows obviously malignant chondrocytes and large cells
with clear cytoplasm often in a background of ossified bone.
On very rare occasions, osteosarcoma can mimic chondroblastoma
and such examples have been called “chondroblastoma-like osteosarcoma.” However, in these cases, there is marked diffuse cytologic
atypia, areas of osteoid and, often, focal permeation of the atypical cells
through the surrounding trabecular bone.
129

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Immunohistochemically, the mononuclear cells of chondroblastoma
express S-100 protein and vimentin.17 In addition, there are a few
reports of them expressing cytokeratin.18–20
The chondroblasts generally express SOX-921 and cellular areas of
chondroblastoma express DOG1.22 Osteoprotegerin is highly expressed
in the stromal cells of chondroblastoma.23
Genetics
According to flow cytometry studies, most chondroblastomas are
diploid with low proliferative fractions. However, there are some
lesions reported with near diploid “aneuploid cells.”24–27 Clonal abnormalities have been described in several benign and one aggressive
chondroblastoma.28–31 Chromosomal structural abnormalities involving chromosomes 5 and 8 have also been reported.30
The CORS-26 (collagenous repeat containing sequence of the 26-kDa
protein) gene has been localized to the short arm of chromosome 5.
CORS-26 mRNA is strongly expressed in chondroblastoma, and this
gene may play a significant role in the pathogenesis of chondroblastoma.
Romeo et al. reported higher levels of the cartilage growth plate signaling molecules PTHHR1, BCL2, and FGFR-3 in chondroblastoma.32
These findings suggest that chondroblastoma is a neoplasm that originated from a mesenchymal cell committed toward chondrogenesis via
active growth plate signaling pathways. Chondroblastoma associated
with joint inflammation shows considerably increased cyclooxygenase-2
expression.33 Superoxide dismutase 1, acid phosphatase, and 5-tartrate
resistant cathepsin K, which are expressed in osteoclastic giant cells, are
more highly expressed in chondroblastoma. In addition, versican and
perlecan expression has been shown to be significantly down-regulated
in chondroblastoma. However, the clinical significance of these changes
is subject to further investigation.34 P.Lys36Met alterations predominantly encoded in H3F3B, which is one of the two genes for histone
H3.3, were found in 95% of chondroblastoma. The mutations were
restricted to the stromal cells and not seen in the giant cells.35
Treatment and Prognosis
Most chondroblastomas are appropriately treated by curettage with or
without bone graft. Recurrences, which are typically treated by curettage, occur in 6% to 15% of cases.14,15 Local recurrence occurs more
commonly in flat bones as opposed to long bones; in the temporal
bone, approximately 50% of cases recur.8
On rare occasions, chondroblastoma grows or recurs so aggressively that the bone is destroyed; in such cases, resection may be indicated. The clinical term “aggressive chondroblastoma” may be used;
however, this is not a pathologic term. Cases of histologically benign
chondroblastoma with pulmonary metastasis have been reported.36–38
However, these metastases are clinically nonprogressive. Only simple
surgical excision or simple observation is needed.
There are reports of extremely rare cases of malignant transformation39,40 and metastasis26,41,42 in chondroblastoma.

Chondromyxoid Fibroma
Definition and Synonyms
Chondromyxoid fibroma (CMF) is a benign tumor composed of
spindle or stellate cells forming lobules. There is abundant myxoid and/
or chondroid intercellular material.
Brief Historical Overview
CMF was first described by Jaffe and Lichtenstein in 1948.43 Previously,
it was considered to be a myxoma or a myxomatous variant of giant
cell tumor of bone.
130

Incidence and Demographics
CMF is extremely rare; it accounts for less than 1% of all bone tumors
and represents approximately 2% of benign bone tumors.44 There is a
male predominance.45,46 More than 50% of CMF cases develop in the
second and third decades of life.46,47
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Most cases of CMF occur in the metaphysis of tubular bones of the
lower extremity. Approximately one third of the cases involve the knee
joint; the most common site of involvement is the proximal tibial
metaphysis, followed by the distal femoral metaphysis. Rarely, there are
reports of cases that involve the cortex or juxtacortical region of
bones.48,49 Small bones of the feet are among the frequently involved
sites. Upper extremity involvement is rare. In the pelvis, the ilium is
the most frequent site of involvement. CMF has also been reported in
craniofacial bones, ribs, spine, clavicle, calcaneus, and sternum.50–57
Clinically, patients present with pain that can persist for several
months to several years and/or local swelling. On very rare occasions,
they suffer from rickets, which is reported as an oncogenic
osteomalacia.58
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
CMF appears as a sharply circumscribed, eccentric radiolucent lesion
with an elongated shape (Fig. 9-6A). Cortical expansion, exuberant
endosteal sclerosis, and coarse trabeculation can be seen. Extensive
periostitis and pathologic fracture are rare, and calcification is infrequent. Size ranges from 2 to 10 cm; large lesions can be seen as hemispheric osseous defects with destruction of cortex. CMF arising in a
flat bone may show irregular contours with osteolysis and bone
expansion.
On MRI, CMF shows a multilobulated pattern with low signal
intensity on T1-weighted imaging and high signal intensity on
T2-weighted images. With contrast enhancement, the central portion
of the lesion may show no enhancement due to the myxoid component
(see Fig. 9-6B–D).
Curettage specimens are composed of blue-gray or white tissue that
rarely shows necrosis, cystic change, or liquefaction. Typical hyaline
cartilage is not present. When the surrounding bone is available for
evaluation, the multilobulated appearance and sharp delineation from
bone can be appreciated.
Histopathology
CMF has a distinct microscopic appearance characterized by variably
sized lobules that are sharply demarcated from the surrounding bone.
The lobules tend to be hypocellular centrally with greater cellularity at
the periphery (Fig. 9-7), and they are separated by fibrous bands that
contain blood vessels and giant cells (Fig. 9-8).
The lesional cells are spindled to stellate and are distributed in an
abundant extracellular chondroid matrix (Fig. 9-9). Frequently, there
is abundant pink cytoplasm, producing an epithelioid appearance.
Approximately 50% of CMF cases have scattered benign giant cells.
Large, bizarre hyperchromatic nuclei can be seen in 20% to 30% of
cases, but they are usually focal and associated with large amounts of
cytoplasm, sometimes with smudgy or degenerative features.44,59
Because of these cytologic features, chondrosarcoma may be considered as a differential diagnosis.
Mitoses are extremely rare in CMF, and atypical mitoses have not
been noted.46,47 Microscopic cystic or liquefactive change is uncommon
and is usually focal when present. Well-formed hyaline cartilage is
present in less than 20% of cases.46 Calcification is present in about
one-third of cases, either as fine granules or dense plaques (Fig. 9-10).
Hemosiderin deposition is present at the lobular periphery associated

Lesions Characterized by Cartilage Deposition and Nonaggressive/Intermediate Radiology

Figure 9-7. At low magnification, chondromyxoid fibroma shows a typical lobulated
appearance. The lesion is characterized by hypocellular center and hypercellular

Figure 9-8. At the periphery of the lobule, the cells are condensed. Benign giant cells
are present.

131

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 9-9. Within the lobule, the cells are stellate in shape with abundant extracellular chondroid matrix.

Figure 9-10. Calcification is present as a plaque pattern.

with inflammatory cells and lymphocytes. A secondary aneurysmal
bone cyst is rarely seen.

expression of cyclin D1, p16, and BCL2 has been reported.73 There is
a single report that tumor growth factor β1 is derived from partial
myofibroblastic differentiation in CMF.74 There are higher expression
levels of CD166, cyclin D1, and p16INK4A in CMF, in contradistinction to low levels present in high-grade chondrosarcoma.34

Differential Diagnosis
Chondrosarcoma enters the differential diagnosis, particularly in
tumors involving the pelvic bones. Correlation with the radiologic
features is essential: chondrosarcomas are usually poorly circumscribed and contain calcification. Histologically, high-grade chondrosarcoma can be differentiated from CMF by (1) brisk mitotic activity;
(2) permeation of marrow spaces by tumor cells; and (3) the appearance of the myxoid stroma, which stains more uniformly in CMF.
Another important distinguishing feature is the presence of multinucleated giant cells, which are common in CMF but extremely rare in
chondrosarcomas.
Extragnathic myxomas may be distinguished from CMF by their
lack of a lobular growth pattern and the absence of pleomorphism or
atypia in the stellate or spindle cells. Furthermore, from a practical
point of view, extragnathic myxoma is very rare.

132

Treatment and Prognosis
Primary treatment of CMF should be en bloc resection. Although
some CMFs may be treated by curettage and bone grafting, this modality is associated with an approximately 15% recurrence rate. Recurrences do not correlate with any histologic features of the lesion.
Radiation therapy has been used to treat surgically inaccessible tumors.
An interesting and rare complication is soft tissue growth of nodules
secondary to implantation during surgery.75
Malignant transformation of CMF has rarely been reported.76
However, it remains a pathologic curiosity.

Enchondroma

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Immunohistochemically, S-100 protein has been reported in CMF,
especially within the areas that exhibit more mature chondroblastic
differentiation.60–62 In the loose myxoid areas, S-100 protein may show
only focal positivity. At the periphery of the lobules, smooth muscle
actin and CD34 have been noted.63 Ultrastructurally, the stellate cells
have irregular cell processes, scalloped cell membranes, and cytoplasmic fibrils and glycogen, features of both chondroblastic and fibroblastic differentiation.64,65

Definition and Synonyms
Enchondroma is a common, benign, intramedullary neoplasm composed of mature hyaline cartilage. The term “chondroma” is reserved
for such tumors of periosteal origin.

Genetics
Only limited cytogenetic studies have been done on CMF. Clonal
abnormalities of chromosome 6 appear to be nonrandom.66–71 In particular, rearrangements of the long arm of chromosome 6 at bands q13
and q25 are recurrent. Pronounced expression of hydrated proteoglycans (a major constituent of the myxoid matrix) and focal expression
of collagen type II (a marker of chondrocytic cell differentiation) as
well as collagen types I, III, and VI are characteristic of matrix composition and gene expression patterns in CMF.72 In CMF, N-cadherin,
PTHLH, and PTHR1 are expressed at a significantly lower level
than in articular chondrocytes but, conversely, significantly higher

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
More than 50% of enchondromas arise in the hands and feet,44,77,78
usually in the small tubular bones of the hands, where it is the most
common tumor in this site. Among the long tubular bones, the proximal humerus and proximal and distal femur and tibia are most commonly affected. Enchondroma rarely involves the pelvic bone, ribs,
scapula, or sternum, and enchondroma of the spine is very rare.
Enchondromas almost never occur in the craniofacial bones, which
develop through intramembranous ossification.
In adults, enchondromas are usually found on routine radiologic
examination or as hot spots on bone scan without specific symptoms.

Incidence and Demographics
Enchondromas account for 10% to 25% of all benign bone tumors.44,77
However, this does not likely reflect the true incidence since most
tumors are detected accidentally and biopsy is not performed.

Lesions Characterized by Cartilage Deposition and Nonaggressive/Intermediate Radiology

9

Figure 9-12. At low magnification, enchondromas are composed of mature hyaline
cartilage.

A

B

Figure 9-11. A, The middle phalanx of the index finger shows a osteolytic lesion with
endosteal erosion and contour bulging. B, Sagittal reconstruction of the index finger
shows an osteolytic lesion with tiny calcification of matrix.

In the hands and feet, enchondromas present as palpable swelling with
or without pain. In this location, they are usually associated with
pathologic fracture associated with pain.79
Enchondromas can occur at any age; however, they are most commonly diagnosed in individuals between the ages of 15 and 40 years.
The male-to-female ratio is 1 : 1.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographic findings of enchondroma of the hand and foot are characterized by a sharply defined border, mild endosteal scalloping, and
calcifications resembling a ring or an arc (Fig. 9-11). Calcification may
be dense. Cortical expansion or pathologic fracture is a potential
feature.
In the long tubular bones, enchondroma typically appears as a
central to eccentrically placed, lytic lesion with calcifications arising in
the medullary portion of the involved bone. Endosteal erosion of the
cortex is a typical finding. Enchondromas in flat or irregular bones may
be challenging to recognize radiographically.
On MRI, the margin of the tumor is well defined with a low signal
intensity on T1-weighted images and a high signal intensity on
T2-weighted images. The margins are lobulated, and low signal intensity is seen at the calcified zones. Differentiating between a low-grade
chondrosarcoma and enchondroma can be challenging, and radiology
is a useful adjunct in this process. In the tubular bones, extensive
endosteal scalloping, ambiguous matrix calcification, a periosteal reaction, destruction of cortex, and soft tissue mass are findings that point
toward low-grade chondrosarcoma. An epiphyseal location of the
tumor also strongly suggests chondrosarcoma.
Most enchondromas are curetted, so it is very rare to examine an
intact gross specimen. They consist of confluent masses of bluish, semitranslucent hyaline cartilage with a distinct lobular arrangement. The
size of the lobules varies from 1 mm to 1 cm; only extremely rarely are
they greater than 5 cm in size. The periphery of the lesion is somewhat

Figure 9-13. The lobule is surrounded by normal hematopoietic marrow and trabecular bone.

irregular, because individual lobules bulge into the surrounding
marrow spaces. The punctate calcifications seen radiographically can
also be appreciated grossly. Mucoid or myxoid changes are frequently
observed in chondrosarcoma but only rarely in enchondromas.
Multiple enchondromas show a similar gross appearance to that of
solitary tumors. However, in multiple enchondromas, normal marrow
elements encircle the well-circumscribed nodules, which can be misinterpreted as an aggressive pattern of permeation.
Histopathology
Histologic features are variable according to the location of the lesion.
Accordingly, it is very important to consider the location of the lesion,
radiologic findings, and clinical features together.
Enchondromas are composed of mature hyaline cartilage arranged
in lobules (Fig. 9-12) that are typically surrounded by normal hematopoietic marrow (Fig. 9-13). Occasionally, a solitary enchondroma
can show numerous mature bony trabeculae at the periphery of the
lesion, which is not true permeation. Calcification can be observed as
a fine purple granular precipitate or as large chunks (Fig. 9-14).
133

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 9-14. Calcification can be seen as fine purple granulations within the chondroid matrix.

Enchondral ossification may also be seen. In general, there is low cellularity and abundant extracellular matrix.
Usually, chondrocytes are located in lacunae and the nucleus has a
small, round, regular appearance (Fig. 9-15). On low-power view, the
nucleus is hardly visible. Rarely, chondrocytes can be seen as clusters
or sheets. Occasionally, chondrocytes can be seen as large cells or as
double nucleated cells.
Prominent myxoid change or necrosis is hardly ever observed in
enchondromas. However, these features can be observed occasionally
in the small bones of the hands and feet. When these histologic features
are present, even if the cellularity is low, the possibility of malignancy
should be considered.
Enchondromas arising in the small bones of the hands and feet may
show greater cytologic atypia and architectural disorganization than
that seen in other sites. To make a diagnosis of chondrosarcoma in
these locations, there should be marked myxoid change and prominent
nuclear pleomorphism and hyperchromasia.
Histologic Variants and Grading
Enchondromas arising in the metaphysis of long bones typically consist
of radiologically dense, ringlike or flocculent, radiopacities or as
heavily calcified or ossified material with small, scarce cartilage cells.
Sometimes these were called calcifying or ossifying enchondromas.
Differential Diagnosis
Enchondroma should be differentiated from low-grade chondro­
sarcoma, especially if arising in the metaphysis of long bones in
middle-aged to elderly patients (see Chapter 10). Radiologically, chondrosarcoma of the long bone exhibits expansion of bone and thickening of the cortex. Enchondroma does not show cortical erosion.
Histologic features that support a diagnosis of chondrosarcoma include
increased cellularity, marked myxoid change of the stroma, and malignant cytomorphology.
The most important differential diagnostic feature is intramedullary permeation. In chondrosarcoma, the trabeculae of preexisting
bone are embedded in the tumor tissue. Although radiographically,
enchondroma of the small bones of the hands and feet can show cortical thinning, permeation into the cortical tissue is not observed.
Enchondroma of the pelvis, spine, ribs, and sternum is very rare.
Chondroid lesions occurring in these locations should be considered
134

Figure 9-15. The chondrocyte nuclei are small, round, and regular in appearance.

as aggressive cartilage lesions, if they are not reactive or metaplastic in
nature.
Chondroid differentiation observed in fibrous dysplasia may also
raise the possibility of enchondroma. However, in fibrochondroid dysplasia, fibro-osseous elements are found in addition to cartilaginous
nodules.
Genetics
A diploid pattern with low cell proliferative activity is present in typical
enchondromas using DNA flow cytometric/cytofluorimetric techniques.81 However, near diploid complements with simple structural
abnormalities in chromosomes 6 and 12 using conventional cytogenetic analysis have also been reported.69,82–85
According to selective molecular studies, the HMGA2 gene has
been shown to demonstrate altered transcriptional activity through
various mechanisms in enchondroma, soft tissue chondroma, and
chondrosarcoma.86Indian hedgehog (IHH) signaling is thought to be
critical in normal growth plate cartilage differentiation, whereas in
enchondroma, parathyroid-related protein (PTHrP) signaling is active
but independent of IHH signaling regardless of the presence or absence
of enchondromatosis.87
A mutant parathyroid hormone/PTHrP (PTH/PTHrP) type I
receptor in Ollier disease has also been identified, which abnormally
signals through the IHH pathway in vitro and causes enchondromalike lesions in transgenic mice. It was thus argued that this mutant
receptor constitutively activates IHH signaling, causing formation of
enchondroma-like lesions.88
However, in 2004, Rozeman and colleagues investigated the presence of PTHR1 (PTH receptor type 1) mutations, using allele-specific
oligonucleotide hybridization (and confirmed by sequence analysis) in
tumors from 26 patients with enchondromatosis. They failed to confirm
the aforementioned mutation or find any other mutations in the
PTHR1 gene and concluded that the this gene is not the culprit in
enchondromatosis.89
Cytogenetic analysis of low-grade chondrosarcoma in a patient with
Ollier disease in other work revealed an interstitial deletion del(1p)
(p11p31.2) as the only chromosomal abnormality. Such patients are
thought to be at risk of developing chondrosarcoma because del(1p) is
frequent in chondrosarcoma.90 In an autopsy-based study of Ollier
disease patients, inactivation of putative tumor suppressor genes at 9p21

Lesions Characterized by Cartilage Deposition and Nonaggressive/Intermediate Radiology
and 13q14, as well as overexpression of p53, has also been identified in
chondrosarcomas and their metastases but absent in enchondroma.91
A number of other studies have reported on cytogenetic or molecular genetic findings in enchondroma. Ozaki and coworkers analyzed
molecular-chromosomal aberrations in three benign cartilaginous
tumors (two enchondromas and one chondromyxoid fibroma).92 The
two enchondromas showed two (0 gains and two losses) and six (one
gain and five losses) aberrations, respectively. Loss of chromosomes 19
and 22q were seen in all three benign cartilaginous tumors.92 Using
array comparative genomic hybridization, Morrison and colleagues
identified gains at 8q24.12–q24.13, the region of the C-Myc oncogene,
as a frequent site of change in high-grade chondrosarcoma, but no
amplification of the NYC gene or polysomy 8 in any of the enchondromas.93 A further distinction between enchondromas and chondrosarcoma was identified by Yu and associates, who noted that NOV
(nephroblastoma overexpressed), a member of the CCN family of
genes that are involved in chondrocyte differentiation, is decreased in
enchondromas in comparison to chondrosarcoma.94
Lastly, a correlation between INK4A/p16 protein expression and
tumor grade in malignant cartilaginous tumors along with the retention
of INK4A/p16 expression in enchondromas indicates that the loss of
expression may be important. It may be significant during tumor progression from enchondroma to conventional central chondrosarcoma
and in the progression in grade after recurrence of chondrosarcoma.95
Treatment and Prognosis
Incidental solitary enchondromas of the long or flat bones can be
simply followed radiologically. Enchondromas of the small bones of
the hands and feet typically require treatment because of the risk of
pathologic fracture. In these cases and in instances of symptomatic
enchondroma of the long bones, simple curettage is the appropriate
treatment.
In the context of radiologic suspicion for a low-grade malignancy,
complete removal is warranted. Rarely, enchondroma may recur
several years later, and malignant transformation has been described.96

Ollier Disease
Definition and Synonyms
Ollier disease (chondrodysplasia, skeletal enchondromatosis) is a rare,
nonhereditary developmental abnormality related to heterotopic proliferation of epiphyseal chondroblasts with consecutive failure of
enchondral ossification and shortening of the involved long bones.
This syndrome is characterized by multiple intraosseous and subperiosteal cartilaginous tumors, ranging in size from microscopic foci
to bulky masses. The skeleton is affected bilaterally, but most frequently
an asymmetric distribution is observed with a common pattern of
unilateral involvement of the lower extremities. The ribs and scapula
are only rarely affected.
Brief Historical Overview
This disorder was originally described in 1900 by Ollier as “dyschondroplasia.”97 The association of enchondromatosis with multiple hemangiomas of soft tissue was reported 19 years earlier by Maffucci, and
this clinical presentation is referred to as Maffucci syndrome.98
Incidence and Demographics
Both Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome are extremely rare. The
sexes are affected equally. The risk of sarcomatous transformation is
substantially higher than that seen in sporadic enchondromas; chondrosarcomas develop in about 40% and 53% of patients with Ollier
disease and Maffucci syndrome, respectively.99–102

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Most patients display skeletal deformities such as short stature or localized swelling within several years of birth. Occasionally, pathologic
fracture can be a presenting symptom. The number of involved bones
and the extent of skeletal involvement varies from patient to patient,
although most cases affect a single extremity or one side of the body.
In bilateral involvement, one side of the body is more severely affected
than the other.
Ollier disease can present as multifocal in one or two bones, or in
severe cases, can be present as generalized skeletal involvement causing
a crippling deformity. The hand is the most common site of involvement, although foot, femur, humerus, and forearm can be involved.
Flat bones can be involved in severe cases.

9

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Enchondromatosis can be easily diagnosed by its characteristic radiographic findings. Lesions involve the metaphysis and diaphysis and
appear as linear or pillar-shaped radiolucent lesions that are oriented
obliquely to the long axis of the bone. Affected bones are often
expanded and somewhat shortened. Popcorn-like cartilaginous-type
calcification is typically present. In some cases, bone erosion and
hypertrophy of the cortical surface can be observed. A portion of the
lesion can regress while the bone is growing, and after normal growth
has stopped there is no increase in size. However, if there is a change
in size after normal growth has stopped, chondrosarcomatous transformation should be considered.
Maffucci Syndrome: Radiologic Findings
In Maffucci syndrome, radiographic findings of hemangiomas are
superimposed on the features of enchondromatosis. Central or eccentric osteolytic lesions with a variable degree of calcification and phleboliths of soft tissue are typical findings. The bone is atypical in that there
are defects of normal bone growth with discordant length of the
extremity and deformities often seen on radiographs.
Grossly, the affected bones are expanded due to bluish masses of
cartilage that are arranged as multiple nodules, measuring from 1 to
several centimeters, surrounded by normal bone marrow. The lesion
may extend through the cortical bone to involve the periosteum or
surrounding soft tissue.
Histopathology
Histologically, the microscopic appearance of enchondromatosis in
both Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome is quite similar to that seen
in enchondroma; however, enchondromatoses may show increased
cellularity, enlarged nuclei, and double-nucleated cells (Fig. 9-16), suggesting the possibility of chondrosarcoma.
Differential Diagnosis
Histologically, it can be very difficult to differentiate multiple enchondromas from low-grade chondrosarcomas. Clinically, in patients with
established enchondromatosis, the onset of pain or the development
of a new lesion raises the possibility of chondrosarcomatous transformation. In such cases, histologic examination typically demonstrates
myxoid stromal change, increased cytologic atypia, and cellularity, as
well as a disorganized architecture, including permeation of medullary
bone.
Genetics
Hopyan and coworkers reported the finding of heterozygous mutations
in the PTH receptor gene, PTHR1 from patients with Ollier disease,88
and Couvineau and colleagues reported similar results.103 In the study of
nonrecurrent enchondromatosis, specific copy number alterations were
135

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 9-16. At low magnification, enchondromatoses show a lobulated pattern with
greater cellularity and nuclear enlargement than that seen in solitary enchondromas.

Figure 9-17. Soft tissue chondroma shows lobulated masses of hyaline cartilage.

found at FAM86D, PRKG1, and ANKS1B. Loss of heterozygosity with
copy number loss of chromosome 6 was found in two cases of enchodromatosis.104 In recent studies, 81% of cases of Ollier disease and 77% of
cases of Maffucci syndrome carried isocitrate dehydrogenase (IDH) 1
(98%) or IDH2 (2%) mutations.105 A group of patients with a dominantly inherited disorder with multiple cartilaginous exostoses and with
more irregular radiographic features showed loss-of-function mutations in the PTPN11 gene.106,107 Recessive mutations in acid phosphatase
5 (ACP5) were found in patients with a different phenotype of enchondromatosis characterized by spondylar dysplasia and enchondromatosis.108 A heterozygous missense mutation in COL2A1 was identified in
one patient with generalized spinal involved enchondromatosis.109

single most common site is the region of the fingers (64% to 80%).112
Less frequent sites are the hands, toes, feet, trunk, and head and neck
region.117 Reported unusual sites of involvement are the dura,118
larynx,119 pharynx,120 oral cavity,121 skin,122 parotid gland,123 and
salpinx.124
Most tumors are solitary and present as lobulated, painless, soft
tissue masses arising in the vicinity of tendons and joints. By definition, there is neither bony attachment nor intra-articular synovial
attachment.

Treatment and Prognosis
For both Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome, treatment focuses
primarily on alleviating symptoms due to skeletal deformity. In Ollier
disease, the incidence of malignant conventional chondrosarcomatous
transformation is approximately 40%.99,101,102 In the Mayo clinic series,
among the 54 patients with skeletal chondromatosis, 24 patients had
secondary chondrosarcoma. Among them, 10 had features of Ollier
disease and 5 had features of Maffucci syndrome.3 The majority of these
cases are low grade; however, high-grade and dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma have been described.110

Soft Tissue Chondroma
Definition and Synonyms
Soft tissue chondroma is a benign cartilaginous tumor occurring in
extraosseous and extra-articular locations. It is predominantly composed of benign hyaline cartilage. Synonyms for this condition include
extraskeletal chondroma, chondroma of soft parts, extraskeletal fibrochondroma, and myxochondroma.
Incidence and Demographics
The majority of patients are middle aged. However, there are reports
of the disease occurring from infancy111 to 79 years of age.112–114 There
is a slight male predominance.112,113
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Most soft tissue chondromas occur primarily in the soft tissue of the
hands and feet without connection to the underlying bone.115,116 The
136

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologically, soft tissue chondroma is seen as a well-defined
extraskeletal soft tissue mass. Ringlike, curvilinear or nodular calcifications are seen within the lesion. Ossification or peripheral rim calcification may be present. Adjacent bone may show remodeling, cortical
sclerosis, and/or erosion due to secondary changes by the soft tissue
mass.
On MRI, soft tissue chondroma shows intermediate signal intensity
on T1-weighted images and high signal intensity on T2-weighted
images. Calcifications show decreased signal intensity here and in all
chondromas.
Grossly, soft tissue chondroma is a well-circumscribed, lobulated,
oval to round mass with a light blue, cartilaginous cut surface. Myxoid
and cystic changes can be seen. Nearly all are small, rarely exceeding
3 cm in greatest diameter.
Histopathology
Microscopically, soft tissue chondromas are composed of lobulated
masses of adult hyaline cartilage and may have a flattened layer of cells
at the periphery47 (Fig. 9-17).
The chondrocytes are in lacunae and have a tendency to cluster,
with large amounts of intervening chondroid matrix. The chondrocytes
generally have enlarged hyperchromatic irregular nuclei. Double
nucleated cells are common. These features overlap with chondrosarcoma. However, the typical location and the characteristic clustering
arrangement should lead to the correct diagnosis.
Histologic Variants
Prominent fibrosis warrants a designation of fibrochondroma, whereas
cases with notable ossification or myxoid changes have been classified
as osteochondroma125 or myxochondroma. The term “osteochondroma”

Lesions Characterized by Cartilage Deposition and Nonaggressive/Intermediate Radiology
and local excision is curative.112,113,131 The development of chondrosarcoma is extremely rare.

9

Surface/Periosteal Chondroma
Definition and Synonyms
Periosteal chondroma is a benign hyaline cartilage tumor of the bone
surface that develops from the periosteum (see Chapter 8). Synonyms
for this condition include juxtacortical chondroma, parosteal chondroma, and surface chondroma.
Brief Historical Overview
In 1952, Lichtenstein and Hall reported six cases as a distinct entity132
under the name “periosteal chondroma.” Jaffe reported another nine
cases and proposed the term “juxtacortical chondroma.”133

Figure 9-18. Calcification tends to be more pronounced in the center of the lobule.

should be avoided because it is a totally separate disease entity; to avoid
confusion, a descriptive name such as “soft tissue chondroma with
ossification” is preferred.126 Less frequently, the mononuclear cells have
large oval nuclei with longitudinal grooves, simulating those seen in
chondroblastoma; such cases are referred to as “chondroblastoma-like
variants.”127,128 Calcification tends to be more pronounced in the center
rather than in the periphery of the lobule (Fig. 9-18).
Rarely, the tumor will exhibit extensive myxoid stoma with plump
immature cells resembling a myxoid chondrosarcoma. However,
typical chondroblastic areas are present in the periphery of the soft
tissue chondroma lobules. About 10% to 15% of cases have foci of
epithelioid cells admixed with giant cells, suggesting a diagnosis of
tenosynovial giant cell tumor.129 These histologic findings are most
conspicuous at the tumor margin. Sparse mitoses may be encountered,
but abnormal mitoses are never present.
Differential Diagnosis
Both conventional chondrosarcoma and extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma may be considered in the differential diagnosis. Chondrosarcoma of pure hyaline cartilage rarely occurs in soft tissues. The
characteristic clustering arrangement of the chondrocytes is almost
never seen in chondrosarcoma. Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
can be recognized by cords or sheets of epithelioid cells distributed
in a myxoid stroma. Translocation analysis may assist in excluding
this entity.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
As with normal chondrocytes, the cells of soft tissue chondroma are
positive for S-100 protein and vimentin.129 Ultrastructurally, the cells
show typical features of cartilage cells with large indented nuclei, abundant rough endoplasmic reticulum, free ribosomes, and short irregular
microvillous processes surrounded by aggregates of calcium crystals.112
Genetics
Clonal chromosomal abnormalities have been reported in some soft
tissue chondromas, including monosomy 6, trisomy 5, and rearrangements of chromosome 11.70,85,130
Treatment and Prognosis
Soft tissue chondroma is a benign tumor, and simple surgical excision
is the treatment of choice. Between 15% and 20% of cases recurred,

Incidence and Demographics
Periosteal chondromas are rare lesions and account for fewer than 1%
of chondromas.44,110,134–136 Patients are usually between 10 and 30 years
of age, and males are more commonly affected than females.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The proximal humerus is the most frequently affected site followed by
the distal femur. The small tubular bones are also common sites of
involvement.44,110,134,136,137 Tumors usually arise at the metaphysis.134,136,137
Periosteal chondroma presents as palpable, often painful masses.134,135
Sometimes periosteal chondromas are found near tendon insertion
sites and cause joint disturbances.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
On radiography, periosteal chondromas appear as one or more cortically situated, lobulated soft tissue masses with erosion or cortical
saucerization. There may be associated medullary sclerosis and periostitis. Cortical buttressing and thickening are typical findings at the
distal and proximal margins of the tumor. Chondroid calcifications of
the tumor are observed in about 50% of cases, and some show extensive calcification.
Computed tomography shows detailed calcification and saucer-like
cortical erosion of the tumor. Cortical scalloping with a variable degree
of sclerosis is related to the buttressing of periosteal new bone.
On MRI, the tumor shows low to intermediate signal intensity on
T1-weighted images and high signal intensity on T2-weighted imaging.
Calcification and matrix mineralization of the tumor show decreased
signal intensity on all pulse sequences. Images with contrast enhancement show variable findings.
Differentiating periosteal chondroma from periosteal chondrosarcoma based purely on radiologic findings is often difficult. Most reliable diagnostic criteria involve the size of the lesion; malignancy
should be suspected when the lesion is larger than 3 cm.
Grossly, periosteal chondroma is a well-circumscribed, lobulated
cartilaginous mass. The outer surface is covered by the fibrous periosteum of the involved bone. The cortex beneath is indented and
thickened. The tumors are usually less than 3 cm in greatest
dimension.44,134,135
Histopathology
Histologically, periosteal chondromas show characteristic lobules of
hyaline cartilage (Fig. 9-19). This cartilage tumor does not penetrate
into cancellous bone nor permeate the surrounding soft tissues. Scalloping and erosion of the underlying cortex may be seen.
The cartilage is mildly cellular, and the nuclei are plump and hyperchromatic (Fig. 9-20). Double nucleated cells are common, and
137

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 9-19. At low power, periosteal chondroma shows well-circumscribed, hypocellular, lobulated hyaline cartilage.

Figure 9-20. At higher magnification, the chondrocyte nuclei are plump and
hyperchromatic.

moderate myxoid change may be seen in the matrix. Staining for S-100
protein is positive.

Yang X, Yang ZJ, Liu FX, et al. Inhibition of mTOR and HIF pathways dimishes chondro-osteogenesis
and cell proliferation in chondroblastoma. Tumour Biol. 2013;34:3111-3119.

Differential Diagnosis
Periosteal chondrosarcoma and periosteal osteosarcoma are the main
differential diagnoses. Periosteal chondrosarcomas are typically large
(more than 5 cm) and show no radiologic evidence of solid periosteal
new bone buttressing at the margins. Microscopically, periosteal
chondrosarcomas show variation in the size and shape of nuclei,
and multinucleated chondrocytes are frequently present. Also, periosteal chondrosarcomas permeate into the surrounding soft tissue and
lack the limiting periosteal shell that is present in periosteal
chondromas.
Periosteal osteosarcoma can be recognized by the feathery perpendicular calcific striae seen on radiographs. Periosteal osteosarcomas
lack the characteristic periosteal new bone buttress formation typical
of periosteal chondromas and tend to fade into the soft tissues. Microscopically, periosteal osteosarcomas have spindled primitive mesenchymal cells between and in the periphery of the chondroid lobules.

References

Genetics
Cytogenetic abnormalities have been studied in few cases of periosteal
chondroma.70,85,138 One case reported in the literature showed structural changes on the same band of both chromosome 12 homologs,
leading to speculation that a homozygous gene alteration, possibly an
inactivating mutation of a tumor suppressor gene, was the salient
DNA-level event.138 An abnormality of the long arm of chromosome 4
has also been detected in two cases.85
Treatment and Prognosis
The treatment recommended in most cases of periosteal chondroma is
conservative surgical excision and curettage of the eroded, sclerotic
cortical bone and overlying perichondrium. In selected sites such as
the ribs and fibula, segmental resection is preferred over curettage.
The recurrence rate following any type of surgery is very low.134,135
