Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Ancillary Techniques
The immunophenotype of fibromyxoma has not been formally studied
because of the rarity of this tumor. In theory, there could be some
staining for smooth muscle actin, reflecting a degree of myofibroblastic
differentiation not infrequently present in such fibroblastic tumors.
However, immunohistochemistry would be of little use in distinguishing fibromyxoma from other histologic mimics in the differential
diagnosis.

T1-weighted images and a homogeneous or heterogeneous hyperintense signal on T2-weighted images. Marked enhancement is seen on
T1-weighted images with gadolinium.41 However, these radiologic features are not sufficiently specific to differentiate myofibromatosis from
other benign bone tumors. Grossly, myofibromatosis of bone is similar
to soft tissue lesions and is typically a well-circumscribed, firm, nodular
mass having cut surfaces that are gray to white or light tan to focally
yellow.42

Genetics
Genetic analysis of extragnathic fibromyxoma of bone is lacking due
to the rarity of this tumor.

Histopathology
Microscopically, myofibromatosis is characterized by interdigitating
fascicles, whorls, and nodules of spindle cells with myofibroblastic features, including pale eosinophilic cytoplasm, elongated, tapered nuclei,
and small distinct nucleoli. On low power, pale, nodular areas consisting
of cells with abundant eosinophilic, myoid cytoplasm alternate with
more cellular areas of short and more rounded spindle cells with indistinct cytoplasm. The myofibroblastic proliferation is accompanied by
cleft-like and variably dilated branching (hemangiopericytoma-like)
vessels. The stroma is collagenous and often hyalinized, particularly in
the nodular areas containing myoid-appearing cells. A chondroidappearing stroma may be seen as well. Small perivascular or intravascular tumor nodules are often present at the periphery. Occasional mitotic
figures can be observed as well as necrosis and calcification. Cytologic
atypia is absent.

Treatment and Prognosis
There is little collective experience regarding treatment of this condition. However, fibromyxomas occurring in the appendicular and axial
skeleton tend not to recur locally following conservative surgical intervention. In contrast, gnathic myxomas can be locally aggressive and
infiltrate surrounding soft tissue, resulting in recurrence after local
excision.

Myofibromatosis
Overview
Juvenile/infantile myofibromatosis (fibromatosis) is an uncommon
disease initially described by Stout more than a half century ago.34 It is
characterized by a nodular proliferation of myofibroblasts occurring in
soft tissue, bone, and/or visceral organs. The generalized form of the
disease consists of multifocal involvement of visceral organs, soft
tissue, and less commonly bone in infants and young children. More
common than the generalized form are multifocal lesions affecting soft
tissue and/or bone without visceral organ involvement.35 A solitary
lesion (myofibroma) of soft tissue is the most common manifestation
of the disease and may occur in adults as well as children.
Myofibromatosis of bone was first described as part of the generalized form of the disease. Solitary lesions of bone were subsequently
described.
Incidence and Demographics
Solitary lesions in bone are very rare, with fewer than 50 cases reported
in the literature.36,37 Myofibromatosis is most commonly seen in
infancy. More than half of all patients are younger than 2 years of age.
The majority of the remaining cases occur in the second decade, but
cases do occasionally occur in adults.37 The generalized form with
visceral involvement occurs exclusively in infants and children.
Location and Clinical Manifestations
More than 80% of solitary myofibromas of bone occur in the craniofacial bones, with preferential involvement of the mandible.36 Extracranial sites affected include the femur, tibia, and ulna, as well as the bones
of the axial skeleton.36,38–40 Patients with solitary lesions or with bone
involvement as part of multifocal disease present with pain localized
to the affected bone. Lesions may be found incidentally.38
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographs of myofibromatosis of bone usually show a welldemarcated, purely lytic lesion that most often has a sclerotic rim of
varying thickness.36 Occasional cases lack a sclerotic rim. There may
be expansile remodeling of the cortex, but cortical disruption is not
identified. In long bones the metaphysis or diaphysis are affected. CT
imaging shows features similar to conventional radiographs.36 MRI
examination demonstrates a hypointense to isointense signal on

168

Differential Diagnosis
Given the rarity of myofibromatosis of bone and its myofibroblastic
phenotype, this lesion may be mistaken for other more common bone
tumors, including desmoplastic fibroma (DF), a smooth muscle neoplasm, or possibly a spindle cell sarcoma. DF is composed of longer
fascicles with abundant collagenous matrix that lacks the nodular
architecture as well as the well-developed myoid cells and dilated
branching vessels of myofibromatosis. The more myoid, eosinophilic
cells of myofibromatosis raise the possibility of a smooth muscle neoplasm. Smooth muscle tumors of bone overwhelmingly represent
metastatic disease, particularly from uterine leiomyosarcoma. As such,
cytologically they have a uniform smooth muscle appearance with
fibrillary eosinophilic cytoplasm and perinuclear clearing. In addition,
cytologic atypia in the form of nuclear hyperchromasia and enlargement is usually present in metastatic leiomyosarcoma. Spindle cell
sarcomas of bone, including myofibrosarcoma, have overt cytologic
atypia, marked cellularity, and mitotic activity as well as aggressive
radiologic features not seen in myofibromatosis.
Ancillary Studies
By immunohistochemistry, the tumor cells are variably positive for
smooth muscle actin and less often desmin, typical of a myofibroblastic
phenotype. Therefore, the immunophenotype can help confirm
the myofibroblastic nature of the lesion but does not aid in distinguishing myofibromatosis from other benign and malignant bone tumors
with myofibroblastic or smooth muscle differentiation. Such distinction requires recognition of the characteristic histopathologic features
of myofibromatosis in conjunction with clinical and radiologic
correlation.
Genetics
The genetics of myofibroma/myofibromatosis are not well described.
An autosomal dominant mode of inheritance has been described in a
rare familial pedigrees with myofibromatosis.43 Individual case reports
have described chromosomal abnormalities in solitary myofibroma,
including monosomy 9q and trisomy 16q.44

Lesions Characterized by a Fibrous Spindle Cell Proliferation Replacing Trabecular Bone
Treatment and Prognosis
Solitary lesions of bone are benign lesions that can be managed by
conservative surgical treatment (curettage). Local recurrence and
spontaneous regression have both been well documented in solitary
soft tissue lesions and in the multifocal form of the disease. Solitary
lesions of bone are usually completely removed for diagnosis, making
it difficult to follow the natural course of the disease and document
spontaneous regression. In contrast, the generalized form of the disease
with visceral organ involvement has a guarded prognosis.

Liposclerosing Myxofibrous Tumor
Overview
The term “liposclerosing myxofibrous tumor” (LSMFT) refers to a
benign fibro-osseous lesion of bone with a marked predilection for the
intertrochanteric region of the proximal femur. It is characterized by
heterogeneous histologic components, including myxoid fibrous tissue,
lipomatous areas, and intralesional bone formation.45 Specifically,
“liposclerosing” refers to bone formed within lesional adipose tissue
that is generally a combination of pseudopagetic woven bone, lamellar
bone, and the bone seen in a bone infarct. “Myxofibrous” refers to
fibrous or myxoid tissue in the lesion that may be accompanied by
fibrous dysplasia-like bone trabeculae or dystrophic mineralization of
necrotic adipose tissue. LSMFT is considered by some to represent a
distinct lesion rather than a variant of fibrous dysplasia.46 It has also
been speculated that LSMFT represents end-stage degenerative changes
in other benign bone lesions such as simple bone cyst, intraosseous
lipoma, or bone infarct secondary to trauma from forces exerted on
the intertrochanteric region of the femur. Despite the debate over the
precise etiology of LSMFT, its complex mixture of histopathologic
components is characteristic of this tumor and does arguably allow it
to be defined as a distinct clinicopathologic entity. Importantly, LSMFT
is associated with a risk of malignant transformation.45,47
Incidence and Demographics
LSMFT is uncommon, with fewer than 200 cases reported in the literature.45,48 The exact incidence of LSMFT is difficult to determine because
the tumor may go undetected as an asymptomatic lesion and its
complex histopathology may be interpreted as other benign bone
lesions. In characteristic cases, there may be a slight male predominance, and adults are most often affected, with a peak age in the fourth
to fifth decade.45,47–48 Cases in patients younger than 20 years of age are
rare.

margin and possibly intralesional calcifications, which correlate histologically with lesional bone formation or mineralized fibrous tissue, are
apparent (see Fig. 12-6B). Areas of decreased attenuation on CT scan
correlate with more myxoid regions within the tumor. On MRI, such
myxoid areas show as high signal intensity on T2-weighted images (see
Fig. 12-6C). Although often present histologically, adipose tissue is
usually not identified on MRI.
Grossly, LSMFT has a heterogeneous appearance, depending on the
amount of each component present. Lipomatous areas are pale yellow
and may contain bone trabeculae. Fibromyxoid regions have a consistency ranging from gelatinous, gray, myxoid tissue to more firm, white,
fibrous tissue, possibly with admixed yellow areas representing xanthomatous change. Areas of cystic change can be seen as well.

12

Histopathology
On microscopic examination, LSMFT is composed of a complex
mixture of histologic elements. Lipomatous elements are composed of
small sheets or clusters of mature adipocytes admixed with trabeculae
of woven and/or lamellar bone. Osteoblastic activity may be identified.
The trabeculae of bone are irregular and curvilinear in shape and are
often located at the periphery of capillary-type vessels and are associated with a fibrous stroma (see Fig. 12-6D). Xanthoma cells may be
present admixed in the fibrous tissue as well. lntralesional bone may
also be arranged in thick trabeculae or small sheets and contain numerous cement lines, imparting a Paget-like architecture (see Fig. 12-6E).
In addition, so-called ischemic ossification, consisting of hypocellular
fibrous tissue that mineralizes along an irregular front, may be present45
(see Fig. 12-6F). Lesional adipose tissue may have adipocytes with
small single or multivacuolated cytoplasmic vacuoles similar to fat
atrophy (see Fig. 12-6G). Overt fat necrosis may also be seen. Myxoid
fibrous tissue is generally hypocellular and populated by small, bland,
spindled to stellate fibroblasts (see Fig. 12-6H) and may contain eosinophilic, fibrinous, cementum-like deposits similar to those seen in
simple bone cysts. In addition, dense membranous fibrous tissue as
seen in a simple bone cyst can be identified. Rarely, aneurysmal bone
cyst changes may be present.48

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
LSMFT characteristically occurs in the intertrochanteric region of the
femur in 80% to 90% of cases. Rarely, lesions with similar histopathology occur in other long bones (distal femur, tibia, and humerus), pelvic
bones, and ribs.45,47 The most common presenting clinical symptom is
pain, which may be present in up to 50% of patients.47 The duration of
pain varies from a few weeks to up to 10 years. Most commonly, lesions
are discovered incidentally on imaging performed for other reasons. A
small minority (fewer than 10%) of patients present with pathologic
fracture.

Differential Diagnosis
LSMFT shares similar histopathologic features with other benign bone
lesions. The differential diagnosis most often includes fibrous dysplasia,
intraosseous lipoma, simple bone cyst, and bone infarct. In general,
LSMFT lacks the woven bone pattern, osteoblastic and fibroblastic cellularity, and intimate relationship between the fibrous tissue and woven
bone trabeculae of fibrous dysplasia. The spectrum of changes that can
be seen in intraosseous lipoma—from lesions with viable adipose tissue
to lesions with extensive fat necrosis, cystic change, and ischemic-type
ossification—can be difficult to distinguish from LSMFT. Similarly, the
membranous fibrous tissue and cementum-like material of a simple
bone cyst is not infrequently identified in LSMFT. Overall, when a single
histologic pattern predominates, the case should be classified on the
basis of that pattern. LSMFT should be used for lesions, particularly in
the intertrochanteric region of the femur, that have a complex mixture
of the previously described histopathologic features.

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The radiographic appearance of LSMFT is very characteristic. The
tumor presents as a well-defined lesion with an extensively sclerotic
margin in the intertrochanteric region of the proximal femur (Fig.
12-6A). The contour of the bone is generally unaltered, although some
mild expansile remodeling may occur. Foci of mineralization within
the lesion are frequently observed. On CT scans, a dense sclerotic

Ancillary Studies
The diagnosis of LSMFT rests on recognizing its various histologic
patterns by light microscopy and correlating them with imaging
studies. Therefore, there is no significant role for ancillary studies such
as immunohistochemistry. However, detection of the G-protein mutation characteristic of fibrous dysplasia by molecular analysis would
support a diagnosis of fibrous dysplasia over LSMFT.

Genetics
The molecular genetics and cytogenetics of LSMFT have not been well
studied. Such studies may help better define the relationship between
LSMFT and the other benign bone lesions with overlapping histologic
features.
Treatment and Prognosis
LSMFT is a benign bone tumor that can be managed by conservative
surgery (curettage). However, malignant transformation to a highgrade spindle cell sarcoma has been reported in 10% to 16% of
cases.45,47,49 Therefore, the decision to completely excise a benign
lesion must be weighed against the risks of a major surgical procedure. Certainly, close clinical follow-up is warranted if curettage is
performed.

Fibroxanthoma (Nonossifying Fibroma)
Overview
Fibroxanthomas consist of subperiosteal (fibrous cortical defects) and
intracortical (nonossifying fibromas) proliferations of bland, spindled
fibrous cells and histiocytes. They represent the most common lesion
of bone.50 We prefer the term “fibroxanthoma” as a descriptor of the
composition of the lesions. Fibrous cortical defects are usually found
incidentally in young children or adolescents; are eccentric, juxtacortical or intracortical, metaphyseal to metadiaphyseal in location; have a
lucent center and peripheral border of sclerosis; and usually resolve
spontaneously.51 Fibroxanthomas share many histologic characteristics
with benign fibrous histiocytomas and have been known by various
appellations, including fibrous cortical defect, nonossifying fibroma,
fibrous medullary defect, and nonosteogenic fibroma.52,53 These lesions
are commonly found in areas of bone where there are tendinous or
ligamentous insertions and thus may be related to mechanical/
traumatic forces.
Pathologists rarely encounter these lesions (unless a fracture or
other complication is present) because they are readily identified radiologically and usually do not get larger.
Incidence and Demographics
Although fibroxanthomas affect individuals who range in age from 3
to 45 years, they usually occur in children and early adolescents.

Studies have reported incidences as high as 35% in this population.
The male-to-female ratio is 2 : 1. These lesions are commonly found
incidentally on imaging studies performed for other reasons, so it is
difficult to determine the exact incidence.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
These lesions are usually asymptomatic and are rarely biopsied
unless a fracture is present. Longitudinal radiologic studies have
shown that they typically are present for less than 3 years and
often involute and “sclerose in.” Fibroxanthomas are typically solitary,
but can be multiple. Multiple lesions can be associated with neurofibromatosis type 1, and when seen with café-au-lait spots, they are
part of the Jaffe-Campanacci syndrome. The diagnosis of this
rare disease is usually made in the peripubertal years (10 to 15 years),
although the age of presentation may range from 4 to more than
18 years.54
Fibroxanthomas occur predominantly in the distal femur, distal
tibia, and proximal tibia. These sites account for 80% of all lesions. The
fibula accounts for approximately 10% of all fibroxanthomas.
Intracortical fibroxanthomas are eccentric lesions that can extend
longitudinally or involve the medullary cavity. They reveal a lobulated
contour with a sharply circumscribed sclerotic rim. The cortex may
occasionally appear permeated as a result of expansile remodeling, but
there is no associated soft tissue mass. Most fibroxanthomas show a
varying degree of radiodensity but can appear lytic or in some cases
be denser than the adjacent cortical bone.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
These lesions can be diagnosed with close to 100% accuracy using
conventional radiology alone52,55 (Fig. 12-7A and B). Occasionally,
other studies are necessary, especially when the lesions are symptomatic. Bone scans demonstrate minimal to mild uptake. There can be
low or high signal intensity on T2-weighted MRI sequences, depending on the relative composition of the lesions.
These lesions may persist or grow, but the usual course is for the
lesion to mature to sclerotic bone with eventual remodeling. Pathologic
fracture is more common in lesions larger than 3 cm or involving more
than 50% of the bone width. Fractures are seen in weight-bearing
bones and often come to the attention of patients engaged in strenuous
activity (i.e., sports).
171

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

is thinned, but there is no cortical destruction,
confirming the indolent nature of the lesion.

Lesions Characterized by a Fibrous Spindle Cell Proliferation Replacing Trabecular Bone
Histopathology
Histologically, these lesions share several features. Bland whorls and
bundles of spindle cells are noted (see Fig. 12-7C), often in a storiform
pattern.50,55 There is variable cellularity and density. Xanthoma cells
(foamy macrophages) are apparent (see Fig. 12-7D) and can be single
cells scattered within the fibrous tissue or large aggregates.56 Giant cells
are a component of most of the lesions, thus placing fibroxanthomas
in the differential of giant cell–containing lesions of bone.57 Areas of
hemorrhage and hemosiderin can also be noted, especially in association with a fracture (see Fig. 12-7E). New bone can be seen with
fracture repair; however, some lesions can show new bone formation
without an obvious overlying fracture.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis is relatively broad given the nonspecific
nature of the tissue components,50 thus stressing the importance of
correlating the histologic findings with the radiologic features.53 Fibroxanthomas have overlapping histologic features with benign fibrous
histiocytomas, yet the radiologic images are different (for comparison,
see Benign Fibrous Histiocytoma, Radiologic Features and Gross
Pathology). The new bone formation seen occasionally in fibroxanthomas can mimic that seen in fibrous dysplasia, but again the radiology
is different. Desmoplastic fibroma can be considered; however, the
collagen component of fibroxanthomas is minimal to absent and this
can be helpful in differentiating the two conditions. The presence of
giant cells can suggest a giant cell tumor; however, the storiform
pattern in fibroxanthomas, absence of abundant large giant cells, morphology of the background stroma, and radiologic appearance distinguishes these lesions.

Desmoplastic Fibroma
Overview
Desmoplastic fibroma (DF) is an extraordinarily rare benign but
locally aggressive bone tumor that accounts for 0.2%, at most, of
primary bone tumors. The tumor was first described by Jaffe in 1958
as a tumor composed of fibroblastic cells involving the medullary
region of bone that was histologically similar to fibromatosis of soft
tissue and distinct from other fibrous lesions of bone.58–67
Incidence and Demographics
Although DF can occur in individuals over a wide age range, the vast
majority of patients are in the first three decades of life.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Pain or swelling is the most common symptom of DF. Pathologic
fracture may occasionally be the presenting sign.66 A small minority of
cases present as incidental findings on imaging studies performed for
other reasons. Although numerous bones may be affected, more than
50% of cases reported occur in the long bones of the extremities or in
the ilium. The mandible is another commonly involved site.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The radiologic appearance of DF is variable, but the typical case presents as a lytic lesion in the metaphysis of a long bone that has remodeled and expanded the contour of the bone with cortical thinning but
no periosteal reactive bone formation62,65 (Fig. 12-8A). Characteristically, DF has a lobulated and bubbly appearance frequently accompanied by coarse trabeculations. Tumors usually are well defined, with a
narrow zone of transition.66,68 Cortical breakthrough occurs in a
minority of cases and is best seen on CT imaging (see Fig. 12-8B). MRI
findings are generally nonspecific, but they often reveal the fibrous
nature of the lesion as demonstrated by low signal on T1- and

T2-weighted images.61,69 T2-weighted images may have a more heterogeneous signal depending on the relative amounts of collagen and
myxoid matrix in the tumor. Overall, the radiologic appearance is
usually suggestive of a benign tumor, with the differential diagnosis
including aneurysmal bone cyst, simple bone cyst, giant cell tumor of
bone, and CMF, among others. However, when there is soft tissue
involvement, a malignant neoplasm enters the differential diagnosis.
On gross examination, DF tumors are grayish white with a rubbery to
firm consistency. They may have the trabecular architecture typical of
a fibrous tumor.

12

Histopathology
Microscopically, DF are composed of uniformly sized, fibroblastic
spindle cells with elongated, tapered nuclei often containing a small,
distinct nucleolus and having bipolar eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm (see Fig. 12-8C and D). Pleomorphism and hyperchromasia are
absent. The fibroblastic cells are arranged in long, sweeping fascicles
alternating with shorter fascicles or sheets of more loosely arranged
cells.63,64,67,70,71 The amount of collagenous stroma varies within and
between tumors, ranging from thin, wavy strands between cells in more
cellular areas to thick bands in less cellular areas.70 Some regions may
have more hyalinized collagen or may be reminiscent of a Gardner
fibroma of soft tissue. Zones with a myxoid matrix may alternate with
the collagenous stroma. The vasculature is composed of dilated capillaries. At the periphery, the tumor may infiltrate trabecular bone and
break through the cortex to infiltrate soft tissue. Overall, the cytomorphologic features and growth pattern mimic fibromatosis of soft tissue.66
Differential Diagnosis
The morphologic differential diagnosis of DF includes other benign
and malignant fibrous lesions of bone.65,68–71 Regions of fibrous dysplasia lacking bone formation can simulate DF. Additional sampling may
be required to identify the characteristic curvilinear bone of fibrous
dysplasia. Fibroxanthoma (nonossifying fibroma) can have similar
fibroblastic cytology but has shorter, tightly arranged fascicles with
storiform architecture as well as foam cells, scattered osteoclastic giant
cells, and hemosiderin deposition. Rarely, less cellular tumors with
thicker collagen bands may resemble neurofibroma. An S-100 protein
immunohistochemical stain facilitates distinction of these two entities.
Low-grade fibrosarcoma poses the most difficult differential diagnosis,
and a definitive distinction may be problematic in some cases. The
malignant nature may only become apparent in recurrent lesions with
more frank cytologic atypia. Low-grade fibrosarcoma is generally more
cellular, with crowded and overlapping nuclei that exhibit nuclear
enlargement and hyperchromasia. Although mitotic activity may be
increased in fibrosarcoma, mitotic activity alone is not a distinguishing
feature. Low-grade fibroblastic osteosarcoma can be excluded by the
identification of bone formation.
Genetics
Involvement of the adenomatous polyposis coli (APC)/beta-catenin
pathway in soft tissue fibromatosis is well documented in sporadic
cases and in less common cases of Gardner syndrome,71 a hereditary
syndrome with familial adenomatous polyposis coli, osteomas, fibromatosis, epidermal cysts, and development of malignant neoplasms. In
sporadic fibromatosis, mutations in beta catenin occur more frequently
than somatic mutations in the APC gene and result in stabilization of
beta catenin that accumulates in the nucleus, as can be detected by
immunohistochemistry. Immunohistochemical and molecular analysis of a limited number of cases of DF did not detect any mutations in
the beta-catenin gene and only one case demonstrated nuclear accumulation of beta catenin. In addition, no association between DF and
173

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C

D

Figure 12-8. A, Desmoplastic fibroma seen on conventional imaging is remarkable for expansion of the femoral neck with cortical loss superior and probably a pathologic fracture.
B, Axial CT of the same case. C, Fibroblasts with vesicular chromatin and small distinct nucleoli and eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm are characteristically seen in desmoplastic
fibroma. D, Fibroblasts are arranged in long sweeping fascicles with collagenous stroma, similar to fibromatosis of soft tissue.

familial adenomatous polyposis or with colon cancer has been documented. Therefore, despite morphologic and biological overlap between
soft tissue fibromatosis and DF, the apparent absence of involvement
of the APC/beta-catenin pathway in the pathogenesis of DF and the
absence of concordant occurrence of familial adenomatous polyposis
or colon cancer raises doubt as to the long-established view that DF is
the bony counterpart of soft tissue fibromatosis.
Treatment and Prognosis
The biological behavior of DF is characterized by locally aggressive
growth without the capacity to metastasize. Because of the infiltrative
growth of DF, the local recurrence rate historically had been at least
40% following simple curettage and bone grafting.60 Today, en bloc
resection is associated with a very low recurrence rate, with most
patients being cured.68 Therefore, this has been the preferred treatment
modality.
174

Benign Fibrous Histiocytoma
Overview
Benign fibrous histiocytomas are tumors of fibroblasts and mononucleated or multinucleated cells that morphologically resemble histiocytes, but they can be difficult to define immunophenotypically.72 These
lesions are on a morphologic spectrum depending on the cellular composition and often have a large component of foamy histiocytes and
thus may be called xanthomas or fibroxanthomas.
Benign fibrous histiocytoma has virtually identical morphology to
nonossifying fibroma, and this designation can therefore be controversial. It differs from nonossifying fibroma on clinical and radiographic
grounds. Benign fibrous histiocytoma is painful, even in the absence
of fracture, whereas nonossifying fibroma is painless unless fractured.
Many benign fibrous histiocytomas involve the ribs or ilium, bones
that are never involved by nonossifying fibroma.72 If a long bone is
involved, benign fibrous histiocytoma is frequently centered in the

Lesions Characterized by a Fibrous Spindle Cell Proliferation Replacing Trabecular Bone
diaphysis, rather than cortically based. Lesions centered in the epiphysis are also seen. Benign fibrous histiocytoma has a tendency to recur
and occasionally behave in an aggressive fashion.
Incidence and Demographics
Patients range in age from 5 to 75 years.72 Benign fibrous histiocytoma
of bone is rare, but the exact incidence is unknown given the histopathologic overlap with nonossifying fibroma.

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The wing of the ilium is the most common site of involvement followed
by the femur, but lesions in the head and neck and spine (as well as
other sites) have been reported.

12

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Benign fibrous histiocytomas are lytic and usually have sharply defined
margins73 (Fig. 12-9A). A sclerotic rim may be present, and

A

B

C

D

Figure 12-9. A, Conventional image showing a large circumscribed lesion predominantly involving the ilium and acetabulum with a sclerotic margin and a trabecular pattern.
B, Similar to soft tissue lesions, benign fibrous histiocytoma is characterized by bland fibroblastic spindle cells arranged in short fascicles, often with storiform architecture. Giant
cells (C) and foam cells (D) may be present in variable amounts. The histologic features are similar to fibroxanthoma (nonossifying fibroma).
175

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
trabeculation is sometimes seen. Rarely, there is an ill-defined margin
with destruction of the cortex and invasion into adjacent soft
tissues. In long bones, the metaphysis, epiphysis, or diaphysis may be
involved.
Histopathology
Microscopically, there are variable proportions of fibrous tissue (see
Fig. 12-9B), multinucleated giant cells (see Fig. 12-9C), and foam
cells.74 The fibrous tissue often has a storiform pattern.74 Occasionally,
the foam cells are numerous, and careful attention and correlation
with the radiographs is necessary to ensure that the lesion is not a
xanthoma or nonossifying fibroma (Fig. 12-9D). Cytologic atypia is
usually absent, but when present it is often associated with an overlying
fracture. Cholesterol clefts and hemosiderin deposits may be evident.
Occasional mitotic figures are seen, but there are no atypical forms.
Reactive new bone formation may be present, but it is not an intrinsic
part of the lesion.
Differential Diagnosis
The mixture of histiocytic cells and fibrous tissue is not specific for
benign fibrous histiocytoma. Many other lesions contain an identical
fibrohistiocytic component that is presumably reactive. This component is seen in broad areas of benign lesions such as giant cell tumor,
giant cell reparative granuloma/solid aneurysmal bone cyst, and eosinophilic granuloma, as well as in malignant neoplasms such as osteosarcoma or undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma of bone (UPS/
MFH). However, the latter two conditions are generally easy to differentiate diagnostically.75 In effect, the diagnosis of benign fibrous
histiocytoma is one of exclusion.
Treatment and Prognosis
Most benign fibrous histiocytomas are treated with curettage and may
recur locally.

Fibrosarcoma
Overview
Fibrosarcoma of bone is a malignant spindle cell neoplasm with fibroblastic and variable myofibroblastic differentiation. The morphologic
spectrum ranges from lesions with relatively low cellularity and abundant collagen deposition reminiscent of DF to highly cellular tumors
with numerous intersecting fascicles of poorly differentiated monotonous fibroblastic cells, often arranged in a “herringbone pattern”
similar to classic adult fibrosarcoma of soft tissue. The latter pattern is
not specific to fibrosarcoma and can be seen in other sarcoma subtypes
such as synovial sarcoma, malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor
(MPNST), and even some cases of osteosarcoma; thus, it is a diagnosis
of exclusion. Some high-grade fibrosarcomas have marked pleomorphism and a storiform architecture (pleomorphic fibrosarcoma) that
morphologically overlaps with undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma
(UPS) (previously termed “malignant fibrous histiocytoma [MFH]
of bone”).
Historically, following the introduction of UPS/MFH into the
lexicon of bone pathology, cases of what had been called fibrosarcoma
were often subsequently classified as UPS/MFH of bone. The clinicopathologic distinction between fibrosarcoma of bone with pleomorphism (pleomorphic fibrosarcoma) and UPS/MFH is not well defined.
Conceptually, similar to soft tissue UPS/MFH, the morphology of
UPS/MFH may represent a poorly differentiated form of several types
of sarcoma arising in bone, including fibrosarcoma and osteosarcoma.
The diagnosis of fibrosarcoma of bone is most appropriately used
after the exclusion of other sarcomas that can have similar morphology.
It may be used for low-grade sarcomas with a well-differentiated
176

fibroblastic phenotype, including DF-like histopathologic features, or
for high-grade cellular sarcomas composed of a monotonous population of atypical fibroblastic spindle cells forming intersecting fascicles
with a “herringbone” architecture and without marked pleomorphism
as described by Bertoni and colleagues.76 This morphology is identical
to classic adult fibrosarcoma of soft tissue and is similarly a diagnosis
of exclusion. Cases with marked pleomorphism and a storiform
architecture are usually classified as UPS/MFH. Importantly, fibrosarcoma of bone is a distinct entity that needs to be distinguished
from fibroblastic osteosarcoma by the identification of bone formation
(tumor osteoid) by malignant cells in osteosarcoma. Fibrosarcoma
may arise in association with Paget disease, as the malignant transformation component of giant cell tumor of bone, or secondary to
radiation.77,78
Incidence and Demographics
Fibrosarcoma of bone is uncommon and represents less than 5% of
primary bone sarcomas. According to the historical literature, male
patients have been affected slightly more often than females and
tumors most commonly occur in adults in the third to sixth decade,
but individuals of a wide age range may be affected.78,79 It is important
to note that historical literature on fibrosarcoma of bone likely represented a heterogeneous group of tumors, as mentioned previously.
More work remains to be done to better define true fibrosarcomas of
bone.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The skeletal distribution is similar to UPS/MFH and osteosarcoma,
with the distal femur and proximal tibia being most often involved
followed by the proximal femur, distal tibia, and proximal humerus.79
The mandible may be affected in up to 12% of cases.78 Pelvic bones may
occasionally be affected. As with most primary bone sarcomas, patients
present with pain and/or swelling of the affected site. Pathologic fracture may occur in up to 30% of patients.77
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologically, high-grade fibrosarcoma presents as a purely lytic,
aggressive-appearing lesion with irregular tumor borders (wide zone
of transition), cortical destruction with a “moth-eaten” or geographic
pattern, and often a soft tissue mass. A lamellar periosteal reaction
(or less commonly Codman triangle) is present in most cases.80
Low-grade tumors can have well-defined margins, or even sclerotic
margins, with intralesional trabeculations and lobulations sometimes
producing a “soap bubble” appearance virtually identical to the
radiologic appearance seen with DF. The gross appearance of fibrosarcoma depends on the amount of collagen matrix. Low-grade
tumors with abundant collagen matrix have a firm consistency and
trabeculated white surfaces on sectioning. High-grade tumors with
little or no matrix tend to be soft and variegated whitish tan, often
with yellow areas of necrosis similar to other high-grade sarcomas.
Tumors may be grossly circumscribed or may have infiltrative
margins.
Histopathology
Histologically, fibrosarcomas of bone are characterized by a uniform
population of spindle cells with fibroblastic cytomorphologic features,
including elongated, tapered nuclei often with a distinct nucleolus,
bipolar or stellate amphophilic cytoplasm, and associated variable collagen matrix production growing in an infiltrative pattern. Low-grade
tumors are of low to moderate cellularity, with uniform fibroblastic
cells arranged in fascicles of various lengths, haphazard patterns, or
sheets with associated abundant collagen deposition (Fig. 12-10A).

Lesions Characterized by a Fibrous Spindle Cell Proliferation Replacing Trabecular Bone

12

A

B

C
Figure 12-10. A, Low-grade fibrosarcoma of bone appears similar to desmoplastic fibroma with fibroblasts arranged in long fascicles or sheets with a collagenous stroma. In
this field, the fibroblasts are more haphazardly arranged and there is some variation in the size and shape of the cells. B, Closer inspection of the lesion reveals crowding and
overlapping of fibroblastic cells, some of which have overt mild to moderate cytologic atypia in the form of nuclear enlargement, nuclear contour irregularities, and prominent
nucleoli (center). C, Classic fibrosarcoma of bone is histologically higher grade with marked cellularity and mitotic activity. It is composed of primitive-appearing fibroblasts arranged
in short intersecting fascicles, forming a herringbone pattern.

Cytologic atypia is usually mild to moderate and in the form of nuclear
enlargement, slight nuclear contour irregularities, and hyperchromasia
(see Fig. 12-10B). Mitotic activity is generally low or absent.81 Higher
grade tumors consist of a cellular proliferation of fibroblastic cells.
These cells have a greater degree of nuclear hyperchromasia and
enlargement and less cytoplasm (higher nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios)
and are arranged in tight intersecting fascicles that, when intersecting
at approximately 45-degree angles, produces the classic herringbone
pattern of fibrosarcoma (see Fig. 12-10C). Mitotic activity is obvious,
and there may be tumor necrosis. In simplistic terms, low-grade
fibrosarcoma is more likely to be confused with DF than with a
sarcoma. In contrast, high-grade tumors are usually overtly malignant,
and difficulties arise more with classification than determination of
malignancy.

Fibrosarcomas may show myxoid changes in the matrix similar to
myxofibrosarcoma of soft tissue. More rarely, distinct variants of
fibrosarcoma that usually arise in soft tissue, such as sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma, may present as a primary bone neoplasm.82
More than 80% of fibrosarcomas of bone are high-grade sarcomas.76
Although there is no specific grading scheme for fibrosarcoma of
bone, general guidelines for grading bone sarcomas should be
applied. In general, low-grade tumors have cellularity similar to DF,
a relatively abundant collagen matrix, inconspicuous mitotic activity,
and mild nuclear atypia. Intermediate- or high-grade tumors have
a high degree of cellularity with nuclear overlapping and crowding,
greater nuclear atypia with marked hyperchromasia, overt mitotic
activity (in the range of 20 mitoses/10 high-power fields), and
necrosis.
177

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis of low-grade fibrosarcoma is essentially
limited to DF. Low-grade fibrosarcomas have areas of increased cellularity where there is crowding and overlapping of nuclei, and there should
be at least occasional atypical fibroblasts with nuclear hyperchromasia
and enlargement. Both can be infiltrative, making the growth pattern a
less reliable distinguishing feature. In some cases, the distinction may
not be possible, at least at the time of initial presentation. The possibility
of low-grade fibrosarcoma should be considered in cases of DF with
cytologic atypia or in patients with multiple recurrences. The differential
diagnosis of intermediate- or high-grade fibrosarcoma includes fibroblastic osteosarcoma and other spindle cell sarcomas with a monotonous population of atypical spindle cells, such as malignant peripheral
nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) and monophasic spindle cell synovial
sarcoma. Fibroblastic spindle cell sarcomas with bone formation by the
tumor cells are classified as osteosarcoma. MPNST is exceedingly rare
in bone and generally affects bones with large intraosseous nerves such
as those of the jaw. Pathologically, a diagnosis of MPNST requires evidence of nerve sheath differentiation by morphology and immunohistochemistry (e.g., S-100 protein, neurofilament) or electron microscopy,
an association with a nerve, or an association with neurofibromatosis.
Involvement of bone by monophasic synovial sarcoma is almost always
by extension from an adjacent soft tissue mass. However, a few cases of
monophasic spindle cell synovial sarcoma likely arising in bone have
been reported. It can be distinguished from fibrosarcoma by immunohistochemistry (positive staining for cytokeratins and epithelial membrane antigen [EMA]) and identification of a t(X;18) translocation by
molecular testing. As mentioned previously, tumors with fibroblastic
differentiation and marked pleomorphism are often classified as UPS/
MFH, although pleomorphic fibrosarcoma may be a better designation
at least for a subset of these cases.
Ancillary Studies
As a fibroblastic neoplasm, fibrosarcoma may stain focally for smooth
muscle actin and/or desmin, indicative of a degree of myofibroblastic
differentiation. Focal staining for EMA may also be observed on occasion. Essentially, the diagnosis relies on morphologic recognition of
fibroblastic differentiation and exclusion of other entities in the differential diagnosis.
Genetics
Little data exist on the genetic characteristics of fibrosarcoma of bone.
Most cases have complex genomic alterations, with most abnormalities
identified at the chromosomal level, such as loss of 6q, 8q, 10, and 20p
as well as gains of 1q, 4q, 5p, and 22. For example, comparative genomic
hybridization has demonstrated frequent chromosome 22q gains, suggesting that overrepresentation of genes located at this chromosomal
region may be important for the pathogenesis of fibrosarcoma of bone,
particularly the PDGF-8 gene.83
Treatment and Prognosis
Patients with low-grade fibrosarcoma can be managed by complete
resection of the tumor with clear margins. The 10-year survival rate
for these patients is very good, at more than 80%.76 Patients with
high-grade tumors have a much worse outcome, with fewer than 40%
having a 10-year survival. These patients should be offered multimodality therapy, including chemotherapy.

Undifferentiated Pleomorphic Sarcoma
(Malignant Fibrous Histiocytoma)
Overview
Malignant fibrous histiocytoma (MFH) is a neoplasm that was originally described by Stout and coworkers more than 40 years ago as a
178

high-grade mesenchymal tumor occurring in soft tissue that is composed of malignant fibroblastic and histiocytic-appearing cells.84,85
Early cell culture studies documented phagocytic properties and fibroblastic features of the tumor cells. The morphologic features of some
of the neoplastic cells suggested a monocyte lineage. However, subsequent studies, including ultrastructural analysis, confirmed that the
cells in MFH are primitive mesenchymal cells, often with features of
fibroblastic or myofibroblastic differentiation. After the development
of diagnostic immunohistochemistry, many tumors diagnosed as MFH
of soft tissue were reclassified as more specific tumor types. Cases in
which no definite line of differentiation is identified are now classified
as UPS, with the terminology “MFH” being phased out. The histopathologic features of UPS/MFH are now generally thought to represent a common morphologic pattern in poorly differentiated spindle
cell sarcomas.
Following the original descriptions of UPS/MFH of soft tissue,
cases with similar histopathology were described arising in bone
throughout the 1970s. Previously, most of these cases had been diagnosed as osteosarcoma or fibrosarcoma.86 These tumors often had evidence of fibroblastic differentiation and were not of histiocytic origin
as once believed. Currently, UPS/MFH of bone is defined as a sarcoma
composed of nondescript or fibroblastic-appearing spindle cells and
poorly differentiated pleomorphic cells arising in bone. It often has a
storiform architectural arrangement of spindle cells that lacks evidence
of neoplastic bone formation and cannot be more specifically classified
by morphology, immunohistochemistry, or ultrastructural examination. Most cases arise as de novo lesions, but up to 33% occur secondary to a preexisting condition, most commonly radiation, Paget disease,
fibrous dysplasia, or a bone infarct.87 Secondary cases also include
UPS/MFH as a component of dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma or as
malignancy in multiple hereditary exostoses. Finally, UPS/MFH can
arise in association with bone dysplasia, specifically diaphyseal medullary stenosis. Similar to its soft tissue counterpart, UPS/MFH of bone
is a diagnosis of exclusion when no clear line of differentiation can be
identified by morphology and immunohistochemistry. At least a subset
of UPS/MFH may be better classified as pleomorphic fibrosarcoma of
bone.
Incidence and Demographics
MFH of bone is uncommon and accounts for less than 3% of primary
malignant bone tumors.87 The age of affected individuals ranges from
the first to the eighth decade, with the majority of cases occurring in
patients older than 40 years of age and a significant minority occurring
in patients around 20 years of age.88 Males are more often affected than
females.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
UPS/MFH, as reported in the literature, has a predilection for long
bones, with the majority of cases occurring in the proximal and distal
femur, proximal tibia, and proximal humerus.88,89 The pelvis is the most
frequently affected region of the axial skeleton, with involvement of the
vertebral column and skull being less common. The most common
clinical presentation is pain and swelling of the affected site followed
by pain alone.87,88 Pathologic fracture may be seen in up to 25% of
cases.89
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologically, UPS/MFH presents as a purely osteolytic lesion with an
ill-defined border and evidence of an aggressive growth pattern, most
commonly in the form of geographic destruction of bone followed by
a permeative growth pattern or by a “moth-eaten” appearance (Fig.
12-11A). Matrix mineralization should be absent. A periosteal reaction
is infrequent, and rarely there may be a sclerotic margin.87 UPS/MFH

Lesions Characterized by a Fibrous Spindle Cell Proliferation Replacing Trabecular Bone

12

A

C

arises in the intramedullary region of the metaphysis with occasional
extension into the epiphysis. Tumors arising in the diaphysis represent
only 10% of cases.87 Overall, the imaging features are nonspecific and
can overlap with other high-grade sarcomas of bone having no radiologically detectable mineralized matrix. Given the appearance of a
destructive lesion in bone in an older patient, metastatic disease is
commonly in the radiologic differential diagnosis. Radiologic features
of an underlying bone disorder are identified in cases of secondary
UPS/MFH. Grossly, UPS/MFH of bone consists of a fleshy tan-gray
tumor with ill-defined borders and frequent hemorrhage and necrosis.
Extension into soft tissue is frequent. Bone and cartilage formation are
not identified.
Histopathology
Microscopically, the fibroblastic cells have elongated, hyperchromatic,
tapered nuclei with bipolar amphophilic cytoplasm. These cells are
usually arranged in short, intersecting fascicles radiating from a central
point forming a “storiform” architecture, which has become the most
characteristic feature of this neoplasm (see Fig. 12-11B). A second cell
population has more abundant polygonal, amphophilic cytoplasm with
oval and often grooved nuclei reminiscent of histiocytes. Numerous
pleomorphic malignant giant cells, which can have very bizarre cytologic features including multilobulated, irregular nuclei, are a constant
cell population among the fibroblastic cells (see Fig. 12-11C). There is
variable collagen deposition by the cells. Again, these cytomorphologic

B

Figure 12-11. A, Destructive lesion in the proximal femur with poorly defined
margins and no evidence of matrix mineralization is characteristic of UPS/MFH of bone.
In older adults, the differential diagnosis for such a destructive lesion usually includes
metastatic disease and myeloma. B, Malignant fibroblastic cells arranged in short
intersecting fascicles define the storiform architecture of UPS/MFH. A benign foamy
histiocyte (center) and an atypical mitotic figure (right center) are present as well.
C, Numerous large bizarre pleomorphic cells are present in UPS/MFH in contrast to
the uniform population of malignant fibroblasts in classic fibrosarcoma of bone.

features overlap with other sarcoma subtypes. Mitotic figures, including atypical forms, are readily identified. Necrosis may be present as
well. A population of benign-appearing, foamy histiocytes as well as
scattered mononuclear inflammatory cells may be admixed among the
tumor cells.
Histologic Variants and Grading
Subtypes of UPS/MFH of bone have been classified as they have been
for soft tissue UPS/MFH. The majority of cases (more than 80%) have
the spindled and pleomorphic cells with storiform architecture (pleomorphic and storiform types). The remaining minority of cases may
contain abundant osteoclastic giant cells (giant cell–rich UPS/MFH),
areas with myxoid matrix (myxoid UPS/MFH), or abundant inflammatory cells (inflammatory UPS/MFH). In more than 95% of cases,
UPS/MFH of bone is a high-grade sarcoma as described earlier.87 Lowgrade UPS/MFH is less cellular and usually has abundant collagenous
matrix. Mitotic activity is low, and necrosis is generally not seen.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes fibroblastic osteosarcoma, fibrosarcoma, primary pleomorphic spindle cell sarcomas with myoid dif­
ferentiation (leiomyosarcoma and myofibrosarcoma), metastatic
sarcomatoid carcinoma, metastatic melanoma, and metastatic sarcoma
derived from soft tissue. Extensive sampling and a careful search for
malignant bone formation on microscopic examination should exclude
179

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
osteosarcoma. Fibrosarcomas, as defined here, are composed of a
monotonous fibroblastic proliferation with a herringbone arrangement
of cells and little or no pleomorphism. In contrast, UPS/MFH has
marked pleomorphism and a storiform architecture. Although immunohistochemical stains for smooth muscle actin and desmin may be
focally positive in UPS/MFH, leiomyosarcoma of bone generally stains
more diffusely for desmin and/or actin and has morphologic features
indicative of smooth muscle differentiation such as blunt-ended nuclei,
eosinophilic cytoplasm, and perinuclear clearing.
Distinguishing UPS/MFH from a high-grade myofibroblastic
sarcoma is somewhat arbitrary because many cases of UPS/MFH can
have evidence of fibroblastic/myofibroblastic differentiation, particularly at the ultrastructural level. Staining for cytokeratins can be focally
seen in UPS/MFH but should always raise the consideration of sarcomatoid carcinoma (often from a renal primary). However, sarcomatoid
carcinomas generally do not have a well-developed storiform architecture. Correlation with clinical findings may be necessary if metastatic
disease is a consideration. Melanoma can be excluded by negative
staining for S-100 protein and melanocytic markers. Patients with
metastatic sarcoma to bone generally have a documented soft tissue
primary. Some cases of UPS/MFH may have low-grade cytomorphologic features and may be confused with fibroxanthoma (nonossifying
fibroma). Low-grade UPS/MFH is associated with cytologic atypia in
the form of nuclear enlargement and hyperchromasia that should generally not be seen in fibroxanthoma.
Ancillary Studies
Ancillary diagnostic studies such as immunohistochemistry and transmission electron microscopy should be used to exclude a specific line
of differentiation of other tumors in the differential diagnosis and possibly to confirm the fibroblastic nature of the cells in at least a subset
of UPS/MFH. Cases with fibroblastic differentiation may be better
classified as pleomorphic fibrosarcoma of bone.
Genetics
Characterizing the genetic abnormalities of UPS/MFH of bone is a topic
of ongoing debate. Cases of UPS/MFH of bone can have complex cytogenetic aberrations similar to UPS/MFH of soft tissue. However, loss of
heterozygosity at chromosome 9p21-22 seems to characterize at least a
subset of sporadic cases of UPS/MFH of bone as well as cases occurring
in diaphyseal medullary stenosis.90 The latter is characterized by endosteal cortical thickening with narrowing of the medullary canal of long
bones (particularly of the lower extremity), abnormal metaphyseal trabecular bone, bone infarcts, and the development of UPS/MFH of bone
in up to 33% of affected patients.91 Controversy persists as to the relationship between UPS/MFH of bone and osteosarcoma because fibroblastic osteosarcomas can have UPS/MFH-like morphology and there is
overlap with respect to clinical features and response to therapy. Continued molecular studies should ultimately discern this relationship.
Treatment and Prognosis
Patients with high-grade UPS/MFH of bone are generally treated with
preoperative chemotherapy and surgery following a model similar to
that for osteosarcoma.92 Patients with localized disease (no metastases)
have a 5-year survival rate of 50% to 60%87,88 following adequate
therapy. Distant metastatic disease occurs in approximately 45% of
cases, and the prognosis for these patients is poor. UPS/MFH arising
secondary to an underlying condition runs a very aggressive course,
with most patients succumbing to disease in 2 years regardless of treatment. Low-grade UPS/MFH is rare, and experience managing such
patients is limited; however, these patients would be expected to have
a better prognosis than their high-grade counterparts.
180

Infantile Fibrosarcoma
Overview
Infantile fibrosarcoma is a neoplasm of infancy and early childhood
that arises in soft tissue of the extremities. Bone involvement when
present is usually by local extension.93 On rare occasions, infantile
fibrosarcoma presents as a primary bone tumor.94,95 Stout reported the
first clinicopathologic study of this entity over 50 years ago and suggested that it was more indolent than its adult counterpart.96 This
neoplasm was confirmed to be a distinct entity in subsequent larger
series of cases.97,98 Histologically, infantile fibrosarcoma resembles
adult fibrosarcoma, but the infantile form is considered a separate
entity because of different clinical behavior and characteristic molecular abnormalities.
Incidence and Demographics
The data available apply overwhelmingly to soft tissue cases. Infantile
fibrosarcoma is relatively rare. The majority of tumors are discovered
in the first year of life, with more than 33% being present at birth.97,98
Males are affected slightly more often than females. Primary infantile
fibrosarcoma of bone is exceedingly rare, with only a few case reports
in the literature.94,95 Therefore, no conclusions can be drawn regarding
sex predilection. Like soft tissue lesions, primary bone cases are clinically discovered at birth or very shortly thereafter.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Soft tissue and bone lesions clinically manifest as painless, nontender
masses or swellings that can be rapidly growing. The most common
site of soft tissue involvement is the lower extremity followed by the
upper extremity. The next most common sites include the trunk and
the head and neck region. Rarely, the retroperitoneum or visceral
organs are affected.99–101 Primary tumors of bone have been reported
to arise in the metaphysis of the femur and humerus.93,95,102
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The most common radiographic feature of infantile fibrosarcoma is
the presence of a large soft tissue mass.93 There may be deformity
of the adjacent bone characterized by cortical thickening and failure
of normal tabulation. However, frank destruction of the adjacent bone
is rare.97 Tumors arising in bone are centered in the metaphyseal or
metadiaphyseal region and present as a lytic, aggressive lesion as
evidenced by cortical breakthrough and soft tissue extension. A
periosteal reaction may develop over time.94 In addition, a striking
trabeculated appearance may be seen.95 The gross appearance of soft
tissue and bone cases is similar. Tumors vary considerably in size,
ranging from 1 to more than 10 cm in soft tissue. However, most cases
are relatively small, with a median size of less than 4 cm.97 Most cases
are fusiform, round, or poorly circumscribed. The cut surfaces are
remarkable for being grayish white in color. Areas of necrosis, hemorrhage, and myxoid cystic changes are particularly evident in large
tumors.
Histopathology
On microscopic examination, the tumor bears a similar resemblance
to adult fibrosarcoma (Fig. 12-12), including intersecting fascicles
forming a herringbone pattern. The cells are monotonous, showing
little pleomorphism. Mitotic activity is readily identified but can vary
from region to region within a tumor. The amount of collagen in the
stroma can vary and may be abundant enough to mimic fibromatosis.
Focal areas of myxoid stroma may be encountered as well. In cellular
areas with little or no stroma, the cells often have a primitive appearance, with smaller, more rounded nuclei and little or no evidence of

Lesions Characterized by a Fibrous Spindle Cell Proliferation Replacing Trabecular Bone
Infantile fibrosarcoma should be distinguished from fibroblastic
osteosarcoma by the identification of bone formation by tumor cells.
However, osteosarcoma is uncommon in this age group.

12

Ancillary Studies
As discussed, the diagnosis of infantile fibrosarcoma rests predominantly on recognition of the uniform population of fibroblastic spindle
cells, solid growth pattern, and fascicular architecture characteristic of
this neoplasm. Ancillary studies such as immunohistochemistry and
electron microscopy are used to exclude other forms of cellular differentiation (e.g., rhabdomyoblastic differentiation) when there is diagnostic
doubt. Of note, as a fibroblastic neoplasm, infantile fibrosarcoma may
show focal expression of smooth muscle actin but usually does not
express other markers of myoid or rhabdomyoblastic differentiation.
Molecular analysis of the t(12;15)(p13;q25) translocation seen in infantile fibrosarcoma (see later discussion) is a helpful adjunct to diagnosis.
Figure 12-12. Infantile fibrosarcoma is histologically virtually indistinguishable from
its adult counterpart, being composed of uniform, primitive-appearing, atypical fibroblasts arranged in fascicles that often intersect to form a herringbone pattern.

fibroblastic differentiation. In contrast to adult fibrosarcoma, infantile
fibrosarcoma contains scattered chronic inflammatory cells, particularly lymphocytes, which can be rather abundant in some cases. The
vasculature frequently is composed of dilated or cleftlike capillaries. A
hemangiopericytoma-like vascular pattern can be quite prominent.
Primary tumors in bone have similar histologic features as described
for soft tissue cases.
There are no specific variants of infantile fibrosarcoma as can be
seen in adult fibrosarcoma. Infantile fibrosarcoma is not formally
assigned a grade because it has no reliable prognostic value (see later
discussion).
Differential Diagnosis
The histopathologic features may be confused with other monotonous,
primitive-appearing mesenchymal sarcomas of childhood. Most
importantly, infantile fibrosarcoma should be distinguished from
childhood rhabdomyosarcoma, given the significant differences in biological behavior and treatment. Spindle cell rhabdomyosarcoma has a
monotonous population of spindle cells and a collagenous stroma. It
most commonly arises in the paratesticular and head and neck regions,
but it may also occur in the extremities. In contrast to infantile fibrosarcoma, the cytoplasm of the tumor cells is more abundant, eosinophilic, and fibrillary, indicative of rhabdomyoblastic differentiation.
Embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma also has cells with rhabdomyoblastic
cytologic features and characteristically has areas of high cellularity
alternating with areas of low cellularity with an edematous-appearing
stroma lacking a well-developed collagenous stroma. Immunohistochemically, both spindle cell and embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma
express markers of rhabdomyoblastic differentiation, such as desmin,
myogenin, and MyoD1, that are usually absent in infantile fibrosarcoma. Rhabdomyosarcoma arising as a primary bone tumor is exceedingly rare.
Some cases of infantile fibromatosis can be very cellular, resulting
in the most challenging differential diagnosis with infantile fibrosarcoma. In fact, the histopathologic overlap can be extensive, with both
tumors potentially having microscopic fields that are indistinguishable.
In such cases, detection of the characteristic chromosomal translocation of infantile fibrosarcoma t(12;15) allows for distinction between
the two neoplasms (see later discussion).

Genetics
Most cases of infantile fibrosarcoma have a characteristic chromosomal
translocation t(12;15)(p13;q25) that results in fusion of the ETV6 gene
on chromosome 12 with the neurotrophin-3 receptor NTRK3 gene on
chromosome 15.103,104 Interestingly, congenital mesoblastic nephroma
shares the same translocation and thus may be the renal counterpart
of infantile fibrosarcoma. This translocation can be difficult to detect
by conventional cytogenetics but can be more reliably detected by
reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) or fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) techniques, even in paraffinembedded tissue.105–107 Other chromosomal abnormalities have been
detected in cases of infantile fibrosarcoma including nonrandom gains
of chromosomes 8, 11, 17, and 20108; however, cytogenetic and molecular genetic studies have only rarely been performed on such cases.
Treatment and Prognosis
Most data related to treatment and prognosis refer to the experience
in the soft tissue literature. In contrast to adult fibrosarcoma, the prognosis of infantile fibrosarcoma is very favorable. Local recurrence is
possible up to 10 years following initial excision, and the 5-year survival rate has been reported to be 84%, with less than 10% of patients
succumbing to metastatic disease.97 Histologic features typically used
in grading sarcomas such as cellularity, mitotic activity, and extent of
necrosis do not necessarily correlate with clinical behavior. Patients
who did not develop recurrent or metastatic disease had more radical
initial surgical procedures. Therefore, it is believed that most patients
are cured with wide local excision. Chemotherapy can be effective, but
its value remains to be fully elucidated.109

Surface Lesions
Juxtacortical Chondromyxoid Fibroma
Overview
Chondromyxoid fibroma (CMF) is a rare benign fibroblastic and cartilaginous bone neoplasm. The details of this tumor are discussed in
Chapter 9. Like other bone tumors, CMF can occur in a juxtacortical
location (on the surface of bone) arising from the cortex or inner or
outer layer of periosteum.110–114 This is a rare occurrence in an already
rare neoplasm. However, it is important to be aware of juxtacortical
CMF because it can be mistaken for more aggressive malignant surface
lesions of bone.
Incidence and Demographics
Juxtacortical CMF is exceedingly rare, constituting a fraction of 1% of
all benign and malignant bone tumors and at most 6% of all cases of
181

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
CMF.115 The age range of patients with juxtacortical CMF is 12 to 81
years, with a median age of approximately 40 years. This is a slightly
older demographic than patients with conventional intramedullary
CMF.115 There may be a slight male predilection.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
At least 50% of cases of juxtacortical CMF occur in the proximal tibia,
and approximately 33% occur in the distal femur. Other sites that can
be affected include the proximal humerus, proximal femur, rib, and
metacarpal bones.110–115 The most common clinical presentation is a
slowly growing mass with or without dull pain of long duration.115 Pain
alone is the second most common presentation.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Conventional radiographs and CT imaging typically demonstrate a
fairly well-circumscribed soft tissue mass immediately adjacent to the
cortical bone that may show saucerization changes, expansile remodeling, and sclerotic changes.112,113,115 Although often present microscopically, foci of calcification are usually not identified on imaging studies.
On MRI, the chondroid and myxoid matrix of the tumor appear as
areas of intermediate to high signal intensity on T2-weighted images
and low signal intensity on T1-weighted images.113 The gross pathology
is remarkable for a circumscribed, lobulated, variably myxoid mass
similar to intramedullary CMF that is located on the surface of
bone.
Histopathology
The microscopic features of juxtacortical CMF are essentially identical
to conventional intramedullary CMF. One seemingly unique feature is
“chunky’’ calcification within peripheral zones of tumor lobules containing myxoid and chondroid areas.115
Differential Diagnosis
On occasion, CMF can display areas with cytologic atypia in the form
of nuclear hyperchromasia, slight nuclear enlargement, and contour
irregularities. As such, cases of CMF have been confused with chondrosarcoma. In general, juxtacortical chondrosarcoma is larger than
juxtacortical CMF, has hyaline cartilage matrix not seen in CMF, and
has atypical chondrocytes in lacunae rather than the fibroblastic cells
of CMF. Juxtacortical CMF can occur in older adults; thus, awareness
of the potential for cytologic atypia in CMF in conjunction with recognition of the characteristic features and correlation with radiologic
findings should help avoid confusion with chondrosarcoma or other
malignant surface tumors of bone.
Ancillary Studies
The results of immunohistochemical analysis of CMF with antibodies
to S-100 protein have been variable. In general, focal and often weak
staining for S-100 protein may be seen in conventional CMF, particularly in areas with chondroid differentiation; however, it is not entirely
clear what percentage of cases show positive staining.116–118 Cases of
juxtacortical CMF stained for S-100 protein using different antibodies
and detection protocols show no evidence of immunoreactivity.115
Therefore, S-100 protein staining is of no use in establishing a diagnosis
of juxtacortical CMF.
Genetics
Cytogenetic and molecular genetic characterization of conventional
intramedullary CMF has begun to be elucidated, for example, with the
discovery of a pericentromeric inversion inv(6)(p25q13).119 However,
such studies have not been performed specifically on cases of juxtacortical CMF.
182

Treatment and Prognosis
Although there is limited clinical experience with juxtacortical CMF,
in the limited number of patients studied the recurrence rate is reportedly less than 10% following conservative surgery.118 Juxtacortical CMF
is a benign neoplasm that has not been reported to metastasize or
undergo malignant transformation.114,119

Cortical Irregularity Syndrome (Periosteal Desmoid)
Overview
Cortical irregularity syndrome is a site-specific reactive disorder that
likely results from an avulsion injury. It characteristically occurs at the
posteromedial aspect of the distal femur at the insertion of the adductor magnus muscle.120 This disorder was originally termed “periosteal
desmoid” because fibroblastic tissue seen on microscopic examination
was interpreted as appearing similar to fibromatosis (desmoid tumor).
However, this is a misnomer because cortical irregularity syndrome is
not related to fibromatosis. Rather, it is a reparative fibroblastic and
woven bone proliferation similar to avulsion injuries seen at other
tendon insertion sites, such as the trochanteric region of the proximal
femur and the ischial apophysis.
Incidence and Demographics
Cortical irregularity syndrome has only infrequently been reported in
the peer-reviewed literature and thus is thought to be rare. However,
its classic locations, age at recognition, and occasional characteristic
radiologic features (see later discussion) probably engender watchful
waiting without biopsy in a large percentage of patients, thus masking
its actual incidence.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
As mentioned, cortical irregularity syndrome specifically occurs along
the posteromedial aspect of the distal femur at the insertion of the
adductor magnus muscle. Athletic individuals, usually younger than
15 years of age, are most often affected.120 Benign bone irregularities
of the distal femur have been reported to occur in up to 11.5% of males
and 3.6% of females.121 Lesions can present as incidental findings.
Symptomatic patients present with knee pain and localized tenderness
over the medial femoral condyle. Rarely, a palpable swelling can be
appreciated on physical examination.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographs generally show cortical outline irregularities and excavations of the distal femoral metaphysis.121–123 A periosteal reaction
can be seen on occasion and may include bone spicules extending
into adjacent soft tissue.120 The constellation of radiographic findings
may raise the possibility of a malignant neoplasm.120,124,125 Bone scans
are usually normal in cortical irregularity syndrome and may be
helpful in guiding management and determining the need for
biopsy.120,125 MRI may also be helpful when other imaging studies are
inconclusive.123 The gross pathology usually consists of fragments of
nonspecific fibrous tissue and possibly bone taken at the time of
biopsy.
Histopathology
Lesions of cortical irregularity syndrome consist of bland reactive
fibroblastic tissue of low cellularity without cytologic atypia. Trabeculae of reactive woven bone variably lined by osteoblasts may be
identified in the fibrous tissue. If tendon tissue is present, it shows
reparative changes including fibrosis and neovascularization. Osteoclastic resorption of native cortical and cancellous bone may also
be seen.

Lesions Characterized by a Fibrous Spindle Cell Proliferation Replacing Trabecular Bone
Differential Diagnosis
The fibrous tissue of cortical irregularity syndrome may be confused
with fibromatosis. Fibromatosis (desmoid tumor) of soft tissue is a
separate distinct clinicopathologic entity composed of a more cellular
proliferation of fibroblasts arranged in long, well-formed fascicles that
infiltrate soft tissues. The fibrous tissue in cortical irregularity syndrome is less cellular and lacks a well-developed fascicular architecture.
Identification of other reparative changes, including reactive woven
bone formation, neovascularization, and fibrosis of tendon tissue, also
allows distinction from fibromatosis. In addition, fibromatosis would
not be centered on the cortical surface of bone. The characteristic location and radiologic changes of cortical irregularity syndrome should
provide a resolution to any possible histologic confusion. High-grade
surface sarcomas (fibrosarcoma or fibroblastic osteosarcoma) of bone
demonstrate overt cytologic atypia and pleomorphism, increased cellularity, and high mitotic activity.
Ancillary Studies
The diagnosis of cortical irregularity syndrome rests on the identification of the reactive nature of the fibrous tissue and associated reparative changes in conjunction with clinical and radiologic correlation. As
such, ancillary studies should not be required.
Genetics
Given the reactive etiology of cortical irregularity syndrome and its
recognition on imaging studies, studies investigating the cytogenetics
and molecular genetics of this lesion have not been published.
Treatment and Prognosis
When cortical irregularity syndrome is recognized based on clinical
and radiologic findings, which is the usual clinical scenario, the
lesion can be followed with conservative nonsurgical management
because it will heal with bone remodeling over time. If there is doubt
as to the diagnosis based on the radiologic findings, then a biopsy is
needed.

13
Vascular Tumors of Bone
Wendong Yu, MD, PhD, Sathiyamoorthy Selvarajan, MBBS, PGDCP,
and Andrew E. Rosenberg, MD

Benign Vascular Tumors 187
Arteriovenous Malformation 187
Conventional Hemangioma 188
Lymphangioma 191
Skeletal Angiomatosis 192
Epithelioid Hemangioma 193
Kaposiform Hemangioendothelioma 196
Malignant Vascular Tumors 197
Epithelioid Hemangioendothelioma 197
Pseudomyogenic Hemangioendothelioma 198
Kaposi Sarcoma 200
Angiosarcoma 201

Vascular tumors of bone are defined as a neoplastic proliferation of
endothelial cells that are biologically benign or malignant. This is a
heterogeneous group of tumors; it includes a wide variety of different
clinicopathologic entities whose biological potential ranges from those
that are relatively indolent and slow growing to those that are extremely
aggressive and frequently fatal. Clinically significant vascular tumors
are infrequent and account for a small fraction of primary bone tumors.
Vascular tumors are one of the more common neoplasms of the
skeleton, and benign variants greatly outnumber their malignant counterparts. Careful autopsy studies have shown that hemangiomas of
vertebral bodies are present in 10% of the population, and magnetic
resonance imaging (MRI) investigations have identified them in 27%
of imaged patients.1 The majority of lesions are discovered incidentally,
and many are multicentric; multiple tumors are identified in 33%
of patients with spinal hemangiomas.1 Vascular tumors may be a
component of different syndromes, including posterior fossa
malformations–hemangiomas–arterial anomalies–cardiac defects–eye
abnormalities–sternal cleft and supraumbilical raphe (PHACE),
Klippel-Trenaunay, and Sturge-Weber syndromes. However, in these
settings, the vascular lesions usually affect the soft tissues and are not
osseous in origin.2
Vascular tumors of the skeleton can arise in any age group, and in
any bone, but the majority develop in adults and affect the vertebral

column, followed by the craniofacial and long bones. The clinical presentations are influenced by the tumors’ location and size. Generally,
they are asymptomatic. When symptomatic, they produce pain, swelling, and sometimes a pathologic fracture. Imaging studies help define
their precise location, size, extent, and multifocality. In addition, these
studies provide valuable information regarding the degree of destruction of the underlying bone. Radiographically, vascular tumors are
lucent but can elicit varying degrees of reactive sclerosis that sometimes can be extensive.
The classification of vascular tumors of bone continues to evolve as
the understanding of their biology increases and new entities are codified (Box 13-1). Controversy persists in defining many benign vascular
tumors as malformations or neoplasms,3 and the scheme utilized in
this chapter is based on their biological behavior (benign versus malignant), extent of disease, and morphologic characteristics. The vast
majority of tumors are considered to be neoplastic.

Benign Vascular Tumors
Arteriovenous Malformation
Vascular malformations and anomalies of bone are rare. The lesions
usually present at birth or during infancy and early childhood.4 In
many patients, the bone and adjacent soft tissues are affected, and in
severe cases large portions of an extremity are involved, resulting
in significant morbidity. Presenting signs and symptoms include pain,
a pulsating mass, bruit, overgrowth of an involved anatomic region,
osteolytic lesions, arteriovenous shunting producing heart failure, and
visible abnormal cartographic vascular patterns in the skin. Arteriovenous malformations often affect the extremities, especially the femur,
tibia, radius, and humerus, jaw bones, and spine. Multiple bones are
involved in a little more than one third of cases. Treatment depends
on the extent of disease and may consist of embolization and surgery.5,6
Radiographically, the lesion can present as multiple tubular and
oval lucencies in the bone. The soft tissues contain abnormal tangles
of large and small vessels, often associated with increased amounts of
adipose tissue.
Gross specimens show irregular large vessels with prominent walls
that course through the cortices and medullary cavity of the involved
187

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
