Angiosarcoma

Figure 13-1. Arteriovenous malformation involving a resected segment of a long
bone. It consists of large vessels that course through and disrupt the cortex and medullary cavity.

skeleton. The vast majority of cases are asymptomatic, and those that
require surgical intervention are uncommon and account for less than
1% of all bone neoplasms requiring surgery.
The morphology of hemangiomas of bone is similar to those neoplasms that arise in soft tissues and skin, and most are classified as
capillary, cavernous, venous, or mixed. The cavernous variant is the
most common, constituting approximately 50% of all cases, followed
by capillary hemangioma, which accounts for about 10% of cases. The
tumors are usually centered in the medullary cavity and rarely are
intracortical or subperiosteal in location.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Intraosseous hemangiomas are encountered most commonly in vertebrae followed by the bones of the skull (frontal and parietal bones),
face, and the long bones of the extremities.7 The age range of affected
patients is broad, but most are middle-aged adults (fifth decade).
Rarely do these tumors develop in children. Males are affected almost
twice as often as females. Most hemangiomas are detected as an incidental finding, and when clinically significant the tumors can present
as a masslike deformity, causing localized pain or neurologic symptoms when compressing nerve roots or the spinal cord. In a minority
of cases, a pathologic fracture is the heralding event.
Radiologic Features
Hemangioma manifests as an oval, radiolucent lesion with a trabeculated pattern.8 In the vertebral bodies, the vertically oriented bony
trabeculae are thickened, giving the lesion a “corduroy” appearance
(Fig. 13-3). In the craniofacial skeleton, the tumors may evoke a prominent sclerotic reaction that can have a starburst or spoke wheel–like
appearance (Fig. 13-4). In the long bones and the pelvis, the tumors
are irregular in shape and have a lytic, honeycomb pattern (Fig. 13-5A
and B). Bone expansion is a feature of larger lesions, and locally aggressive tumors may erode the cortex and extend into the soft tissues. On
computed tomography (CT), the tumor has the density of soft tissue
and sclerotic bony trabeculae, which produce a polka-dot appearance

Figure 13-2. Large dilated veins of an arteriovenous malformation grow into the
cortex, resulting in significant bone resorption.

bones (Fig. 13-1). Histologically, large, architecturally abnormal arteries, veins, and collections of capillaries course throughout the bone and
adjacent soft tissues (Fig. 13-2). The veins and capillaries may show
organizing thrombi with phleboliths and papillary endothelial
hyperplasia.

Conventional Hemangioma
Definition
Conventional hemangioma is a benign vasoformative neoplasm of
endothelial cells. It is the most common type of vascular tumor of the
188

Figure 13-3. Lateral view of the spine demonstrating a hemangioma of the vertebral
body that manifests as sclerosis of the vertically oriented bony trabeculae, producing a
corduroy-like appearance.

Vascular Tumors of Bone
on axial images. MRI is the best modality to demonstrate the extent of
the tumor, impingement on other structures, and extension into the
soft tissues (Fig. 13-6). MRI shows that fat-rich hemangiomas have
high signal intensity on T1-weighted images and that fat-poor lesions
have intermediate or low signal intensities. The fluid in hemangiomas
causes them to have high signal intensity on T2-weighted images.

Figure 13-4. A specimen radiograph of a hemangioma of the calvarium shows the
lytic tumor expanding the bone with associated prominent central sclerosis that has a
sunburst-like appearance at the periphery.

13

Gross Pathology
The tumors are centered in the medullary canal, and they distort the
bone to varying degrees, depending on their size and associated reactive changes. Grossly, they present as red, hemorrhagic, spongy masses
that vary in consistency (Fig. 13-7). Lesions rich in reactive bone are
gritty, with thin, radiating trabeculae subcompartmentalizing the
tumor; in contrast, those that lack bone are soft and friable. Curettage
specimens can appear as fragments of blood clot admixed with spicules
of bone.
Figure 13-5. A, Hemangioma of the ilium presents as a
mixed lytic and sclerotic mass that has a trabeculated
pattern of mineralization. B, Computed tomography shows
expansion of the bone and the honeycomb pattern of
reactive bone that is present within the tumor.

A

B

Figure 13-6. Hemangioma of the calvarium has high signal intensity on this axial
T2-weighted, fat-saturated magnetic resonance image. The tumor impinges on the
surface of the brain.

Figure 13-7. Hemangioma expands the rib and appears as a granular red-tan mass.

189

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 13-8. Cavernous hemangioma replaces the marrow and surrounds preexisting
bony trabeculae.

Figure 13-10. Strips of vessel wall in a curettage specimen of a hemangioma composed of a thin layer of flat endothelial cells.

Figure 13-9. Endothelial lining of cavernous hemangioma consists of bland spindle
cells.

Histopathology
Hemangioma often has a lobular architecture with tumor vessels
coursing through the marrow spaces, encasing preexisting bony trabeculae (Fig. 13-8). Conventional hemangiomas are composed of
blood vessels lined by flattened, cytologically bland endothelial cells
(Fig. 13-9). The vessels usually have thin walls. Those tumors composed of round, dilated capillaries are classified as cavernous hemangiomas, whereas those consisting of small diameter capillaries are the
capillary variant. Tumors composed of veinlike structures are known
as venous hemangiomas; however, some consider these lesions to be
variants of vascular malformations rather than true neoplasms.5 It is
not uncommon for tumors to have overlapping histologic features. In
some cases, the vessels consist merely of an endothelial layer overlying
reactive or preexisting trabecular bone (Fig. 13-10), which makes their
recognition challenging, especially in curettage specimens.
Secondary changes may develop and these include organizing
thrombosis with the formation of calcified phleboliths and papillary
endothelial hyperplasia. These changes most commonly accompany
tumors with a prominent venous component.
190

Figure 13-11. The endothelial cells of a cavernous hemangioma express CD31.

Ancillary Methods
Immunohistochemically, the endothelial cells express CD31, CD34,
ERG, and factor VIII–related antigen (Fig. 13-11).
Genetics
Recently, a single case report of a hemangioma documented the
presence of a novel t(18; 22) (q23; q12) translocation resulting in a
EWSR1-NFATC1 gene fusion.9 This is evidence that hemangiomas
may be true neoplasms and not hamartomas or malformations,
although this remains a point of controversy.
Differential Diagnosis and Pitfalls
The differential diagnosis of conventional hemangioma includes
lymphangioma and normal intraosseous vascular structures, and the
distinction can be difficult in certain situations. Unlike hemangiomas,
the vessels in lymphangioma often have irregular contours; the lumens
may be filled with lymph fluid; and the endothelial cells express D2-40,

Vascular Tumors of Bone

13

Figure 13-12. A nutrient artery and its branches are composed of an aggregate of
well-formed, thick-walled vessels.

Prox-1, and LYVE-1, in addition to the conventional endothelial
markers. It should be noted that none of the lymphatic endothelial
markers is entirely specific; therefore, the immunophenotype of a vascular tumor needs to be interpreted in the context of the morphology
of the tumor and the clinical findings.
Sometimes, sections of the nutrient artery and its branches (Fig.
13-12) are prominent in biopsy or resection specimens and thereby
cause confusion with a hemangioma. Unlike the vessels of a hemangioma, however, the nutrient artery and its tributaries have prominent
muscular walls, are limited in number, and do not grow in a diffuse
pattern.

Lymphangioma
Definition
Lymphangioma is a benign vascular tumor composed of neoplastic
endothelial cells that form lymphatic-like vessels. Its etiology and incidence are unknown; however, it is very rare and much less common
than hemangioma.10 This may be related to the fact that normal bones
do not have a lymphatic supply.11 The tumor can affect all age groups,
although most present within the first two decades of life.

Figure 13-13. Lymphangiomatosis presenting as multiple oval radiolucencies in both
proximal femurs and pelvis.

images. Multiple separate lesions can affect an individual bone or many
bones throughout the skeleton.
Gross Pathology
Grossly, lymphangioma is well circumscribed, honeycomb or cystic in
appearance, and may contain clear or chylous fluid-filled spaces,
divided by septa and thin bony trabeculae. This differs from hemangiomas, which are usually hemorrhagic in appearance.
Histopathology
Lymphangioma is composed of dilated, cystic channels that may
contain valvelike structures and that are lined by flat, banal endothelial
cells (Fig. 13-14). The endothelial cells may appear to cover the surfaces
of preexisting bony trabeculae or overlie a thin layer of woven bone.
The cystic spaces can contain eosinophilic proteinaceous fluid and
scattered red blood cells. Aggregates of lymphocytes are often found

Localization, Clinical Manifestations, and
Treatment and Prognosis
Lymphangioma may be solitary or multifocal and associated with
lesions arising in the soft tissues.12 The tumor can develop in any bone,
and most originate in the metaphysis or diaphysis of long bones, spine,
and jaw. The presence or absence of symptoms depends on the anatomic site and extent of disease. The lesions can be asymptomatic,
locally painful, or large masses that can cause pathologic fracture.
Significant soft tissue involvement may be associated with lymphedema and swelling of an extremity. Chylous effusions may complicate
lesions involving the chest wall.
Treatment depends on the extent of disease and associated symptoms. Bone lesions are usually effectively treated by curettage, and the
recurrence rate is extremely low.
Radiologic Features
The findings vary based on size, extent, and multiplicity of the tumor.
Solitary lesions are well demarcated, radiolucent, multiloculated, and
may be expansile10 (Fig. 13-13). On CT, the lesion is hypointense. On
MRI, lymphangioma has increased signal on T1- and T2-weighted

Figure 13-14. This lymphangioma vessel has thin walls and an irregular configuration.
The lining cells are flat spindle cells, and the adjacent stroma contains scattered small
lymphocytes. The lumen contains occasional red blood cells.
191

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 13-15. Endothelial lining cells in a lymphangioma expressing D2-40.

in the loose fibroconnective tissue surrounding the vessels. When the
tumors contain numerous blood vessels, they may be difficult to distinguish from hemangioma.
Ancillary Methods
Similar to hemangiomas, lymphangiomas expresses CD31, CD34,
factor VIII–related antigen, FLI1, and ERG. Prox-1, LYVE-1, and
D2-40 are expressed by lymphatic endothelium, but they may also be
expressed by endothelial cells in some hemangiomas; therefore, their
specificity is limited (Fig. 13-15).
Differential Diagnosis and Pitfalls
Lymphangioma can be difficult to diagnose in needle biopsy and curettage specimens because the vessel walls are delicate, thin, and easily
disrupted. Helpful findings are long, thin, smooth strips of tissue that
are lined by attenuated spindle cells. When the differential diagnosis is
between lymphangioma and hemangioma, the presence of eosinophilic fluid and valvelike structures supports lymphangioma. Expression of Prox-1, LYVE-1, and D2-40 also provides further evidence of
the nature of the lesion.

Skeletal Angiomatosis
Definition, Synonyms, and Classification
Skeletal angiomatosis is a multicentric disease composed of benign
vascular tumors that involve multiple bones in one or more anatomic
regions.13 The tumors may also affect the soft tissues and viscera. Synonyms include cystic angiomatosis of bone, lymphangiectasis of bone,
Gorham disease, Gorham Stout disease, disappearing bone disease,
phantom bone disease, and aggressive or massive osteolysis.
In this chapter, skeletal angiomatosis is classified into two major
types. One, multiple discrete hemangiomas or lymphangiomas may
involve one or more bones. Two, lymphangioma associated with extensive bone resorption may originate in one bone and progress to involve
adjacent structures, cross joints, and invade articulating bones, eventually resulting in the loss of extensive amounts of bone, which leads to
structural instability and potentially significant morbidity.14
Localization, Clinical Manifestations, and
Treatment and Prognosis
Patients may be asymptomatic or present with pain, pathologic fractures, and soft tissue masses (rarely). The tumors frequently arise in
the bones of the axial skeleton and proximal extremities.
192

No proven etiology has been identified and there is no known
familial predisposition. The age at diagnosis ranges from young children to the elderly, with a peak in the second to third decades of life,
and the sexes are affected equally.
Treatment is based on symptomatology and, when necessary,
usually entails surgical curettage or limited resection. The prognosis is
usually good and only rarely does a hemangioma undergo malignant
transformation into an angiosarcoma.
Gorham disease, first described in 1838 by Jackson, is more
aggressive than the typical disease because it is associated with progressive bone resorption that may be extensive.15 The disease presents
in young adults with no gender predisposition; common sites of
involvement include the skull, jaw, extremities, shoulder and pelvic
girdles, spine, and ribs.16 The location of the lesion determines the
symptoms, which often include weakness, pain, and swelling. Chylous
pericardial and pleural effusions can be seen when the pericardium
or pleura are involved. Once the tumor forms, it induces resorption
of the surrounding bone and continues to grow along the length of
the bone and may cross joints into articulating bones and extend into
the adjacent soft tissues and viscera. Numerous treatment modalities,
including surgery, radiotherapy and medical therapy, have been used,
often in combination. The goal is to halt progression of the disease
therapy (bisphosphonates, thalidomide, and interferon) and then to
resect the tumor with subsequent reconstruction to maintain structural stability. Recent studies have elucidated molecular pathways
involved in the disease and have identified potential targets that can
be treated with tyrosine kinase inhibitors and blockers of angiogenic
growth factors.17 The prognosis is variable and depends on the location and severity of the disease. It can range from minimal disability
to a fatal outcome (15% of patients) due to damage to vital organs
such as the brainstem or spinal cord secondary to tumor involvement
of neighboring supportive bones.
Radiologic Features
Radiographically, skeletal angiomatosis presents as multiple round to
oval radiolucencies that have a honeycomb appearance and are well
circumscribed (see Fig. 13-13). The lytic nature of the tumors on radiographs is what gave rise to the appellation “cystic.” The margins are
sharp with variable degrees of sclerosis and endosteal scalloping. In
some cases, the tumors are expansile and associated with soft tissue
extension due to erosion of the cortex and involvement of adjacent
structures.
Gorham disease first presents as poorly circumscribed intramedullary and cortical lucencies that merge and result in progressive dissolution, fracture, fragmentation, and complete disappearance of the
involved bone16 (Fig. 13-16). The adjacent soft tissues may undergo
atrophy. In the early stages, the lesion may show increased uptake on
bone scintigraphy, but subsequently the site of lost bone tissue shows
decreased uptake. CT reveals loss of the bone substance, and the ends
of the residual portions may have a pointed configuration. MRI shows
that the involved bone demonstrates low signal intensity on
T1-weighted images and increased signal intensity on T2-weighted
images.
Gross Pathology and Histopathology
Skeletal angiomatosis presents as a circumscribed, round or oval, hemorrhagic, spongelike mass. The vessels are capillaries or lymphatics that
may be dilated, filled with blood or lymph, and lined by flattened
endothelial cells. The vessels may replace the marrow and encase preexisting bony trabeculae.
In Gorham disease, there is no obvious solid mass; the bone or soft
tissue is destroyed and replaced by thin-walled lymphatic spaces that

Vascular Tumors of Bone

13

Figure 13-18. Gorham disease manifests as dilated, thin-walled vascular spaces that
have caused extensive osteoclastic bone resorption.

lymphatic spaces express CD31 as well as the more lymphatic-specific
markers LYVE-1, Prox-1, and D2-40.
Figure 13-16 Gorham disease previously treated with a fibular strut graft and metal
hardware. The tumor has resulted in resorption of much of the proximal femur.

Figure 13-17. Resected proximal femur with involvement by Gorham disease shows
loss of bone and screw holes of the metal hardware that was used to support the bone.
Note the absence of a mass.

vary in size18 (Fig. 13-17). The lumens are often empty, and the endothelial cells are flat and cytologically banal (Fig. 13-18). A thin cuff of
loose connective tissue forms a supportive framework.
Ancillary Methods
The endothelial cells lining blood vascular spaces express factor VIII–
related antigen, CD31, CD34, and ERG. The endothelial cells lining the

Epithelioid Hemangioma
Definition and Synonyms
This benign, locally aggressive vascular neoplasm is composed of
characteristic epithelial-like appearing (epithelioid) endothelial cells. It
most commonly arises in the skin, soft tissues, and lymph nodes, and
approximately 15% of neoplasms originate in the skeleton.19 Synonyms,
which are outdated terms, include angiolymphoid hyperplasia with
eosinophilia, histiocytoid hemangioma, and hemorrhagic epithelioid
and spindle cell hemangioma.
Localization, Clinical Manifestations, and Treatment
Epithelioid hemangioma is uncommon, and its exact incidence is
unknown. Patients range in age from 10 to 75 years, with an average
of approximately 40 years. There is a slight male predominance, with
a male-to-female ratio of 1.4 : 1. In the skeleton, the tumors tend to
involve the long tubular bones (38%), distal lower extremities (18%),
flat bones (18%), vertebrae (16%), and small bones of the hands (8%).19
Epithelioid hemangioma of the skin and subcutis is multifocal in as
many as 50% of cases, whereas approximately 20% of those developing
in the skeleton are multicentric. Involvement of widely separate sites,
including simultaneous involvement of skin and bone and lymph node,
is uncommon.
Patients commonly present with pain localized to the involved
bone. Rarely is it detected as an incidental finding during investigation
for another medical problem.
Treatment usually consists of thorough curettage that is associated
with a recurrence rate of 10% to 15%. In cases in which a small bone
has been largely destroyed, such as in the digit, an amputation or en
bloc excision may be required.
Radiologic Features
Conventional imaging and CT reveal a geographic, lucent, lesion with
well-defined margins19,20 (Fig. 13-19). Tumors in small bones may
produce expansion of the bone with erosion through the cortex and
extension into the soft tissues and large lesions can produce significant
bone destruction (Fig. 13-20). MRI demonstrates that the tumors have
low signal intensity on T1-weighted images and intermediate or high
signal intensity on T2-weighted images (Fig. 13-21).
193

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 13-19. Radiograph of epithelioid hemangioma of the second metatarsal that
expands the bone and shows osteolysis.

Figure 13-21. A coronal T2-weighted magnetic resonance image of a multicentric
epithelioid hemangioma shows numerous bright foci of tumor involving the tibia and
femur.

Figure 13-22. A resected proximal femur containing two nodules of recurrent epithelioid hemangioma adjacent to metal hardware. The tumor nodules are red-tan and well
circumscribed.

Figure 13-20. An axial computed tomography scan of epithelioid hemangioma shows
an eccentric tumor with cortical destruction. However, the soft tissue component is
surrounded by a thin layer of reactive bone.

Gross Pathology
The tumors typically range in size from 1 to 15 cm (mean, 5 cm) and
are centered in the medullary cavity. They have a well-circumscribed,
lobulated, soft, solid, and hemorrhagic appearance (Fig. 13-22). Large
tumors may expand the bone, erode and transgress the cortex, and
grow into the soft tissues with pushing margins.
194

Histopathology
The tumor has a nodular growth pattern and generally has well-defined
margins; however, neoplastic vessels replace portions of the marrow
and surround preexisting bony trabeculae (Fig. 13-23). The hallmark
of the epithelioid endothelial cells is their polyhedral shape and their
abundant, densely eosinophilic cytoplasm that frequently contains one
or more large vacuoles19 (Fig. 13-24). The vacuoles represent the earliest stage of vessel lumen formation, and they may be empty or contain
intact or fragmented red blood cells. As vacuoles from neighboring
cells fuse, they create vascular spaces of varying degrees of differentiation.21 The tumor cell nuclei are oval or bean shaped and lobated with
grooves and clefts, and they have fine chromatin and small nucleoli.

Vascular Tumors of Bone

13

Figure 13-23. Epithelioid hemangioma has a lobular architecture.

Figure 13-25. Sheetlike growth pattern of epithelioid cells in an epithelioid hemangioma. Some of the tumor cells have clear cytoplasmic vacuoles.

Figure 13-24. Epithelioid hemangioma containing well-formed vessels lined by large,
epithelioid endothelial cells with dense, eosinophilic cytoplasm.

Figure 13-26. Epithelioid hemangioma with foci of hemorrhage and proliferating
spindle cells.

Well-formed vessels are numerous in epithelioid hemangioma, and
the epithelioid endothelial cells bulge into their lumens in a “tombstone” or hobnail-like fashion. In some cases, the cells are arranged in
solid cords and even sheets, which can cause confusion with more
aggressive lesions22 (Fig. 13-25). In a minority of tumors, there is abundant intralesional hemorrhage admixed with groups of cytologically
bland spindle cells, with little or no accompanying inflammation23
(Fig. 13-26). Mitoses are present but usually not numerous and are
of normal structure. Necrosis may be seen; however, it is not
significant.
The stroma is composed of loose connective tissue and may contain
numerous inflammatory cells, including plasma cells and eosinophils
(Fig. 13-27). In some cases the inflammation is so dense that it can
mimic osteomyelitis. No myxohyaline stroma is present. The periphery
of some of the tumor nodules are rich in arteriole-like blood vessels
that are lined by normal-appearing endothelial cells.
Other infrequent findings variably noted within the tumor are
abundant reactive bone formation and intra-lesional osteoclastic multinucleated giant cells.19

Figure 13-27. A mixed inflammatory infiltrate rich in eosinophils can obscure the
neoplastic cells in epithelioid hemangioma.
195

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
cells in epithelioid hemangioma and the carcinoma cells will not
express CD34, CD31, and factor VIII.

Kaposiform Hemangioendothelioma

Figure 13-28. Epithelioid hemangioma is strongly positive for CD31.

Kaposiform hemangioendothelioma is a very uncommon, potentially
locally aggressive tumor of childhood that usually arises in the skin
and soft tissues, and very rarely presents as a primary bone tumor.25 In
the soft tissues the tumor can be associated with a consumptive coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia known as the Kasabach-Merritt
phenomenon.
Primary bone lesions have been reported in the spine and long
bones, where they present as a lytic or mixed lytic and sclerotic mass
that may be expansile without evoking a prominent periosteal reaction25,26 (Fig. 13-30). Treatment is variable, but complete excision with
clear margins is nearly curative with very rare recurrences.
Grossly, the tumors are firm, gray-white, multinodular masses composed of neoplastic spindle cells with elongate nuclei with fine chromatin and scant eosinophilic cytoplasm. The tumor cells are arranged
in solid sheets or fascicles. In areas, some of the tumor cells are epithelioid and their cytoplasm may contain vacuoles, small eosinophilic
hyaline globules, or fine deposits of hemosiderin.26 The epithelioid
endothelial cells are often arranged in small aggregates forming glomeruloid structures (Fig. 13-31). The vascular lumens formed by the
tumor cells may be slitlike, crescentic, or well-formed capillary or
lymphatic-like structures, and the latter can be numerous resembling
hemangioma or lymphangioma.26 The vascular spaces may be empty
or contain scattered intact or fragments of red blood cells.
Immunohistochemically, the tumor cells express CD31, CD34, and
FLI1 but they do not express GLUT-1 or human herpesvirus-8
(HHV-8). The spindle cells are usually negative or weakly positive for
factor VIII–related antigen. This immunoprofile helps distinguish
kaposiform hemangioendothelioma from juvenile hemangiomas,
which are positive for GLUT-1, and Kaposi sarcoma, which expresses
HHV-8.

Figure 13-29. The neoplastic cells in epithelioid hemangioma can be strongly positive
for keratin.

Ancillary Methods
Immunohistochemistry is helpful in documenting the endothelial phenotype of the tumor cells but does not distinguish benign from malignant epithelioid vascular tumors. The epithelioid endothelial cells are
positive for CD31, CD34, FLI1, ERG, and factor VIII–related antigen
(Figs. 13-28 and 13-29). Approximately 50% of cases express the epithelial markers keratin and epithelial membrane antigen, and the positivity may be extensive and intense.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes epithelioid hemangioendothelioma,
epithelioid angiosarcoma, and metastatic carcinoma. Epithelioid
hemangioendothelioma is distinguished by the presence of myxohyaline or chondroid stroma and the lack of well-formed blood vessels.
Fluorescence in situ hybridization to detect the t(1;3)(p36;q25) translocation in epithelioid hemangioendothelioma can be used in difficult
cases.24 Epithelioid angiosarcoma can be differentiated by the significant cytologic atypia of the neoplastic cells and the presence of numerous mitoses including atypical forms. Metastatic carcinoma cells will
also demonstrate a greater degree of cytologic atypia than the tumor
196

Figure 13-30. Kaposiform hemangioendothelioma presenting as multiple, oval, lytic
masses in the fourth and fifth metacarpals and proximal phalanges.

Vascular Tumors of Bone

13

Figure 13-31. Groups of epithelioid endothelial cells associated with spindle cells
forming a glomeruloid structure in kaposiform hemangioendothelioma.

Malignant Vascular Tumors
The term hemangioendothelioma typically refers to a vascular neoplasm that has biologic behavior intermediate between a hemangioma
and an angiosarcoma. Biologically, two have limited metastatic
potential (i.e., epithelioid hemangioendothelioma, pseudomyogenic
hemangioendothelioma), and another (i.e., kaposiform hemangioendothelioma) may have extensive biologic potential and result in severe
morbidity and even death (although it does not metastasize). Other
types of hemangioendotheliomas such as retiform hemangioendothelioma and composite hemangioendothelioma arise in the skin and soft
tissues and so rarely affect bone that they are not presented.

Figure 13-32. A coronal computed tomography scan of epithelioid hemangioendothelioma showing an oval, well-circumscribed lytic and blastic mass in the vertebral
body.

Epithelioid Hemangioendothelioma
Brief Overview
Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma is the most aggressive of the group
of hemangioendotheliomas. It is associated with the greatest risk of
developing distant metastases resulting in death.27
Incidence and Demographics
Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma occurs in most age groups, with a
peak frequency in the second and third decades of life.28 The tumor
affects both sexes equally and has a higher incidence in whites than in
people of other races.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Like other vascular tumors of bone, epithelioid hemangioendothelioma often manifests with multifocal disease; 33% to 50% of patients
have multiple lesions in either a single bone or multiple separate bones.
Approximately 10% of patients who present with a solitary tumor
progress to develop multifocal disease.29 The tumor most frequently
arises in the long and short tubular bones of the extremities, pelvis,
and spine. Patients typically present with localized pain, which may be
associated with swelling. In addition to the skeletal lesions, some
patients also have concurrent tumor burden in the soft tissues, liver,
and lung.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma tends to have a variable appearance on imaging studies.30 The tumors are frequently round or elongate
and predominately lytic, and they range in size from 1 to 5 cm in

Figure 13-33. Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma appears as a well circumscribed,
gray-tan mass that is partially surrounded by reactive bone.

greatest dimension (Fig. 13-32). The margins may be well-delineated
or poorly defined, and the adjacent bone is often sclerotic. The extent
and pattern of tumor-induced bone destruction is best visualized on
CT and MRI.
Grossly, epithelioid hemangioendothelioma is typically firm,
rubbery, and pale tan, in contrast to the red and hemorrhagic appearance of conventional hemangioma and angiosarcoma (Fig. 13-33).
Histopathology
Microscopically, the neoplastic cells are arranged in cords and nests
embedded within a basophilic to hyalinized stroma that may resemble
cartilaginous matrix (Fig. 13-34). The tumor cells are epithelioid and
spindle shaped, with round or elongate nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and
abundant densely eosinophilic cytoplasm. One of the prominent features of the tumor is the presence of intracytoplasmic lumens or vacuoles that may contain intact or fragmented red blood cells (Fig. 13-35).
Subtle, poorly formed vascular channels that develop from the coalescence of neighboring vacuoles may be observed; however, well-formed
vascular lumens are rarely present. In general, the tumor cells demonstrate limited cytologic atypia and mitotic activity. In a minority of
197

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
stroma instead of the characteristic basophilic or hyalinized stroma is
typically seen in metastatic carcinomas, and importantly, carcinomas
do not stain for endothelial markers. Chondrosarcoma can be distinguished because the neoplastic chondrocytes do not grow in nests, they
lack relatively abundant densely eosinophilic cytoplasm, they express
S-100 protein, and they are negative for endothelial markers. Although
rare as a primary bone tumor, epithelioid sarcoma is also composed of
large eosinophilic cells with prominent nucleoli and is positive for
antibodies to cytokeratin and CD34. Unlike epithelioid hemangioendothelioma, the tumor nodules in epithelioid sarcoma usually exhibit
central necrosis, and the tumor cells do not express factor VIII–related
antigen or CD31; furthermore, INI-1 expression is lost in epithelioid
sarcoma but maintained in epithelioid hemangioendothelioma.

Figure 13-34. Cords of epithelioid and spindle cells in a basophilic hyalinized stroma
characterize epithelioid hemangioendothelioma.

Figure 13-35. Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma cells have eosinophilic cytoplasm
that may harbor round clear vacuoles. Note the solid-appearing, hyalinized stroma.

cases, there may be significant nuclear hyperchromasia and pleomorphism that is accompanied by numerous mitoses. Some have designated this variant as malignant epithelioid hemangioendothelioma. This
variant can be difficult to distinguish from angiosarcoma.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis of epithelioid hemangioendothelioma
includes other epithelioid vascular neoplasms, metastatic carcinoma,
and several sarcomas. Epithelioid hemangioma contains more wellformed vessels and does not have the solid-appearing basophilic or
hyalinized stroma typical of epithelioid hemangioendothelioma. This
stroma is also absent in epithelioid angiosarcoma, which demonstrates
greater cytologic atypia and mitotic activity than epithelioid hemangioendothelioma. The epithelioid features of the tumor cells, their
cohesive nature, and intracytoplasmic vacuoles make epithelioid
hemangioendothelioma a great mimicker of metastatic adenocarcinoma. Compounding this problem is the fact that epithelioid hemangioendothelioma also frequently stains with the epithelial markers
keratin and epithelial membrane antigen. However, desmoplastic
198

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
The full spectrum of immunohistochemical endothelial markers,
including factor VIII–related antigen, CD34, CD31, FLI1, and ERG,
are expressed in epithelioid hemangioendothelioma. Additionally, like
other epithelioid vascular tumors, epithelioid hemangioendothelioma
may show intense and extensive positive staining for keratin and epithelial membrane antigen in approximately 50% of cases. The tumor
cells typically do not stain for S-100 protein or desmin.
On electron microscopy, the cells have the characteristics of endothelial cells, including pinocytotic vesicles and basal lamina material.
Additionally, they also contain abundant intermediate filaments and
intracytoplasmic lumens, which are typically absent in normal endothelial cells.
Genetics
Recent studies have identified a recurrent translocation t(1;3)
(p36.3;q25)31 with rearrangement between the CAMTA1 gene (at
1p36.3) and WWTR1 gene (at 3q25), resulting in a WWTR1-CAMTA1
fusion in epithelioid hemangioendothelioma.32 An additional study
demonstrated identical breakpoints in different lesions from the same
patient, indicating that multifocal epithelioid hemangioendotheliomas
is a manifestation of metastatic disease rather a “field defect” or true
multicentric disease.33
More recently, a TFE3 rearrangement with a YAP1-TFE3 gene
fusion, instead of the WWTR1-CAMTA1 gene fusion, was found in a
subset of epithelioid hemangioendotheliomas, characterized by a
somewhat different morphology with focally well-formed vasoformative features.34
Treatment and Prognosis
Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma is best treated by complete surgical
excision rather than curettage, because wide resection is likely associated with a lower rate of local recurrence.29 Radiation or thermal ablation may be needed for extensive multifocal lesions, which are not
amenable to surgery. Overall, patients with osseous epithelioid hemangioendothelioma usually have a good prognosis; the local recurrence
rate is approximately 20% and the rate of metastasis and death due to
disease is less than 10%.29 However, when there is concurrent visceral
tumor, up to 20% of patients may succumb to the disease.28

Pseudomyogenic Hemangioendothelioma
Brief Historical Overview
Pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma is a recently coined term for
a hemangioendothelioma35 that was originally called fibroma-like
variant of epithelioid sarcoma36 and subsequently designated epithelioid
sarcoma-like hemangioendothelioma.37,38 This rare form of hemangioendothelioma arises in soft tissue more frequently than in bone, and
there are very few descriptions of purely osseous lesions.

Vascular Tumors of Bone
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma occurs predominantly in
patients between 20 and 50 years of age and has a marked male predominance. Tumors are usually multifocal and arise in the extremities.
Soft tissue lesions frequently affect multiple tissue planes. In the largest
study analyzing 50 patients with a total of 205 lesions, 14% of tumors
affected bone, which accounted for 20% of the patients; only one
patient had purely osseous disease.35 Another case report describes a
10-year-old child with lesions in the distal femur and distal tibia.39 The
tumor has a relatively favorable long-term prognosis despite its
multifocality.35
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographically, pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma of the
bone tends to show multifocal disease with involvement of multiple
tissue planes, such as skin, subcutaneous fat, muscle, and the cortex
and medullary canal of bone. Bone lesions vary and range from small,
oval, cortical lucencies, often with well-defined borders, to larger
destructive masses that are centered in the medullary cavity and erode
the cortex (Fig. 13-36). Grossly, pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma is solid and gray-tan in color.

has a basophilic or hyalinized stroma with prominent intracytoplasmic
vacuoles that are not seen in pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma.
Epithelioid angiosarcoma is composed of tumor cells that demonstrate
a significantly greater degree of nuclear atypia than pseudomyogenic
hemangioendothelioma and also displays vasoformative areas. All of
the epithelioid vascular tumors can also be distinguished from pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma by their expression of CD34.
As suggested by one of its names, epithelioid sarcoma-like hemangioendothelioma, pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma is often
mistaken for epithelioid sarcoma because of its similar clinical and
histopathologic appearance. Pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma
differs from epithelioid sarcoma in that it lacks a nodular growth
pattern with central necrosis and it does express CD31, ERG, and
INI-1, unlike epithelioid sarcoma. Additionally, more than 50% of
cases of epithelioid sarcoma can be positive for CD34, whereas nearly
all cases of pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma are negative.
Additionally, pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma may be
confused with myogenic tumors. Immunohistochemistry is very
helpful in this distinction; myogenic tumors express desmin, whereas
pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma is negative for this antigen.

13

Histopathology
Pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma is a poorly marginated
tumor composed of spindle and epithelioid cells that grow in a fascicular and sheetlike pattern (Fig. 13-37). The tumor cells have abundant
densely eosinophilic cytoplasm and oval or elongate vesicular nuclei
that have variably prominent nucleoli (Fig. 13-38). Cytoplasmic vacuoles are not present. Occasionally, tumor cells have a rhabdomyoblastlike appearance with eccentric cytoplasm. Nuclear atypia is typically
mild, and mitotic activity is generally low. In more than 50% of cases,
there is a significant neutrophilic inflammatory infiltrate and the
stroma is inconspicuous. Well-formed vascular lumens are absent.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis of pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma
includes other epithelioid vascular neoplasms; metastatic carcinoma;
and sarcomas, particularly epithelioid sarcoma. Epithelioid hemangioma can be distinguished from pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma by its well-defined vascular spaces and the absence of large spindle
cells arranged in fascicles. Epithelioid hemangioendothelioma typically

Figure 13-37. Nodules of pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma surrounded by
reactive woven bone.

Figure 13-36. A coronal computed tomography scan of the hip shows multiple, wellcircumscribed lesions of pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma.

Figure 13-38. Spindle cells have dense eosinophilic cytoplasm in pseudomyogenic
hemangioendothelioma.
199

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 13-39. Cytoplasmic staining of tumor cells for CD31 in pseudomyogenic
hemangioendothelioma.

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
The tumor cells are positive for the endothelial markers FLI1 and ERG,
and in approximately 50% of cases, the tumor cells express CD31 in a
linear, membranous staining pattern (Fig. 13-39). The tumor cells are
diffusively positive for keratin AE1/AE3, with a subset also variably
positive for CAM5.2. The tumor cells are, however, almost always negative for CD34, desmin, and S-100 protein.
On transmission electron microscopy, the neoplastic cells have
abundant cytoplasm containing prominent rough endoplasmic reticulum and aggregates of intermediate filaments. Some cells also have
numerous subplasmalemmal pinocytotic vesicles. These features are
consistent with endothelial differentiation, although definitive WeibelPalade bodies have not been identified.
Genetics
Recent studies have identified a recurrent translocation t(7;19)
(q22;q13) that results in a fusion of the SERPINE1 and FOSB genes
in pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma.40,41 The translocation
appears to be specific for this type of tumor.
Treatment and Prognosis
Most cases of pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma arise in soft
tissue and have been treated by local surgical excision. Radiation
therapy and chemotherapy have been used infrequently. The optimal
treatment for primary bone lesions remains to be elucidated but will
likely include thorough curettage and complete resection, when possible. The local recurrence rate for soft tissue lesions is 58%, and lymph
node or distant metastases have been rarely reported. In a study with
short-term follow-up, overall mortality rate is very low (2%).35

Kaposi Sarcoma
Brief Historical Overview
Kaposi sarcoma is a vascular lesion of low-grade malignant potential
that was first described by Kaposi in 1872.42 It is associated with HHV-8
infection. There are four major epidemiologic forms of Kaposi sarcoma,
including acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS)-related,
classic, endemic (African), and the rare iatrogenic (transplantassociated). Kaposi sarcoma is a multifocal tumor that frequently
involves mucocutaneous sites, lymph nodes, and viscera organs such
as the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts.
200

Figure 13-40. A sagittal T1-weighted magnetic resonance image of the spine shows
multiple, bright deposits of Kaposi sarcoma.

Incidence and Demographics
Kaposi sarcoma involvement of bone is rare. It usually affects adults
with a wide age range and occurs as a manifestation of any of the four
different epidemiologic forms.43 Males are more commonly affected in
the AIDS-related and endemic (African) subtypes. Skeletal disease in
the pediatric population is usually present only in the setting of the
endemic (African) subtype.
Radiologic Features
Most cases of osseous Kaposi sarcoma lesions are osteolytic and have
ill-defined margins. In some cases, there are numerous lytic areas that
produce a bubbly appearance on imaging studies (Fig. 13-40). Infrequently, a periosteal reaction can be seen, particularly in patients with
the endemic (African) type. CT and MRI are better at detecting osseous
Kaposi sarcoma lesions than radiographs or bone scans. Additionally,
MRI is more accurate than CT for the assessment of soft tissue
extension.44
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Endemic and classic Kaposi sarcoma lesions usually involve the peripheral skeleton, whereas AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma tends to affect the
axial skeleton (vertebrae, ribs, sternum, and pelvis) and maxillofacial
bones.43 Almost all patients have concomitant nonosseous Kaposi
sarcoma lesions. Patients typically present with bone pain and limited
mobility. Joint involvement is unusual, and pathologic fractures
are rare.
Histopathology
Like cutaneous Kaposi sarcoma, osseous Kaposi sarcoma is composed
of well-formed fascicles of monomorphic relatively bland spindle cells
that delineate slitlike vascular clefts that are filled with erythrocytes
(Fig. 13-41). In cross section, the fascicles have a sievelike or honeycomb appearance. Commonly present are hemosiderin deposits, a
lymphoplasmacytic inflammatory infiltrate, and hyaline globules.
These periodic acid–Schiff (PAS)-positive, diastase-resistant, small

Vascular Tumors of Bone

13

Figure 13-41. Spindle cells of Kaposi sarcoma infiltrate between bony trabeculae.

spherules are located both within the cytoplasm of the tumor cells and
the adjacent extracellular space. Infrequently, anaplastic features such
as nuclear pleomorphism, epithelioid tumor cells, increased mitotic
activity, and necrosis are present.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis of osseous Kaposi sarcoma includes other
vascular tumors such as kaposiform hemangioendothelioma, spindle
cell hemangioma, angiosarcoma, and other sarcomas. Kaposi sarcoma
can be distinguished from kaposiform hemangioendothelioma because
it does not have either a glomeruloid architecture or capillary
hemangioma-like areas and is positive for HHV-8 and latencyassociated nuclear antigen-1 (LANA-1), which are not expressed in
kaposiform hemangioendothelioma. Spindle cell hemangioma differs
from Kaposi sarcoma by the presence of cavernous, thin-walled vessels;
furthermore, the neoplastic cells are negative for antibodies to HHV-8
and LANA-1. Some angiosarcomas may have Kaposi sarcoma–like
features, and some Kaposi sarcomas may have anaplastic features that
can be seen in angiosarcomas. The distinction can be made based on
the staining of HHV-8 and LANA-1. Kaposi sarcomas may demonstrate a herringbone pattern and thereby be confused with fibrosarcoma, which can be excluded based on the presence of slitlike vascular
clefts and hyaline globule as well as expression of HHV and
LANA-1.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
The tumor cells in Kaposi sarcoma typically express endothelial
markers, including factor VIII–related antigen, CD31, CD34, FLI1,
and ERG. Additionally, they also express lymphatic endothelial markers
D2-40 and VEGFR-3. LANA-1, a viral protein encoded by HHV-8, is
the most specific immunohistochemistry marker for Kaposi sarcoma
(Fig. 13-42). The tumor cells typically do not stain with antibodies to
keratin, S-100 protein, or desmin.
Ultrastructurally, the tumor cells show fragmented basal lamina
with few or no pericytes, suggesting lymphatic differentiation. The
spindled “perithelial” cells also have lysosomes and ferritin and appear
to be actively phagocytic.
Treatment and Prognosis
A wide variety of modalities has been used to treat osseous Kaposi
sarcoma, including surgery, radiation, antiretroviral therapy, and
chemotherapy.43 There is no curative therapy for Kaposi sarcoma.

Figure 13-42. Cells of Kaposi sarcoma show nuclear staining for human herpesvirus-8
by immunohistochemistry.

Radiation therapy is considered to be highly effective locally. For systemic therapy, antiretroviral agents may be combined with immune
modulation agents or systemic chemotherapy.45

Angiosarcoma
Incidence and Demographics
Primary angiosarcoma of bone is very uncommon. It accounts for
slightly more than 1% of primary bone sarcomas.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Angiosarcoma of bone typically arises in the appendicular skeleton.
Compared to other vascular tumors of bone, angiosarcomas are more
frequently solitary, although up to one third of cases involve multiple
bones. A painful enlarging mass, which may cause pathologic fracture,
is the typical clinical presentation. Rarely, angiosarcoma may be associated with previous radiation, Paget disease, bone infarction, or skeletal
hemangiomatosis. Primary angiosarcoma of bone is usually aggressive
and has a high mortality rate.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographically, angiosarcoma of bone is typically a lytic lesion with
poorly defined margins and prominent destruction of the underlying
bone. Tumors range in size from 5 to 10 cm in greatest dimension (Fig.
13-43). Cortical destruction and extension into the soft tissues are

Figure 13-43. An axial computed tomography scan of epithelioid angiosarcoma of
the pubis showing destruction of the cortex and extension into the soft tissues.
201

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 13-45. Angiosarcoma contains well-formed vascular spaces.

Figure 13-44. Resected angiosarcoma of the femur has destroyed the bone and
caused a pathologic fracture.

associated with a periosteal reaction. When multicentric, tumors can
simulate metastatic carcinoma or multiple myeloma.
Grossly, angiosarcoma of bone is usually dark red and hemorrhagic
(Fig. 13-44). It generally has poorly defined irregular borders, with
frequent invasion into the neighboring soft tissues. Additionally, tumor
necrosis can be prominent, and the underlying bone appears partially
destroyed.
Histopathology
Microscopically, angiosarcoma is composed of cytologically malignant
epithelioid or spindle endothelial cells growing in solid sheets with foci
of rudimentary blood vessel formation or anastomosing, well-formed,
vascular channels (Figs. 13-45 and 13-46). The epithelioid cells usually
predominate and have abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm that may
contain prominent intracytoplasmic vacuoles. A neutrophilic infiltrate
is prominent in a subset of tumors.46 Mitoses are often numerous and
atypical mitotic figures are frequently present. The stroma is inconspicuous and consists of small amounts of fibrous tissue. Necrosis and
hemorrhage are frequently encountered.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes other vascular tumors, especially
epithelioid hemangioma and epithelioid hemangioendothelioma, as
well as metastatic carcinoma, metastatic melanoma, and other highgrade sarcomas. Epithelioid angiosarcoma can be distinguished from
epithelioid hemangioma by its significant cytologic atypia. It differs
from epithelioid hemangioendothelioma in that it lacks basophilic or
hyalinized stroma. Even though angiosarcoma, particularly the epithelioid variant, may demonstrate positive staining of various epithelial
markers, immunohistochemistry is still very useful in excluding the
diagnosis of metastatic carcinoma and malignant melanoma because
of the lack of expression of various endothelial markers, including
factor VIII–related antigen, CD31, and CD34. Therefore, when unusual
epithelioid tumors of bone are encountered, it is prudent to perform
202

Figure 13-46. Epithelioid angiosarcoma displaying cytologically atypical tumor cells
admixed with neutrophils.

immunohistochemistry with antibodies directed against endothelial
markers to rule out the possibility of epithelioid angiosarcoma. Similarly, electron microscopy can be very useful in demonstrating endothelial features of the tumor cells and the lack of those characteristic
of carcinoma or melanoma.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
The tumor cells in angiosarcoma typically express one or more of the
endothelial markers, including factor VIII–related antigen, CD31,
CD34, FLI1, and ERG. As with other epithelioid vascular tumors, the
tumor cells in epithelioid angiosarcoma may show diffuse strong staining for keratin and epithelial membrane antigen. By electron microscopy, the tumor cells have the features of primitive endothelial cells,
including pinocytotic vesicles, basal lamina, and intercellular and
intracellular lumina.
Genetics
A novel t(1;14) (p21;q24) translocation has been identified in a case of
angiosarcoma of bone.47 However, the significance of this translocation
is not clear.

Vascular Tumors of Bone
Treatment and Prognosis
Angiosarcoma of bone is best treated with complete en bloc excision.
Adjuvant radiation and chemotherapy have no proven therapeutic
benefit. For patients with localized disease, the 5-year overall survival
rate has been reported to be 33%; patients with metastases usually
succumb to disease.48
