Permeative lesions involving the bone represent many different disease
processes and radiographically may mimic one another. The permeative lesions discussed in this chapter represent a diverse group that
include benign tumors, malignant tumors, a variety of histiocytic disorders, hematolymphoid-derived tumors that primarily involve bone,
and osteolytic lesions secondary to localized or systemic infection.
These entities have certain histomorphologic, immunocytochemical,
and molecular features that provide for a definitive diagnosis; may
guide surgical, medical, and oncologic management; and predict prognosis and outcome.

Adamantinoma
Background
First described in 1913,1–4 adamantinoma is a low-grade biphasic
malignant bone tumor of probable epithelial origin with a basaloid
character and a prominent mesenchymal component, resembling
osteofibrous dysplasia. This tumor occurs almost exclusively in the
midshaft of the tibia and sometimes the fibula. It is a rare tumor, representing 0.1% to 0.5% of all primary bone tumors. The term adamantinoma was used because of the close resemblance to ameloblastoma
(adamantinoma) of the jaws. The derivation of the tumor is uncertain;
the most widely accepted conjecture is basal epithelium displacement

from skin during embryogenesis. In comparison to other long bones,
the anterior tibia is in close proximity to the skin during endochondral
bone formation. The epithelial origin is supported by immunohistochemical and ultrastructural studies.
Clinical Features
Adamantinoma occurs most commonly in the second to fifth decades
of life, with 75% of affected patients presenting in the second and third
decades; the median age is 25 to 35 years.1–4 There is a slight male
predilection, with a male-to-female ratio of 1.25 : 1.0. This tumor rarely
occurs in the pediatric population (3% of cases). Long bones are
affected in more than 95% of cases, with the vast majority occurring
in the tibia (80% to 85%) and some involving the fibula (10% to 15%).
There are rare case reports of adamantinoma arising in the pretibial
soft tissue without underlying bone involvement.
The symptoms are indolent and nonspecific, and the tumor progresses slowly.1–4 Often, symptoms are present for several years before
medical care is sought. The presenting symptom is swelling with or
without pain, and there is a history of trauma in about two thirds of
affected patients. A tibial bowing deformity may be present due to the
anterior placement of the tumor, and one fourth of patients present
with a pathologic fracture. Rare cases with hypercalcemia have been
reported with adamantinoma with pulmonary metastases.
Radiologic Features
The typical adamantinoma occurs as a mid-diaphyseal osteolytic lesion
in the tibia, which is expansile, eccentric, and medullary in location.1,2
This tumor involves the anterior metadiaphysis of the tibia in 85% to
90% of cases and may be seen in combination with ipsilateral fibula
involvement. Rare cases may involve the ulna, humerus, femur, ribs,
spine, and short bones of the feet. The lesion has a “soap bubble”
appearance with multifocal ring-shaped radiolucencies surrounded by
sclerotic rims. The well-circumscribed lesion usually has a thickened,
sclerotic border with the adjacent uninvolved bone, indicative of slow
growth, and may be up to 11 cm in length. Due to the anterior tibial
location of the lesion, bowing of the bone is a characteristic finding on
imaging. There may be a periosteal reaction and extension of the lesion
into the pretibial soft tissues (15%). Computed tomography (CT) and
magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) findings tend to be nonspecific.
205

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
MRI is important in planning surgical resection in limb salvage procedures in order to determine the extent of soft tissue and intramedullary involvement and distant cortical foci of tumor (skip lesions).
Nuclear bone scans may show increased blood flow and blood pooling
in the region of the tumor.
Certain radiologic features mimic osteofibrous dysplasia and
fibrous dysplasia.1,2 However, adamantinoma may be distinguished
from these entities by the midshaft diaphyseal location, anterior cortical bone involvement, lesion extension toward the medullary space,
and nodular appearance.
Pathologic Features
Gross examination of the resected specimen demonstrates anterior
cortical involvement by tumor and tan intracortical tumor nodules
often associated with a periosteal reaction.1–4 A sclerotic rim of bone
adjacent to uninvolved bone may also be appreciated. The tumor may
extend into the marrow space and overlying soft tissues. Usually, such
features cannot be appreciated on a bone biopsy specimen.
The tumor is composed of epithelial cells in a fibrotic dense stroma,
illustrating the biphasic nature of the adamantinoma (Fig. 14-1). There
may be portions of irregular woven bone rimmed by osteoblasts,
resembling osteofibrous dysplasia. Adamantinoma has been divided
into classic and differentiated (osteofibrous dysplasia–like) types. With
classic adamantinoma, the epithelial component is prominent, and the
osteofibrous dysplasia–like component is less obvious. There are four

epithelial types associated with classic adamantinoma: (1) basaloid,
composed of spindle cells surrounded by palisading basal cells at the
periphery; (2) squamoid, composed of epithelial cell nests with keratinization; (3) tubular, with glandlike spaces lined by cuboidal to flattened cells; and (4) spindle cells arranged in interlacing fascicles,
mimicking fibrosarcoma.1–4 The differentiated adamantinoma is composed of predominantly osteofibrous dysplasia–like areas with irregular woven bone spicules lined by osteoblasts in a fibrous background
with inconspicuous epithelial nests.
The epithelial cells immunoreact with cytokeratins 5 (75%), 14
(100%), 17 (50%), and 19 (100%), similar to basal cells in the normal
epidermis.1–3 The epithelial tumor cells also exhibit epithelial membrane antigen (EMA), podoplanin (D2-40), p63, p53, epidermal
growth factor receptor, and fibroblastic growth factor 2 reactivity. The
presence of p63 reactivity may be helpful in distinguishing metastatic
carcinoma with a sclerotic background from adamantinoma. On ultrastructural examination, the epithelial cells demonstrate desmosomes,
gap junctions, keratin filaments, tonofilaments, microfilaments, and
basal lamina.
The distinction between classic and differentiated adamantinoma
was originally defined based on histopathologic evaluation, and this
has allowed for characterization of these types of tumors based on age,
radiology, histopathology, and behavior characteristics.1–4 Classic types
occur in patients older than 20 years of age, whereas differentiated
types occur in those younger than 20 years of age. Radiologic

A

B

C

D

Figure 14-1. Adamantinoma. A, Predominantly dense fibrous background with embedded basaloid cells. B, Highly cellular spindle cells. C and D, Squamoid epithelial nests associated with keratin formation in a background of basaloid cells.
206

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction
examination of classic types identifies soft tissue or intramedullary
involvement. In contrast, differentiated types are typically intracortical
tumors similar to those seen with osteofibrous dysplasia. Classic-type
tumors tend to be aggressive in behavior, whereas differentiatedtype tumors tend to have an indolent course.
Genetics
Cytogenetic information regarding adamantinoma is sparse due to the
rarity of this tumor.1,2,4 Extra copies of chromosomes 7, 8, 12, 19, and
21 have been reported. A single case with nonrecurring translocations
has been reported [t(1;13;22); t(15;17)]. Gene array and whole exome
or genome analyses have not been published to date.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis for adamantinoma includes aneurysmal
bone cyst, unicameral (solitary) bone cyst, fibrous dysplasia, nonossifying fibroma, chondromyxoid fibroma, giant cell tumor of bone,
eosinophilic granuloma (Langerhans cell histiocytosis), osteomyelitis,
chondrosarcoma, hemangioendothelioma, angiosarcoma, and metastatic carcinoma.1–4 Recently, adamantinoma-like Ewing sarcoma has
been reported but was easily differentiated from adamantinoma using
CD99 membranous staining patterns and the presence of the Ewing
sarcoma breakpoint region 1–Friend leukemia integration 1 (EWSR1FLI1) translocation diagnostic of Ewing sarcoma.5 The primary differential diagnoses are osteofibrous dysplasia, which lacks aggregates
of epithelial cells, and metastatic carcinoma.
Clinical Course
Certain clinical features have been associated with aggressive disease
and include classic-type morphology, male gender, female gender at a
young age, rapid onset of symptoms, pain, lack of squamous differentiation, increased epithelial-to-stroma ratio, and inadequate initial surgical treatment.1–4 Local recurrence may occur many years later (up to
7 years), and metastatic disease may occur even decades after the initial
diagnosis. Local recurrence is reported to range from 18% to 32%, with
metastatic disease ranging from 10% to 30%. This tumor may metastasize to regional lymph nodes and lungs, and less frequently it spreads
to bones, liver, and brain. Mortality rates appear to be approximately
15% after limb salvage resection.
Treatment of adamantinoma is en bloc resection with wide surgical
margins, limb salvage, and limb reconstruction.1,2 These strategies have
resulted in a 10-year survival rate of nearly 90%. Limb amputation has
not shown any improvement in survival compared to en bloc tumor
resection with limb salvage. Radiation therapy and chemotherapy have
not proven to be beneficial. Note that tumor recurrence after nonaggressive surgery may be as high as 90%.

Myeloma
Background
Myeloma (multiple myeloma, plasma cell myeloma) is a clonal B-cell
neoplasm composed of a proliferation of plasma cells within the bone
marrow and extramedullary sites, including cortical bone.6–14 This
entity is the most frequent tumor that occurs primarily in bone.
Myeloma accounts for 10% to 15% of all hematologic malignancies and
is responsible for 20% of its deaths. The disease, first recognized in the
mid-1800s at autopsy, was characterized by soft bones and bone infiltration by large oval cells with prominent nucleoli (plasma cells).
Abnormal proteins were found in the urine of affected patients (Bence
Jones proteins), which almost 100 years later were determined to be
similar to serum light-chain immunoglobulins. With electrophoresis
technique development in the 1930s, it became possible to identify the

proteins and provide a clinical test for detection of Bence Jones proteins in urine and light-chain immunoglobulins in serum. By 1961, it
was possible to distinguish polyclonal and monoclonal gammopathies
and characterize the associated diseases (myeloma, benign monoclonal
gammopathy of undetermined significance, Waldenström macroglobulinemia, and AL amyloidosis).

14

Clinical Features
The majority of patients are in the sixth and seventh decades of life,
and the median age is 65 years.6–8 Less than 10% of cases occur in
patients younger than 40 years of age. There is a slight male predilection for this disease. The incidence in blacks is twice as high as that in
whites. In the United States, 20,000 new cases are diagnosed each year,
with 10,000 deaths per year attributed to this disease. The classic clinical phenotype is bone abnormalities, hypercalcemia, anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, renal dysfunction, recurrent infections, and
peripheral neuropathy. Bone disease occurs in about 60% of cases and
is characterized by lytic lesions. The axial skeleton associated with
hematopoiesis is most often involved; the most common sites of
involvement are the vertebrae, ribs, skull, pelvis, femur, clavicle, and
scapula. Vertebral fractures and compression, osteopenia, and osteoporosis are common and are usually not associated with pain. Hypercalcemia is the most common metabolic abnormality. This is due to
osteolysis and bone resorption with calcium being transported into
extracellular fluids. Parathyroid hormone–related proteins are not
usually elevated in myeloma. Renal dysfunction with renal tubular
calcium reabsorption further contributes to hypercalcemia. Renal
dysfunction occurs in about 20% to 30% of affected individuals at
diagnosis and in more than 50% of patients during the course of
the disease. Accumulation of excess light chains in the kidney is primarily responsible for renal impairment. The interaction of light
chains with Tamm-Horsfall protein results in tubular casts and
obstructive nephropathy (myeloma kidney). Other factors that contribute to renal dysfunction include hypercalcemia, hyperuricemia,
dehydration, nephrotoxic medications, radiologic contrast materials,
and AL-amyloid/light-chain deposition. Deficiencies in antibodymediated and cellular immunity lead to recurrent infections, most
often involving the upper respiratory tract. Peripheral neuropathy is
often present at diagnosis and may be further aggravated by medication toxicity. Extramedullary disease is present in only 1% of cases at
diagnosis and in 8% of cases in later stages of the disease.
The vast majority of patients initially have an asymptomatic premalignant form of the disease: monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined
significance (MGUS).6–13 MGUS is present in 3% of the population by
50 years of age. There is a 1% per year transformation rate from MGUS
to myeloma. Most individuals diagnosed with MGUS have had this
asymptomatic condition for more than 10 years. There is also an
asymptomatic intermediate premalignant form called smoldering
(multiple) myeloma. Individuals affected by this form progress to
classic myeloma at a rate of 10% per year during the first 5 years following diagnosis, then 3% per year over the next 5 years, and 1.5% per
year thereafter.
The diagnosis of myeloma requires (1) >10% plasma cells in the
bone marrow or a biopsy-proven plasmacytoma; (2) serum or urine
monoclonal protein level >3 g/dL; and (3) evidence of end-organ
involvement (hypercalcemia, renal insufficiency, anemia, bone
lesions).6–13 The presence of at least 60% clonal plasma cells in the bone
marrow is diagnostic of myeloma regardless of end-organ damage.
Radiologic Features
In contrast to many metastatic malignancies involving bone that
induce new bone formation, myeloma is characterized by osteolytic
207

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B
Figure 14-2. Myeloma. Total replacement of bone marrow elements by plasma cells.

lesions.6–14 The lytic lesions tend to be sharply demarcated and not
surrounded by a sclerotic rim. Cortical erosion without new periosteal
bone formation is common. Multiloculation is present in about 50%
of lesions. Initial and most severe changes are detected in the skull,
vertebrae, ribs, and pelvis. Generalized osteoporosis with no lytic bone
lesions may be present in 10% to 25% of patients. Small subtle lesions
may be discovered with CT, MRI, and positron emission tomography
(PET) scans. Sclerotic bone lesions may be seen in the clinical condition known as POEMS (polyneuropathy, organomegaly, endocrinopathy, monoclonal gammopathy, skin changes).
Pathologic Features
The bone marrow of the affected bone may show several different
patterns, including interstitial, nodular, focal, obliterative and, less
frequently, sclerotic.6–8,10–14 Most often, the tumor is composed of
typical-appearing plasma cells: ovoid cells with eccentric nuclei with
clock-face condensed chromatin, indistinct nucleoli, and basophilic
cytoplasm (Fig. 14-2). A perinuclear amphophilic zone (hof) corresponds to a prominent Golgi complex with well-developed rough
endoplasmic reticulum on electron microscopic examination. Less frequently, the tumor cells are composed of cells with scant cytoplasm
that resemble small lymphocytes (lymphoplasmacytic or small
lymphocyte-like myeloma). This type of myeloma may be mistaken for
lymphoma; however, the diagnosis of myeloma can be confirmed by
molecular identification of its characteristic translocation [t(11;14)
(q13;q32)]. In less differentiated myeloma, the neoplastic cells may
have a plasmablastic appearance with prominent nucleoli and a high
nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio. Poorly differentiated myeloma may
resemble large cell lymphoma or leukemia. An anaplastic morphology
may occasionally be seen. The plasma cells may contain Russell bodies
(cytoplasmic immunoglobulin globules), Dutcher bodies (intranuclear
inclusions), or rarely Auer rod–like crystals. In about 10% to 15% of
cases, the bone marrow contains deposition of light-chain amyloid.
With numerous cytoplasmic fibrils, myeloma cells may take on an
appearance similar to Gaucher cells. Amyloid may be deposited in
vessel walls, and the supporting tissues as masses between plasma cells.
A foreign body–type giant cell reaction to the amyloid deposition may
be present.
The immunohistochemical profile of myeloma includes the plasmacytic antibodies CD138, CD38, and MUM1. CD117 (30% to 60%),
CD56 (75% to 90%), and CD10 are expressed in neoplastic plasma
cells.6–8,10–14 EMA may be present as well. Myeloma cells express
monoclonal cytoplasmic immunoglobulin by kappa and lambda
208

immunostaining, but surface immunoglobulin is absent. Both heavychain and light-chain immunoglobulins are expressed in 85% of cases.
Light-chain immunoglobulins only are expressed in the remaining
15% of cases. The vast majority of myeloma cells lack CD19 expression.
The lymphoplasmacytic type of myeloma expresses CD20 in up to 30%
of cases.
Genetics
Reciprocal translocations involving the immunoglobulin heavy-chain
locus (IGH 14q32.33) and several other gene partners occur in about
40% of myelomas.6–8,10–14 The translocation gene partners are CCND1
at 11q13, CCND3 at 6p21, MAF at 16q23, FGF3R at 4p16.3, WHSC1/
MM-SET at 4p16.3, and MAFB at 20q11.1. The translocation breakpoints are near the heavy-chain switch regions.
Reduced life span and high-risk disease are associated with immunoglobulin heavy-chain rearrangement (IgHR) translocations t(4;14)
IGH-FGF3R and t(4;16) IGH-MAF, partial loss of chromosome 13 at
q14, complete loss of chromosome 13, and partial loss of chromosome
17 at p13.6–8,10–14 Improved outcome with prolonged survival is associated with hyperdiploidy due to multiple trisomies; polysomy of chromosomes 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15, 19, and 21; low frequency of monosomy;
and the IgHR translocation t(11;14).
Late secondary genetic changes occur in myeloma and several of
these are associated with disease progression.6–8,10–14 C-MYC (8q24)
translocations, activating mutations of NRAS, KRAS, and FGRF3, as
well as inactivating mutations and deletions of TP53, RB1, PTEN,
CDKN2A, and CDKN2C are associated with disease progression.6–8,10–14
Differential Diagnosis
The radiologic differential diagnosis includes metastatic carcinoma,
lymphoma, chronic osteomyelitis, and hyperparathyroidism.6–9 Metastatic carcinoma and lymphoma may be detected on nuclear imaging
bone scans, and myeloma is usually not detected. Biopsy of a radiologically lytic lesion can usually differentiate among these different
entities. Of particular note, plasma cells in chronic osteomyelitis are
polyclonal, whereas plasma cells in myeloma are light-chain restricted
(monoclonal).
Clinical Course
Myeloma is considered to be an incurable disease, with survival
ranging from 6 months to 10 years.6–8,10–14 The median survival is about
3 years, with only 10% survival at 10 years. With solitary plasmacytoma, two thirds of patients progress to myeloma or develop additional

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction
plasmacytoma lesions within a median time of 2 to 3 years. The
remaining one third of solitary plasmacytoma patients remain disease
free for more than 10 years.
With myeloma, survival time is decreased with higher clinical stage,
renal insufficiency, increased percentage of bone marrow infiltration
by tumor cells, an increased proliferative index, and certain karyotypic
alterations.6,8,10,14 Poor prognosis is linked to t(4;14), t(14;16), and
deletion of TP53 (17p13). Disease progression and poor prognosis
are seen with amplification of 1q, loss of 1p, deletion of 13q, and
monosomy 13.
MGUS, smoldering multiple myeloma, and asymptomatic early
stage multiple myeloma are usually simply followed closely clinically
with no treatment.6–8,10–14 With disease-related symptoms or organ
involvement, systemic chemotherapy with melphalan and prednisone
is considered to be the standard of care. Addition of bortezomib, a
ubiquitin-proteasome pathway inhibitor, has been used with a certain
degree of success. The immunomodulators thalidomide and lenalidomide have been used in smoldering multiple myeloma with diminished
bone density due to the plasma cell disorder. Intravenous bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid, pamidronate) are used to lessen bone resorption in smoldering and early stage multiple myeloma. This has not been
shown to affect disease progression or overall survival.
High-dose chemotherapy followed by autologous bone marrow
transplant or reduced intensity conditioning followed by allogeneic
stem cell transplant have shown a certain degree of promise.6–8,10–14
Stem cell transplantation has shown improved progression-free survival, 5-year event-free survival, and overall survival in newly diagnosed myeloma patients. Allogeneic stem cell transplantation has been
restricted to younger patients with a human leukocyte antigen (HLA)–
identical sibling.
Future novel therapeutic targets involved in the pathogenesis of
myeloma have been identified.6,8,10,14 These include the genes KRAS,
RAF1, MAP2K1, PIK3, AKT, JAK, STAT3, PRKC, NFKB, FGFR3, and
WNT.

Mast Cell Disease (Mastocytosis)
Background
Mast cells were first described in 1878 due to cytoplasmic metachromasia of the cells with toluidine blue dye.15–21 Mast cells are derived
from pluripotent bone marrow cells that express CD13, CD34, and
CD117 (C-KIT). These myeloid lineage cells undergo proliferation and
maturation within the body’s tissues and organs. Mast cells are found
throughout the body, but in particular are located around blood
vessels, in well-vascularized tissues, and on surfaces that interface with
the external environment. Mast cells function in innate immunity,
allergic reactions, and autoimmunity, and they have a high affinity for
IgE receptors. Mast cells produce histamine, heparin, proteoglycans
(heparin, chondroitin sulfate), leukotrienes (leukotrienes C4 and B4,
prostaglandin D2), cytokines (interleukins [ILs] 3, 4, 5, and 8; tumor
necrosis factor alpha), and proteases (chymases, tryptase, carboxypeptidase A).
Mast cell diseases are heterogeneous entities that are characterized
by mast cell proliferation in various organ systems.15–21 Cutaneous mastocytosis involves the skin and primarily occurs in infants and children
with spontaneous resolution, usually during puberty. Systemic mastocytosis may involve the bone marrow, liver, spleen, and/or gastrointestinal tract with or without cutaneous involvement. This form of mast
cell disease represents 10% of all mast cell diseases and occurs primarily in adults.
The World Health Organization (WHO) classifies mast cell disease
as a myeloproliferative disorder with seven types of distinct

presentations15–21: (1) cutaneous mastocytosis, (2) indolent systemic
mastocytosis, (3) systemic mastocytosis with associated clonal hematologic non–mast cell lineage, (4) aggressive systemic mastocytosis,
(5) mast cell leukemia, (6) mast cell sarcoma, and (7) extracutaneous
mastocytosis. As with all schemes arrived at by consensus committee,
there is a certain degree of controversy regarding this classification
system.

14

Clinical Features
As noted previously, the clinical phenotype of mast cell disease is quite
variable, and it ranges from an indolent asymptomatic disorder to
progressive tissue destruction and death in extreme cases.15–21 Cutaneous mastocytosis presents with red-brown papules and macules that
are well demarcated and found primarily on the trunk and proximal
extremities (urticaria pigmentosa). Diffuse thickening of the skin, telangiectasia macularis eruptiva perstans, and bullous mastocytosis may
also be seen in the diffuse form. With temperature changes and physical irritation, there may be a flare of urticarial lesions (Darier sign).
Skin biopsy showing mast cell accumulations usually provides the
correct diagnosis.
Systemic mastocytosis tends to involve the bone marrow as well as
truncal and proximal extremity sites.15–21 Extraosseous sites are
involved in about two thirds of patients, with the spleen, lymph nodes,
liver, and gastrointestinal tract mucosa being most often affected. Mast
cell infiltration of these organs may lead to cytopenia, osteoporosis,
pathologic fractures, hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy, and
malabsorption. Symptoms at presentation are associated with the
release of mast cell products and may include edema, flushing, diarrhea, pruritus, dyspepsia, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, musculoskeletal symptoms, fatigue, hypotension, syncope, tachycardia, and
dyspnea. Triggers for a mast cell “attack” include physical exertion,
stress, emotional upset, heat, cold, alcohol ingestion, radiologic contrast, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications, opioids, and
general anesthesia. Anaphylactic episodes occur in 20% to 50% of
patients with systemic mastocytosis. Differentiation from carcinoid
syndrome and symptoms associated with pheochromocytoma/
paraganglioma is necessary.
Radiologic Features
In systemic mastocytosis, the most common findings with bone
involvement are lytic and sclerotic lesions.15–21 The lytic lesions are
secondary to heparin- and prostaglandin-induced bone resorption,
whereas the sclerotic lesions are associated with heparin-induced bone
formation. Bone lesions are found in 70% of cases, and most are of a
diffuse rather than focal nature. Bone scans show four distinct patterns
(normal, focal, multifocal, and diffuse). The lesions may have a mixed
sclerotic-lytic appearance. Osteoporosis is seen in 30% to 40% of
patients.
Pathologic Features
Mastocytosis involving bone is characterized by oval to somewhat
spindled cells with round hyperchromatic nuclei with modest amphophilic to basophilic cytoplasm.15–22 The mast cells may be seen in a
paratrabecular pattern with paratrabecular fibrosis. At times, there
may be intermixed eosinophils. With mast cell disease (Fig. 14-3), mast
cells may be identified based on routine histomorphology, metachromasia of cytoplasmic granules with Giemsa or toluidine blue staining,
or by immunohistochemical reaction with CD117 (C-KIT), CD25,
CD2, or tryptase. Normal mast cells are negative for CD25 but positive
for myeloid markers CD117 and CD33. Bone marrow infiltration
occurs in three patterns: (1) focal accumulation with normal hematopoiesis in uninvolved areas (type I), (2) focal accumulation with
209

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

C
increased granulopoiesis in uninvolved areas (type II), and (3) diffuse
infiltration by atypical mast cells (type III).
Genetics
Mast cell disease is characterized by an activating mutation in C-KIT
in more than 95% of cases.15–21,23 The most common codon involved is
816 (D816V mutation). The C-KIT proto-oncogene is a member of the
tyrosine kinase receptor family and is expressed by mast cells, hematopoietic stem cells, melanocytes, germ cells, and gastrointestinal interstitial (stromal) cells of Cajal. Mast cells have a much greater degree of
expression of C-KIT than other cells. Stem cell factor is a cytokine of
great importance in development and differentiation of mast cells, and
this cytokine interacts with the C-KIT receptor. Gain of function via a
C-KIT mutation results in ligand-independent constitutive activation
of C-KIT signaling, leading to uncontrolled mast cell proliferation via
stem cell factor–independent activation. The C-KIT mutation leads to
resistance to tyrosine kinase inhibitors, such as imatinib.
Several other genetic alterations occur in mast cell disease. Plateletderived growth factor receptor A (PDGFRA) and FIP1L1 fusion occurs
in about 50% of mastocytosis with eosinophilia cases.15–21,23 This entity
has been recently classified by the WHO as a myeloid neoplasm with
eosinophilia, with PDGFRA rearrangement (fusion) between plateletderived growth factor B and PRKG2 also occurring less commonly.
These fusion types are responsive to tyrosine kinase inhibitor
treatment. Recently identified oncogenic mutations in systemic
mastocytosis include the TET-2 oncogene (4q24) and NRAS.
Microphthalmia-associated transcription factor (MITF) is a critical
210

B

Figure 14-3. Mast cell disease. A, Typical-appearing mast cells with total replacement of bone marrow elements. B, Characteristic cytoplasmic metachromasia associated with Giemsa staining of mast cells. C, Mast cells immunoreact intensely in
cytoplasmic pattern with CD117 (C-KIT) antibody.

transcription factor in mast cell development and has been shown to
be upregulated in systemic mastocytosis. Interestingly, MITF expression is regulated by C-KIT.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis is primarily related to separation of mast cell
disease from a benign mast cell reactive proliferation.15–21 As noted
earlier, normal and reactive mast cells lack CD25 expression, allowing
for differentiation. Differentiation of mast cell disease from a myeloid
neoplasm may require molecular analysis for C-KIT mutations and
translocation studies to classify the proliferation as a myeloid neoplasm
with eosinophilia or a PDGFRA rearrangement. Clinical and radiologic
findings are also important in appropriately defining this disease entity.
Clinical Course
The WHO cutaneous and indolent types of mast cell disease typically
have a relatively benign prolonged course with appropriate treatment,
compared with other forms of systemic mastocytosis. Patients with
these lesser forms of mast cell disease typically have a normal life span,
and the lesions very rarely transform to a more aggressive mastocytosis
type.15–21 Of particular importance is correlation of high serum beta
2-microglobulin levels and D816V mutations with disease progression
in patients initially diagnosed with indolent systemic mastocytosis.
Fatality due to complications associated with visceral organ involvement may occur in a minority of cases. Patients with the WHO mast
cell leukemia and mast cell sarcoma succumb to their disease within 1
to 2 years, even with cytoreductive therapy and chemotherapy.

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction
Treatment is individualized and dependent on molecular testing for
the specific C-KIT mutation.15–21 As noted previously, certain C-KIT
mutations are resistant to imatinib (a tyrosine kinase inhibitor). There
are other C-KIT mutations, such as F522C or K509I, that are highly
sensitive to imatinib therapy. Other cytoreductive agents for the treatment of mastocytosis include interferon-alpha, 2-chlorodeoxyadenosine
(cladribine, 2CdA), hydroxyurea, and investigational agents (dasatinib,
midostaurin, masitinib mesylate).

Primary Bone Lymphoma
Background
Secondary bone involvement occurs in about 20% of patients with
lymphoma.24–31 Primary lymphoma of bone represents 5% to 7% of all
malignant bone tumors, less than 1% of all lymphomas, and less than
5% of extranodal lymphomas. Most often, lymphoma involving bone
occurs as a secondary event when extranodal or lymph node sites are
involved or in a patient with known lymphoma. In order to be classified
as primary bone lymphoma, the bone lesion must be a single skeletal
tumor without regional lymph node involvement or multiple bone
lesions without visceral or lymph node involvement. In fact, there
should be no evidence of extraosseous distant disease within 4 to 6
months of the initial diagnosis and no evidence of leukemic involvement of bone. Staging with CT examination of the chest, abdomen,
and pelvis is necessary to exclude primary nodal disease or distant

A

C

extranodal disease. MRI is performed to determine the extent of
disease within the affected bone.

14

Clinical Features
Primary bone lymphoma occurs in older individuals with a median
age of 48 years, with a minority of children and adolescents affected.24–31
This entity affects males more commonly than females; the male-tofemale ratio is 1.5 : 1.0. The typical presentation is bone pain (87%) and
less commonly a palpable mass/swelling (28%), erythema, and pathologic fracture. Nerve compression may also be a presenting symptom.
Hypercalcemia, fever, anemia, fatigue, and lethargy are infrequent. The
mean duration of symptoms is 8.5 months. The most common sites of
involvement include the femur (27%), pelvis (15%), tibia/fibula (13%),
humerus (12%), spine (9%), mandible (2%), radius/ulna (1%), scapula
(1%), skull (1%), and other sites (5%). Bones of the hands and feet are
rarely involved. Lesions may be polyostotic (13%).
Most primary bone lymphomas are sporadic and not associated
with known diseases or cancer predilection syndromes.24–31 These
lesions are rarely reported in human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)
infection, long-standing osteomyelitis, and Paget disease of bone.
Radiologic Features
The appendicular skeleton is more commonly involved than the axial
skeleton (Figs. 14-4 through 14-7).24–31 The bone lesions have a “motheaten” appearance with mixed sclerotic and osteolytic areas. Primary

B

D

Figure 14-4. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. A, Lytic permeative “moth-eaten” lesion involving the proximal femur. B and C, Lymphoma cells with prominent nucleoli in a background of non-neoplastic lymphocytes. D, Diffuse and intense cytoplasmic CD20 (pan-B cell) immunoreactivity within lymphoma cells.
211

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

C

B

D

Figure 14-5. Anaplastic large-cell lymphoma. A, Relatively well-circumscribed mixed sclerotic and lytic lesion with sclerotic border. B and C, Lymphoma cells with lymphohistiocytic
histomorphology with abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm and nuclei with distinct nucleoli in a background of small lymphocytes. D, Lymphoma cells immunoreact intensely in a
cytoplasmic pattern with ALK1 antibody.

osteolytic lesions (72%) are more common than mixed lesions (25%)
and sclerotic lesions (3%). A permeative pattern of bone destruction
is seen in 54% of cases. Most arise in the diaphyseal regions as large
destructive lesions that may erode the cortex and extend into the adjacent soft tissues. A periosteal reaction with onion skinning may be
present. MRI and CT scans are helpful in defining the extent of soft
tissue and bone involvement. Primary lymphomas with a sclerotic
appearance may be mistaken for metastatic cancer with an osteoblastic
reaction or for Paget disease of bone.
Pathologic Features, Genetics, and Differential Diagnosis
The majority of patients with primary bone lymphomas undergo
biopsy procedures, only without resection of the mass or adjacent
bone. In the case of a pathologic fracture, the bone tissue and a considerable portion of the mass may be submitted. The tumor appears as
gray-white fleshy tissue with focal areas of necrosis, similar to nodal
and extranodal lymphoma. Triaging tissue for flow cytometry and
cytogenetic and molecular studies, as well as routine and immunohistochemical analysis, is required.
The vast majority of primary bone lymphomas in adults are diffuse
large B-cell lymphomas (90%), with a small proportion represented by
follicular lymphoma (5%), lymphoblastic lymphoma (1%), anaplastic
large-cell lymphoma (1%), Hodgkin lymphoma (1%), marginal zone
lymphoma (<1%), T-cell lymphoma (<1%), and Burkitt lymphoma
212

(<1%).24–37 With children, lymphoblastic lymphoma represents about
40% of primary bone lymphomas, with diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
still being most common. Because of crush artifact within bone biopsies, it may be necessary to perform additional biopsies in order
to acquire adequate tissue for a definitive diagnosis. The immunophenotypic, genetic, and cytogenetic features of the particular primary
bone lymphomas are identical to those for nodal and extranodal
lymphomas.
Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (see Fig. 14-4) expresses leukocyte
common antigen (LCA, CD45), CD19, CD20, PAX5, CD79a,
CD10 (<50%), and BCL2 (>50%), and lack CD3 and CD5
immunoreactivity.24–26,32,33 This lymphoma in bone has been shown to
have a BCL2 (18q24) breakpoint in 25% of cases, MYC (8q24) breakpoint in 10% of cases, and BCL6 (3q27) breakpoint in some cases.
Anaplastic large-cell lymphoma (see Fig. 14-5) is of T-cell origin,
and it immunoreacts with CD30 (100%), CD2 (45%), CD43 (44%),
CD4 (40%), CD3 (12%), CD8 (5%), EMA (>80%), and ALK (100% in
tumors with an ALK rearrangement).24–26,34,35 Most often, this tumor
also shows an ALK/NPM translocation [t(2;5)]. There are nine other
ALK translocation partners, which occur less commonly (ALK/TPM3,
ALK/TPM4, ALK/TFG, ALK/ATIC, ALK/CLTL, ALK/MSN, ALK/
CARS, ALK/MYH9, and ALK/ALO17). Conventional karyotype cytogenetics and fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH) for ALK gene
rearrangement may be helpful in identifying the less common

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction

translocations. The tumor also has clonal TCR gene rearrangement in
85% to 100% of cases, and a concurrent IgHR in 10% of cases. This
tumor more commonly involves the axial skeleton. In children, the
small-cell variant may be confused with small round cell tumors,
eosinophilic granuloma, and osteomyelitis, and in adults, this variant
may be confused with metastatic tumors, sarcomas, and melanomas.
Of note, ALK may be negative in adult patients, whereas ALK-positive
tumors are more commonly seen in children. In ALK-negative tumors,
EMA and T-cell markers are helpful in establishing the diagnosis.
Lymphoblastic lymphomas (see Fig. 14-6) are T cell in origin in the
vast majority of cases (90%) and immunoreact with CD10, CD43,
CD99, FLI1, and TDT. CD45 may be weakly positive or negative in
this lymphoma.24–26,36,37 Of note, Ewing sarcoma is also positive for
CD99 and FLI1, and it is negative for CD45.
Burkitt lymphomas (see Fig. 14-7) are B cell in origin and express
CD19, CD20, CD22, and surface Ig (IgM most often).24–26 In addition,
follicular center markers are usually positive for CD10, BCL6 and
negative for CD5, BCL2, and TdT. Burkitt lymphoma expresses Ki-67
to an extremely high level (>90%) and may possess the CD21 (C3d/
EBV receptor) with positive EBER-1 by in situ hybridization. EBV-LMP
is universally negative, because Burkitt lymphoma exhibits EBV type
1 latency. This tumor has several tumor-defining translocations associated with MYC at 8q24 [t(8;14)(q24;q32); t(2;8)(p12;q24); t(8;22)
(q24;q11)]. Burkitt lymphoma may be confused with lymphoblastic

lymphoma, small B-cell lymphoma, unclassifiable B-cell lymphomas,
and diffuse large B-cell lymphomas.
Clinical Course
The outcome with primary bone lymphoma is dependent on the specific type of lymphoma.24–29 With primary bone diffuse large B-cell
lymphoma, combined chemotherapy and radiation therapy with the
addition of anti-CD20 regimens (rituximab) has shown an overall
survival of greater than 90%. Older age (>60 years) has been associated
with a poorer prognosis. Axial or appendicular skeletal involvement
does not affect survival.
The overall survival for nodal and extranodal anaplastic large-cell
lymphoma is dependent on the presence or absence of an ALK
rearrangement.24–29,32,33 ALK-positive lymphomas have an 80% 5-year
survival compared with only about 50% for ALK-negative lymphomas.
The overall survival of primary bone anaplastic large-cell lymphoma is
not known because of the rarity of this subtype.
Nodal and extranodal lymphoblastic lymphomas with a T-cell
origin tend to have less favorable outcomes than tumors of B-cell
origin.24–29 Outcomes in children are significantly better than those in
adults. In children, the cure rate is 80%, whereas in adults, the cure
rate is less than 50%.
Nodal and extranodal Burkitt lymphomas of a low stage have cure
rates of up to 80%, and those of a high stage have rates of 60% to 80%
213

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

C

E

with intensive chemotherapy regimens.24–29 Survival for children is
greater than that seen in adults.

Langerhans Cell Histiocytosis
Background
Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH) is a rare disease and accounts for
less than 1% of all primary bone lesions.38–50 The disease was first
described in 1865 in a 4-year-old child with osteolytic lesions of the
skull and skin lesions resembling impetigo. From 1892 to 1919,
214

B

D

Figure 14-7. Burkitt lymphoma. A, Proximal femur with an ill-defined lytic lesion,
loss of the cortex, and pathologic fracture. B and C, Characteristic “starry sky” pattern
ascribed to Burkitt lymphoma with a monotonous lymphomatous infiltrate comprised
of medium-sized cells with readily identified tingible body macrophages. D, Diffuse
nuclear immunoreactivity with Ki-67 (MIB-1), indicative of an extremely high proliferative fraction in Burkitt lymphoma. E, EBER-1 (EBV) in situ hybridization demonstrating
nuclear reactivity within the lymphoma cells.

Hand-Schüller-Christian disease was characterized by exophthalmos,
polyuria, polydipsia, osteomalacia, and osteolytic skull defects in
several reports. In 1924 and 1933, Letterer-Siwe disease was reported
as a fulminant nonleukemic disorder with splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, lymphadenopathy, bone tumors, hemorrhagic diathesis, anemia,
and proliferation of nonlipidized histiocytes. In 1930, eosinophilic
granuloma was initially described as a rare, unknown storage disease,
and in 1940, it was characterized as a viral granulomatous disease that
destroyed bone. During the 1950s, these three entities were considered
to be the same disease process with a variable degree of severity and

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction
became known as histiocytosis X. With identification of Langerhans
cell histiocytes by electron microscopy and immunohistochemical
means, the current name, Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH), was
used. Some practitioners still use the original disease designations—
Hand-Schüller-Christian disease, Letterer-Siwe disease, and eosinophilic granuloma—to describe the clinical presentation of LCH. It is
now known that the cell of origin is a dendritic cell, which has recently
been shown to share a distinct gene expression profile with bone
marrow–derived immature myeloid dendritic cells that recruit activated lymphocytes.
Clinical Features
LCH occurs usually before 20 years of age, although the age range
spans from the neonatal period through the eighth decade of life.38–50
More than 75% of cases occur before 30 years of age. Disseminated
LCH occurs in the first 2 years of life. There is a male predilection, with
a male-to-female ratio of 2 : 1. Bone (80% of cases) and skin are the
most common sites involved, but any organ system may be affected,
including the lung, liver, spleen, bone marrow, lymph nodes, and pituitary gland. Although any bone may be involved, the most common
site is the skull (calvarium, craniofacial, 50%), with other frequent
bony sites including the femur (20%), pelvis (12%), scapula (12%),
vertebrae (10%), distal limbs (5%) and mandible. With adults, ribs may
be frequently involved. Monostotic disease is three to four times more
common than polyostotic disease. LCH is typically classified as single
system disease at a single or multiple sites or a multisystem disease.
With multisystem disease, the most severe cases occur before 2 years
of age.
The conventional clinical classification is often used by
practitioners.38–45 Eosinophilic granuloma usually affects patients older
than 30 years of age with a solitary lesion involving the craniofacial
bones, vertebrae, rib, pelvis or tubular long bones. Hand-SchüllerChristian disease typically affects children younger than 3 years of age
with multifocal skeletal lesions involving the craniofacial bones. The
classic triad is lytic skull lesions, exophthalmos, and diabetes insipidus.
Letterer-Siwe disease usually affects children younger than 2 years of
age with multifocal skeletal and extraskeletal lesions. There is massive
visceral organ involvement, including lymph nodes, lungs, skin, and
bone marrow.
The most common presenting symptom is pain and swelling of the
involved bone or soft tissue.38–45 With skin lesions, the children may
present with a skin rash mimicking eczema or a superficial fungal
infection in a diaper-rash distribution. Umbilicated skin lesions resembling molluscum contagiosum may occur. Papulosquamous skin
lesions often involve the scalp and may be confused with seborrhea or
cradle cap. With mandibular involvement, there may be loosening
(floating) of the teeth and gingival recession. Temporal bone involvement may be mistaken for otitis media and mastoiditis. Vertebral
lesions may result in compression fractures with neurologic symptoms.
Musculoskeletal pain may be associated with vertebral, pelvic, and long
bone lesions.
Radiologic Features
LCH tends to involve the medullary space as relatively well-delineated
osteolytic lesions with periosteal new bone formation in long bones
(Fig. 14-8A).38–45 The lesions are often round to ovoid, and they are
usually between 1 to 2 cm in the largest dimension, with some lesions
being up to 6 cm, especially in long bones. Skull lesions are described
as a “hole in a hole” (punched out) because of the differential rate of
osteolysis of the two osseous tables of the skull. The mandibular lesions
show osteolytic lesions with the teeth appearing to be floating within
the lytic area. The radiologic features in LCH may mimic osteomyelitis

and Ewing sarcoma. The healing process in LCH may be indicated by
progressive sclerosis of the adjacent bone.

14

Pathologic Features
The hallmark is the Langerhans cell histiocyte (see Fig. 14-8B–D)
embedded in a background of lymphocytes, eosinophils, neutrophils,
plasma cells, and typical histiocytes.38–45 The number of Langerhans cell
histiocytes may vary from infrequent to abundant. These cells are
characterized by reniform, grooved, or folded oval nuclei with abundant lightly eosinophilic cytoplasm. These cells lack cytoplasmic projections. There may be giant cells present, which represent a merger of
Langerhans cell histiocytes, and these have nuclei with the same
nuclear features as the mononuclear Langerhans cell histiocytes.
Osteoclast-like giant cells may also be seen.
Langerhans cell histiocytes immunoreact with CD207 (langerin;
see Fig. 14-8E), CD1a, S-100 protein, and CD68. CD207 and CD1a are
more specific for LCH, and S-100 protein and CD68 are nonspecific
findings. Other immunomarkers that may be positive in LCH include
CD4, CD11c, and HLA-DR. CD45, CD15, CD30, EMA, and cytokeratins are nonreactive with Langerhans cell histiocytes. By electron
microscopy, pentalaminar structures (Birbeck granules; see Fig. 14-8F)
are identified within the cytoplasm of Langerhans cell histiocytes and
at times resemble a “tennis racket” morphologically with a terminal
bulbous end.
Genetics
There is controversy regarding whether LCH is a clonal proliferation
or a reactive process driven by a “cytokine” storm.38–41,45–47,51–55 Langerhans cell histiocytes have failed to show consistent recurrent cytogenetic abnormalities in several studies and are diploid cells. Isolated
studies have reported recurrent abnormalities with 9q and 22q and loss
of 1p. However, reactive lesions have shown similar chromosomal
abnormalities. Certain studies have demonstrated clonality by
X-chromosome inactivation in nonpulmonary LCH. Mutational analysis for TP53 and MDM2 has detected no mutations in LCH.
Of interest is the finding of V600E BRAF mutations in almost 60%
of LCH lesions51–55; proliferating normal Langerhans cells lack this
BRAF mutation. BRAF functions in the signaling cascade associated
with activation of a tyrosine kinase receptor and plays a role in the
phosphorylation activation of the RAS-RAF-MEK pathway. The presence of this V600E BRAF mutation provides a potential treatment
avenue for tyrosine kinase inhibitors in LCH lesions with this BRAF
mutation.
The most recent model for LCH pathogenesis involves bone
marrow–derived myeloid dendritic cell precursors that migrate to the
sites of LCH lesions and differentiate into CD207-positive Langerhans
cell histiocytes.38–41,46,47,51 In this model, blood-derived myeloid precursors are recruited to the lesion site, where they accumulate and recruit
activated T cells. Both the myeloid precursors and activated T cells
produce mediators of inflammation and tissue destruction. It is not
certain whether LCH arises from transformed clonal populations of
precursor cells or is due to a functional response to a pathologic stimulus. Lack of differential expression of proliferation markers supports
the concept that CD207-positive LCH cells arise from the accumulation of “pathologic” cells. By gene expression analysis, it has been
shown that CD207-positive LCH cells play a role in lymphocyte activation, recruitment, and dendritic cell interaction. The overexpressed
genes in LCH involved in this process are osteopontin (SPP1),
neuropilin-1, and vanin-1. Of interest, CD44 is overexpressed by LCH
cells and is a major receptor for osteopontin, leading to autocrine
stimulation. Neuropilin-1 is a membrane-bound coreceptor with
homology to a tyrosine kinase receptor for vascular endothelial growth
215

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C

D

E

F

Figure 14-8. Langerhans cell histiocytosis. A, Intramedullary lytic lesion involving the femur. B, Admixture of Langerhans cell histiocytes with reniform to grooved nuclei and
abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm within a background of eosinophils and lymphocytes. Note the presence of giant cells with nuclei with similar histomorphology as Langerhans
cell histiocytes. C and D, Langerhans cell histiocytes with grooved (C) and reniform (D) nuclei and abundant eosinophilic cytoplasm readily identified. E, CD207 intensely and diffusely reacts in the cytoplasm of Langerhans cell histiocytes. F, Pentalaminar structures (Birbeck granules) in Langerhans cell histiocytes by electron microscopy.

factor and semaphorin. Neuropilin-1 is an important factor in establishing T cell–dendritic cell synapses, as well as migration and adhesion of thymocytes. Vanin-1 is essential in migration of hematopoietic
progenitor cells to the thymus. The overexpression of osteopontin
(SPP1), neuropilin-1, and vanin-1 in CD207-positive LCH cells
strongly suggests a role in recruitment of T cells to LCH lesions, lymphocyte trafficking, and lymphocyte–dendritic cell interaction.
216

Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis for LCH includes osteomyelitis; granulomatous inflammation; parasitic and fungal infections; Hodgkin lymphoma with a mixed eosinophil, lymphocyte, and plasma cell
population; and non-Hodgkin lymphoma.38–47 Other histiocytic disorders such as Rosai-Dorfman disease, Erdheim-Chester disease, and
early phase juvenile xanthogranuloma are also considerations.

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction
Clinical Course
Single LCH lesions may resolve either spontaneously or with curettage
or intralesional steroid administration.38–50 Low-dose radiation for
localized bone involvement is restricted to optic nerve or spinal core
compression. Patients with multifocal bone disease, severe pain, or
restricted mobility may require systemic chemotherapy (prednisone,
vinblastine), indomethacin, and bisphosphonates. Chemotherapy is
often recommended for craniofacial bone lesions associated with an
increased risk of developing diabetes insipidus. Multisystem LCH has
several oncologic protocols that have been developed by the LCH–
Working Group of the Histiocyte Society. These treatment protocols
have shown promising results, using multiagent chemotherapy, immunomodulating drugs and antibodies, and in some instances bone
marrow transplantation. Reactivation of disease may occur in 10% of
monostotic LCH cases and in 25% of polyostotic LCH cases.
The overall mortality with LCH is approximately 10%.38–50 Single
system disease has an excellent prognosis. With multisystem disease,
the involvement of bone marrow, liver, spleen, and lungs is associated
with a poor prognosis. The majority of deaths are seen in disseminated
disease in children younger than 2 years of age.

with a male-to-female ratio of 3 : 1. There is no known racial
predilection.
Patients afflicted with ECD develop characteristic multifocal osteosclerotic lesions of the long bones. Involvement of extraskeletal sites,
including the pituitary gland, orbit, retroperitoneum, kidneys, heart,
lung, and central nervous system, may also occur.56–62 The manifestations of this xanthogranulomatous disease consist of bone pain for
months to years (40% to 50%), diabetes insipidus (25%), exophthalmos
(25%), orbital fibrosis, retroperitoneal fibrosis with associated perirenal (68%) and ureteral obstruction with hydronephrosis, xanthelasma
(19% to 28%), interstitial lung disease (22% to 43%), central nervous
system symptoms resembling multiple sclerosis (15% to 51%), testicular infiltration, and cardiovascular (42% to 66%) infiltration with
cardiac failure. Fever, weight loss, weakness, and soft tissue swelling
may also be present. The clinical phenotype varies in severity from
asymptomatic bone lesions to debilitating and life-threatening multiorgan involvement, with a poor prognosis leading to demise (26% to
60%).

Background
Erdheim-Chester disease (ECD) was first described in 1930 as lipoid
granulomatosis; it is a rare disease, with fewer than 500 cases reported
in the literature.56–62 ECD is a systemic disease characterized by xanthogranulomatous lesions that involve long bones, soft tissues, visceral
organs, and the central nervous system. This disease leads to osteosclerosis, fibrosis, and organ failure. The diagnosis is difficult to render due
to lack of definitive diagnostic criteria. Most diagnoses are made based
on the combination of radiologic and histopathologic features as well
as correlation with clinical signs and symptoms. In the past, ECD has
also been referred to as lipogranulomatosis, lipid granulomatosis, and
polyostotic sclerosing histiocytosis. With increasing knowledge of this
entity and familiarity with its clinical features by pathologists, radiologists, and clinicians, increased numbers of patients are being appropriately diagnosed with ECD.

Radiologic Features
The characteristic skeletal finding of ECD is bilateral symmetrical
osteosclerosis of the metaphyseal and diaphyseal regions of long
bones in the lower extremities, with epiphyseal sparing.57–62 The distal
femur, as well as the proximal tibia and fibula, are often involved.
Ribs, vertebrae, and craniofacial bones are infrequently involved.
There is diffuse medullary fibrosis with obliteration of the distinction
between the medullary space and cortex. Also, lytic lesions occur in
about 33% of patients. Periostitis and endostitis are present in 50% of
cases. Bone marrow infarction may be present. On MRI, there are
generalized marrow signal abnormalities with high T2-intensity and
low T1-intensity signals. Nuclear bone scans show increased uptake in
affected regions of the bone. Imaging features that are highly suggestive
of ECD are constant trace uptake of long bones by technetium-99m
(96%), ultrasound changes demonstrating perirenal infiltration (68%),
and a “hairy kidney” appearance on CT (50%). Orbital, dural, periaortic, mediastinal, and retroperitoneal lesions may be seen as soft tissue
lesions on CT scans. There may also be pulmonary infiltrates with
pleural, septal, intralobular, and peribronchial thickening.

Clinical Features
ECD develops most commonly during the fifth to seventh decades of
life, with a mean age of 55 years. Fewer than 10 cases have been
described in the pediatric population. There is a male predilection,

Pathologic Features
On gross examination of a bone biopsy, the tissue has a xanthomatous
appearance with tan to brown tissue with yellow areas. Histopathologic
examination shows numerous foamy histiocytes (Fig. 14-9) often

Erdheim-Chester Disease

A

14

B
Figure 14-9. Erdheim Chester disease. Numerous histiocytes with vacuolated (A) to foamy (B) cytoplasm admixed with lymphocytes.
217

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
embedded in a fibrotic background. Intermixed lymphocytes, plasma
cells, and typical histiocytes may be seen.57–62 On occasion, multinucleated Touton giant cells may be present. The bony trabeculae are thickened and formed by woven and lamellar bone. The immunocytochemical
profile has a xanthogranulomatous pattern with the foamy histiocytes
immunoreacting with factor XIIIa, CD68, CD163, CD14, and fascin,
and they are weak to negative for S-100 protein and negative for both
CD1a and CD207 (langerin). Long-standing lesions may lose reactivity
with factor XIIIa and fascin. Electron microscopy has failed to identify
pentalaminar structures (Birbeck granules) in histiocytes from ECD
lesions. Lysosomes, sparse organelles, and numerous cytoplasmic lipid
inclusions are seen, attesting to the xanthomatous nature of the histiocytes. Considering the histomorphologic, immunocytochemical
profile, and ultrastructural features, ECD appears to be a member of
the juvenile xanthogranuloma family of histiocytosis.
Genetics
ECD has now been shown to be (1) a clonal disease by cytogenetics
and by X-chromosome inactivation analysis using the human androgen receptor (HUMARA) assay and (2) polyclonal by nested polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for polymorphism for phosphoglycerate
kinase and androgen receptors in a limited number of patients.56,62–64
At the present time, controversy exists regarding whether ECD is a
monoclonal neoplasm or a polyclonal reactive histiocytic hyperplasia.
Of interest is the recent finding of V600E BRAF mutations in 47% to
51% of ECD patients.65,66 LCH lesions have a similar proportion of
cases with V600E BRAF mutations, which may imply a shared progenitor cell of origin for ECD and LCH. BRAF mutations have not been
identified in circulating blood monocytes.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes LCH, sinus histiocytosis with
massive lymphadenopathy (Rosai-Dorfman Disease), Gaucher disease,
and chronic osteomyelitis.57–62 LCH may be distinguished by the presence of (1) histiocytes with grooved and reniform nuclei that immunoreact with CD1a and CD207 (langerin) and (2) pentalaminar
structures (Birbeck granules) on electron microscopy. Also, LCH has
lytic lesions in contrast to bilateral, symmetrical osteosclerotic lesions
characteristic of ECD. Sinus histiocytosis with massive lymphadenopathy (Rosai-Dorfman disease) is characterized by lytic bone lesions and
histiocytes with readily identified emperipolesis and S-100 protein
immunoreactivity. Foamy histiocytes in ECD are negative for S-100
protein. Gaucher disease is a storage disorder with characteristic histiocytes with a “wrinkled tissue paper” cytoplasm appearance and
cytoplasm positive for periodic acid–Schiff stain. Imaging shows osteoporotic lesions and an “Erlenmeyer flask” appearance of the affected
bones. Bone sclerosis may occur with infarction. Osteomyelitis of a
chronic nature may be mistaken for ECD; however, clinical, radiologic,
histopathologic, and microbiologic findings eliminate this from the
differential diagnosis.
Clinical Course
In the past, the majority (60%) of patients died from their disease
within 3 years of presentation, with a mean survival of 19 months.56–62
However, with the implementation of interferon-α therapy, mortality
is now 26% with a 5-year survival of 68%. Interferon-α has now
become the primary therapy and is known to induce terminal differentiation in certain histiocytes and dendritic cells. This therapy is effective because ECD is associated with TH1 activation of the immune
system with markedly increased levels of interferon-α; the interleukins
IL-1, IL-1RA, IL-6, and IL-12; and monocyte chemotactic protein-1
(MCP-1). It is necessary to use interferon-α on a long-term basis,
218

which may lead to side effects such as depression, fatigue, and development of autoimmune antibodies. Treatment of ECD with recombinant
IL-1Rα has shown promise in clinical trials. Also, cladribine
(2-chlorodeoxyadenosine) for central nervous system symptoms in
cases of ECD refractory to interferon-α has been used with improvement in symptoms. Chemotherapy and corticosteroids, used in LCH
and other histiocytic disorders, have not proven to be an effective treatment for ECD. Additional future targets may be tumor necrosis factor
α, IL-6, IL-8 (chemoattractant for neutrophils and monocytes), and
BRAF inhibitors. Central nervous system disease and widespread systemic involvement are independent predictors of mortality in ECD.

Sinus Histiocytosis with Massive
Lymphadenopathy (Rosai-Dorfman Disease)
Background
Sinus histiocytosis with massive lymphadenopathy (SHML [RosaiDorfman disease]) is a rare non-neoplastic proliferative histiocytic
disorder of unknown etiology, which was first described in 1965.67–74
It was recognized as a distinct entity on review of “malignant reticuloendotheliosis” cases. Various infectious agents and immunologic disorders have been postulated as the etiologic agents but have yet to be
proven.
Clinical Features
SHML most commonly affects children and young adults, with a
median age of 27 years (range, 3 to 56 years).67–74 There is an equal
gender predilection. Asians are less commonly affected, with whites
and blacks equally affected. The classic presentation of SHML is usually
painless cervical lymphadenopathy with fever, malaise, weight loss,
anemia, leukocytosis, polyclonal hypergammaglobulinemia, and an
elevated sedimentation rate. Fever or pharyngitis may herald the onset
of the disorder. Cervical lymph nodes are involved in up to 90% of
cases; other commonly involved nodal sites include axillary, paraaortic, inguinal, and mediastinal locations. Extranodal involvement
has been shown to occur in 43% of cases. Of interest, about 25% of
cases have only extranodal involvement, and nodal disease only occurs
in about 33% of cases. The extranodal sites involved are the skin and
soft tissue (16%); nasal cavity and paranasal sinuses (16%); orbit, eye,
and ocular adnexa (11%); bone (11%); salivary gland (7%); central
nervous system (7%); oral cavity (4%); kidney and genitourinary tract
(3%); respiratory tract (3%); liver (1%); tonsil (<1%); breast (<1%);
gastrointestinal tract (<1%); and heart (<1%). Multiple extranodal and
nodal sites may be involved. Because of the diversity of tissues that are
affected, clinical presentation may be quite varied and lead to a workup
for other diseases.
Rheumatologic symptoms, such as polyarthralgia and rheumatoid
arthritis, may also be present.67–74 Rheumatoid factor, antinuclear antibodies, and reversal of the CD4-to-CD8 ratio may be noted. Antibodies to human herpesvirus 6 (HHV6) and Epstein-Barr virus (EBV)
have been reported in the serum of SHML patients. Additionally, glomerulonephritis, asthma, and diabetes mellitus may also occur in association with SHML. A small proportion of SHML patients may have
concurrent non-Hodgkin lymphoma, histiocytic disorders, myeloma,
melanoma, or carcinoma.
Primary SHML of bone is rare, and fewer than 50 cases are reported
in the literature.67–73 The age range (1.5 to 63 years) and mean age (28
years) are similar to SHML in general. The sites of primary bone
disease reported include the radius, femur, skull, tibia, fibula, maxilla,
thoracic spine, ilium, sacrum, metacarpals, phalanx, talus, clavicle, and
triquetrum. The most frequent sites are the long bones. Presenting
symptoms include pain and/or swelling at the site of bone involvement.

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction

14

A

B

C

D

Figure 14-10. Sinus histiocytosis with massive lymphadenopathy (Rosai-Dorfman disease). A, Lytic lesions with sclerotic borders that involve the epiphyseal plate and the medullary space. B, Histiocytes with abundant slightly eosinophilic, finely granular cytoplasm in a background of chronic inflammatory cells. C, Readily identified histiocytes demonstrating
emperipolesis. D, Diffuse S-100 protein immunoreactivity.

With spinal and sacral lesions, the tumor may extend into the spinal
canal and lead to neurologic symptoms.
Radiologic Features
With primary bone lesions, the affected areas are usually located in
the medullary cavity with ill-defined borders and a lytic character
(Fig. 14-10).67–69,72 The lesions may be located at any site within the
bone (epiphyseal, metaphyseal, or diaphyseal). Sclerosis and welldefined lesions may be seen in long-standing, healing, or regressing
lesions. Cortical destruction may be present with an associated periosteal reaction. The lesions may be solitary (80%) or multifocal (20%).
Pathologic fracture is unusual but may occur.
Pathologic Features
The histopathologic features of osseous SHML are identical to those at
nodal and extranodal sites (see Fig. 14-10).67–73 There is marrow space
cellular infiltration by an admixture of histiocytes, lymphocytes,
plasma cells, and infrequent eosinophils and neutrophils. The histiocyte population includes markedly enlarged histiocytes with an abundant amphophilic to slightly eosinophilic cytoplasm. These histiocytes
have nuclei that vary from round to oval to reniform, with fine to
vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli. The cytoplasm of these
enlarged histiocytes is vacuolated and demonstrates the hallmark
emperipolesis, characterized by intracytoplasmic lymphocytes, plasma

cells, and neutrophils that are intact and appear to be passing through
the cytoplasm of the histiocyte. These cells do not undergo degradation
during their transit through the cytoplasm. Histocytes vary in number
and distribution and may be absent in some areas, causing a problem
in providing an appropriate diagnosis on a limited biopsy. It should be
noted that microabscesses and necrosis with associated small histiocytes mimicking granulomas may be present and raise a concern for
infection.
The characteristic immunocytochemical feature of the enlarged
histiocytes in SHML is cytoplasmic reactivity with S-100 protein (see
Fig. 14-10), indicative of its interdigitating dendritic cell origin.67–73
Additional histiocytic immunomarkers with SHML histiocytes include
CD68, HAM56, CD163, CD14, CD64, CD15, and EBM11. SHML histiocytes are negative for CD1a, CD207 (langerin), CD21, CD23, and
CD35. It has been noted that a subset of SHML exhibits features of
IgG4-related disease, with plasma cells expressing IgG4.75,76 In as many
as 30% of SHML cases, there are variable degrees of sclerosis with an
increased proportion of IgG4-expressing plasma cells.
Genetics
No definitive genetic alterations are detected to date with SHML. There
are certain factors that have been implicated in SHML pathogenesis.67–72
Infectious etiologic agents, such as HHV6, EBV, and parvovirus, have
been suspected. HHV6-specific DNA and ORF1 HHV6 protein have
219

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
been identified within histiocytes in SHML lesions. In addition, HHV6specific p101K antigen has been detected in dendritic cells in SHML.
Elevated EBV serology has been reported in some SHML cases, but
EBV latent and lytic factors have not been discovered in SHML
histiocytes.
Cases of SHML associated with autoimmune lymphoproliferative
disease have been reported.67–72 Such autoimmune lymphoproliferative
diseases involve inherited disorders with defective lymphocyte programmed cell death (apoptosis). Murine models of defective apoptosis
with lymphoproliferative disease exist that may allow for in-depth
study of this possible pathogenetic mechanism in SHML, specifically
in determining if an acquired apoptosis disorder leading to dysregulation of signaling pathways involved in apoptosis is associated with
SHML.
As noted previously, a substantial subset of SHML is associated with
features of IgG4-related disease.75,76 IgG4 disease is characterized by an
abundant number of plasma cells that express IgG4, with associated
tissue sclerosis and elevated IgG4 serum levels. This particular disease
is thought to represent a disorder that affects regulation of IgG4 expression by type 2 CD4 T lymphocytes and regulatory T cells. This leads
to increased cytokine expression (IL-4, IL-5, IL-10, IL-13, transforming growth factor-beta), and lymphoproliferative dysregulation. Additional investigation is necessary to determine if IgG4 expression plays
a role in SHML pathogenesis.
Differential Diagnosis
The radiologic differential diagnosis includes metastatic disease, osteomyelitis, eosinophilic granuloma (LCH), ECD, giant cell tumor of
bone, lymphoma, fibrous dysplasia, chondromyxoid fibroma, enchondroma, tuberculosis, myeloma, sarcoidosis, and Ewing sarcoma.67–74
The histopathologic differential diagnosis may include reactive histiocytosis, LCD, histiocytic sarcoma, metabolic storage disorders,
Hodgkin lymphoma, osteomyelitis, and metastatic disease with a
prominent histiocytic response.
Clinical Course
Most patients with classic SHML have complete and spontaneous
remission of their disease, and a minority of patients have recurrent or
persistent disease.67–69,72 With infrequent to rare cases, the disease
follows an aggressive course leading to death. Certain sites of disease
(liver, lower respiratory tract, kidney) and multiple site involvement
are associated with worse outcome. Other poor prognostic factors
include widespread disseminated disease, immunologic abnormalities,
and anemia.
Isolated primary bone disease may be treated surgically by curettage or resection with a good outcome.67–69,72 No recurrences over a
12- to 18-month period have been reported in about 60% of primary
osseous SHML patients following local surgical treatment. However,
40% of patients develop extraosseous disease.

to therapy among the subtypes. A distinct Ewing sarcoma is the malignant small round cell tumor of thoracopulmonary region (Askin
tumor). This tumor arises from the chest wall and may also involve
the lung.
Clinical Features
Ewing sarcoma accounts for 5% to 8% of primary malignant bone
tumors.77–83 Osteosarcoma and chondrosarcoma are more frequent in
adults, whereas osteosarcoma is more common in children and adolescents. The prevalence of Ewing sarcoma is 2.93 cases per 1,000,000.
The majority of tumors occur in individuals between 10 and 15 years
of age; 80% of patients are younger than 20 years of age. Ewing sarcoma
is uncommon after 30 years of age. Males are more commonly affected
than females (male-to-female ratio, 1.4 : 1). This tumor occurs rarely in
blacks, Asians, and Native Americans (prevalence, 0.2 per 1,000,000),
with more than 90% to 95% of cases affecting whites.
Most often, the tumor involves osseous sites (80% to 90%), with
extraosseous sites representing 10% to 20% of cases. Both tubular long
bones and flat bones (ilium, pelvis, and ribs [Askin tumor]) may be
tumor sites. The femur is most often affected, followed by the ilium,
tibia, humerus, fibula, and ribs. Sites that are less often involved include
the skull, vertebrae, scapula, and short tubular bones of hands and feet.
The diaphysis or metaphysis is the site of origin in tubular long bones.
Pain is the presenting symptom in more than 90% of cases, with or
without a mass. Fever, weight loss, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation
rate, leukocytosis, and anemia may also be presenting symptoms; these
findings may lead to an errant clinical diagnosis of osteomyelitis.
Pathologic fracture occurs in about 20% of cases.
Radiologic Features
Imaging studies (Fig. 14-11A and B) show an ill-defined extensive
lesion that most often appears to involve the entire bone, often with
adjacent soft tissue edema.77–84 The tumor has a permeative, destructive
character with variable osteolytic and osteosclerotic “moth-eaten”
areas. There is often a multilayered neocortex with a periosteal reaction
(onion-skinning, sunburst pattern). Most osseous tumors have an
associated soft tissue mass, which is readily identified on MRI or CT
studies (see Fig. 14-11B). The extraosseous soft tissue component may

Ewing Sarcoma
Background
This tumor was first described by James Ewing in 1921 as endothelioma of bone, and it was later renamed Ewing sarcoma.77–83 Since that
time, this tumor has been proven to be of neuroectodermal origin, with
variation in the histomorphology, immunophenotype, and ultrastructure based on the degree of neuroectodermal differentiation. The
current classification, the Ewing family of tumors, is composed of
classic Ewing sarcoma (undifferentiated), atypical Ewing sarcoma
(poorly differentiated), and peripheral neuroectodermal tumor (PNET;
differentiated). There is no difference in clinical outcome or response
220

A

B

Figure 14-11. Ewing sarcoma, radiologic appearance. Permeative mixed osteolytic
and osteoblastic lesion of the distal femur with a soft tissue reaction.

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction
result in “saucerization” (erosion) of the outer cortex, with impending
pathologic fracture. The tumor has low signal intensity on T1-weighted
MRI and high signal intensity on T2-weighted MRI. These studies also
assist in identifying the extent of intramedullary involvement. Radionucleotides have a high avidity for the tumor. Following oncologic
management, residual tumor assessment for tumor necrosis is assessed
optimally with MRI.

Pathologic Features
The Ewing family of tumor is comprised of three subtypes based on
histomorphology and to a certain extent on the expression of neuroectodermal markers by immunohistochemistry (Figs. 14-12 through
14-16).77–84 Classic (undifferentiated) Ewing sarcoma accounts for 65%
to 75% of all Ewing sarcomas and is composed of small round cells
that are closely apposed and lack intervening stroma (see Fig. 14-12A).

A

B

C

D

14

E

E

Figure 14-12. Ewing sarcoma, histomorphology. A, Classic pattern with small round
cells with bland dispersed chromatin and closely apposed tumor cells with no evident
supporting stroma. Note the “dark” and “light” tumor cell pattern. B, Atypical pattern
with increased cell and nuclear size, cytologic atypia, and readily identified nucleoli.
C, Vague rosette pattern associated with peripheral neuroectodermal differentiation.
Note the denser chromatin and presence of nucleoli. D, Membranous CD99 antibody
immunoreactivity associated with Ewing sarcoma. E, Diffuse nuclear FLI1 antibody
immunoreactivity.

small round cell tumor embedded in dense connective tissue (top) and diffuse immunoreactivity with CD99 (bottom).

membranous pattern by ghostlike tumor cells.

Figure 14-15. Metastatic Ewing sarcoma to lung. A, Lung nodule on computed tomography (arrow). B, Small round cell tumor consistent with metastatic Ewing sarcoma.
