Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction

A

B

C

D

Figure 14-16. Ewing sarcoma, method for evaluation of tumor necrosis following
oncologic management. A, Gross appearance of proximal tibial and proximal fibula
head following limb salvage procedure. B, Band saw longitudinal section of limb salvage
specimen following removal of soft tissue. C and D, Mapping of limb salvage specimen
for evaluation of tumor necrosis.

There are occasional capillary-sized vessels seen between sheets of
tumor cells. The tumor cells may have a “light” cell and “dark” cell
appearance with minimal slightly eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm and round nuclei with dispersed chromatin and inconspicuous
nucleoli. Mitotic figures are rare. Necrosis may be minimal to extensive
in a geographic pattern.
Atypical (poorly differentiated) Ewing sarcoma (see Fig. 14-12B)
accounts for 15% to 20% of all tumors. This subtype has a variable
histomorphology but typically has increased cell size, somewhat irregular and large nuclei, and more prominent nucleoli. Mitotic figures
are seen occasionally. Some tumors may have abundant eosinophilic
cytoplasm, mimicking rhabdoid cells. The atypical subtype usually
is organized into sheets of tumor cells; however, other patterns
may be identified, such as adamantinoma-like, spindled cell,
hemangiopericytoma-like, and sclerosing.
The PNET (differentiated) tumor subtype represents 10% to 15% of
Ewing sarcomas (see Fig. 14-12C). It displays typical Ewing sarcoma
cells with the formation of rosette structures.
Both atypical and PNET subtypes express neuroectodermal
markers by immunohistochemistry, such as chromogranin and
synaptophysin, whereas classic Ewing sarcoma lacks this feature.

Immunohistochemical staining for membranous CD99 (see Fig.
14-12D) in 99% of cases and nuclear FLI1 (see Fig. 14-12E) in 75% of
cases support the diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma. However, it must be
realized that membranous CD99 is not specific to Ewing sarcoma and
may be seen in lymphoblastic lymphoma, poorly differentiated synovial sarcoma, certain rhabdomyosarcomas, desmoplastic small round
cell tumor, small-cell osteosarcoma, mesenchymal chondrosarcoma,
myeloid sarcoma, and neuroendocrine tumors. Lymphoblastic lymphoma also expresses FLI1, and it is usually negative for CD45. Of note
is that Ewing sarcoma may express cytokeratin in 20% to 40%, neurofilament protein in 20%, and desmin in less than 5% of tumors.
Electron microscopy may also be of assistance in diagnosis when a
tumor has a confusing immunophenotype. Classic Ewing sarcoma
shows tumor cells that are closely apposed with glycogen accumulation
in the cytoplasm, rare dense core granules, and round nuclei with
dispersed chromatin and fine nucleoli. Atypical Ewing sarcoma shows
occasional dense core granules within the cytoplasm. PNET has
increased numbers of dense core granules and development of neurallike (neurite) cell processes containing dense core granules.
The current trend in diagnosis of tumors is by fine needle core
biopsy (see Fig. 14-13). Often, these biopsies provided limited tumor
or crushed tumor cells for evaluation. Membranous expression of
CD99 (see Fig. 14-13B) and nuclear FLI1 are supportive of the
diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma.77–84 FISH for EWSR1 and reverse
transcriptase–polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) for EWSR1-FLI
and EWSR1-ERG translocations may be performed on formalin-fixed
paraffin-embedded tissue from a needle core biopsy.83–92 Cytologic
touch preparations performed on fresh needle cores allows for FISH
for EWSR1 to be performed, which can also be performed on paraffinembedded specimens. Often, the amount of tissue obtained is inadequate for performing conventional cytogenetic karyotype analysis.
As noted previously, Ewing sarcoma may have geographic areas of
necrosis (see Fig. 14-14A), with only ghostlike tumor cells remaining
with histomorphologic features suggestive of Ewing sarcoma. Of particular interest is the fact that immunostaining with CD99 often reveals
a certain degree of retention of membranous reactivity (see Fig.
14-14B), suggesting a diagnosis of necrotic Ewing sarcoma. In particular, fine needle core biopsies may yield such samples, when the biopsy
is performed without immediate assessment for viability by a
pathologist.

14

Genetics
Tumor-defining EWSR1 translocations83–92 are the hallmark of Ewing
sarcoma, with more than 90% of tumors with the EWSR1-FLI translocation (t[11;22][q24;q12]) and 5% of tumors with the EWSR1-ERG
(t[21;22][q22;q12]) translocation. There is also a minor subset of
Ewing sarcomas (<1%) that have one of at least eight other EWRS1
translocations. These include EWSR1-ERG variant (t[19;der[ins.
inv[21;22); EWSR1-ETV1 (t[7;22][ p22;q12); EWSR1-ETV4 (t[17;22]
[q12;q12]); EWSR1-FEV (t[2;22][q33;q12]); EWSR1-POU5F1 ([t6;22]
[p21;q12]); EWSR1-PATZ1 ([t1;22;][q36;q12]); EWSR1-SP3 ([t2;22]
[q31;q12]); and EWSR1-NFATc2 (t[20;22][q13;q12]).
The most common translocations, EWSR1-FLI1 (>90%) and
EWSR1-ERG (5%), are most often the targets for RT-PCR molecular
tests performed in diagnostic laboratories.83–92 If the tumor harbors one
of the rare translocations, typical RT-PCR testing will not identify these
rare translocations, but FISH for EWSR1 will identify a rearrangement;
however, it should be noted that there are many other small round cell
tumors that also have EWSR1 translocations (desmoplastic round cell
tumor, clear cell sarcoma of soft tissue, angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma, extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma, and myxoid and round
cell liposarcomas).
223

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Of particular interest is the more recent discovery of Ewing
sarcomas that lack EWSR1 translocations but possess other nonEWSR1 translocations.83–92 Ewing sarcomas that have non-EWSR1
translocations may possess one of the following translocations: FUSERG (t[16;21][p11;q22]); CIC-DUX4 (t[4;19][q35;q13]); BRD4-NUT
(t[15;19][q14;p13.1]); and BCOR-CCNB3 (t[X;X][p11.4;p11.22]).
The recent discovery of rare EWSR1 and non-EWSR1 translocations
emphasizes that (1) negative RT-PCR for EWSR1-FLI and EWSR1-ERG
and (2) negative FISH for EWSR1 rearrangements do not exclude Ewing
sarcoma as the diagnosis with a tumor that has histomorphologic,
immunohistochemical, and ultrastructural features associated with
Ewing sarcoma. This also demonstrates the importance of submitting
tissue for conventional cytogenetic karyotyping in order to identify rare
EWSR1 and non-EWSR1 translocations associated with Ewing sarcoma
or to discover a novel translocation associated with Ewing sarcoma.
Other cytogenetic and molecular abnormalities occur in Ewing
sarcoma.83–92 Common findings are trisomy 8 (50%), gains in chromosome 12 and 1q (25%), gains in chromosome 20 (20%), unbalanced
translocation t(1;16) with variable breakpoints, and 1p36 loss.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis of Ewing sarcoma includes numerous small
round cell tumors, such as lymphoblastic lymphoma, poorly differentiated synovial sarcoma, certain rhabdomyosarcomas, desmoplastic
small round cell tumor, small-cell osteosarcoma, mesenchymal
chondrosarcoma, myeloid sarcoma, small-cell carcinoma, small-cell
melanoma, esthesioneuroblastoma (olfactory neuroblastoma), and
neuroendocrine tumors.77–84 These entities may be differentiated from
Ewing sarcoma with an appropriate panel of immunohistochemical
markers and in some cases by molecular evaluation for tumor-defining
translocations.
Clinical Course
The overall 5-year survival for localized nonpelvic Ewing sarcoma following appropriate oncologic management (chemotherapy with or
without radiotherapy) and surgical resection is approximately 70%.77–84
In contrast, Ewing sarcoma with metastatic disease at presentation has
a 5-year survival rate of only 25%. With bone or bone marrow metastatic disease, 2-year event-free survival is markedly decreased to about
20%. Patients with pulmonary metastases (see Fig. 14-15) have a better
survival than those with skeletal metastases. Event-free survival at 5
years with tumor relapse is only 10%.
Poor outcome is associated with high tumor stage, large tumor
volume, central versus peripheral skeletal tumor location, metastatic
disease, and tumor relapse.77–84 Translocation type and neuroectodermal differentiation do not influence prognosis. Several molecular and
cytogenetic factors are associated with poor outcomes, including TP53
mutation, CDKN2A loss, telomerase expression, gains in 1q and chromosome 12, and loss of 1p36 and chromosome 20.
One of the most important factors influencing outcome is tumor
necrosis at the primary site following oncologic management (see Fig.
14-16). Mapping the tumor and assessing the degree of necrosis in the
same manner as performed with osteosarcoma are important in assessing tumor necrosis following oncologic management. Tumor necrosis
greater than 90% is considered a favorable histologic response to oncologic management.

Osteomyelitis
Background
Osteomyelitis is caused by infectious agents and results in a progressive
inflammatory process that may cause osseous destruction, necrosis,
224

and new bone formation. It may involve the marrow, cortex, periosteum, and surrounding soft tissues.93–102 There are several different
means of classifying osteomyelitis. The disease may be classified as (1)
acute hematogenous osteomyelitis, (2) osteomyelitis contiguous with
soft tissue involvement with or without vascular inadequacy, (3) vertebral osteomyelitis, or (4) chronic osteomyelitis. It may also be classified based on the histopathologic pattern associated with classes of
responsible microorganisms, as (1) suppurative osteomyelitis (pyogenic, bacterial), (2) granulomatous osteomyelitis (mycobacterial,
fungal), (3) microangiopathic osteomyelitis (spirochetal), (4) neuropathic osteomyelitis (mycobacterial, spirochetal, diabetic), or (5) fibrocystic osteomyelitis (parasitic, cystic hydatid).
Overwhelmingly, the most common organism responsible for
osteomyelitis is Staphylococcus aureus in adults and children.93–102 The
next most common responsible organisms in children are group
A streptococcus, Streptococcus pneumoniae, and Kingella kingae.
Methicillin-resistant S. aureus accounts for more than 30% of all staphylococcus isolated from osteomyelitis cases. Organisms involved in
chronic osteomyelitis include Staphylococcus epidermidis, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Serratia marcescens, and Escherichia coli. Most often,
osteomyelitis in individuals with impaired immune function is due to
fungal and mycobacterial organisms.
The most common bone infections are osteomyelitis due to a contiguous soft tissue focus of infection and direct inoculation, followed
by osteomyelitis due to vascular insufficiency and subsequent soft
tissue infection and then by blood-borne osteomyelitis due to infection
at a distant site.93–99
Often, osteomyelitis is simply divided into acute and chronic osteomyelitis. Acute osteomyelitis is associated with osseous inflammation
caused by pathogenic organisms within 2 weeks of acquisition of the
infection. Symptoms from chronic osteomyelitis are not present until
about 6 weeks following onset of the infection and are characterized
by necrotic (nonviable) bone.
Clinical Features
Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis occurs most often in children presenting with localized erythema, swelling, tenderness overlying the
involved bone, limited mobility, and fever.93–99 The metaphysis is the
most common site due to its high vascularity and susceptibility to
trauma. The proximal and distal regions of the femur and tibia are
involved in 75% of cases. Poor nutrition and hygiene, systemic disease,
and immunologic disorders predispose to acute hematogenous osteomyelitis. Presentation with pain, but without systemic symptoms, is
termed subacute osteomyelitis. Chronic osteomyelitis is less common
in children.
Neonates tend to have more severe and destructive osteomyelitis.
This leads to septic arthritis, septicemia, multiple bone involvement,
and potentially visceral organ infections.93–97,99 Neonatal osteomyelitis
may be associated with a known site of infection, such as an infected
umbilical cord.
Acute hematogenous infection is less common in adults.93,94,96,98
When it does occur, there is usually an underlying debilitating medical
condition, such as diabetes or chronic renal disease. The most common
sites are the vertebrae, followed by the long bones, clavicle, and pelvis.
Back pain and low-grade fever are most often seen with vertebral
osteomyelitis.
Chronic osteomyelitis is usually associated with fractures, bacteremia, and adjacent soft tissue infections.93–102 Osteomyelitis occurs in
about 25% of open fractures, typically within 3 months. In contrast,
prosthetic joints have an infection rate of only 1% to 2%. Chronic
osteomyelitis may occur in the background of recent urinary tract
infection, cholecystitis, diverticulitis, pneumonia, local vascular

Lesions Characterized by Permeation of Cells through Trabecular Bone with or without Bone Destruction
Figure 14-17. Acute osteomyelitis. A, Lytic lesion involving the distal tibia (arrow). B, Nonviable, necrotic bone with loss
of osteocytes in lacunae, absence of reversal (cement) lines, associated with a neutrophilic infiltrate and cellular debris.
C, Gram-positive cocci suggestive of Staphylococcus species.

14

A

B
disorder, or an underlying immunologic or metabolic disease. Chronic
osteomyelitis associated with contiguous soft tissue infection is becoming more common because of diabetes-related foot infections and
peripheral neuropathies. The diagnostic criteria for chronic osteomyelitis require imaging studies demonstrating contiguous soft tissue
infection or bone destruction, clinical signs (exposed bone, persistent
sinus tract, tissue necrosis overlying bone, and chronic wound overlying surgical hardware or bone fracture), as well as laboratory findings
of a positive blood culture, elevated C-reactive protein, or elevated
sedimentation rate. Note that chronic infections of bone tend to be
polymicrobial with sampling of the involved bone. Superficial wound
cultures correlate with bone cultures in only about 33% of cases. Bone
biopsy is necessary in many cases to identify the causative organism(s).
Bone biopsy with necrosis (nonviable bone) and a positive culture from
the biopsied bone are preferred in order to make a definitive diagnosis
of osteomyelitis.
Radiologic Features
During the first week of infection, only soft tissue swelling is evident
on radiologic examination.93–96 Radiologic evidence of bone involvement requires 30% to 50% loss of mineral, and this does not occur until
at least 2 weeks after the onset of infection. The typical findings on
routine radiologic examination are a nonspecific periosteal reaction
and osteolysis, usually after several weeks. Bone scan and MRI show
increased marrow uptake and increased signal intensity. Osteomyelitis
may be detected within 3 to 5 days of onset of the bone infection by
MRI with 90% sensitivity and specificity. Radiologic imaging may

C
show a lytic lesion subsequently (Figs. 14-17 and 14-18), with disease
progression demonstrating a “moth-eaten” pattern due to permeative
bone destruction.
Pathologic Features
The bone biopsy histopathologic features depend on the stage of infection (see Figs. 14-17 and 14-18).93–98 In the first few days of infection,
there is an acute inflammatory infiltrate, edema, and possible trabecular bone and marrow necrosis due to an intraosseous pressure effect.
Active osteoclastic bone resorption then occurs with fibrous tissue
ingrowth and reactive bone formation. The infectious process may
spread through the bone marrow and Haversian system into the periosteum and periosteal soft tissues, potentially leading to a subperiosteal abscess formation. The infection may continue into a chronic stage
after weeks to months with the acute inflammatory infiltrate being
replaced by a lymphoplasmacytic infiltrate. With appropriate treatment, the infectious agent is eliminated and the inflammatory infiltrate
subsides. There is repair of the lytic lesion by new bone formation,
resulting in sclerotic bone and bone marrow fibrosis.
Bone biopsy cultures may identify the organism (see Fig. 14-17).93–102
The most common cultured organisms (>50% of cases) are S. aureus
and coagulase-negative Staphylococcus. Organisms cultured in about
25% of cases include Streptococcus, Enterococcus, Pseudomonas, Enterobacter, Proteus, Escherichia coli, Serratia, Peptostreptococcus, Clostridium, and Bacteroides fragilis. Rarely cultured organisms (<5% of cases)
include mycobacteria, candida, brucella, salmonella, actinomyces, and
dimorphic fungi.

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Figure 14-18. Chronic osteomyelitis. A, Distal femur demonstrating a “moth-eaten”
lytic lesion with areas of sclerosis and probable soft tissue extension (arrow). B, Same
patient several months later with a densely sclerotic and less prominent lytic distal
femur and joint fusion (arrow). C, Admixed plasma cells and neutrophils in marrow
space. D, Numerous plasma cells with associated minute residue of necrotic, nonviable
osteoid matrix.

C

Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis encompasses permeative lesions of bone,
including neoplastic and non-neoplastic diseases, as well as the unique
entity SAPHO (synovitis, acne, pustulosis, hyperostosis, osteitis).93–99
The histopathologic differential diagnosis may include histiocytic disorders, LCD, metabolic storage disorders, leukemia, lymphoma, myeloma,
osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, and metastatic disease.
Clinical Course
Treatment of osteomyelitis depends on identification of the organism(s)
responsible for the lytic disease and selection of appropriate antibiotic
therapy.93–102 Antibiotic therapy is dependent on the susceptibility of
the isolated organism to the antimicrobial agent. It is recommended
that antibiotic therapy be delayed until the organism is isolated and
sensitivity is determined to avoid inducing antibiotic resistance and
prescribing ineffective therapy. In patients who have begun antibiotic
therapy prior to biopsy and microbiologic cultures, there are high false
negative blood culture and biopsy culture results. In the absence of
blood and bone marrow culture and sensitivity results, broad-spectrum
antibiotic therapy may be administered. In particular, a beta-lactam
antibiotic effective against S. aureus should be included in the regimen.
If more than 10% of community isolates have proven to be methicillinresistant Staphylococcus, then antibiotic therapy needs to be adjusted
226

D

appropriately for these methicillin-resistant organisms. Indications for
surgical intervention include antibiotic therapy failure or resistance,
infected surgical hardware, and chronic osteomyelitis with necrotic
bone and soft tissues.
Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis in the pediatric population
usually requires a shorter course of antibiotics than chronic osteomyelitis in the adult population.93–102 In children with immunocompromise, prolonged antibiotic therapy is recommended due to higher
recurrence rates. In adults, the recurrence rate in chronic osteomyelitis
even with long-term antibiotics and surgical intervention is about 30%
after 12 months. Pseudomonal infections have a 50% recurrence rate.
In chronic osteomyelitis, surgery may be necessary (1) to preserve viable
soft tissue and bone and (2) to prevent recurrent systemic infection.

Cystic Lesions in Bone
Yong-Koo Park, MD, PhD

Aneurysmal Bone Cyst 229
Epidermal Cyst 231

fibroblasts, osteoclast-type giant cells, and woven bone. Synonyms
include multilocular hematic cyst and giant cell reparative
granuloma.

Intraosseous Ganglion 232
Simple Bone Cyst 233

Cystic lesions of bone comprise a diverse number of entities, including
simple or unicameral bone cyst, aneurysmal bone cyst (ABC), epidermal cyst, and intraosseous ganglion. Simple bone cysts are typically
lesions of boys in the long bones and are relatively easy to recognize
on conventional imaging and histopathology. They are treated with a
combination of curettage, bone grafting, and injection of steroids into
the luminal cavity. In contrast, ABCs have a broader age range and
affect the sexes equally. Although they also often occur in the metaphysis of long bones, the posterior elements of the spine are also a common
site. Radiologically, the presence of internal septation with fluid–fluid
levels is a key finding. Histologically, key features include the identification of blood-filled cystic cavities lacking endothelial linings and the
presence of fiber osteoid embedded within the walls. Solid forms exist.
Separating primary from secondary ABCs is critical and is typically
accomplished with the help of molecular genetics identifying a CDH11/
USP6 chimeric gene. Epidermal or keratinous cysts occur most commonly on the terminal phalanx or skull as painful masses in middleaged men. They histologically mimic epidermal cysts found in soft
tissue and skin and, although they may generate a large differential
diagnosis radiologically, they are easily recognized histologically.
Curettage is curative. Lastly, intraosseous ganglia are typically found
in a subchondral location adjacent to a knee or hip joint in middleaged patients. Radiologically, they are oval and have a prominent sclerotic margin. Histologically, they mimic soft tissue ganglion cysts.
They, too, are usually easily treated by curettage.

Aneurysmal Bone Cyst
Definition and Synonyms
An ABC is a benign cystic lesion of bone composed of multiple, bloodfilled cavities separated by reactive connective tissue containing

Brief Historical Overview
The term aneurysmal bone cyst was first introduced by Jaffe and
Lichenstein in 1942 in their description of solitary bone cysts. In this
treatise, there are two reported cases with peculiar “blown-out” distention of the contour of the affected bone. In 1950 these researchers
named this lesion aneurysmal bone cyst.
Incidence and Demographics
It is difficult to establish the actual incidence of primary ABCs, because
many cases go unpublished. The estimated annual incidence is 0.15 per
million subjects.1 The male-to-female ratio is approximately 1 : 1.2,3
ABCs occur at any age; however, almost 80% of the lesions are in
patients younger than 20 years of age.3,4
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Almost any bone may be involved, but the region around the knee,
including the distal femur and the proximal tibia, is the most common
site. The spine is also frequently involved, most commonly in the cervical region. In the long tubular bones, ABCs tend to involve the metaphysis, whereas in the spine, they predominantly involve the posterior
elements. About 5% of ABCs, typically the solid variant, involve the
axial skeleton (i.e., the spine or innominate bone).
The presenting symptoms vary according to the affected bone. In
the long bones, the most common sign is swelling, and the most
common symptom is pain. In the vertebrae, an ABC can compress
nerves or the spinal cord, resulting in paresthesias and numbness of
the extremities.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographic findings of an ABC are similar to those of a giant cell
tumor of bone, enchondroma, giant cell reparative granuloma, brown
tumor of hyperparathyroidism, osteoblastoma, and rarely, infection.
Specifically, the radiographic findings are usually typical and easily
diagnosed on conventional radiographs. The main radiographic
229

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
findings are osteolysis and ballooned osseous expansion. In tubular
bones, ABCs show eccentric and osteolytic lesions with or without a
well-defined margin. Sclerosis can also be present. Sometimes, ABCs
have a soap-bubble appearance. When ABCs show expansion of the
cortical surface, they may be mistaken as being malignant due to the
loss of cortical margin as well as the extension into the soft tissue. In
short tubular bones, ABCs are located closer to the central portion and
undergo symmetric expansion of the metaphysis. The typical site of
spinal involvement is in the posterior elements, and the lesion is similar
in appearance to that arising from tubular bones.
On magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), ABCs show heterogeneous
signal intensity. They show internal septation with fluid–fluid levels
that are seen as a high signal on both T1- and T2-weighted images due
to uncoagulated blood.
The lesions are frequently curetted, and red-brown, granular, fragmented materials are received. If the lesion is resected, the cut surface
shows characteristic hemorrhagic, multilocular, cystic spaces filled
with blood, separated by fibrous septa of different thicknesses. More
solid areas can also be found.

Figure 15-1. On low-power examination, this aneurysmal bone cyst shows multiple
blood-filled cavities of varying sizes.

Histopathology
On low-power examination, the characteristic histologic feature is
multiple blood-filled cavities of varying sizes (Fig. 15-1). The cystic
walls lack an endothelial lining and consist of spindle cells, giant cells,
capillaries, and varying amounts of matrix. The stromal cells sometimes show abundant mitotic figures; however, the stromal cells are
uniform, and atypical mitoses are lacking. Osteoid and bony matrix
may be prominent (Fig. 15-2). Often, a thin layer of osteoid (so-called
fiber osteoid) is found in the septa. The reactive woven bone, rimmed
by osteoblasts, is found in these septa (Fig. 15-3). In approximately one
half of cases, the bone is basophilic and termed blue bone. Sometimes
a peculiar calcification called a chondroid aura is present. This is not
seen in any other bone lesions. Necrosis is rare unless there is a pathologic fracture. The solid portions may be predominantly fibrous and
present a lacework of osteoid or newly formed bony trabeculae. The
bony trabeculae tend to anastomose and to have prominent osteoblastic rimming.
Histologic Variants and Grading
Secondary Aneurysmal Bone Cyst
The term secondary ABC is applied to ABCs that are superimposed on
preexisting conditions. Neoplasms most frequently encountered with
ABCs are chondroblastoma, giant cell tumor of bone, chondromyxoid
fibroma, and fibrous dysplasia.2,3,5 However, ABC-like changes are also
found in association with various malignant tumors such as osteosarcoma and metastatic prostate carcinoma. The prognosis and behavior
mirror those of the underlying condition.

Figure 15-2. Abundant osteoid is present in the septa. Note also the focal
calcification.

Solid Variant Aneurysmal Bone Cyst
The term solid variant of ABC was first introduced by Sanerkin et al.
in 1983.6 This variant has the same components as the septa of conventional ABC and is also identical to the histology of giant cell reparative granuloma. Histologically, the lesion shows a spindle cell
proliferation with a loose arrangement of embedded cells. Also found
is abundant reactive new bone formation with prominent osteoblastic
activity7 (Fig. 15-4).
Differential Diagnosis
Primary aneurysmal bone cysts may be confused with giant cell
tumors, low-grade osteosarcomas, and telangiectatic osteosarcomas.
Before a diagnosis of primary ABC is made, every effort must be made
to rule out an underlying condition to confirm that the lesion is not a
230

Figure 15-3. Reactive woven bone, rimmed by osteoblasts, is present in the fibrous
septa.

Cystic Lesions in Bone
healed spontaneously.20 Recurrence is typical within 6 months, and
reoperation results in a complete cure. Malignant transformation has
been reported by Kyriakos and Hardy,21 but such incidents are extremely
rare.

15

Epidermal Cyst
Definition and Synonyms
An epidermal cyst is a relatively infrequent cyst lined by squamous
epithelium and filled with keratin. Synonyms include keratin cyst,
sebaceous cyst, and squamous epithelial cyst.
Incidence and Demographics
The peak incidence is in the age group of 25 to 50 years.22 However,
one case was reported in an 8-year-old child.23 The male-to-female
ratio is 3 : 1.22
Figure 15-4. The solid portion shows a spindle cell proliferation with a loose arrangement of the cells.

secondary ABC. Less frequently, large numbers of giant cells may be
present similar to a giant cell tumor of bone. However, giant cell
tumors occur in skeletally mature patients and typically involve the
ends of bones, whereas ABCs occur in skeletally immature individuals
and are metaphyseal in location. Low-grade osteosarcomas are
much less cellular and show greater mitotic activity than conventional
ABCs.
The most difficult problem is to differentiate an ABC from a telangiectatic osteosarcoma. Histologically, the septa in telangiectatic osteosarcoma are composed of very pleomorphic anaplastic cells, whereas
those in ABC reveal no cytologic atypia.
Genetics
Panoutsakopoulos and colleagues8 reported three cases with clonal
chromosomal aberrations, two with a t(16;17)(q22;p13) translocation
and one with a del(16)(q22) deletion, providing support for a clonal
basis for this lesion. This was followed by several other studies suggesting that a rearrangement of the chromosome band 17p13 was the
characteristic genetic feature of an ABC, with t(16;17)(q22;p13) being
the most frequently observed chromosomal aberration.9–15 This t(16;17)
translocation generates a fusion gene in which the strong promoter of
the osteoblast cadherin 11 gene (CDH11) at 16q22 is fused to the entire
ubiquitin-specific protease 6 (USP6; also known as Tre2) coding
sequence at 17p13.16,17 The result is a CDH11/USP6 chimeric gene that
is transcriptionally up-regulated.
The fusion genes of the variant translocations t(1;17)(p34;p13),
t(3;17)(q21;p13), t(9;17)(q22;p13), and t(17;17)(q12;p13) have been
reported in ABCs.18 In each translocation, the entire USP6 coding
sequence is subsequently fused downstream to the promoter region of
the partner gene: THRAP3 (also known as TRAP150) at 1p34, CNBP
(previously ZNF9) at 3q21, osteomodulin (OMD) at 9q22, and collagen
1A1 (COL1A1) at 17q21.18 In a series of 18 ABCs, only a single case
of these chimeras was detected.18 USP6 and CDH11 oncogenes have
been identified only in primary ABCs and are absent in secondary
ABCs.18 In solid variant ABCs, a novel (X;9) translocation has also
been reported.19
Treatment and Prognosis
The preferred treatment is surgical removal of the entire lesion and if
possible, bone grafting of the resulting defect. Prognosis in ABCs is
excellent. In extremely rare cases, ABCs have been reported to have

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The most common site for a bone-associated epidermal cyst is the
terminal phalanx of a finger, especially the left middle finger; occasionally the midphalanx is the primary site.22,24 Another typical site is the
skull.25,26 Additional sites include the maxilla, mandible, temporomandibular joint, vertebrae, tibia, femur, and toe.24,27–32 Usually epidermal
cysts are slow-growing and remain asymptomatic for a protracted
period. These cysts commonly present with swelling and pain/
tenderness in the affected area.33
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Keratinous cysts in the terminal phalanges of fingers and toes appear
radiologically as well-defined osteolytic lesions with sclerotic margins.
A disrupted cortex or swelling of soft tissue can be seen. Keratinous
cysts can usually be distinguished from other radiologic mimics such
as enchondromas or glomus tumors of the terminal phalanges.
Well-marginated radiolucent lesions can also be seen when these
cysts involve the skull; almost 50% are superimposed on erosions of
the inner and outer table. Compared to the osteolytic lesion seen in
skeletal infection or metastasis, the sharp edge of an epidermoid cyst
is one of its characteristic findings.
On MRI, the lesion is well circumscribed on T1-weighted imaging,
showing a low signal intensity. High signal intensity, even higher than
that of muscle, is often seen on fat-saturated T2-weighted imaging.
There is no enhancement on fat-suppressed, enhanced T1-weighted
images.
Grossly, most cysts are white-yellow intraosseous masses, up to
2 cm in size, that contain cheeselike material. A layer of connective
tissue may be seen separating the cyst from the surrounding bone.
Histopathology
Histologically, epidermal cysts in bone reveal identical characteristics
to epidermal cysts seen in soft tissue. The cyst is lined by stratified
squamous epithelium and is filled with laminating keratin material
(Fig. 15-5). Epidermal appendages are not present. In ruptured cysts,
secondary granulomatous inflammation can be seen. Other infrequent
histological findings are cholesterol granulomas and extensive bony
destruction with periosteal new bone formation.25,26,34,35
Differential Diagnosis
Numerous benign and malignant lesions presenting as radiolucent
lesions of the phalanx can be considered in the radiologic differential
diagnosis: enchondroma, intraosseous ganglion, dermoid cyst, osteoid
osteoma, simple bone cyst, ABC, giant cell tumor, giant cell reparative
granuloma, and even metastasis. Among these, a dermoid cyst has to
231

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
A characteristic site is the juxta-articular, subchondral location, commonly in long tubular bones. The hip, knee, and ankle regions are most
frequently affected.44,47,51–56 Other typical sites are shoulder57 and wrist
regions.58
In many cases the lesion is clinically silent and is incidentally discovered by a radiograph taken for another reason. The most common
symptom is pain in close relation to a joint that may increase on effort
and/or exercise. The symptoms may range over a widely variable
period (months to several years).

Figure 15-5. The cyst is lined by keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium with
abundant keratin filling the lumen.

be distinguished from an intraosseous epidermal cyst. The dermoid
cyst is also lined by keratinizing stratified squamous epithelium;
however, it contains one or more skin appendages, such as sebaceous
glands, sweat glands, or hair follicles. Its lumen is filled with keratin or
sebaceous material.
Genetics and Etiology
Neither the cytogenetics nor molecular genetics of this lesion has been
reported, probably because it is presumed to be unremarkable. The
origin of phalangeal epidermal cysts is thought to be traumatic and
secondary to implantation of epidermal elements into the bone.27,36–38
A second theory attributes the pathogenesis to embryonal misplacement of epithelial cells within the bone,36 and a third theory proposes
that an epidermal cyst within soft tissue erodes into underlying bone.39
Treatment and Prognosis
Curettage of the cystic component with or without bone grafting is not
only adequate treatment but can avoid unnecessary further treatment.
If part of the cystic lining is left, the cyst may recur.

Intraosseous Ganglion
Definition and Synonyms
Intraosseous ganglion is a benign cyst consisting of fibrous tissue
without lining cells. It is filled with mucoid viscous material and typically situated in the subchondral medullary cavity adjacent to a joint.
Historically, this lesion has been called by many, often anatomically
incorrect, names, including bone cavity caused by a ganglion,40 synovial cyst in bone,41,42 subchondral bone cyst,43 intraosseous ganglia,44–47
capsulosynovial intraosseous inclusion,48 ganglion cyst of bone,49,50 and
ganglionic cystic defect of bone.51
Incidence and Demographics
The true incidence of this disease is unknown because many cysts are
discovered incidentally on radiographs obtained for other, unrelated,
reasons. According to one of the largest series of reports,52 the lesion
occurs more frequently in adults between 20 and 59 years of age and
is rare in younger individuals. There is a slight male predominance,
with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 1.2 : 1.
232

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Intraosseous ganglion cysts are well demarcated, have defined margins,
and are of an oval or round shape. On conventional imaging, they
appear as unilocular or multilocular osteolytic lesions of variable size
and are eccentrically located with thinning or expansion of the adjacent cortex. The adjacent articular surface is typically normal. A sclerotic margin, pathologic fracture or soft tissue mass can sometimes be
detected (Fig. 15-6). On MRI, the lesion is of low signal intensity on
T1-weighted imaging and of high signal intensity on T2-weighted
images. Fluid levels can be seen, as well as adjacent soft tissue lesions.
Certain lesions need to be distinguished radiologically such as giant
cell tumors of bone and chondroblastoma, both of which are typically
located at the epiphyseal and subchondral portion of long bones. The
presence of the sclerotic margin seen in ganglion cyst and the absence
of calcification can be helpful in arriving at the correct diagnosis.
Radiographic findings of a ganglion cyst are similar to those of subchondral cysts in osteoarthritis. Subchondral cysts are typically located
more centrally and rarely appear as a single cyst. In addition, subchondral cysts tend to be smaller.
There are no gross differences between intraosseous ganglia and
classic ganglia commonly found in juxta-articular soft tissues. An
intraosseous ganglion is usually a small cavity (<1 cm) that is surrounded by a thin fibrous connective tissue capsule filled with mucinous material. Occasionally, the cyst contains thin fibrous strands.
Histopathology
Histologically, an intraosseous ganglion is identical in appearance to a
soft tissue ganglion cyst. The cyst wall is lined by a connective tissue
membrane formed by parallel fascicles of collagen fibers with a relatively small number of fibroblasts. There is no definite lining epithelium. Areas of myxoid change within the capsule can be seen focally
(Fig. 15-7). Usually the cyst is filled with acellular mucoid material.
Differential Diagnosis
An intraosseous ganglion must always be included in the differential
diagnosis of cystic epiphyseal bone lesions located in the vicinity of a
joint. In addition to the lesions listed above, clear cell chondrosarcoma
and chondromyxoid fibroma must also be excluded, as must cysts
associated with degenerative joint disease. Such cysts also may contain
mucoid material; however, there is typically accompanying radiographic evidence of osteoarthritis.
Genetics
No genetic studies have been reported for intraosseous ganglion cysts.
Treatment and Prognosis
An intraosseous ganglion is treated successfully by removal of the
myxomatous tissue and curettage of the fibrous lining. Bone grafts are
necessary to fill large defects. A small percentage of patients may have
a recurrence; however, these cysts can be removed by repeat
curettage.

osteolytic bone lesion in the subchondral portion of the medial tibial condyle. The
lesion has a sclerotic rim with an indistinct outer margin. B, Coronal reconstructed
computed tomography (CT) image of the knee demonstrating an oval-shaped, osteolytic bone lesion in the subchondral portion of the medial tibial condyle. The lesion is
of low density without mineralization. There are focal depressions of subchondral bone
and diffuse sclerosis around the lesion. C, Sagittal reconstructed CT image of the knee
shows a defect in the subchondral bone at the posterior edge of the lesion. The

Simple Bone Cyst
Definition and Synonyms
A simple bone cyst (SBC) is an intramedullary, usually unilocular,
cystic lesion filled with serous or serosanguinous fluid. It does not have
a lining epithelium. Synonyms include unicameral bone cyst, solitary
bone cyst, juvenile bone cyst, and essential bone cyst.
Incidence and Demographics
About 85% of SBCs are discovered in the first two decades of life. SBCs
have a predilection for male patients, and the overall male-to-female
ratio is approximately 3 : 1. SBCs are infrequently diagnosed in subjects
older than 20 years of age.

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
With SBCs, there is predilection for long bones, especially the proximal
humerus, proximal femur, and proximal tibia, with more than 85% of
the cases found in these sites.59 Rare cases found in older patients usually
involve the ilium, talus, and calcaneus.60–64 These cysts also may be found
in other sites such as the spine, sacrum, radius, and facial bones.65–69
Clinically, SBCs may produce local pain. However, in most patients,
the lesion is discovered after pathologic fracture. In adults, a simple
bone cyst may be an incidental radiographic finding.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Since SBCs are typically located centrally in the medullary cavity of the
metaphysis of a long bone, they tend to be radiolucent with mild
233

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 15-7. The cyst wall is composed of loose fibrous connective tissue demonstrating myxoid change.

symmetrical expansion of the cortex on conventional imaging. The
cortex is typically not penetrated. The margin is sharply defined by a
narrow transitional zone. SBCs commonly abut the epiphyseal plate
but rarely extend into the epiphysis or the apophysis. They move away
from the epiphyseal plate as the bone grows, and some cysts may show
a multilocular appearance. In tubular bones, the long axis of the lesion
is parallel to the axis of the bone with involvement from metaphysis to
the diaphysis. Because SBCs usually show “typical” radiographic findings, making the correct diagnosis is often not challenging using conventional imaging techniques. Pathologic fracture is commonly
observed with the lesion.
The calcaneus is the most common site of involvement outside of
long tubular bones. In the calcaneus, a SBC is located anteriorly and
shows a purely lytic and well-marginated lesion. Here, the expansion
of bone and pathologic fracture are rare (Fig. 15-8). The SBC tends to
be more lucent than lipoma, and there is no bony trabeculation in the
inner portion, which helps differentiate the lesion from a pseudocyst.
The “fallen leaf ” sign, which occurs when a fragment of cortical bone
from a pathologic fracture drops into the fluid-containing cavity, is

A

pathognomonic of SBC. When SBC arises in an unusual location or in
the pelvic bone, it is more difficult to characterize with plain radiographs. Computed tomography (CT) or MRI examinations can be very
helpful.
On CT, an SBC appears as a well-defined, cystic lesion with homogenous fluid attenuation. Sometimes, gas–fluid or fluid–fluid levels can
be seen. Intracystic fibrous septa and an accompanying periosteal reaction can also be seen. On MRI, the SBC is of low signal intensity on
T1-weighted imaging and of high signal intensity on T2-weighted
imaging. A fluid–fluid level can also often be seen, but such findings
are not specific. Imaging studies of the SBC are more challenging to
interpret in the setting of a pathologic fracture. In such cases, there is
typically a more heterogeneous signal intensity on the T1-weighted
image and focal, thick peripheral enhancement pattern on the
T2-enhanced image.
It is almost impossible to grossly evaluate an intact solitary bone
cyst. Usually, in situ SBCs appear as large intramedullary cavities filled
with a clear or yellowish-green fluid of low viscosity. In general, there
is a single cavity; however, sometimes the cyst wall has ridges separating depressed zones resulting in variable sized cavities. The cyst wall is
composed of thin fibrous tissue with multiple bony ridges. Occasionally, a curetted specimen consists of fragments of thin whitish membranous tissue with bony spicules.
Histopathology
The cyst wall is composed of a very thin layer of fibrous tissue (Fig.
15-9). Scattered multinucleated giant cells, inflammatory cells, dilated
blood vessels, hemosiderin pigments, and foamy histiocytes are commonly present. Because of the multinucleated giant cells, this lesion
must be distinguished from giant cell tumor of bone. Another histologic characteristic is the irregular bands or masses of fibrin-like material, often calcified and resembling odontogenic cement (Fig. 15-10).
This lesion has been mistaken for a cementoma of the long bones.
Cysts often fracture, and reparative reactive processes may mimic
those of an ABC. In a fractured lesion, granulation tissue and fracture
callus may be abundant.
Differential Diagnosis
Fibrous dysplasia and giant cell lesions are the two main lesions to
differentiate from SBCs.

B

Figure 15-8. A, Lateral view of the calcaneus showing a well-defined osteolytic lesion with a thin, sclerotic rim. B, Sagittal reconstruction of the calcaneus again shows a cystic
lesion with a thin, sclerotic rim.

Figure 15-9. At low-power magnification, the simple cyst has a thin layer of fibrous
tissue containing scattered giant cells.

Figure 15-10. Eosinophilic aggregates resembling odontogenic cement are present
in this simple bone cyst.

Genetics
A very complicated clonal structural rearrangement involving chromosomes 4, 6, 8, 16, 21, and both chromosomes 12 has been described in
an 11-year-old boy.70
Treatment and Prognosis
The classical treatment of simple bone cyst has been curettage and
autogenous or homogeneous bone grafts. However, Scaglietti and
coworkers showed dual needle aspiration of the cyst and injection of
methylprednisolone acetate71 to be effective. In children, the recurrence
rate is reported as about 20%. There are rare cases of secondary malignancy arising in SBCs reported in the peer-reviewed literature.72,73
