Paget Disease 250

Benign “Soft Tissue” Tumors Primary in Bone
Schwannoma
Definition and Synonyms
Schwannoma, also known as neurilemmoma and neurinoma, is a
benign nerve sheath tumor that occurs primarily in soft tissue but can
also occur primarily within bone or secondarily involve bone.
Incidence and Demographics
Intraosseous schwannomas are rare, with a reported incidence of less
than 1%.1 They typically affect young adults in the second to fifth
decades of life; however, they can occur at any age. Their rarity in bone
is thought to be due to the low density of sensory nerves within bone
from which schwannomas are belived to arise.2
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The most common intraosseous sites include the mandible and the
sacrum. Schwannomas have also been reported in the humerus, tibia,
femur, ribs, scapula, and maxilla, as well as in the small bones of the
hands and feet and vertebral bodies.3,4 These tumors are usually solitary
and asymptomatic, but they can give rise to pain and swelling, especially in those cases that impinge on nerves or arise within the spinal
canal.

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Schwannomas present as well-defined lucencies with sclerotic borders.
Cortical expansion, thinning and perforation may also occur with
secondary soft tissue extension. These tumors can thus mimic giant
cell tumor of bone or aneurysmal bone cyst radiologically.4–6 On magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), they are isointense on T1-weighted
images and hyperintense on T2-weighted images. A low-signal intensity rim is sometimes noted and reflects the encapsulated nature of this
neoplasm. Intraosseous schwannomas are rarely suspected radiologically, given their rarity and nonspecific findings. A case of subperiosteal schwannoma has been described.7 Schwannomas arising from the
sensory roots of the spinal cord may secondarily involve bone and
often have a dumbbell appearance.8
Grossly, schwannomas appear as circumscribed white or yellow
masses. Larger lesions may have cystic and hemorrhagic areas.
Histopathology
Schwannomas in bone are similar to their soft tissue counterparts.
They show the classic Antoni-A and Antoni-B areas; the former is
characterized by increased cellularity with prominent or inconspicuous nuclear palisading and the latter by less cellular areas with a
myxoid background. A fibrous capsule may not be present in
schwannomas of bone; however, scattered thick hyalinized vessels are
typically seen.9 Cystic degeneration, histiocyte infiltration, and hemosiderin deposition are more common in larger tumors. The lesional
cells are usually bland. However, significant degenerative-type atypia
(i.e., “ancient change”) is occasionally seen, especially in large, longstanding tumors.10 Melanin pigment deposition, psammomatous calcifications, and adult adipose cell metaplasia are expected in the
melanotic variant.9
Histologic Variants and Grading
Although any form of schwannoma can occur in bone, the majority of
tumors occurring in bone display classic morphologic features. Less
common variants include cellular and epithelioid subtypes. Myers et al.
reported a case of melanotic schwannoma of the ilium.11 In this rare
variant of schwannoma, the cells express melanin and the tumor shows
scattered psammomatous calcifications.
237

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Differential Diagnosis
In its classic form, a diagnosis of schwannoma is usually straightforward, especially when adequate tissue is obtained. Histologic mimics
in bone include desmoplastic fibroma and fibrous histiocytoma.
Although neurofibromas and schwannomas are histologically and
nosologically related, the former rarely, if ever, occur in bone. In the
cellular form of schwannoma, one must exclude a smooth muscle tumor
as well as a monophasic synovial sarcoma. Spindle cell/desmoplastic
melanoma may also be a consideration, particularly in the head and
neck region; however, this usually presents more aggressively and shows
more uniform atypia. Radiologically, the differential diagnosis includes
giant cell tumor of bone and aneurysmal bone cyst.4 In the mandible,
odontogenic cysts and tumors should also be considered.12
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Schwannomas diffusely express S-100 protein. Glial fibrillary acidic
protein and CD57 have also been reported but are less sensitive and
specific. Electron microscopy shows that schwannomas are composed
almost exclusively of Schwann cells, which are characterized by
complex processes that emanate from the cell body and are coated with
basal lamina.13 Melanotic schwannomas show patchy positivity for
melanocytic markers by immunohistochemistry and, ultrastructurally,
feature melanosomes in various stages of development.9
Genetics
The majority of intraosseous schwannomas are sporadic.9 In the setting
of neurofibromatosis, schwannomas have an inactivating germline
mutation of the tumor suppressor gene NF2 located on chromosome
22.1 Psammomatous melanotic schwannomas are associated with
Carney complex, an autosomal dominant condition linked to the
17q22 chromosomal region. This condition features myxomas, lentiginous pigmentation, blue nevi, and endocrine hyperactivity.9
Treatment and Prognosis
Schwannomas are benign tumors and are best treated with conservative local excision. Recurrence is rare if adequately excised.9,14 Ang and
colleagues reported a recurrent schwannoma of the tibia; however, the
tumor in this case was large (14 cm) with cortical destruction and soft
tissue extension.15

Lipoma
Definition and Synonyms
Lipomas are benign tumors composed of mature adipocytes. They may
arise in intramedullary, intracortical, or parosteal sites. Parosteal
lipoma is characterized by an intimate attachment to the periosteum
of bone. Although they have been considered by some to represent
reactive lesions, the result of hyperlipidemia, the end-stage of an
infectious/inflammatory process, or part of the spectrum of bone
infarct,16–18 the presence of cytogenetic alterations argues to the
contrary.
Brief Historical Overview
Intraosseous lipoma was first described by Seerig in the German literature in 1836.19 Parosteal lipoma was suggested by Power in 1888.20
Incidence and Demographics
Although lipoma is the most common mesenchymal neoplasm in soft
tissue, it is decidedly rare in bone. The reported incidence is less than
0.1%; however, this is believed to be an underestimate, since many of
these lesions are not biopsied or excised because of their benign
appearance on imaging.19 They can also be misclassified as other
benign bone lesions, particularly bone infarcts. Lipomas span a wide
238

age range but are most common in adults. Intraosseous lipomas tend
to present in persons who are a decade younger (median age, 37 years)
than parosteal lipomas.21
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Lipomas can affect any bone but are most common in the proximal
femur (intertrochanteric and subtrochanteric regions) and calcaneus.
Other reported sites include the pelvis, tibia, fibula, humerus, vertebrae, sacrum, skull, mandible, maxilla, and ribs.16 In long bones,
they involve the metaphysis and sometimes the diaphysis.20 The small
bones of the hands and feet are typically spared. Lipomas can be
intramedullary, parosteal, and rarely cortical. Parosteal lipomas are
most common in the femur and humerus but have also been reported
in other sites, including the clavicle, pelvis, and ribs.22 Lipomas
may also involve joints and tendon sheaths. Within the joint they can
form a discrete mass or be diffuse, often referred to as lipoma arborescens (see Chapter 21). Finally, lipomas have been reported within the
spinal canal, where they can lead to medullary compression and neurologic symptoms.23
Lipomas tend to be asymptomatic and solitary, and they are usually
discovered incidentally. Pain has been described in 50% to 70% of cases
in some series; however, it may not be related to the lipoma itself but
rather what draws the patient’s attention to the lipoma.16,20 Pathologic
fractures are rare. Motor or sensory deficits from nerve compression
caused by parosteal lipomas are common, and the nerve most commonly involved is the posterior interosseous nerve.24 Jebson and
coworkers described a case of intraosseous lipoma with extraosseous
extension and associated nerve palsy.25
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The radiologic characteristics of lipoma depend on its composition and
the presence of cystic degeneration and/or osseous metaplasia (see
subsequent discussion). In its classic form, it appears as a well-defined
lucency, which is often associated with cortical expansion and a sclerotic rim. A calcified nidus within a radiolucent lesion in the neutral
triangle of the calcaneus is pathognomonic (target-like or cockade
image).20,26 Trabeculation may be evident. Computed tomography
(CT) and/or MR help confirm the fatty nature of the lesion, which has
a low attenuation value on the former modality and high signal intensity on the latter. Cyst formation may also be noted.16 Parosteal lipoma
appears as a juxtacortical radiolucent lipomatous mass with varying
degrees of septation associated with surface bone reactive changes such
as cortical thickening, cortical erosion, ossific protuberances and, occasionally, bowing of the bone19,20 (Fig. 16-1A).
Macroscopically, both intramedullary and parosteal lipomas appear
as well-circumscribed yellow masses. Firm, cystic, and calcified areas
may be noted. Parosteal lipomas are usually firmly attached to the
underlying bone, and they may have portions of the periosteum and a
rim of bone attached to them on resection. In addition, they tend to
grow larger than intraosseous ones and can measure 4–10 cm as compared to 3–5 cm for the latter.
Histopathology
The histopathologic features of a lipoma are only meaningful in the
context of the radiologic imaging studies.26 Similar to their soft tissue
counterparts, bone lipomas are composed of lobules of mature fat.
Entrapped bone trabeculae may or may not be present. Varying degrees
of fibrosis, myxoid change, fat necrosis, and calcification are expected.16
Osseous and cartilaginous metaplasia and cyst formation have been
described.27 The involuted lipoma as described by Milgram may not
contain any areas of typical lipoma but have cystic spaces, necrotic pink
acellular tissue, calcified fat, and reactive woven bone that stains dark

“Soft Tissue” Tumors of Bone, Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct, and Paget Disease

16

B

A

Figure 16-1. Lipoma. A, Radiograph showing an area of lucency in the proximal femoral shaft with opacification in the center (corresponding to calcification), surrounded by prominent sclerosis. B, Benign mature fat surrounding bone
trabeculae. Note the absence of bone marrow elements. C, An area of fat
necrosis and calcification in the same lesion depicted in B.

purple and lacks a lamellar pattern. This type of reactive ossification
related to infarcted fat is quite distinctive21 (see Fig. 16-1B and C).
Histologic Variants and Grading
Milgram divided intraosseous lipomas into three different stages based
on what he considered their degree of involution. In stage 1, they are
composed of viable fat cells; in stage 2, they show focal fat necrosis and
calcification, and in stage 3, they have undergone extensive fat necrosis,
cyst formation, and new bone formation.28 The presence of these
changes accounts for the variability in the radiographic features of
lipomas as described previously.
Differential Diagnosis
On conventional radiography, lipomas may be confused with a large
number of benign bone lesions, including bone infarct, Brodie abscess,
fibrous dysplasia, nonossifying fibroma, giant cell tumor, simple bone
cyst, aneurysmal bone cyst, osteoblastoma, plasmacytoma, and benign
chondroid tumors.17 However, CT and/or MR help confirm the fatty
nature of the lesion. Histologically, lipomas appear distinct from most
of the abovementioned lesions. They can be distinguished from normal

C

marrow fat by the presence of a mass lesion. Bone infarcts can
look quite similar to lipomas; however, lipomas show resorption of
normal bone within the lesion, which is an important defining
characteristic.28,29
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
No ancillary studies are necessary to make a diagnosis of lipoma other
than correlation with radiologic features. Adipocytic nuclei stain positive for S-100 protein. If well-differentiated liposarcoma of bone is a
consideration, assessment for MDM2 amplification is a possibility;
however, there is no report of MDM2 evaluation of these tumors in the
literature.
Genetics
A (3;12) translocation has been described in parosteal lipomas.30 Kubo
et al. suggested that the presence of this translocation can be used to
confirm the benign nature of a lipoma in cases where the radiologic
features are worrisome for well-differentiated liposarcoma.31 This
translocation results in an HMGIC/LPP fusion transcript, which is also
a common finding in lipomas of soft tissue. Hsieh and colleagues have
239

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
shown that loss of PTEN leads to lipoma formation along the spines
of mice.32

respiratory insufficiency immediately after delivery.41 Rapid growth of
the lesion has also been described.

Treatment and Prognosis
If asymptomatic, conservative clinical and radiologic follow-up for a
lipoma is sufficient. In symptomatic cases, simple curettage with or
without bone grafting is the treatment of choice, and the prognosis is
excellent. Indications for surgery include suspicion of malignancy,
nerve entrapment, cosmetic deformity, and pain. Radl and coworkers
had no recurrence after a mean follow-up period of 32 months for 27
lipomas treated by curettage.17 Because parosteal lipomas tend to be
strongly adherent to the underlying periosteum, they require subperiosteal dissection.33 Milgram described malignant transformation in
four cases of intraosseous lipomas.34 Whether these cases represent
true malignant transformations or a sarcoma occurring in association
with a benign lipoma is difficult to tell, especially given that the malignant tumor is often described as a malignant fibrous histiocytoma and
a pathologist was not part of the reporting team. In any case, biopsy
and/or excision are warranted in cases with worrisome clinical and/or
radiologic features.

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiographs show an expansile mass, often with cartilage-type calcifications, involving one or more ribs. The degree of bony destruction
may be suggestive of malignancy. Ultrasound shows a heterogeneous
mixed echoic mass with solid and cystic portions, as well as highly
echogenic areas with posterior shadowing suggesting calcification or
ossification.35 Hemorrhagic cavities with detectable fluid–fluid levels
can be appreciated on CT scan.46 MR shows alternating low and high
signals on T1-weighted and T2-weighted sequences, reflecting both
solid and cystic components.35 Some degree of parenchymal compression and mediastinal shift is often present47 (Fig. 16-2A).
Grossly, this is a well-circumscribed, lobulated mass composed of
cystic hemorrhagic areas admixed with meaty red-brown soft tissue
and gray to white solid nodules of cartilage. The main mass ranges in
size from 2 to 15 cm (average, 6.3 cm).41

Infantile Hamartoma of the Chest Wall
Definition and Synonyms
Infantile hamartoma of the chest wall is a benign, non-neoplastic
lesion that presents as an expansile rib lesion. It is characterized by
nodules of cartilage and mesenchymal tissue with secondary aneurysmal bone cyst–like changes. The term chondromesenchymal hamartoma has been proposed because it more accurately describes the
pathologic features and is less likely to cause confusion with the entity
fibrous hamartoma of infancy, a distinct benign soft tissue neoplasm.
Other names used for this entity include chest wall hamartoma of
infancy, mesenchymoma, mesenchymal hamartoma of chest wall, and
vascular hamartoma of infancy. It is generally accepted that this lesion
represents a locally disordered overgrowth of normal skeletal elements
and is not neoplastic.35,36
Brief Historical Overview
In 1962, Le Ber and Stout used the general rubric benign mesenchymoma to describe a group of pediatric mesenchymal tumors composed
of various elements that occurred in different areas of the body.37 The
term hamartoma was first used by Mcleod and Dahlin in their report
of nine cases in 1979 to reflect their belief that these are not true neoplasms.38 The common term mesenchymal hamartoma was then used
by Odell and Benjamin in 1986.39
Incidence and Demographics
More than 60 cases of infantile hamartoma of the chest wall have been
recorded in the literature. The reported incidence is 0.03%.40 Most
tumors are diagnosed at or shortly after birth; Dounies and colleagues
found that 55% of cases were discovered at birth and 97% were documented by 1 year of age.41 Some cases have been diagnosed in utero by
CT scan or ultrasound.42,43 The male-to-female ratio is 2 : 1.41
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Infantile hamartoma of the chest wall is thought to develop antenatally
with a variable rate of growth in early childhood.36 They arise almost
exclusively from the posterior or lateral portions of the ribs, although
a similar lesion has been described in the nasal passages.44,45 They are
often multiple and are bilateral in 9% of cases.41 Most present as an
asymptomatic mass; however, they may cause difficulties during childbirth and often result in respiratory distress. Groom and colleagues
reported respiratory symptoms in 58% of cases.46 One infant died of
240

Histopathology
If biopsy is attempted, it should be done under controlled settings
because these lesions may bleed profusely.47 The tumor is composed of
solid and cystic areas; the latter are indistinguishable from an aneurysmal bone cyst and are composed of hemorrhagic spaces lined by spindled stromal cells without significant atypia or abnormal mitotic
figures admixed with numerous giant cells.36 Peripherally, cellular cartilaginous nodules are present that may show enchondral ossification.
The cartilage shows variable maturation and may be quite cellular and
appear primitive, leading to a misdiagnosis of chondrosarcoma. Areas
of chondroblastoma-like proliferation, woven bone, and osteoid have
also been described.48 Histologic maturation of multiple lesions
resected sequentially is thought to support the hamartomatous nature
of these lesions.39 Fine needle aspiration cytology has been used with
success, especially when correlated with clinical and imaging findings49,50 (see Fig. 16-2B to D).
Differential Diagnosis
The presence of a chest wall mass with both solid and cystic components in an infant is suggestive of a chest wall hamartoma. Awareness
of this entity with its unique clinical and imaging features is the most
important factor in the diagnostic process.36 Aneurysmal bone cyst
and cartilaginous tumors such as osteochondroma, chondromyxoid
fibroma, and chondroblastoma are considerations in the differential
diagnosis. Other lesions to be considered include fibrous dysplasia,
Langerhans cell histiocytosis, and hemangioma. The combination of
an aggressive radiologic appearance, rapid growth, and primitive histologic appearance has resulted in misdiagnosis as chondrosarcoma,
osteosarcoma, and ostoblastoma.46 Ewing sarcoma may be considered
in the radiologic differential diagnosis, but the histologic features are
not those of a small, round, blue cell tumor.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
The cartilaginous nodules are positive for S-100 protein. Baretton and
coworkers showed a heterogeneous distribution of various types of
interstitial collagens in accordance with their presumed hamartomatous nature.51
Genetics
Cytogenetic studies performed on one case showed a normal karyotype.49 A case of chest wall hamartoma in association with WiedemannBeckwith syndrome has been reported, suggesting a possible link to
the 11p15.5 gene locus associated with this syndrome.52

“Soft Tissue” Tumors of Bone, Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct, and Paget Disease

16

A

C

B

D

Figure 16-2. Infantile hamartoma of chest wall. A, Computed tomography image showing a cystic expansile mass with well-defined borders involving the right eighth rib.
B, Section shows rib bone (bottom) with large nodules of mature hyaline cartilage arising in association with the bone. C, Aneurysmal bone cyst–like changes. D, High-power view
showing benign hyaline cartilage.

Treatment and Prognosis
The natural course is unpredictable, and although some of these
lesions regress spontaneously, others progressively enlarge, especially
early in the first year of life.43,47 It has been noted that symptomatic
lesions tend to progress, whereas incidental ones remain stable. Most
cases are surgically resected because of symptoms, suspicion of malignancy, or chest wall deformity. If surgical removal is attempted, a
conservative approach is recommended to avoid scoliosis,53 and multistage resection may be necessary; however, recurrence is common if
there is no en bloc resection. If observation is elected, close clinical
follow-up is necessary to monitor tumor enlargement and the degree
of chest wall deformity.54 Radiofrequency thermoablation has been
reported to be successful in one case.55 There is one report of malignant transformation in the literature; however, it was described by the
authors themselves as a fibrous hamartoma.41 No metastases have
been reported to date.

Malignant “Soft Tissue” Tumors Primary in Bone
Malignant Peripheral Nerve Sheath Tumor
Definition and Synonyms
Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor encompasses a number
of entities and synonyms, including neurofibrosarcoma, malignant
schwannoma, malignant neurilemmoma, malignant perineural
fibroblastoma, and malignant neurofibroma.56,57 However, malignant
peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) is now the most widely
accepted term. There is controversy regarding exactly what criteria are
necessary for this diagnosis; some authors require evidence that a
tumor arises from a nerve or a neurofibroma or, otherwise, occurs in
the setting of neurofibromatosis, whereas other authors require only
that the tumor show classic histologic features of MPNST.58 The most
specific way to define MPNST would be tumors that arise from,
are composed of, or show differentiation toward nerve sheath tissue.
241

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

B

A

C
Figure 16-3. Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor. A, Radiograph showing a large lytic lesion involving the proximal left tibia. B, Sections show a spindle cell neoplasm with
elongated nuclei and pink cytoplasm. Notice the entrapped bone trabeculae. C, Intersecting fascicles of spindle cells with wavy nuclei and focal glandular differentiation. (This case
was kindly provided by Dr. Barry DeYoung, Wake Forest University, Winston-Salem, North Carolina.)

In this context, it is important to recall that the “nerve sheath” is a
heterogeneous structure composed of Schwann cells, perineurial cells,
and fibroblasts.

extension.59,60 Grossly, these tumors are composed of fleshy tan-gray
myxoid tissue.62 Necrosis and hemorrhage have been reported in highgrade tumors58 (Fig. 16-3A).

Incidence and Demographics
These tumors are exceedingly rare as primary tumors of bone. Terry
et al. found only 18 cases in their review, 15 of which occurred in the
mandible or maxilla. The age of presentation is variable (4–76 years),
with the majority of cases occurring in young to middle-aged adults.
Three patients have been reported with a history of neurofibromatosis.59,60 Another well-known association is in the setting of previous
irradiation, particularly in tumors affecting the spine.56,61

Histopathology
The tumor is composed of interlacing fascicles of spindle or polygonal
cells with a clear to eosinophilic cytoplasm and a variably myxoid
stroma. Irregular wavy nuclei, focal nuclear palisading, and subendothelial proliferation are features suggestive of the diagnosis. Cellular
areas may alternate with myxoid areas, and foci of undifferentiated
primitive cells have also been described.58,59 Tactoid bodies reminiscent
of the tactile organs of skin have been described. Increased cellularity,
moderate nuclear pleomorphism, increased mitotic activity, and necrosis are useful in differentiating benign from malignant nerve sheath
tumors.59,62 Heterotopic mesenchymal elements in the form of osteoid,
cartilage, and skeletal muscle (traditionally reported as a Triton tumor)
have been reported.56,58 Glandular differentiation and epithelioid morphology have also been described58,61 (see Fig. 16-3B and C).

Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The majority of the reported cases are in the mandible, especially in
pediatric cases,58,62,63 and only a handful of cases have been reported in
the long bones.56,59 Pain followed by swelling is the most common
presenting symptom and/or sign. Spinal tumors are relatively common;
they arise from the nerve roots and cause neurologic symptoms.61,64
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiology typically shows a lytic lesion with extensive cortical destruction. MR often demonstrates bone marrow infiltration and soft tissue
242

Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes other spindle cell sarcomas, including synovial sarcoma, fibrosarcoma, and clear cell sarcoma. Metastatic
tumors should also be considered, including melanoma and clear cell

“Soft Tissue” Tumors of Bone, Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct, and Paget Disease
sarcoma of kidney.56,58 MPNST with significant glandular differentiation may mimic metastatic carcinoma.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
S-100 protein staining is variable and may be absent, especially in
higher grade lesions. Glial fibrillary acidic protein, myelin basic
protein, and leu-7 (CD57) may be used as supplementary evidence of
nerve sheath differentiation.56,58 Electron microscopy shows elongated
cells with long, slender cytoplasmic processes enveloping each other as
well as other surrounding mesenchymal cells. Although benign and
malignant nerve sheath tumors cannot be distinguished ultrastructurally, electron microscopy can support evidence of Schwann cell
differentiation.65
Genetics
Karyotypic analysis performed on one case revealed the presence of a
complex cytogenetic abnormality.58
Treatment and Prognosis
The clinical course for these tumors is characterized by multiple local
recurrences and hematogenous seeding of the lungs. The prognosis is
uniformly poor. Radical surgery is the mainstay of therapy, with possible benefit from adjuvant radiotherapy.56,60

Leiomyosarcoma
Definition and Synonyms
Leiomyosarcoma of bone is a rare malignant tumor with smooth
muscle differentiation. Before the advent of immunohistochemistry,
many of these tumors were classified as fibrosarcomas.66,67
Brief Historical Overview
Evans and Sankerkin reported a case of primary leiomyosarcoma of
bone in the tibia of a 73-year-old man in 1963.67 In the mandible, there
are reports of this tumor as early as 1944.68
Incidence and Demographics
The exact incidence of primary leiomyosarcoma in bone is unknown.
Berlin and colleagues estimated the incidence in Sweden at 0.09 per
million,66 and leiomyosarcoma of bone composed 0.64% of primary
bone malignancies recorded in the files of the University of Basel
between 1972 and 1990.69 The age distribution is wide and evenly
distributed from the second to the seventh decades, with males and
females affected about equally.70,71 Some of the cases developed following radiation exposure.66 In the series by Antonescu and coworkers,
15% of the cases in the Memorial Sloan Kettering Hospital files developed after a history of irradiation, particularly in the head and neck
region.70,72 Leiomyosarcoma arising in association with a bone infarct
has also been reported.73
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The knee is the most common site followed by the craniofacial bones.70
Other reported sites include the humerus, tibia, pelvis, ribs, clavicle,
scapula, phalanges, vertebra, and foot.71,74–77 Leiomyosarcoma typically
involves the metaphyseal region with secondary epiphyseal or diaphyseal extension.78 Narváez et al. reported a case of subperiosteal leiomyosarcoma.79 Symptoms and signs are not specific. Most patients
present with pain, pathologic fracture, and, occasionally, a palpable
mass.77
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The majority of these tumors present as destructive lytic lesions with
ill-defined margins and cortical destruction, suggestive of malignancy.74

However, leiomyosarcoma can mimic both benign and malignant
tumors of bone, including giant cell tumor, chondrosarcoma, lymphoma, Ewing sarcoma, osteosarcoma, metastatic carcinoma, plasmacytoma, chondromyxoid fibroma, and adamantinoma.70,71,80 Perilesional
sclerosis is usually absent but can be occasionally seen in low-grade
tumors.79 Soft tissue involvement is present in the majority of cases.70
MR shows a hypointense lesion on T1-weighted images and an isointense or hypointense lesion on T2-weighted images.71
Grossly, these tumors have been described as firm and gray in color.
They may also be gelatinous or creamy. Areas of necrosis or hemorrhage can be seen. The average reported size is approximately 6 cm.70

16

Histopathology
Similar to their soft tissue counterpart, these tumors are composed of
intersecting fascicles of spindle cells with a dense pink and occasionally
fibrillar cytoplasm. The nuclei are typically blunt-ended and elongated.
Perinuclear vacuoles, tumor giant cells, and a hemangiopericytomalike vasculature have been described, as has an epithelioid morphology
of the principal neoplastic cells.67,70,74,81 Necrosis is present in one third
of cases (Fig. 16-4A).
Histologic Variants and Grading
Epithelioid leiomyosarcoma is a rare variant.81 There is no clear consensus on grading, with some pathologists opting for a two-grade system
and others following the three-grade system used in soft tissue tumors.
In the largest series of primary leiomyosarcoma of bone, 79% of tumors
were designated high grade using a two-grade histologic grading scale.
That same series classified 12% of tumors as low grade, 21% as intermediate grade, and 66% as high grade using a three-tiered system.70
Differential Diagnosis
The most important consideration to exclude is metastatic leiomyosarcoma, typically from a soft tissue or gynecologic primary.74,82 In general,
tumors originating in bone tend to arise around the knee and be large
and elongated with limited mediolateral expansion. In contrast, metastatic leiomyosarcomas tend to be less elongated and affect the femur
and spine.71,78,83 Other differential diagnoses include fibrosarcoma,
malignant fibrous histiocytoma, nerve sheath tumor, sarcomatoid carcinoma, myofibrosarcoma, and desmoplastic fibroma.70,71 In the facial
bones, myofibromatosis should also be excluded.68
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Given the rarity of these tumors, ancillary studies are often necessary
to confirm the diagnosis80; however, it is important to recognize that
none of the ancillary studies mentioned below is entirely specific and
that context is critical when making a diagnosis of this rare tumor. The
majority of these tumors are positive for smooth muscle actin and
muscle-specific actin (HHF-35)70 (see Fig. 16-4B and C). Desmin positivity is variable. Other less specific stains include collagen IV and
laminin. Significant S-100 protein and cytokeratin positivity have also
been described in a subset of cases and can be a potential pitfall.69,71,74
In their small series, Khoddami and colleagues showed that these
tumors displayed low p53 expression and that the biologic behavior of
the tumors did not correlate well with their proliferative activity as
assessed by MIB-1 immunostaining.84 Electron microscopy has been
used widely and shows intermediate filaments, dense bodies, basal
lamina, attachment plaques, and pinocytic vesicles.66,67,74
Genetics
Wirbel and colleagues discovered a high rate of genomic instability in
four of the five tumors studied in their series. In their opinion, this
may denote a high grade of malignancy.72
243

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C

Figure 16-4. Leiomyosarcoma. A, Section showing fascicles of smooth muscle cells
with oval nuclei and pink cytoplasm infiltrating bone. B, Smooth muscle actin diffusely
stains the tumor cells. C, Desmin is also diffusely positive in this case. (This case was
kindly provided by Dr. Carrie Inwards, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, Minnesota.)

Treatment and Prognosis
There is scant information regarding treatment and prognosis; however,
these tumors tend to be aggressive, and early surgical intervention by
means of radical or wide resection seems to offer the best chance for
cure.69 Response to adjuvant therapy is variable and disappointing in
general.72 Approximately 50% of patients develop lung metastasis.74 In
the head and neck region, radical neck dissection for lymph node
metastases has been advocated.68,85 In the study by Antonescu et al.,
low-grade tumors seemed to behave less aggressively than higher grade
ones, but the number of low-grade tumors was limited.70

Synovial Sarcoma
Definition and Synonyms
Synovial sarcoma is a malignant mesenchymal tumor that often shows
epithelial differentiation. It is characterized by a t(X;18)(p11;q11)
translocation that fuses SS18 to one of three SSX genes.
Incidence and Demographics
Synovial sarcoma is an aggressive neoplasm occurring chiefly in the
soft tissue of the extremities of children and young adults. It is the
second most common soft tissue sarcoma in children.86 It accounts for
approximately 10% of soft tissue tumors,87 and the median age for
patients is 35 years.88 Although synovial sarcoma of soft tissue may
secondarily involve bone, fewer than 10 cases of primary bone synovial
244

sarcoma have been well documented in the literature. Interestingly,
there appears to be a bimodal age distribution, with clustering in
patients younger than 30 years of age and another group in patients
older than 60 years of age.89–94
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Cases have been reported in tibia, radius, sacrum, calcaneus, and mandible. Most cases present with pain and/or swelling.86,89–94
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologic studies demonstrate an ill-defined radiolucent lesion,
whereas CT scans often show a well-circumscribed, heterogeneously
enhancing mass with occasional calcifications. MR shows low to intermediate signal intensity on T1-weighted images and high heterogeneous signal intensity on T2-weighted images.89 Occasionally, a
so-called “triple signal intensity” pattern is present.90 MR is particularly
useful in delineating the true extent of the neoplasm and soft tissue
involvement. Grossly, the tumor has a heterogeneous appearance with
necrosis, hemorrhage, and cystic areas.
Histopathology
Synovial sarcoma can present as (1) a biphasic neoplasm composed of
both spindled and epithelioid components, (2) a monophasic (spindled
or epithelioid) neoplasm, or (3) a poorly differentiated round cell

“Soft Tissue” Tumors of Bone, Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct, and Paget Disease

16

A

B

C

Figure 16-5. Synovial sarcoma. A, Low-power view showing a cellular spindle cell
neoplasm infiltrating bone. B, High-power view showing intersecting fascicles of
spindle cells with scant cytoplasm, oval nuclei, and small nucleoli. C, Cytokeratin stain
showing characteristic focal staining. (This case was kindly provided by Dr. Carrie
Inwards, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, Minnesota.)

neoplasm. The monophasic spindle cell tumors are composed of
compact fascicles of uniform spindle cells, resembling fibrosarcoma.
Abundant collagen and focal calcifications have been described.91
Hemangiopericytoma-like vasculature changes may be prominent in
some cases but, as in soft tissue, are not specific. The epithelial component may show distinct gland formation, or it may be in the form
of solid clusters or nests of epithelioid cells with abundant cytoplasm.
The majority of cases arising in bone appear to be of the monophasic
spindle cell type (Fig. 16-5A and B).
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes sarcomatoid carcinoma, fibrosarcoma, extrapulmonary solitary fibrous tumor, and MPNST. In the rare
cases that display predominantly epithelial morphology, metastatic
carcinoma may be a consideration, but this can be excluded by immunohistochemical and/or molecular analysis. The patchy staining of
cytokeratin helps differentiate synovial sarcoma from carcinomas,
where cytokeratin staining is expected to be diffuse and intense.86
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Immunostains show the tumor to be positive for epithelial membrane
antigen, cytokeratin, and vimentin. Cytokeratin staining tends to be

focal, especially in the monophasic form (see Fig. 16-5C). Staining for
S-100 protein and CD99 may also be present.90–94 Electron microscopy
shows occasional intercellular junctions and intermediate filaments.92
All bone tumors tested have been positive for the SYT-SSX molecular
transcript.89,92,93,95
Genetics
Synovial sarcoma is characterized by a chromosomal translocation
involving the SS18 gene (known as SYT or SSXT) on chromosome 18
with one of the three SSX genes, SSX1, SSX2 or SSX4, on the X chromosome. Although the incidence of these various translocations and
their morphologic associations have been well described in synovial
sarcomas of soft tissue,96,97 similar analysis has not been possible for
these rare tumors of bone.
Treatment and Prognosis
The treatment of choice is wide surgical excision. Although the 5-year
survival rate has been reported to be 60% to 75%, the 10-year survival
rate is only 34%.88 Tumors smaller than 5 cm have a better prognosis.
Postoperative radiation therapy is recommended to prevent local
recurrence, which is common; metastatic disease primarily involves
the lungs.86,91,93,98
245

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Liposarcoma
Definition and Synonyms
This malignant neoplasm of fat encompasses entities that are pathogenetically diverse such as well-differentiated liposarcoma, myxoid liposarcoma, and dedifferentiated liposarcoma. Given the rarity of these
tumors in bone and their relatively common occurrence in soft tissue,
it is important to rule out a metastatic tumor from soft tissue before
considering a liposarcoma to be of primary bone origin.99 In addition,
the tumor must have the characteristic features of liposarcoma similar
to those described in soft tissue. In fact, the existence of this entity as
a primary bone tumor was long doubted.100,101 Although a soft tissue
liposarcoma could conceptually invade bone, in reality this is a very
rare occurrence,102 and the fact that some of these tumors are completely confined to bone argues against this possibility.103 Cohen suggested that these tumors may be arising from the periosteum.104
Malignant transformation of benign lipomas of bone is exceedingly
rare and often presents in the form of a pleomorphic sarcoma rather
than a well-differentiated liposarcoma.105
Brief Historical Overview
In 1928, Ewing added this category to the classification of bone
tumors.103 In 1931, Stewart reported three cases of unusual bone
tumors that he cautiously interpreted as representing primary liposarcomas of bone.106
Incidence and Demographics
This is an extraordinarily rare tumor in bone. The reported incidence
is 0.03% among all bone neoplasms.107 The majority of tumors are
reported in adult patients; however, the age range appears to be younger
than it is for soft tissue liposarcoma (average, 37 years), and males seem
to be affected more often than females. Patients as young as 15 years
of age have been reported.101,102,108
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Liposarcoma usually affects long tubular bones such as tibia and femur.
Other reported sites of involvement include the humerus, fibula,
scapula, maxilla, mandible, and spine.101,107,109,110 The tumor may involve
any part of the bone and typically manifests as a painful mass. Liposarcomas have been reported in association with other benign bone
lesions such as bone cyst and nonossifying fibroma.101,102
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
There are no specific radiographic features for osseous liposarcoma.
The majority are described as a large osteolytic mass with cortical
destruction. CT and MR may or may not demonstrate fat in the
lesion.107,108 Lmejjati and coworkers described a spinal tumor as
hypointense on T1-weighted images and hyperintense on T2-weighted
images.109 Grossly, these tumors are described as large, lobulated, tan
to yellow masses. Myxoid tumors may be glistening and mucinous in
appearance.
Histopathology
The histomorphology depends on the variant of liposarcoma present
in a particular case. Well-differentiated liposarcoma is composed of
adipocytes of various sizes with thin fibrous septa and scattered large
hyperchromatic nuclei. If dedifferentiation is present, it manifests as a
pleomorphic spindle cell malignancy. Myxoid liposarcoma is composed of sheets of relatively bland-appearing ovoid nuclei suspended
in a myxoid background displaying a fine capillary network. Pleomorphic liposarcoma is composed of sheets of highly atypical lipoblasts. It
appears from the morphologic description and the provided photographs that the majority of the reported tumors in bone are of the
246

pleomorphic subtype with abundant lipoblasts. Most of the earlier
studies refer to it as the mixed subtype.100,106,111 Areas of undifferentiated malignant spindle cells have also been described.106,108 Kenan et al.
reported a case of myxoid liposarcoma with round cell areas arising in
a juxtacortical location.112
Histologic Variants and Grading
Grading follows the same rules used for soft tissue liposarcoma. Welldifferentiated and myxoid liposarcomas are considered low grade by
definition. In contrast, the presence of a dedifferentiated component
in the former and a round cell component (>5%) in the latter confer a
high grade to the tumor. Cases with osteosarcomatous differentiation
have been reported, mostly in young individuals, and they are referred
to as osteoliposarcoma or malignant mesenchymoma.113–115
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis includes involuted lipoma, liposclerosing
myxoid tumor, pleomorphic undifferentiated sarcoma, and myxofibrosarcoma. Radiologically, these tumors may resemble Ewing sarcoma,
metastatic tumor, myeloma, giant cell tumor of bone, or desmoplastic
fibroma.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Oil red O and Sudan IV stains can be used on frozen tissue to confirm
the presence of fat.100,102,103,106,113 Electron microscopy performed on
one case showed irregular nuclei, prominent nucleoli, cytoplasmic
membrane-bound lipid droplets of varying sizes, mitochondria with
electron dense matrices, and bundles of microfilaments.100 Although
one would expect the well-differentiated tumors to harbor a MDM2
amplification, similar to their soft tissue counterpart, we are not aware
of any cases reported in the literature to be positive for this molecular
aberration.
Genetics
No genetic studies have been conducted on liposarcoma of bone, but
this tumor is expected to harbor the same cytogenetic aberrations as
its soft tissue counterparts.
Treatment and Prognosis
Radical resection is the treatment of choice. Prognostic information
regarding liposarcoma of bone is limited. A mean survival rate of 2
years has been reported in some series.107 Lung and occasionally liver
metastases develop in the majority of cases.108 The aggressiveness of
these tumors is probably related to the fact that the majority present
as high grade pleomorphic liposarcomas. Patients with tumors arising
in smaller long bones (e.g., ulna and fibula) seem to have a better
chance of survival. Traditional chemotherapy and radiotherapy are of
limited value in advanced cases.101,116

Rhabdomyosarcoma
Definition and Synonyms
This is a malignant tumor showing differentiation toward skeletal
muscle.
Brief Historical Overview
The first well-documented case of primary rhabdomyosarcoma of bone
was reported by Pasquel and colleagues in 1976.117
Incidence and Demographics
This is an exceedingly rare tumor in bone. In its pure form, only a
handful of cases are reported in the literature. More commonly, rhabdomyosarcoma involves the bone marrow secondarily in pediatric

“Soft Tissue” Tumors of Bone, Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct, and Paget Disease
patients with alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma,118 in which case it often
infiltrates the bone marrow space diffusely and does not form a discrete
mass. Rhabdomyosarcoma may also be a component of another
sarcoma such as osteosarcoma (also known as malignant mesenchymoma) and dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma.119–121 The very few cases
of pure primary rhabdomyosarcoma of bone predominantly affect children and young adults (11 to 45 years of age), and there was a predilection for males.117,122–124
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Three of the reported cases arose in the femur and one affected the
ilium. Multiple cases involving the temporal bone have also been
described but are believed to have originated in soft tissue and secondarily invaded bone.125 Pain and pathologic fracture are the typical presenting symptoms and signs.122
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiologically, most tumors are overtly aggressive and present as lytic
lesions with cortical destruction.117,122–124 Grossly, the tumor appears as
a large, tan-yellow mass destroying bone with soft tissue extension.117,123
Hemorrhage and necrosis may be present.122
Histopathology
Embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma is composed of bundles of spindle cells
with hyperchromatic or vesicular nuclei and abundant eosinophilic
cytoplasm. Strap cells, tadpole forms, and ribbon-shaped cells with
cross striations are observed. Most cases seem to have a prominent
spindle cell component,117,122 and in some cases, the tumor is composed
of fascicles arranged in a herringbone-like pattern reminiscent of fibrosarcoma.121,123 Alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma cases show a primitive
round, blue cell tumor morphology with limited differentiation toward
rhabdomyoblasts arranged in clusters.118 Pleomorphic rhabdomyosarcoma shows features of high-grade pleomorphic sarcoma with abundant pink cytoplasm and occasional striations. In some cases, malignant
bone and cartilage is present, in which case the tumor is often referred
to as ectomesenchymoma.119,121
Histologic Variants and Grading
Alveolar, embryonal, and pleomorphic subtypes have all been reported
in bone.117,122–124 Although, as discussed above, alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma is usually metastatic to bone, cases with primary bone marrow
involvement have also been reported.118 These cases often present with
diffuse involvement of bone marrow and can be mistaken for a hematologic malignancy.
Differential Diagnosis
The differential diagnosis is broad and includes malignant fibrous
histiocytoma, malignant mesenchymoma, fibroblastic osteosarcoma,
malignant triton tumor, fibrosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma, and dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma.123,124 The presence of rhabdomyoblasts
and cross striations are very good indicators for skeletal muscle
differentiation.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Given the rarity of this tumor in bone, immunohistochemical and, in
the case of the alveolar subtype, molecular evaluation is essential.
Tumor cells are positive for desmin, muscle specific actin (HHF-35)
and myoglobin.123,124 Because most of the cases were reported before
the nuclear regulatory proteins myogenin and myoD1 were discovered,
only rare cases have been reported to be positive for these markers but
are expected to be positive if tested.118 Electron microscopy shows cells
with abundant cytoplasm and numerous haphazardly oriented bundles

of thin actin and thick myosin filaments with attached ribosomes. In
addition, irregularly distributed Z-bands can be recognized.119,124
Molecular testing can be helpful in confirming the diagnosis (see
Genetics discussion).

16

Genetics
In soft tissue, the majority of alveolar rhabdomyosarcomas are positive
for the characteristic t(2;13)(q35;q14) or t(1;13)(p36;q14) translocations involving the FOXO1 gene and the PAX3 or PAX7 genes, respectively.126 One case of primary alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma in bone was
shown to have the t(2;13)(q35;q14) translocation.118 There are no
specific cytogenetic abnormalities reported in other subtypes of
rhabdomyosarcoma.
Treatment and Prognosis
In one case, lung metastasis occurred despite ablation of the affected
limb.117 Wang and colleagues reported full response to preoperative
chemotherapy and no recurrence 4 years after wide resection and
prosthetic reconstruction.124 Oda and coworkers also reported no
recurrence 4 years after hemipelvectomy and one course of postoperative chemotherapy.123

Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct
Definition and Synonyms
Bone infarction, also known as osteonecrosis, refers to bone death
secondary to interruption of blood supply. It is a relatively common
disorder and can affect any bone in the body. It is usually a localized
process, and the vast majority of cases are secondary to trauma.127,128
However, it may also be the result of many other pathologic processes,
including ischemia; infection (e.g., human immunodeficiency virus);
radiation (e.g., jaw bone); tumors; coagulopathies; metabolic (e.g.,
Gaucher disease), hematologic (e.g., sickle cell anemia) or rheumatologic disorders (e.g., systemic lupus erythematosus); or iatrogenic conditions.127,129–131 In rare cases, pregnancy has been associated with
osteonecrosis132 (Table 16-1).
Avascular necrosis (AVN, also known as aseptic necrosis) is a
special form of bone infarction that affects specific bones under certain
conditions and manifests as extensive necrosis of bone and bone
marrow (see also Chapter 19). It is usually localized to the epiphysis
and is often related to systemic factors. Certain sites are more vulnerable to osteonecrosis due to the small diameter of terminal vessels, lack
Table 16-1. Conditions Associated with Avascular Necrosis
Category

Example

Trauma

Fractures

Hematologic disorders

Sickle cell disease, thrombophilia

Metabolic disorders

Hyperlipidemia, Gaucher disease

Infections

Osteomyelitis, human immunodeficiency virus

Rheumatologic

Systemic lupus erythematosus

Orthopedic conditions

Slipped capital femoral epiphysis, congenital hip dysplasia

Extrinsic factors

Alcohol, smoking, caisson disease

Iatrogenic

Steroids, radiation

Tumors

Marrow infiltrative disorders

Idiopathic

247

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
of collateral circulation, and limited expansion of bone in these areas.
The pathogenesis of AVN is not well understood and may vary depending on the case. Some of the proposed mechanisms include vascular
occlusion, altered lipid metabolism/fat emboli, intravascular coagulation, mechanical stress, and primary cell death.127,128 In certain conditions, more than one mechanism may be involved (e.g., occlusion of
small blood vessels and marrow hyperplasia in sickle cell disease).127
Fracture of the femoral neck is often followed by ischemic necrosis
of the femoral head in elderly patients. Such fractures can cause collapse of the upper weight-bearing segment of the femoral head in a
subset of patients, usually more than 1 year after the fracture, resulting
in a dysfunctional joint and severe pain.133
The relationship between idiopathic subchondral AVN of the
femoral head and steroid therapy and alcoholism became recognized
in the 1950s and 1960s.133 Approximately 60% of persons diagnosed as
having idiopathic osteonecrosis have bilateral disease, and most of
these patients have a history of corticosteroid use or increased alcohol
consumption. Alcohol is thought to compromise blood flow through
fat embolization or fat hypertrophy and also can cause direct cell toxicity.127 Other associated conditions include hyperlipidemia, hypercoagulability, connective tissue disorders, and liver necrosis secondary to
anesthesia or certain medications.
Decompression sickness is a unique type of bone necrosis that
results from the liberation of gas bubbles (notably nitrogen) in the
tissues and blood of persons who have undergone rapid decompression
from a hyperbaric environment.133 Those bubbles act as emboli in the
blood and exert extravascular compression on the microvasculature of
the tissue (especially fatty tissue where nitrogen solubility is high),
causing tissue necrosis. The historic example of this entity is the development of bone infarcts in caisson workers. Femoral and humeral
heads are most commonly affected.127
In children, osteonecrosis of the femoral head (also known as LeggCalvé-Perthes disease) is thought to result from interruption of blood
flow due to trauma (or increased intra-articular pressure) to the vessels
feeding the growth plate, which results in cessation of endochondral
ossification and failure of the cartilage to be converted to bone. Other
suggested mechanisms include thrombophilia and synovitis of the hip
joint.127 Slipped capital femoral epiphysis, a condition commonly seen
in adolescents, may also result in osteonecrosis.
Incidence and Demographics
Osteonecrosis accounts for 10% of joint replacement surgeries in the
United States.5 The disease is seen predominantly in males, and 75%
of patients are in the 30- to 60-year age range. It is associated with 27%
of subcapital femoral fractures and 13% of posterior hip dislocations.
Steroids are the second most common cause of osteonecrosis after
trauma. Ninety-one percent of patients with multifocal necrosis
reported a history of steroid therapy.127 However, because many of
the conditions that are treated by steroids can lead to osteonecrosis
themselves, the exact relationship between steroid use and osteonecrosis is not clear. Of note is the fact that patients with alcohol-induced
osteonecrosis are significantly older than those with idiopathic osteonecrosis and 97% of them are men. Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease affects
children from 4 to 12 years of age and has a prevalence of 1 : 1200 to
1 : 12,000. Symptomatic osteonecrosis occurs in 3% to 5% of sickle cell
patients, whereas asymptomatic disease may affect up to 41%.1 In the
foot, osteonecrosis affects the talus, the first and second metatarsals
(Freiberg disease), and the navicular bone (Kohler disease).128
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Clinical manifestations are variable depending on the size and site of
infarction. Acute bone infarction generally manifests as pain, often
248

followed by decreased range of motion and impaired mobility. Osteonecrosis of the femoral head results in pain in the groin or anterior
thigh,127 whereas in decompression sickness, pain is usually around the
knees (the bends) and may be associated with signs of injury to vital
organs (e.g., central nervous system and lungs).133
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
The early phase of bone infarct may not be seen on conventional radiographs. Later, a moderately thick radiopaque border is typically seen
outlining an area of radiolucency usually discribed as “coil of smoke.”
At this phase, gross examination shows a sharply demarcated pale area
with a hyperemic border.133 In advanced stages, a crescentic lucent zone
(crescent sign) beneath the articular cartilage is often present on a
frog-leg view, which is the result of bone collapse (Fig. 16-6A). MR,
particularly when enhanced by gadolinium, is considered the most
accurate modality in diagnosing early stages of AVN of the hip and
may show a wide range of signal alterations134; a double line on
T2-weighted image is diagnostic. On bone scintigraphy, a combination
of a cold spot and a hot spot is characteristic. Single-photon emission
computed tomography (SPECT) can aid in maximizing resolution.128
CT may show a subchondral fracture. The University of Pennsylvania
staging system best categorizes the different phases of osteonecrosis
(Table 16-2).
In the early phase of Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease, there is widening
of the joint space as a result of continuous growth of cartilage without
ossification. Later, the epiphysis undergoes collapse and deformity.
Characteristically, there is enlargement of the femoral head and widening of the neck.133
Gross examination of the femoral head in patients with AVN shows
alteration in the shape of the articular surface (buckling). A fracture
line is often present below this area on sectioning the bone. In advanced
cases, there is severe deformity of the femoral head and secondary
osteoarthritis, with few clues to the initial event of AVN; however, a
helpful indication is preservation of the articular cartilage in the area
overlying the infarction.133
Histopathology
The early phases are highlighted by bone marrow infarction and fat
necrosis followed by loss of osteocytes in the bone trabeculae. Next,
granulation tissue forms along with new bone at the periphery, which
radiologically manifests as bone sclerosis at the infarct margin. Fibrosis
and calcification are seen in the chronic phase133 (see Fig. 16-6B
and C).

Table 16-2. University of Pennsylvania Radiologic Staging of Osteonecrosis
Stage

Criteria

0

Normal or nondiagnostic radiograph, bone scan, and MRI

I

Abnormal bone scan and/or MRI but normal radiograph

II

Lucent and sclerotic changes in femoral head

III

Subchondral collapse (crescent sign)

IV

Flattening of femoral head

V

Joint narrowing and/or acetabular changes

VI

Advanced degenerative changes

MRI, magnetic resonance imaging.

“Soft Tissue” Tumors of Bone, Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct, and Paget Disease

16

A

B

C

Figure 16-6. Avascular necrosis. A, Radiograph showing collapse, deformity, and increased density of the femoral head with
surrounding sclerosis. B, Scanning magnification image showing
a broad zone of bone marrow necrosis and calcification. C, Zone
of bone marrow necrosis and calcification surrounded by bone
sclerosis.

Differential Diagnosis
The radiologic findings are generally not specific. Several entities
should be considered in the differential diagnosis, including chondroma and chondrosarcoma, both of which tend to have more discrete
calcifications and less well defined margins. Calcified lipoma of bone
and transient bone marrow edema should also be considered in the
differential.134 Bone marrow necrosis is a diffuse condition where the
reticular architecture of the bone marrow is destroyed but the bone
spicules are preserved.135 Hematopoietic marrow, subchondral cysts,
transient osteoporosis, insufficiency fracture, and metastasis are other
possibilities.131 It is important to differentiate osteomyelitis from osteonecrosis, especially in patients with Gaucher disease.136
Treatment and Prognosis
Early detection is important since the prognosis is dependent on the
stage and location of the disease; once bone collapse occurs, the process
is irreversible and secondary osteoarthritis develops over the next 5
years or so. The diagnosis should be suspected in patients on steroid
therapy, particularly if they have other known risk factors.127
The management of osteonecrosis is primarily palliative. Conservative measures such as pain medication and limited weight-bearing

may be helpful but do not halt disease progression. Core decompression, with or without bone grafting, can reduce intramedullary pressure and provide immediate and dramatic pain relief. However, the
results are best when performed in the early stages of the disease.127,130
Osteotomy to shift pressure away from the necrotic bone segment
is another alternative in young patients with small, postcollapse
lesions.127,137 In patients with end-stage osteonecrosis, hemiarthroplasty or total hip replacement may be the only therapeutic option.
Unfortunately, the 5-year survival of the prosthesis in this context is
much lower than that seen in conventional osteoarthritis patients.
Many of these patients require more than one procedure in their lifetime,127 hence the importance of early detection of the disease. In
general, AVN related to steroids or alcohol exposure carries a worse
prognosis.129
Currently, judicial use of steroids, monitoring of at-risk patients,
and the application of highly sensitive imaging techniques give the
greatest potential for controlling the disease. One rare complication of
long-standing bone infarcts is the development of sarcomas, most
commonly malignant fibrous histiocytoma (currently referred to as
undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma), but also fibrosarcoma and
angiosarcoma.127
249

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Paget Disease
Definition and Synonyms
Paget disease, also known as osteitis deformans, is a chronic condition
associated with solitary or multifocal bone remodeling. It was first
described by Sir James Paget in 1877.138 The hallmark of Paget disease
is increased osteoclastic bone resorption followed by disorganized
bone formation.139 The etiology of Paget disease is unclear; it seems to
be a mixture of genetic and environmental factors that result in
derangement of osteoclast formation and activity.139–141 One theory is
that it may be related to a slow viral infection of the myxoviridae type.
This is supported by the presence of characteristic nuclear inclusions
in the osteoclasts that resemble virus particles. However, similar structures have also been reported in osteoclasts from other conditions.
Also, no infectious virus has been isolated from Pagetic cells.142,143
On the other hand, 15% to 40% of patients have a positive family
history, and the risk of developing the disease is seven to ten times
greater in relatives of patients with Paget disease. The disease seems to
be inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion with incomplete penetrance. Mutations in different genes have been associated with the
disease, the most important of which are mutations of the sequestosome1 (SQSTM1) gene, which encodes a p62 protein. This protein
is believed to play an essential role in the osteoclast RANKNF-κB signaling pathway, which promotes osteoclastogenesis and
formation.139–141,144–146
Incidence and Demographics
Paget disease primarily affects white patients, and the condition is rare
in blacks and Asians. There is a geographic distribution with a higher
incidence in northern Europe, Australia, New Zealand, and South
Africa. Paget disease is the second most common metabolic bone
disorder in the United Kingdom,141 and 1 to 3 million people are
believed to be affected with the disease in the United States.139,143
Patients are typically middle aged or elderly, and the prevalence of
disease increases with age.144 It has been reported to affect 2% to 3%
of individuals older than 60 years of age. However, there has been a
decline in the prevalence and clinical severity of Paget disease in the
past 30 years in developed countries, which may be due to changes in
ethnic make-up and/or environmental factors.140,141,146,147 In addition,
the proportion of cases with Pagetic sarcoma is also believed to be
declining.148
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
There is wide variation in disease severity.140 A significant proportion
of cases (25% to 35%) are monostotic and are discovered incidentally
due to an elevated serum alkaline phosphatase or an abnormal radiograph. The most commonly affected bones include pelvis, lumbar
spine, skull, femur and tibia.144,149 On average, three skeletal sites are
affected.150 Most patients with localized disease are asymptomatic.
Patients often present with bone pain, and 10% to 15% of patients
have severe symptoms.143 In patients with involvement of the skull,
hearing loss is the most common complication,145 and cord or nerve
compression may be encountered in spinal disease. Transverse and
incomplete fractures are common in the lower extremities. On
physical examination, there may be warmth, due to increased vascularity, and tenderness over the affected bone. Bone enlargement and
deformity may be noticeable. Arthritis, especially of the hips, is not
uncommon in patients with Paget disease due to bone deformity and
altered mechanics due to accelerated bone remodeling. Cardiovascular abnormalities such as heart failure, secondary to increased demand
on the heart, and vascular calcifications have been reported.149
Very rarely, secondary malignancy will be the presenting problem
(Box 16-1).
250

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Radiography is the mainstay of diagnosis in uncomplicated Paget
disease.149 In the early phase of disease, bone lesions are characteristically osteolytic with a sharp demarcation between normal and affected
bone in long bones. The lesions often start subchondrally in the epiphysis. The advancing edge of the lesion is V-shaped and has a “blade of
grass” appearance. In the skull, patients can develop discrete oval
lesions in the occipital bones called osteoporosis circumscripta.143,149 In
the mixed phase, there is coarsening and thickening of the trabecular
pattern and cortex. The vertebral bodies have a characteristic sclerotic
edge (“picture frame”) appearance.138 In the late sclerotic phase, there
is considerable bone formation with increased density, loss of corticomedullary demarcation, and bone enlargement.151 Vertebral bodies
may become completely sclerotic “ivory vertebra.” Transverse fractures
may also be present. It is very unusual for patients to develop new
lesions in previously uninvolved bones140,143 (Fig. 16-7).
Osteolytic lesions with cortical destruction, soft tissue extension,
and absent marrow fat signal are suggestive of secondary malignancy.152
A periosteal reaction is often not present in Paget sarcoma. Bone
isotope scans are sensitive but not specific, and they are most valuable
in identifying the distribution of polyostotic Paget disease. MR is
helpful in evaluating neural complications, such as spinal stenosis with
nerve entrapment, and differentiating sarcomatous transformation.138
Box 16-1. Complications of Paget Disease
Bone pain
Bone deformity
Fractures
Arthritis
Headache
Deafness
Dental problems
Nerve entrapment
Spinal stenosis
Heart failure*
Hypercalcemia*
*Rare complications.

Figure 16-7. Paget disease. Radiograph showing dense sclerosis of the femur with
osteolysis proximally and a displaced fracture.

“Soft Tissue” Tumors of Bone, Avascular Necrosis and Bone Infarct, and Paget Disease
On gross examination of Pagetic bone, there is thickening of the cortex
and coarse medullary bone.
Histopathology
A diagnostic biopsy is rarely needed to establish the diagnosis of Paget
disease.149 The hallmark of the disease is increased multinucleated giant
osteoclasts. However, the microscopic appearance depends on the
stage. In the early osteolytic phase, there is active bone resorption, the
trabeculae become thin, and there may be resorption cavities. There is
increased osteoclastic and osteoblastic activity with bone resorption
occurring concurrently with new bone formation. The intramedullary
tissue shows richly fibrovascular tissue. The subsequent phase, the
“osteoblastic phase,” is characterized by disorganized bone formation;
thick irregular bone trabeculae are present with prominent cement
lines, giving the bone a “mosaic” appearance. Under polarized light,
the bone appears woven. Despite the increased thickness of the bone,
it is structurally weak, and cracked cement lines can be seen. In the
final “sclerotic” phase, there is less cellular activity and the marrow
returns to its normal appearance. Some studies suggest that both osteoclast precursors and bone microenvironment are abnormal in Paget
disease138,143,149 (see Fig. 16-8A and B).
Differential Diagnosis
The radiologic differential diagnosis includes hyperparathyroidism,
infection (e.g., tuberculosis), infiltrating tumors (e.g., round cell
tumors), osteoblastic metastatic carcinoma (e.g., breast and prostate),
and lymphoma. Pagetoid bone may also be seen in osteosarcoma.

Diffuse vertebral body sclerosis may be seen in sickle cell disease, fluorosis, mastocytosis, myelofibrosis, and renal osteodystrophy.138 Familial Paget disease–like syndromes have also been described such as
familial expansile osteolysis, expansile skeletal hyperphosphatemia,
idiopathic hyperphosphatasia, and hereditary inclusion body myopathy. Although patients with these syndromes may have Paget-like skeletal abnormalities, the age of onset is earlier and the clinical presentation
is different.141,144

16

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Serum alkaline phosphatase is often elevated in Paget disease, and the
level of activity correlates with the degree of skeletal involvement.150
Parathyroid hormone levels are modestly increased in less than 20% of
patients.143 Although hypercalcemia and hypercalciuria can develop in
a subset of patients, serum calcium and phosphorus are usually within
normal limits. Other indicators of bone formation (e.g., procollagen 1
N-propeptide and osteocalcin) and bone resorption (e.g., N-telopeptide,
C-telopeptide and urine pyridinoline) may be increased.145,149
Genetics
Mutations of the sequestosome1 gene have been reported worldwide
in both familial (46%) and sporadic (16%) cases of Paget disease. The
protein encoded by this gene, p62, acts as a scaffold in the NF-κB
signaling pathway. Most of these mutations affect the ubiquitinassociated domain of the protein. It is currently unclear how these
events affect osteoclast activity.140,141 Because there is no evidence that
treatment of Paget disease affects clinical outcome, it is generally not

A

B

C

Figure 16-8. Paget disease. A, Low-power view showing disorganized bone trabeculae with variable thickness and fibrotic bone marrow. B, Section of Pagetic bone
showing remodeling of bone and bone necrosis. C, Section showing a highly malignant neoplasm with chondroid differentiation.
251

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
indicated to screen family members for the disease. Other genes such
as TNFRSFIIA, TNFRSFIIB, and VCP have been linked to other Pagetlike syndromes.141,146,153
Treatment and Prognosis
The goal of therapy is to relieve symptoms and prevent complications.149 Treatment of asymptomatic patients is controversial.145 The
main indication for treatment in Paget disease is bone pain.
Medical therapy consists of drugs that inhibit osteoclastic resorption such as calcitonin and bisphosphonates. Calcitonin, not currently in use, was the first effective therapy for the disease; however,
resistance developed in the majority of patients. Bisphosphonates are
effective drugs with a low risk–benefit ratio that act by targeting and
inducing apoptosis of osteoclasts.140 The newer generation drugs
(alendronate, risedronate, and zoledronate) are generally more potent
than the older ones (etidronate and pamidronate). Side effects include
uveitis, scleritis, and renal impairment (mainly with intravenous
infusion).145
Surgical therapy may be required to treat complications such as
arthritis, fractures, and deformities. The inflexible Pagetoid bone tends
to fracture easily and heals only with difficulty. Joint replacement may
be necessary if severe arthritis develops. It is recommended that one
start medical therapy a few months prior to surgery in order to reduce
blood flow to the affected skeleton.144,149 Patients with Paget disease are
at increased risk of developing heterotopic ossification following total
hip replacement. Transmission of Paget disease through autologous
bone grafting has been reported.149,150
Sir James Paget was the first to describe the association of this
disease with malignancy.152 Sarcomas have been reported in less than
1% of patients and are twice as common in men.146,152,154 They often
present in the sixth decade and are most frequently located in the
femur, pelvis, humerus, and skull.148,155 The most common types are
osteosarcoma followed by malignant fibrous histiocytoma and fibrosarcoma (see Fig. 16-7D). These tumors occur more frequently in
patients with polyostotic disease and tend to be poorly responsive to
therapy. The median survival is 8 months, and the 5-year survival rate
is less than 10%.148,155 Giant cell tumors have also been reported and
tend to affect the skull and facial bones.138 It is important to recognize
that florid Paget disease can extend into soft tissue and simulate malignant processes.138,152 Carcinomas seem to metastasize to Pagetic bone
more often, perhaps due to increased vascularity.138,145
