17
Metastases to Bone
Julie C. Fanburg-Smith, MD, Michael E. Mulligan, MD, and Mark D. Murphey, MD

Overview

255

Radiologic Features of Metastatic Disease

257

Pathologic Features of Metastatic Disease

263

Genetics of Metastatic Disease

268

Overview
Metastases to bone of tumors from nonosseous primary sites account
for the largest percentage of bone tumors. The host bone responds
either by osseous resorption (osteolytic), osseous formation (osteoblastic), or often both processes. This chapter will cover the clinical and
radiologic features as well as the pathologic classification of metastases
to bone in adults and children.
Pathophysiology (Microenvironment)
Metastases to bone occur either by direct extension, hematogenous
spread, or intraspinal involvement such as via subarachnoid spread for
primary central nervous system neoplasms and lymphoma. In the
absence of infection or sepsis, veins are better conduits for tumor
metastasis to bone than arteries, because of the impervious nature of
the arterial wall. The Batson vertebral plexus, a valveless, richly interconnecting venous plexus of the spine that communicates with the
azygous venous system, receives venous drainage from the prostate,
thyroid, breast, lung, and kidney1 and thereby provides a route of
metastasis from these organs to axial bone, including the vertebral
bodies, pelvis, skull, and proximal limb girdles. The much rarer arterial
tree metastasis mechanism is most typically seen in the spread of
pulmonary and renal carcinoma to the distal extremities below the
elbow or knee. Bronchogenic carcinoma accesses the arterial system
via the pulmonary vein. Lymphatics do not play a role in spread to
bone, and an intraosseous lymphatic system has not been demonstrated.2 The liver intercepts some types of metastatic tumors, such as
gastrointestinal stromal tumors, which therefore only rarely secondarily involve bone. Direct extension of tumor to bone is rare but
includes Pancoast tumor to the adjacent ribs or vertebrae; soft tissue
sarcomas with a propensity to involve bone, sarcomatoid or higher
grade squamous cell carcinoma of skin especially of head and neck

region3; and direct spread of tumor in a paravertebral lymph node to
adjacent vertebra.
The formation of osseous metastases follows a complicated series
of events that depends on the intrinsic properties of the primary malignancy and regulation by signaling pathways and molecular interactions.4 Basic steps from primary site to metastasis include local tumor
invasion, intravasation, circulation survival, extravasation, colonization, and neovascularization; these steps occur at various rates for
different primary malignancies.5 In the case of carcinoma, the neoplastic cells at the primary site must first become discohesive and break
free from basement membrane to invade and enter a vascular structure, occasionally by acquiring mesenchymal properties via epithelialmesenchymal transition (EMT). The E-cadherin cell adhesion
molecule on tumor cells modulates release from the primary tumor
into the bloodstream. Next, tumor cells must survive both intravascular transport and host immune system attacks.6 Bone is a particularly
common site of subsequent extravasation due to the large gaps in the
marrow vessel walls for hematopoietic precursors. Tumor cells attach
to bone and target the endothelial layer via integrin cell adhesion molecules. Tumor cell survival in the bone microenvironment depends on
cross-talk7 within the rich vascular milieu and the interaction of bone
cells (osteoclasts, osteoblasts, stem cells) with the mineralized bone
matrix and growth factors, including receptor activator of nuclear
factor κβ ligand (RANKL) and transmembrane signaling receptors of
the tumor necrosis factor (TNF) superfamily, such as TNF-α, runtrelated transcription factor 2 (RUNX2), and transforming growth
factor-beta (TGF-β). Continued tumor growth depends on successful
replication and establishment of a tumor-specific blood supply. In
general, it is estimated that fewer than 0.1% of cancer cells survive the
transport process to the end organ.
An extensive and growing list of factors play a role in the homing of
cancer cells to the bone marrow niche. EMT involves mesenchymal
growth factors, including TGF-β, platelet-derived growth factor, epidermal growth factor (EGF), and hepatocyte growth factor. Hypoxiainducible factor–α and TGF-β signals promote bone metastases
independently by tumor-secreted chemokines such as CXCR4 that
promote tumor cell homing to bone.7 Vascular endothelial growth
factor stimulates tumor angiogenesis and increases osteoclastic and
osteoblastic activity.8,9 Degradation of the local stroma (lysis of
255

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
matrix) uses matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs; produced both by
many cancer cells and fibroblasts), cathepsins (elevated in prostate
carcinoma), urokinase-type plasminogen activator, CD26 dipeptidylpeptidase IV (produced by prostate carcinoma and CD34-positive
marrow progenitor cells), and even prostate-specific antigen (PSA).7
Osteopontin has emerged as a glycophosphoprotein secreted by cancer
cells and osteoblasts that has effects on cell-to-extracellular adhesion,
especially along trabecular bone surfaces, chemotaxis, host immune
suppression, and angiogenesis.7 The ability of breast cancer cells to form
osteolytic metastases requires the production of osteoclast-activating
factors, such as parathyroid hormone–related protein (PTH-rP), interleukins IL-11 and IL-6, TNF-α, and granulocyte–macrophage colony
stimulating factor (GM-CSF). These first three factors in turn promote
the secretion of RANKL that induces osteoclast formation, function,
mobilization, and survival,6,7,10 part of the multifactorial process of bone
destruction. GM-CSF directly promotes osteoclastogenesis. In lung
cancer, PTH-rP accounts for hypercalcemia.5 Activated osteoclasts
degrade the bone matrix, releasing cytokines that are normally stored
in the bone matrix via TGF-β, bone morphogenetic proteins, insulinlike growth factors, and basic fibroblast growth factors.6 These factors,
in turn, can act on the cancer cells and perpetuate a cycle of micrometastasis outgrowth. Osteoblastic bone metastases invoke tumor-secreted
endothelin.8,11 The canonical Wnt pathway is also involved in bone
formation with metastasis, especially prostatic carcinoma.6 Expression
of these secreted factors would be unlikely to provide a selective advantage in another metastatic site or in the primary tumor, yet they are
essential for the development of the osteolytic and osteoblastic lesions
seen in bone.5 The bone marrow microenvironment is affected by multiple factors, including oxygen tension, pH, nutrient substrate, growth
factors, calcium, and phosphorus, that enable circulating cancer cells to
deposit, survive, and develop, making the skeleton, especially red
marrow, the most common site for metastatic disease.12
Incidence and Demographics
Metastatic tumors are diagnosed 25 to 35 times more frequently than
primary osseous neoplasms. Incidence is increasing due to longer survival resulting from improvements in the management of the primary
disease; at least 400,000 people in the United States each year are
reported to have bone metastases.12 However, the true incidence of
metastatic disease to bone is difficult to ascertain because pathologic
examination of the entire skeleton is rarely performed at autopsy. It has
been estimated that 350,000 patients die each year in the United States
with skeletal metastases.
It is generally well accepted that any new osseous lesion in an adult
older than 40 years of age should be considered a potential metastasis
until proven otherwise. Although metastases are more common in
older patients, metastatic disease is well described in the pediatric
population. There is no sex predilection.
In adults, five primary carcinomas (prostate, thyroid, breast, lung,
and kidney) account for 80% of bone metastases. Prostate accounts for
60% of metastases in males; 90% of men with high-grade prostatic
adenocarcinoma have bone metastases. In females, breast accounts for
70% of bone metastases; 80% of women with advanced-stage breast
cancer have bone metastases.8 HER2-positive patients develop bone
marrow metastases earlier than HER2-negative patients. Time to
development of bone marrow metastasis is significantly shorter in
multicentric breast carcinomas with perinodal infiltration. The thyroid
and kidney are the most common solitary bone metastases, and kidney
and lung metastases can enter the arterial system to distal extremities.
Liver and pancreas carcinomas and malignant paraganglioma are also
osteotropic. Melanoma seldom metastasizes to bone,13 representing
approximately 5% to 7% of cases, and may include spindle cell and
256

amelanotic variants. When the site of origin remains occult after initial
clinical and radiographic workup, a lung primary is ultimately diagnosed in approximately 50% of cases. Bone may be the first site of
presentation of malignancy in up to 15% of cancer patients and, in 20%
to 60% of patients, a primary lesion may never be found.14
In children, neuroblastoma, medulloblastoma, osteosarcoma, and
Ewing sarcoma are the most common malignancies to metastasize to
bone. Exceedingly rare are renal clear cell sarcoma and rhabdomyosarcoma metastases to bone, the latter with multiple lytic lesions. Metastases in neuroblastoma may manifest as multiple symmetrical or
solitary lesions, involving skull, ribs, long bone, spine, and pelvis.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The axial skeleton, where “red marrow” is located, is a much more
common metastatic site than the appendicular skeleton. Thoracic vertebrae are the most common site for metastasis overall; in adults, the
proximal femur is the most common site for pathologic fracture due
to metastasis. Tumors arising in the lung apex (Pancoast tumor) or
other locations within the lung or mediastinum may extend directly
into the ribs and cervical vertebra. Aggressive skin or soft tissue tumors
that may also directly invade bone include higher grade sarcomatoid
squamous cell carcinoma, especially of the head and neck, epithelioid
sclerosing fibrosarcoma, and synovial sarcoma. The pelvis may be
affected by bladder urothelial (transitional cell) carcinoma or rectal
adenocarcinoma, the lower thoracic and lumbar vertebrae by pancreatic cancer, and the base of skull by nasopharyngeal carcinoma. Metastases are more common in the proximal than distal extremities.
Metastatic disease rarely involves the bones of the hands and feet, but
when it does occur, tumors most commonly originate in the lung,
kidney, or colon. Metastases are generally multiple but can be solitary,
particularly in cases of metastatic renal cell carcinoma or thyroid carcinomas. Prognosis depends on the type of primary malignancy and
other sites of disease; breast cancer patients have a better outcome with
bone metastases alone than in the setting of concurrent visceral metastases, whereas bone metastases in prostate carcinoma or malignant
paraganglioma currently have an absolutely dismal prognosis.
Bone metastases typically have a significant impact on quality of
life; complications include pain, pathologic fracture, spinal cord and
nerve root compression (paralysis), hypercalcemia, and marrow suppression (anemia).8 Pain is one of the most common presenting symptoms and one of the most significant clinical consequences associated
with skeletal metastases, but the relationship between pain and osseous
metastatic disease is not well understood. In a study examining the
relationship between pain and scintigraphic abnormalities in patients
with breast or prostate carcinoma, Palmer and colleagues found that
20% of patients with osseous metastases reported no pain and 36% of
patients with breast cancer reported pain without a scintigraphic
abnormality.15 In this same study, there was poor correlation between
site of the scintigraphic abnormality and a complaint of pain; however,
it was noted that lumbar spine lesions were most likely to be reported
as painful (45%). In another study, patients with prostate cancer and
pain had metastases 47% of the time, but in patients with breast cancer,
metastases were more common in patients without pain than in
patients with pain (70% versus 30%).16 Therefore, lack of pain does not
exclude the diagnosis of osseous metastatic disease, and pain in and of
itself is a poor predictor of metastatic disease.
Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy, characterized by woven to wovenlamellar periosteal new bone formation of the long and short tubular
bones, is occasionally associated with clubbing and joint swelling. It is
most common in metastatic pulmonary carcinoma (3% to 10% of
patients) (see Radiologic Features of Metastatic Disease, Periosteal
Reaction on Imaging).

Metastases to Bone
Hypercalcemia of malignancy is a paraneoplastic syndrome occurring in 10% of patients with cancer. It is most commonly associated
with bone metastatic disease but may be present without osseous
metastases. Symptoms and signs include confusion, coma, muscle
weakness, polyuria and polydipsia, nausea and vomiting, and dehydration. Hypercalcemia is considered a medical emergency and carries a
very poor prognosis; median survival is only 3 months following recognition of hypercalcemia. By definition, parathyroid hormone is not
elevated. Instead, hypercalcemia is due to PTH-rP, which shares many
of the biologic effects of native parathormone17 and which is detectable
in 80% to 90% of patients with hypercalcemia of malignancy. A number
of additional osteolytic cytokines produced by tumor cells have been
implicated, including TNF, TGF-β, procathepsin D, IL-1 and IL-6, CSF,
and prostaglandins of the E series. The combination of osteolytic bone
metastases and hypercalcemia is most suggestive of squamous cell
carcinoma of lung.

Radiologic Features of Metastatic Disease
Metastases are generally classified radiologically as lytic, sclerotic, or
mixed patterns of bone involvement (Table 17-1).
Radiology of Osteolytic Metastases
Lytic metastases are not specific as to the site of origin of the primary
tumor and may span the range from geographic to permeative
bone destruction (Fig. 17-1). Metastases that are commonly “lytic”
include primary renal cell carcinoma, breast carcinoma, thyroid neoplasms, pulmonary tumors, uterine cancers, pheochromocytoma/
paraganglioma, melanoma, rarely gastrointestinal tumors (colorectal),
hepatocellular carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma of skin, head and
neck tumors, and vascular neoplasms. These tumors are lytic because
they produce an osteoclast-activating factor. These factors include
prostaglandins, TGF-α, PTHrP, TNF, and lytic enzymes. The two most
common primary tumors causing lytic metastases are from the breast
and lung. Lytic lesions with marked expansile remodeling of bone (a
“blowout” pattern) are somewhat more specific for renal cell and
thyroid carcinomas (which may show marked hypervascularity),
although the pattern also may be seen with breast and lung carcinomas
(Fig. 17-2). A mixed lytic/blastic pattern is seen in up to 25% of lytic
metastases as a response to bone loss, particularly with breast, lung,
and gastrointestinal tract primary tumors.6 The amount of lytic
destruction needed before a lesion becomes evident on radiographs
depends on the specific location of the lesion within bone. Cortical or

Table 17-1. Metastatic Radiographic Patterns of Primary Carcinomas

intracortical lesions are readily evident even when the lesions are very
small. On the other hand, intramedullary lesions without cortical
involvement can be quite large before they are readily detectable on
radiographs, particularly in the spine, where greater than 50% to 75%
of vertebral body destruction may be necessary for detection.

17

Radiology of Osteoblastic Metastases
Osteoblastic metastases are most often (90% to 95%) identified with
prostate and breast carcinomas, although carcinoid tumors, medulloblastomas, transitional cell carcinoma of the bladder, mucinous gastrointestinal malignancies, and small cell lung carcinomas also may have
blastic metastases. Radiographs typically reveal multifocal areas of
sclerosis (Fig. 17-3). Osteoblastic activity can be observed on bone scan
and by measuring serum alkaline phosphatase and osteocalcin levels.6
Osteoblastic stimulation results in new woven bone formation. The
addition of this woven bone to the existing trabeculae causes increased
radiographic density.
Imaging of Metastatic Disease
Multiple imaging studies, including magnetic resonance imaging
(MRI), bone scintigraphy, computed tomography (CT), and positron
emission tomography/CT (or PET/MRI) can help with diagnosis and
staging of tumors of all types. Imaging studies should be based on
pertinent findings gleaned from the history, physical examination, and
laboratory results. For example, if the patient is a heavy smoker with
a chronic cough, then chest radiographs and perhaps chest CT would
be appropriate, whereas if patient is an elderly man with an elevated
PSA value, further imaging studies of the prostate and a prostate biopsy
may be useful. Additional imaging studies should be performed with
the intent of establishing a diagnosis of a primary bone tumor or
metastasis and determining the extent of disease. However, despite
advanced imaging, the primary tumor location may not be apparent
in 3% to 60% of cases.
Spinal Involvement on Imaging
Hematopoietic bone marrow, in the spine in particular, is the most
common site of skeletal metastatic disease on pathologic examination.
Spinal involvement is followed, in decreasing order of frequency, by
involvement of the pelvis, ribs, skull, and femur. Clinical presentations
of spinal involvement range from asymptomatic patients to those with
back and/or radicular pain, weakness, sensory deficits, bowel and/or
bladder dysfunction, pathologic fracture, cord compression, and paralysis. Spinal cord compression occurs in approximately 5% of patients
with metastatic disease due to intradural and/or extradural disease.
Imaging of a patient with a known malignancy and suspected spinal
cord compression should be performed emergently because the disease
may progress rapidly. Radiographs should be performed initially. The
thoracic and lumbar vertebrae are the most commonly involved spine
segments, and the vertebral body is more frequently affected than the
posterior elements. The classic radiographic finding of an absent
pedicle (“winking owl sign”) is a late feature representing extension
from vertebral body disease into the pedicle (Fig. 17-4).
Vertebral collapse is a common manifestation of metastatic disease;
however, up to 30% of patients with metastatic disease have a nonmalignant cause for the compression fracture.18 Structural analytic investigations indicate that 51% or more of cross-sectional area destruction
places a vertebral body at increased risk for pathologic fracture.19
Although it may be impossible to radiographically differentiate benign
from malignant collapse, some important diagnostic clues may be
helpful. The presence of intravertebral gas in a collapsed vertebral body
almost invariably indicates fracture or avascular necrosis and virtually
excludes the diagnosis of neoplasm. A benign compression fracture in

A

Figure 17-2. Thyroid cancer metastatic disease with a marked expansile lesion (“blowout” lesion) involving the ischial region (arrows) on an oblique hip radiograph (A). There is

Figure 17-3. Blastic metastases in a patient with breast cancer showing multifocal

the cervical or upper thoracic spine (above T6, particularly without
multiple compressions of the lower thoracic spine) is rare. Fractures in
these regions should raise concern for a neoplasm. Angular end-plate
deformity and osseous destruction (see Fig. 17-4) are not typically
associated with benign osteoporotic compression fractures. These
latter findings should suggest the presence of a pathologic process such
as metastatic disease.
MRI is the single best method to detect metastatic disease in the
spine because it allows accurate, multiplanar assessment of bone

marrow, epidural and intradural spaces, and the spinal cord. Marrow
involvement typically presents as areas of replacement of fat with
tumor demonstrating an intermediate signal on T1-weighted images
and a high signal on T2-weighted images (see Fig. 17-4). Sclerotic
metastases may demonstrate a low signal on both T1- and T2-weighted
images. The entire vertebral body may be involved, or focal rounded
areas of marrow replacement may be seen (see Fig. 17-4). MRI may
help distinguish a benign from a malignant compression fracture. A
benign osteoporotic fracture is suggested when a retropulsed bone
fragment is seen, when the fat signal is preserved on T1-weighted
images throughout the vertebral body, and when there is no high signal
on T2-weighted images and horizontal bandlike areas representing the
fracture plane are seen after administration of gadolinium. A malignant etiology of collapse is suggested when the posterior cortex is
convex toward the spinal canal, an epidural mass is seen, when the
entire vertebral body or pedicles are replaced by a low signal on
T1-weighted images, and when a high or heterogeneous signal is seen
within the body after gadolinium injection or on T2-weighted images.20
Additional MRI sequences such as contrast enhancement and diffusion
imaging also may be used to distinguish pathologic from osteoporotic
spine fractures.
CT is a useful tool to help differentiate benign from malignant
compression fractures and can be used in those patients unable to
safely undergo MRI. In one study,21 CT findings suggesting a benign
fracture included a retropulsed posterior fragment, cortical fragments
without destruction, clearly identifiable fracture lines within cancellous bone, an intravertebral vacuum phenomenon, and a thin diffuse
paraspinal soft tissue mass or thickening. Malignant indicators included
destruction of cortical or cancellous bone in a pedicle or body, a focal
paraspinal soft tissue mass, and an epidural mass (see Fig. 17-4).
Typical bone scintigraphic findings are scattered foci of increased
uptake that are not linear and not along the vertebral end plates or in
the area of the facet joints (Fig. 17-5).

260

Metastases to Bone
Homogeneously dense vertebral bodies are referred to as “ivory”
vertebrae, a term coined by Souques and Lafourcade in 1925 (Fig.
17-6).22 Because this case was reported 15 years before Batson published his work on the vertebral plexus (see previous discussion), the
authors had difficulty explaining the pathway of spread in a breast
cancer patient who had no other evidence of metastatic disease. An
“ivory” vertebra is not specific for metastatic disease; Pagetic involvement accounts for approximately 50% of cases of “ivory” vertebrae.23
Nearly 30% of cases are due to metastatic Hodgkin lymphoma, with
other blastic metastases accounting for most of the remaining cases.
Rarely, chordomas and unusual cases of vertebral osteomyelitis or
osteosarcoma may give an “ivory” vertebral appearance.
Periosteal Reaction on Imaging
Focal proliferative periosteal reaction is an uncommon manifestation
of metastatic disease. With an underlying dense lesion, however, it may
be florid enough to simulate a primary osteosarcoma and is most commonly associated with metastatic prostate carcinoma. Other tumors
associated with a significant periosteal reaction include neuroblastoma, bronchogenic carcinoma (Fig. 17-7), and gastrointestinal tract
malignancies.
Cortical Metastases on Imaging
Bronchogenic carcinoma is the most common source of cortical or
intracortical metastases. Large cortical lesions often have a “cookie
cutter” or “cookie bite” appearance (Fig. 17-8).24 If there is more than
50% cortical destruction, the risk of impending pathologic fracture is
25% to 66% (see Fig. 17-8). Multiple small cortical lesions are seen
most commonly in patients with lung cancer. When multiple, cortical
metastases can mimic primary bone malignancies such as hemangioendothelioma or angiosarcoma.
Synovial Involvement on Imaging
Metastatic involvement of the synovium is rare. It may occur by direct
extension from disease in the adjacent bones or by hematogenous

spread. Breast and lung primary lesions account for most of the cases
that have been reported. The knee is the most commonly affected joint,
and cases have been reported after total joint replacement.25 Very
rarely, arthropathy due to synovial metastatic disease is the initial
manifestation of malignancy. Imaging findings are nonspecific,
although joint effusion and thickening of the synovium have been
described. If a destructive process is evident in the adjacent osseous
structures, these findings are more suspicious, as is aspiration of serosanguineous joint fluid. Synovial biopsy is usually definitive. Metastatic
involvement of the synovium has a poor prognosis.26

17

Pathologic Fracture on Imaging
Pathologic fracture may have a significant impact on the remaining
quality of life and overall survival of a cancer patient. One report of
145 patients with pathologic femur fractures found a mean survival of
only 11 months.27 An increased risk of death of 20% to 30% after
pathologic fracture also has been reported.28
The incidence of pathologic fracture in patients with metastatic
disease is 7% to 27%.29 Pathologic fracture is more likely with breast
metastases (53% to 59%) than other primary tumors and is more
common in the lower extremity than the upper one. Metastatic tumor
should always be considered when a subtrochanteric transverse
fracture is seen and with nontraumatic avulsion of the lesser
trochanter.30
To avoid unnecessary biopsy, pathologic fractures must be differentiated from stress fractures, particularly of the insufficiency type.
Resorptive and reparative changes, which may mimic malignancy histologically, can be particularly prominent in areas of previously irradiated bone. The linear or bandlike morphology of insufficiency fractures
and their distribution, particularly in the pubic rami and sacrum where
the H-shaped (“Honda”) sign31 may be seen, can be helpful in distinguishing insufficiency fractures from pathologic fractures due to
metastases. This sign also can be appreciated on coronal CT and MRI
scans. Cross-sectional imaging typically reveals a masslike marrow
replacement in the setting of pathologic fracture.

sclerosis from prostate carcinoma metastases on a lateral spine radiograph
(A) and sagittal macrosection (B).

Metastases to Bone
Healing of pathologic fractures occurs in fewer than 35% of all
cases32 but is more likely in the setting of breast and prostate cancer.
Pathologic fractures may be associated with an increased risk of later
development of pulmonary metastases. Because this risk may be minimized by prophylactic fixation after irradiation (see Fig. 17-1), recognition of impending pathologic fracture is critical.33 Two criteria for
prophylactic fixation of an impending long bone fracture include (1)
a well-defined lucent defect greater than 2.5 cm in dimension that
involves the cortex or is painful and (2) 50% total cortical destruction
on combined anteroposterior and lateral radiographs.34 These criteria
are based on routine radiographic examinations and may be poor
predictors of fracture. The lesional dimension of 2.5 cm applies only
to the femur, requires a well-defined lytic lesion, and does not account
for baseline mineralization of the affected patient. Also, the criterion
of 50% cortical destruction is limited by the difficulty of accurate
assessment on radiographs and the challenges presented by poorly
defined lesions. Neither criterion addresses blastic lesions.
Because of these limitations, other scoring systems have been developed to help the orthopedic surgeon decide when surgery might be
needed for a long bone lesion. One such scoring system was developed
by Mirels35 and includes lesion location (upper versus lower extremity),
size, appearance (blastic versus lytic), and presence or absence of pain
(Table 17-2). Lesions with a score of 7 or lower are managed nonoperatively, whereas those with a score of 8 or higher are typically considered for surgery because lesions with scores of 9 had a 33% fracture
risk compared with a 4% risk for those with scores less than or equal
to 7. For the flat bones, treatment criteria are not as well defined, except
for acetabular lesions. Harrington categorizes acetabular defects as
follows: class I defects are small, with intact acetabular walls and cortices; class II defects are larger, with involvement of the medial wall;
and class III defects are massive, with additional involvement of the
lateral cortex and dome.36 This surgical classification system also
describes the appropriate reconstruction options for each category.
Radiologic Differential Diagnosis
The radiologic differential diagnosis for multiple bone metastases
includes osteoporosis, multiple myeloma or plasmacytoma, primary
bone tumor, chronic recurrent multifocal osteomyelitis, polyostotic
fibrous dysplasia, polyostotic Paget disease, multiple brown tumors,
Langerhans cell histiocytosis, granulomatous process (e.g., sarcoid),
and insufficiency fracture. By radiologic imaging, metastases in the
hands and feet may resemble infection, rheumatoid arthritis, enchondroma, or giant cell tumor.

Pathologic Features of Metastatic Disease
Histopathology and Immunohistochemistry
In adults, the most common tumors to metastasize to bone are best
considered as the sandwich acronym BLTKP (bacon, lettuce, tomato,
with a kosher pickle) for breast, lung, thyroid, kidney, and prostate.

> 23

These primary sites account for 80% of skeletal metastases. In children,
metastases to bone include “RE-WON” or “OWNER” for osteosarcoma, Wilms tumor, neuroblastoma, Ewing sarcoma, and rhabdomyosarcoma. Neuroblastoma is the most common and emphasizes the
round cell theme for most of these osteophilic childhood neoplasms.
Breast carcinoma is the most prevalent of bone metastases and the
most common to metastasize to bone in females. Although there is
controversy, it is considered by some that the invasive lobular subtype
of breast carcinoma is more likely to metastasize to bone than invasive
ductal carcinoma; however, with a mixed radiologic pattern or with a
low-grade primary carcinoma, ductal is thought to be more frequent
than lobular (Fig. 17-9).37 Invasive lobular carcinoma often demonstrates cords or single infiltrating tumor cells, embedded in a desmoplastic stroma. Classic invasive lobular carcinoma cells are medium to
small in size and epithelioid in shape. Occasionally, pleomorphic highgrade invasive lobular forms exist. Ductal carcinoma has tubules or
more commonly sheets or nests embedded in a desmoplastic or myxoid
stroma. Sometimes, the tumor cells appear plasmacytoid and have
cytoplasmic vacuoles; only a few cells may be noted on biopsy and
these should be immunostained with pankeratin on scant material,
especially if the imaging and clinical history are suspicious. Lobular
carcinoma may be separated from the ductal phenotype by E-cadherin
immunostaining, which is typically negative in lobular cells but positive for ductal phenotype. Mammary carcinoma cells are often positive
for cytokeratin 7 (CK7), negative for CK20, and, occasionally positive
for mammaglobin. Mammaglobin has high specificity but low sensitivity. BRST-2 (GCDFP-12) is better for apocrine-type mammary carcinoma and also can be positive in adnexal tumors. Knowing the estrogen
receptor, progesterone receptor, and HER2/neu status of the original
primary tumor is helpful, but this may be different in a metastasis, and
these prognostic markers should be repeated in metastatic tumor populations, the ER and PR for confirmation of phenotype if previously
positive and the HER2 if previously negative. Caution should be used
for HER2/neu on decalcified specimens. CK7-positive ductal carcinoma of breast can be distinguished from a lung primary by thyroid
transcription factor-1 (TTF-1) because it is nuclear negative in breast
cancer but nuclear positive in lung adenocarcinoma.
Prostatic carcinoma is the most common tumor to metastasize to
bone in males (Fig. 17-10). Antibodies directed against PSA and prostatic acid phosphatase are often used as specific immunostains for
prostatic carcinoma. Prostatic adenocarcinoma is also positive for pankeratin, CK18 by itself, and usually for PSA, although very high-grade
prostatic adenocarcinoma may lose PSA reactivity. Although the risk
of bone metastasis is less with a lower Gleason score (less than 6, welldifferentiated) prostatic adenocarcinoma, there is still a risk of skeletal
metastasis independent of grade or low PSA (less than 10 ng/mL).38 Of
high-grade prostatic carcinomas, a high percentage metastasize
to bone (90%).6 CK7 and CK20 are often negative in prostatic
adenocarcinoma.
Lung is often the primary site of bone metastases of tumors that
initially present with an unknown primary site (Fig. 17-11). Small cell
carcinoma is most likely to metastasize widely, but the most common
subtype diagnosed in skeletal metastases is adenocarcinoma, followed
by squamous cell carcinoma, small cell and large cell carcinoma, and
adenosquamous carcinoma.39 As noted previously, squamous cell carcinoma can cause lytic lesions and hypercalcemia as a paraneoplastic
syndrome. To a lesser extent, small cell carcinoma can also cause
hypercalcemia, but is more often blastic than lytic. Small cell carcinoma has a moderately sized, round nucleus with scant cytoplasm and
a well-dispersed chromatin pattern, often along with nuclear molding.
There is brisk mitotic activity and often crush artifact. Small cell carcinoma tumor cells are positive for pankeratin and the neuroendocrine

has relatively few cells on biopsy and can be difficult to recognize as breast-specific carcinoma (A) without a history or immunostains. Features include cytoplasmic vacuoles and
a plasmacytoid appearance; this could be pleomorphic lobular or ductal carcinoma depending on E-cadherin reactivity (negative in pleomorphic lobular) (B). A second case of metastatic invasive ductal carcinoma of breast primary to bone (C and D) is positive for the following immunostains: estrogen receptor (E), breast markers mammoglobin (F), GCDFP
(G), and E-cadherin (H), supporting a ductal phenotype.

17

A

B

C

Figure 17-10. Prostatic adenocarcinoma is the most common metastasis to bone in males. With a progressively more poorly differentiated
(higher Gleason score) lesion, it is more likely to metastasize to bone
(A–C). The highest grade prostatic adenocarcinoma (C) may completely
lose prostate-specific antigen reactivity, making clinical history even
more important than a positive pankeratin.

markers synaptophysin, chromogranin, and neuron-specific enolase,
along with TTF-1. It is often difficult to distinguish adenocarcinoma
from squamous cell carcinoma of lung, and there are tumors with
mixed features. Squamous cell carcinoma of lung is often at least focally
if not diffusely positive for CK5/6 and p63. Adenocarcinoma may have
intracytoplasmic mucin (positive for mucicarmine), be immunoreactive with TTF-1, CK18, pankeratin, epithelial membrane antigen
(EMA), and CK7 antibodies, and negative for CK20.
Renal cell carcinoma metastatic to bone is notorious for being a solitary lesion (Fig. 17-12). Renal cell carcinoma metastases often mimic
the histologic spectrum presented in primary neoplasms: nests, clear
cell change, and papillary and epithelioid features are common. Of the
renal cell carcinomas, clear cell carcinoma is the most common subtype
to metastasize to bone.40 The renal cell carcinoma markers CD10, EMA,
CK18, and occasionally CK7 can be helpful for chromophobe and papillary subtypes; notably, pankeratin may be negative in all types.
Thyroid follicular carcinoma (hematogenous spread) and less commonly papillary carcinoma (usually lymphatic spread) and only
exceedingly rarely medullary carcinoma metastasize to bone (Fig.
17-13).41 Follicular carcinoma is a well-differentiated thyroid malignancy. It is slow growing and tends to metastasize to remote organs,
including bone, at an advanced stage. In most of the reported cases of
follicular thyroid carcinoma metastasizing to bone, metastases
occurred long after the diagnosis and initiation of treatment for the
primary tumor.42 Follicular thyroid carcinoma also can present as a
solitary metastasis. Histologically, follicular carcinoma typically displays colloid, whereas papillary thyroid carcinoma shows the nuclear
cytologic features typical for that entity. Both follicular and papillary
carcinomas are positive for TTF-1 and thyroglobulin. Medullary carcinoma is positive for calcitonin, chromogranin and rarely TTF-1.

Hepatocellular carcinoma metastasizes to bone and demonstrates
bile production by hepatocytes. These are positive for hepatocellular
carcinoma marker and polyclonal carcinoembryonic antigen. The incidence of bone metastases from hepatocellular carcinoma seems to be
increasing.43
Rarely, gastrointestinal carcinomas, particularly colorectal carcinomas, metastasize to bone. Colorectal carcinoma to bone is a late
finding, typically after metastasis to other sites. Metastatic tumors are
histologically similar to that seen in the primary site and may be cribriform or sheeted, hyperchromatic and pleomorphic, with mitotic
activity and “dirty” necrosis.3,43 Colorectal carcinoma is positive for
CDX2, CK20, and carcinoembryonic antigen and negative for CK7 by
immunohistochemistry. Carcinoid tumor, a more well-differentiated
tumor, also can rarely metastasize to bone and is recognized by its
neuroendocrine immunophenotype (Fig. 17-14).44
Malignant paraganglioma of the retroperitoneum has emerged as a
tumor that has a propensity to metastasize to bone. Although this
tumor45 preferentially metastasizes to lymph node, once it metastasizes
to bone, the prognosis is dismal. These tumors are identified by
their large, confluent, oval, zellballen-like configuration with central
necrosis. Immunostains include S-100 protein positivity at the periphery of larger oval clusters of tumor cells, although the sustantacular
cells when metastasized to bone are often nonreactive and neuroendocrine markers including synaptophysin and chromogranin may be
helpful.
Soft tissue and skin tumors can involve bone, usually by direct
extension, or may be primary within bone (Fig. 17-15). Spindle cell
squamous cell carcinoma of the skin in head and neck locations is often
high grade and nonkeratinizing, and it may directly extend into the
temporal bone from the ear or other site. Some soft tissue tumors have

papillary carcinoma (B) more commonly presents with extrathyroidal metastases,

Figure 17-14. Carcinoid tumors, usually of gastrointestinal origin, can metastasize to

Figure 17-15. Soft tissue tumors, such as malignant fibrous histiocytoma (undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma [UPS]) can metastasize or be primary in bone. This is a

267

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
a propensity to directly invade bone or arise in bone, including sclerosing epithelioid sarcoma (SEF) and synovial sarcoma. Sclerosing epithelioid sarcoma is composed of round tumor cells embedded in a sclerotic
stroma that sometimes mimics osteoid; the tumor is generally negative
for pankeratin, S-100 protein, desmin, and CD34.46 MUC-4 immunoreactivity has been identified. There is morphologic overlap with lowgrade fibromyxoid sarcoma, and some sclerosing epithelioid sarcomas
demonstrate an FUS gene rearrangment.47 There is also morphologic
overlap with ossifying fibromyxoid tumor. Synovial sarcoma can secondarily or rarely primarily involve bone.48 This monophasic or biphasic tumor demonstrates immunostaining for focal keratins, especially
CK7 and EMA, and often has psammoma-like or prominent bone
formation within the tumor. Multiple other soft tissue tumors have
been reported as “primary” in bone, including leiomyosarcoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, and alveolar soft part sarcoma, yet these are far more
common in soft tissue.
Adults can also have lymphoma and multiple myeloma as the differential diagnosis for a bone lesion. Lymphoma primary to bone is
most commonly diffuse large B-cell type. It is composed of large cells
with irregular contours, often showing crush artifact, desmoplasia,
occasional clear cell change, and mitotic activity. Diffuse large B-cell
lymphoma is positive for CD45, CD20, CD79a, BCL-6, and CD10 (of
follicular origin). Multiple myeloma can sometimes be difficult to recognize as sheets of plasma cells, when the cells are dystrophic and
exhibit prominent nucleoli, eccentric nuclei, and are without a hoff.
The cells will be restricted for κ twice as commonly as λ and are immunoreactive for CD38 and 138. A search for other sequelae of multiple
myeloma is helpful (e.g., Bence Jones protein in urine, a spike in serum
protein electrophoresis).
Most childhood tumors that metastasize to bone are small round
blue cell tumors. Round cell tumors can enter the bone via small
vascular channels. Of the small round blue cell tumors that can be
diagnosed in bone, neuroblastoma is the most common tumor to
metastasize to bone. Medulloblastoma, an intracranial embryonal
malignancy of the cerebellum in children, can metastasize to bone
outside of the cranium, although its most common site for metastasis
is bone.49 Composed of small to medium round cells, with neuroendocrine features including rosettes, it demonstrates glial fibrillary acid
protein (GFAP) positivity as well as GFAP-positive intratumoral astrocytes. Medulloblastoma has similar features to neuroblastoma. Neuroblastoma is a neuroendocrine tumor of the sympathetic nervous
system that can arise from the adrenal glands, neck, chest, abdomen,
or pelvis. In any site, neuroblastoma is composed of varying proportions of small round cells (undifferentiated), differentiating ganglion
cells that may be more differentiated after treatment, and neuropil and
schwannian stroma (Fig. 17-16). Immunostains for neuroblastoma
include S-100 protein, GFAP, and the neuroblastoma marker NB84.
MYCN amplification portends a worse prognosis.
Other predominantly childhood round cell tumors that metastasize
to bone include rhabdomyosarcoma (almost exclusively alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma), a tumor that occurs in older children and young
adults. Alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma commonly arises in the extremities; it is less likely to be of paraspinal, perineal, paranasal, urogenital,
or of orbit origin. The tumors can have an alveolar or solid pattern;
often the former can be found at least focally. In addition to desmin,
these tumors are often more strongly positive for myoregulatory
protein MyoD1 than skeletal muscle specific myogenin (myf4), but
both demonstrate definitive skeletal muscle phenotype. Alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma has a specific molecular event—a reciprocal translocation of PAX3 or PAX7 with FOXO1(FKHR). The majority have a (2;13)
(q35;q14) versus less commonly seen t(1;13)(p36;q14) translocation,
respectively. Rhabdomyosarcoma is much more likely to metastastize
268

to bone if there is prior local invasion or lymph node metastasis50
(Fig. 17-17). Round cell tumors in bone in children are most commonly neuroblastoma (in young children with a history) and include
rhabdomyosarcoma, yet acute lymphocytic leukemia or other leukemias (lymphomas) can also be present in bone marrow and must be
correlated with clinical history and peripheral smear. Immunostains
can help separate the small round cell tumors, in addition to specific
molecular events (measured by polymerase chain reaction or fluorescence in situ hybridization) for a specific tumor type.
Clear cell sarcoma of the kidney (CCSK; Fig. 17-18) is an uncommon neoplasm that accounts for fewer than 5% of all pediatric renal
tumors. It has a comparatively and debatably worse outcome than
Wilms tumor, with a higher rate of relapse and greater propensity for
bone metastasis, albeit much more infrequently than neuroblastoma.
CCSK is most common in males, with a mean age of 3 years. Previously
called “bone metastasizing renal tumour of childhood,” it can be quite
large and centered in renal medulla and is composed of nests or cords
of epithelioid or spindled cells separated by regularly spaced, arborizing fibrovascular septa. The extracellular myxoid material mimics clear
cytoplasm. Nuclei are round to oval shaped with fine chromatin and
an absence of prominent nucleoli, with a high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic
ratio, and they may be anaplastic. These tumors are positive for B-cell
lymphoma 2 and vimentin and are negative for CD34, S-100 protein,
desmin, CD99, cytokeratin, and EMA. Although involvement of perirenal lymph nodes is common at diagnosis (29% of cases), bone metastases (17% to 40%) are the most common mode of recurrence. CCSK
can also metastasize to unusual sites, including the brain, soft tissue,
and orbit.51
Pathologic Differential Diagnosis
The histologic differential diagnosis is broad and depends on the specific histologic subtype of the metastatic tumor.

Genetics of Metastatic Disease
In the past decade, microRNAs have emerged as key players in cancer
progression, but the importance of microRNAs in regulating breast
and prostate cancer metastasis to bone is only now being appreciated.52
At every step in the process of metastasis to bone from the primary
site, oncogenes and suppressors genes are involved. The microRNAs
miR-335 and miR-126 suppress the metastasis of breast carcinoma,
whereas mir10b promotes metastasis.
The genes that promote cell motility, EMT, extracellular matrix
degradation, bone marrow progenitor mobilization, angiogenesis, and
evasion of the immune system can be defined as metastasis initiation
genes. Metastasis oncogenes, including TWiST1, SNAi1, and SNAi2
(also known as SluG),53 promote the EMT process.54 During EMT, the
epithelial marker E-cadherin is down-regulated and mesenchymal
markers are up-regulated. Genes that allow transformed cells to invade
the surrounding tissue and attract a supportive stroma also facilitate
the dispersion of cancer cells and probably continue to do so even after
cancer cells infiltrate distant tissues.
Other determinants of invasion are components and modulators of
the hepatocyte growth factor (HGF)–HGF receptor (HGFR) pathway,
such as metadherin in breast cancer and the metastasis-associated in
colon cancer 1 (MACC1) gene in colorectal carcinoma. Metastatic
growth is also initiated by the suppression of noncoding RNAs, such
as miR-126 and miR-335 in both breast and colorectal carcinomas. The
expression of these metastasis initiation genes and their targets predicts a poor prognosis in particular types of cancer.7
Genes involved in tumor initiation functions for growth, survival,
creation of a progenitor-like state, and genomic instability include

Metastases to Bone

17

A

B

C

D

E

Figure 17-16. Neuroblastoma, a round cell sympathetic neuroendocrine tumor of
childhood, most commonly metastasizes to bone and can have undifferentiated features with rosettes (A–C) or differentiated ganglioneuroblastoma to final maturation
as ganglioneuroma (D and E) features, especially common after treatment.

269

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

Figure 17-17. Rhabdomyosarcoma (A, B), especially the alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma subtype, rarely causes multiple lytic lesions in bone. It is most common in patients with local
spread and lymph node involvement.

oncogenes ERBB2, CTNNB1 (β-catenin), KRAS, PI3K, epidermal
growth factor receptor (EGFR), and MYC, as well as tumor suppressors
adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) protein, TP53, phosphatase and
tensin homolog (PTEN), and BRCA1 and BRCA2. Metastasis initiation, angiogenesis, marrow mobilization, and circulation involve
gain of TWiST1 and loss of kisspeptin 1 (KISS1). The list is extensive.7
All of the following are involved in metastasis to bone and could
provide a target for therapy: the gene ANGPTL4, which codes for
angiopoietin-like 4; APC; C-C chemokine ligand 5; Duffy antigen chemokine receptor; EGFR; epiregulin; GM-CSF; G protein–coupled
receptor 56; HGF; inhibitor of differentiation 1; interleukin; KISS1;
lysyl oxidase; MMP1; prostaglandin G/H synthase 2; PTHrP; and
TNF-α.
Treatment and Prognosis
Bone metastases can be associated with considerable morbidity and
significant compromise of patient quality of life. The prognosis is generally dismal, and many patients with bone metastases, including
patients with metastatic pulmonary adenocarcinoma and high-grade
breast carcinoma, often have a median survival of only 6 months to 1
year. Systemic therapy significantly prolongs survival in some cancer
types.55 However, bone metastases, common in men with advanced
prostate cancer, for example, are considered incurable.56 In contrast,
children with metastases (from neuroblastoma, for example) may do
well with therapy. Although 48% of children with neuroblastoma have
bone metastases at the time of diagnosis, 5-year survival rates still
approach 75%.57
The treatment of bone metastases falls into two broad categories.
The first deals with prevention and improvement of immediate quality
of life. The second focuses on systemic or regional treatment of the
original tumor, and it may be less commonly applied if there is a solitary bone metastasis, as, for example, in renal carcinoma. The management of bone metastases requires a multidisciplinary effort and
includes a team composed of medical oncologists, radiotherapists, surgeons, and interventional radiologists.
In adults with multiple bone metastases, palliative care may be
indicated when life expectancy is less than 6 months. The Takuhashi
scoring system has been used by some clinicians for prediction of
outcome in patients with spine metastases.58 Management options
should be determined based on a realistic expectation of the anticipated patient survival.
270

The Food and Drug Administration uses criteria for palliation or
prevention treatment as defined by skeletal-related events (SREs); these
specifically include pathologic fracture, spinal cord compression
(emergency), and bone pain. Prevention of SRE interventions may
include local palliative surgery (including embolization and vertebroplasty), radiation, or systemic therapy using traditional chemotherapy,
bisphosphonates, or newer molecular-targeted therapy against the
original tumor.
Surgery is used for removal of entire solitary metastases, for
impending pathologic fracture or neurologic deficit, and for decompressing tumors in the case of spinal cord metastasis. Surgical options
include a vast array of rods, nails, plates, and prosthetic implants.
Embolization of vascular metastases (particularly of renal or thyroid
origin) is a percutaneous procedure that has been performed by radiologists for more than 20 years. Vertebroplasty and kyphoplasty are
useful procedures for the treatment of painful spinal metastases. Newer
interventional techniques include percutaneous ablation for appendicular metastases or ablation combined with vertebroplasty for some
spine lesions.
Outpatient postoperative external beam radiation has been used for
palliation and pain control, with fractionation schedules to provide
relief of symptoms and prevent the morbidity of metastases (i.e., cord
compression). Specific guidelines have been developed for breast cancer
patients; x-ray therapy can provide pain relief and functional improvement for up to 1 year in 70% of patients.59 Postoperative radiation can be
used unless death is imminent or an area has previously been irradiated.
Postoperative radiation often includes the entire fixation device (e.g.,
entire femur after intramedullary nailing of a femoral lesion).
Chemotherapy may involve conventional chemotherapeutic
agents that treat the systemic malignancy, including strontium-89,
phosphorus-32, and hormonal therapies, depending on the origin of
the primary lesion. Bisphosphonates, including pamidronate or zoledronic acid, have been used to reduce the incidence and delay the onset
of SREs. These prevent osteoclastic bone destruction. These also are
used for hypercalcemia. Molecular-targeted therapy is used for all
types of cancers, both to treat original tumor as well as metastases.
RANKL-inhibitors such as denosumab inhibit osteoclastic function
(just like bisphosphonates), and prevents skeletal complications of
patients with bone metastases. These reduce osteoclastic activity by
interfering with osteoclast function to prevent additional lytic bone
destruction and decrease progression of disease and SREs.60–62

Metastases to Bone

17

A

B

C

D

E

Figure 17-18. Clear cell sarcoma of kidney (A–E) rarely metastasizes to bone, but
it more commonly has skeletal metastases than Wilms tumor.

Other molecular-targeted therapies include kinase inhibitors. The
multikinase inhibitor cabozantinib has been assessed in vitro in an
orthotopic xenograft model of castration-resistant prostate cancer that
demonstrates distinct phases of osteolytic and osteogenic activity. This
experiment demonstrated that titrated doses of anti-RANKL therapy
fully eradicated metastases. Preclinical data confirmed that blockade
of tumor-induced osteoclastogenesis by RANKL inhibition protects63
against bone destruction, inhibits progression of established bone
metastases, and delays the formation of de novo bone metastases in
cancer models.10

Patients with bone metastases with Erbb2 oncogene (also known as
neu or HER2)–positive breast tumors can have improved progressionfree survival rates when treated with ERBB2 antibody therapy.5 Similarly, patients with lung adenocarcinomas with bone metastases and
EGFR mutations respond to the EGFR kinase inhibitors gefitinib and
erlotinib. A caveat is the frequent relapse due to secondary EGFR
mutations that confer resistance, such as amplification of MET (regulated by metadherin), which encodes the tyrosine kinase receptor
HGFR that in turn increases EGFR signaling and promotes survival of
these tumors.5 Loss of BRCA2 function stimulates prostate cancer cell
271

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
invasion and is associated with more aggressive and metastatic tumors.
Concurrently, the receptor tyrosine kinase c-kit is highly expressed
in skeletal metastases of prostate cancer and parallels BRCA2
down-regulation.64
Evidence in the literature has demonstrated that some microRNAs
play a pivotal role in most solid tumor metastasis. Wang and associates
have shown that miR-100 is down-regulated in human prostate cancer
tissue compared to normal prostate and also significantly decreased in
bone metastatic prostate cancer samples compared to primary prostate
cancer. Argonaute 2 (AGO2) is the core effector protein of the
microRNA-induced silencing complex, and overexpression of AGO2
might enhance tumor metastasis. The researchers found that miR-100
negatively regulates migration, invasion, EMT, colony formation,
spheroid formation and expression of the “stemness factors” c-Myc,
Oct4, and Klf4 in PC-3 and DU145 cells and is negatively correlated
with bone metastasis in prostate cancer patients. Therefore, loss of
miR-100 promotes the metastatic ability of prostate cancer cells at least
partially by up-regulating AGO2 expression, both potential targets of
future prevention and therapy.65
In addition, future monoclonal antibodies may be directed against
specific tumor factors such as PTH-rP.
Treatment of the secondary clinical effects of bone metastasis, such
as metastatic hypercalcemia, a medical emergency, includes hydration
and loop diuretics.
Imaging has been used for measuring treatment response, although
there is no universally accepted method for assessing response of skeletal metastases after therapy. Response is assessed by a combination of
imaging, blood and urine laboratory results, and patient symptoms.
Whole-body imaging methods with a functional component, such as
PET/CT or diffusion MRI, may be utilized. Soft tissue response criteria
(RECIST) are not easily applicable to lytic bone lesions but have been
updated (RECIST2). Blastic bone lesion response is even more difficult
to evaluate.

18
Notochordal and Related Lesions
Karen J. Fritchie, MD, and Carrie Y. Inwards, MD

Ecchordosis Physaliphora/Notochordal Cell Remnants 275
Benign Notochordal Cell Tumor 276
Chordoma

278

The notochord is a rod-shaped structure that forms during the third
week of gestation and organizes formation of the axial skeleton.1,2 As
vertebral bodies undergo ossification, the notochord becomes fragmented, and by the second month of development, notochordal tissue
is typically only present in the intervertebral space, forming the
nucleus pulposus of the intervertebral disk.3 Because of this process of
notochordal degeneration, notochordal tissue is not normally found
in the vertebral body after 10 weeks’ gestation and only rarely in
the intervertebral disk after 10 years of age.4 However, studies of
human embryos and fetuses have demonstrated ectopic notochord
remnants, predominantly within the cephalic and caudal ends of the
notochord.5
Persistent notochordal remnants in adults have also been documented, predominantly involving the clivus. Virchow and Luschka
recognized notochordal remnants within the clivus at the base of the
skull as early as 1856.6,7 Virchow initially regarded these lesions as
cartilaginous and proposed the term ecchondrosis physaliphora. Two
years later, Müller suggested these proliferations were of notochordal
derivation (ecchordosis physaliphora), a hypothesis subsequently confirmed by experimental work done in 1865 by Ribbert.8–11 Today, it is
widely accepted that notochordal remnants involving the clivus
(ecchordosis physaliphora spheno-occipitalis) can be found in up to
2% of autopsies.1,12 However, there are only a few documented cases of
its occurrence in adults outside of the clivus.13–15
Today, the family of notochordal lesions includes notochordal cell
remnant(s), benign notochordal tumor, and chordoma (Table 18-1).
Although there has been long-standing general agreement regarding
the existence and significance of notochordal cell remnants at the
benign end of the spectrum, and chordoma at the malignant end, the
more recent concept of benign notochordal cell tumors, initially
described in 2001 by Mirra and Brien as giant notochordal hamartoma,

continues to evolve.16–19 The most recent World Health Organization
(WHO) term for this distinct entity is benign notochordal cell tumor.20

Ecchordosis Physaliphora/Notochordal
Cell Remnants
Definition and Synonyms
Ecchordosis physaliphora is notochordal tissue that persists beyond
the postnatal period. The condition is also known as notochord rest,
notochord cell remnant, notochordal remnant, notochord vestige,
ecchordosis physaliphora spheno-occipitalis, and ecchordosis physaliphora vertebralis.
Incidence and Demographics
As noted above, ecchordosis physaliphora is most commonly found in
the clivus at the base of the skull, where it has been found in 0.4% to
2% of autopsies.12,21,22 Only rare examples involving the mobile spine
(ecchordosis physaliphora vertebralis) and coccygeal area have been
reported.13–15 Persistent notochordal tissue within intervertebral disks,
often referred to as notochordal remnants or notochordal vestiges, of
adults is also very uncommon.3
Clinical Manifestations
Patients are typically asymptomatic. Those with lesions involving the
clivus occasionally experience symptoms including headaches, sensorineural hearing loss, diplopia, and hemiparesis.
Radiologic Features
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of ecchordosis physaliphora demonstrates a lesion that is hypointense on T1-weighted scans and hyperintense on T2-weighted scans. It shows no enhancement after
gadolinium administration.23 Computed tomography (CT) findings of
ecchordosis physaliphora are often nonspecific but may include a stalklike structure projecting from the clivus.24
Gross Features
The lesions are usually small, ranging in size from a few millimeters to
1 to 2 cm, but they occasionally measure as much as 3.5 cm. Grossly

visible lesions appear as soft, gelatinous, and pedunculated masses that
range in size from millimeters to a few centimeters.
Histopathology
Ecchordosis physaliphora is composed of a uniform population of
tumor cells arranged in small nests or cords. Cytologically, the tumor
cells contain small nuclei, granular eosinophilic cytoplasm, and intracytoplasmic vacuoles (physaliferous cells) (Fig. 18-1). Although they
share some histologic features with chordoma, they lack the nuclear
atypia, mitotic activity, mucinous pools, and lobular infiltrative growth
pattern characteristic of chordoma.25
Ancillary Studies
Ecchordosis physaliphora, benign notochordal tumor, and chordoma
all have similar immunohistochemical features. The tumor cells are
positive with antibodies directed against broad-spectrum cytokeratin
and brachyury and variably positive for S-100 protein. One study found
notochordal vestiges (ecchordosis physaliphora) to be negative for cytokeratin 18, whereas chordoma and benign notochordal cell tumors were
positive.25 However, another study found differing results, suggesting
that this marker may not be helpful in distinguishing these entities.3
Treatment and Prognosis
Ecchordosis physaliphora is benign and usually left untreated until
symptoms occur.

A

Benign Notochordal Cell Tumor
Definition and Synonyms
Benign notochordal cell tumor is a notochordal cell intraosseous lesion
of the axial skeleton with distinct histologic and radiologic features that
differ from chordoma. Synonyms include benign notochordal tumor,
benign notochordal lesion, giant notochordal rest, giant vertebral
notochordal rest, and giant notochordal hamartoma. Rare pulmonary
examples have been described.26
Incidence and Demographics
At autopsy, 14% of benign notochordal cell tumors are found in spinal
columns and 11.5% in the clivus.19 To date, the number of reports in
the literature on patients with benign notochordal tumors involving
the vertebrae is limited. There appears to be an equal sex distribution
with a relatively wide age range (11 to 57 years; mean, 39 years; median,
40 years).16 The lesions can involve any level of the vertebral column
and may be solitary or multifocal.
Clinical Manifestations
Benign notochordal cell tumors are often asymptomatic, but the most
frequent clinical presentation is chronic, sometimes severe, pain in the
lower back region.

B

Figure 18-1. Ecchordosis. An incidental autopsy finding within the clivus, measuring 1.8 cm in greatest dimension. The mass is well circumscribed (A). Lesional cells show abundant
eosinophilic to clear multivacuolated cytoplasm surrounding round to oval nuclei without atypia (B).

Notochordal and Related Lesions

A

B

D

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Benign notochordal tumors are being detected with increasing frequency by cross-sectional imaging as the radiologic features become
more widely recognized. Radiographs are usually normal, but they
occasionally show vague sclerosis without expansion of the bone (Fig.
18-2C). CT may also show no abnormality; however, most scans demonstrate some degree of vertebral body sclerosis without bone destruction. Benign notochordal cell tumors are most readily identified on
MRI, demonstrating a low T1-weighted and high T2-weighted signal
intensity that is nonenhancing following administration of contrast27,28
(see Fig. 18-2A and B). Similar to CT, changes in bone contour or
extraosseous extension are absent. Bone scans typically show no uptake
in the involved vertebral body (see Fig. 18-2D).

Figure 18-2. Benign notochordal cell tumor. Axial T2-weighted (A) and sagittal
T1-weighted (B) magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans show a lesion that has
nonspecific signal characteristics with low signal on T1 and heterogeneous predominantly high signal on T2 with the epicenter in the midline. Lateral radiograph
(C) shows a region of subtle sclerosis in the L5 vertebral body (arrow) that
correlates with the lesion on the MRI. The lesion shows no activity on the bone
scan (D).

Histopathology
Benign notochordal cell tumors are characterized by solid sheets of
cells; univacuolated and multivacuolated cells with clear cytoplasm are
mixed with smaller cells exhibiting granular eosinophilic cytoplasm
(Fig. 18-3A). Interestingly, these features bear a striking resemblance
to mature adipose tissue, thus placing them at risk of being overlooked.
The lesional nuclei are pyknotic, round to ovoid, and demonstrate at
most only minimal atypia. Islands of hematopoietic marrow may be
found within the lesion. Importantly, lobulation, extracellular myxoid
matrix/stroma, necrosis, and significant mitotic activity are absent.
The tumor permeates between host bone trabeculae, some of
which may show some sclerosis, but destructive growth is lacking18
(Fig. 18-3B).
277

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

Figure 18-3. Benign notochordal cell tumor. Low magnification shows sheets of cells with clear cytoplasm resembling adipocytes filling the marrow spaces but without destroying
host bone (A). Higher magnification illustrates cells with pale pink to clear cytoplasm, bland nuclei, and no evidence of a myxoid stroma (B).

Ancillary Studies
Benign notochordal cell tumors exhibit the same immunoprofile as
chordomas, including reactivity for cytokeratin (Fig. 18-4), epithelial
membrane antigen (EMA), S-100 protein, and brachyury.25
Differential Diagnosis
Benign notochordal tumors must be distinguished from chordoma
because the former has an indolent course and the latter is a malignant
tumor requiring therapeutic intervention. Although benign notochordal tumors and chordomas are composed of physaliphorous cells
with the same immunophenotype, benign notochordal cell tumors lack
the lobular growth pattern of chordoma, instead showing a more
uniform sheetlike low-power appearance (see Table 18-1). Additionally, benign notochordal cell tumors lack cytologic atypia. Finally, cortical destruction and soft tissue extension are not present in benign
notochordal cell tumors. Benign notochordal cell tumors may also
mimic an adipocytic proliferation; however, the presence of cytokeratin and brachyury reactivity supports the diagnosis of benign notochordal cell tumor.

Treatment and Prognosis
Although studies have suggested a relationship between benign notochordal tumors and chordoma, including the idea that the former are
precursor lesions, convincing evidence to support this theory is still
lacking. Yamaguchi et al. reported two cases of coccygeal tumors designated as “incipient chordoma” characterized by areas with histologic
features of chordoma adjacent to lesions with histologic features of a
benign notochordal cell tumor.29,30 Similarly, in a retrospective review of
82 sacral and coccygeal chordomas, Deshpande and colleagues identified six (7.3%) benign notochordal cell tumors either immediately adjacent to or within separate vertebrae.31 Although these studies document
chordoma associated with benign notochordal cell tumor, there has
been no documented malignant transformation of known cases of
benign notochordal tumors with clinical follow-up.16 Therefore, current
management of these lesions is conservative if discovered incidentally
after histologic confirmation of notochordal origin. Periodic imaging
can be helpful in monitoring for signs of continued growth.

Chordoma
Definition and Synonyms
Chordoma is a slow-growing but malignant notochordal neoplasm
that arises along the axial skeleton and exhibits destructive growth.

Figure 18-4. Benign notochordal cell tumor. The lesional cells are immunoreactive for
pancytokeratin.
278

Incidence and Demographics
Chordoma is a rare tumor, accounting for approximately 4% of primary
bone tumors, with an incidence of 0.08/100,000.20 It is the fourth most
common malignant primary osseous neoplasm, following osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma, and Ewing sarcoma, but it accounts for almost
22% of malignant primary osseous spinal tumors. Chordomas affect
males more commonly than females.32 In data from 400 chordomas
from the Surveillance, Epidemiology, and End Result (SEER) database,
238 tumors occurred in males and 162 tumors occurred in females
(male-to-female ratio, 1.5:1).33 More than 90% of chordomas occur in
Caucasians. The median age of diagnosis is 58.5 years (range, 3 to 95
years); it is distinctly uncommon in patients younger than 30 years.32
Although earlier studies suggested a predilection for the base of skull
and sacrococcygeal location, SEER data showed a relatively even distribution among axial tumors: cranial (32%), spinal (32.8%), and sacral
(29.2%).34 Chordomas in children and adolescents are rare and have a
predilection for the spheno-occipital and cervical regions.35

Notochordal and Related Lesions
Clinical Manifestations
Chordomas vary in clinical presentation depending on the site of the
lesion, but pain is almost a universal finding. Because of the slow
growth of these tumors, they are often clinically silent until late stages
of the disease. The duration of symptoms ranges from months to
years. For those in the spheno-occipital region, mass effect may compress cranial nerves, causing visual field defect or diplopia. Headache,
intranasal obstruction, or signs of pituitary involvement also occur.
Chordomas arising in the mobile spine may compress nerves, causing
radiculopathies related to the spinal level at which they occur.36,37
Patients with sacral chordomas usually complain of local pain,

occasionally associated with bowel or bladder symptoms, or
radiculopathies.

18

Radiologic Features
On plain radiographs, chordomas produce a lytic and destructive
lesion. However, the tumor may be missed on anteroposterior radiographs due to overlying bowel gas. CT imaging shows varying degrees
of sclerotic change. Chordomas involving the vertebral column may
locally invade the intervertebral disk and spread to adjacent vertebral
bodies. MRI studies show a low to intermediate T1-weighted and
high T2-weighted signal intensity mass (Fig. 18-5). Unlike benign

A

B

C

D

Figure 18-5. Chordoma. Anteroposterior (AP) radiograph of the pelvis demonstrates a large osteolytic mass involving the mid and lower sacrum (A, arrows). Sacral chordomas
are often challenging to identify on the initial AP radiographs due to the presence of overlying bowel gas. Computed tomography image of a chondroma containing osseous debris
related to destruction of the underlying bone, a finding that may mimic matrix formation (B). Sagittal T1-weighted magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) (C) and T2-weighted FS MRI
(D) of the sacrum at the midline; these show tumor destroying bone and extending into surrounding soft tissue, where it forms a large heterogenous mass in the presacral space.
279

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Gross Features
Gross examination reveals a gelatinous, semitranslucent and multilobulated tan-gray or red-brown mass (Fig. 18-6). The tumor expands
and destroys native bone contours, resulting in soft tissue extension
and a large soft tissue mass in most cases (Fig. 18-7).

Histopathology
Classic (conventional) chordomas are characteristically heterogeneous
with variable cellularity and atypia from field to field. Low-power
examination reveals a lobulated proliferation of voluminous cells with
bubbly, vacuolated cytoplasm (physaliphorous cells) arranged in cords
and nests (Fig. 18-8A and B). The tumors characteristically demonstrate entrapment of host bone, cortical destruction, and extraosseous
extension (Fig. 18-9). The lesional cells are embedded in a myxoid
intercellular matrix with intervening fibrous septa producing the
lobular architecture (Fig. 18-10). The size and number of intracytoplasmic vacuoles vary from one to several, and these vacuoles may encircle
the nucleus or displace it to the periphery of the cell (Fig. 18-11). The

Figure 18-6. Chordoma. Sacral chordoma forming a predominately red-brown mass
with a glistening cut surface.

Figure 18-7. Chordoma. A tan-gray sacral chordoma with focal myxoid change
forming a destructive mass extending into anterior and posterior soft tissue.

notochordal tumors, chordomas typically enhance following administration of contrast agents and frequently show cortical extension with
an associated soft tissue mass. Spheno-occipital lesions destroy the
clivus and/or sella turcica. Although sacral chordomas typically grow
anteriorly, coccygeal tumors may produce a mass that is either anterior
or posterior to the vertebral column.

A

B
Figure 18-8. Chordoma. The tumor has a lobulated growth pattern (A) and contains chords of physaliphorous cells characterized by intracytoplasmic vacuoles (B).

280

Notochordal and Related Lesions
cytoplasm of the physaliphorous cells is clear to eosinophilic. Occasionally, chordomas contain compact groups of cells with a prominent
epithelioid appearance that mimics carcinoma, particularly renal cell
or hepatocellular carcinoma (Fig. 18-12). Mitotic figures are typically
difficult to identify. Nuclear atypia is usually mild to moderate, but cells
with marked pleomorphism may be found (Fig. 18-13). The presence
of necrosis and marked nuclear pleomorphism have not been found to
correlate with a poorer clinical course.38

18

Ancillary Studies
Brachyury, a transcription factor with a key role in notochordal development, is expressed in virtually all chordomas, making it the most
useful protein to apply immunohistochemistry to in difficult diagnostic settings39,40 (Fig. 18-14). The vast majority of chordomas are positive
for pankeratin, and more specifically cytokeratins 8, 18, and 19 (Fig.
18-15). EMA and HMBE are also usually positive. S-100 protein
expression is variable, ranging from 30% to 90%.40–44
Figure 18-9. Chordoma. Chordoma encasing preexisting bony trabeculae as it
destroys host bone.

Variants
Chondroid Chordoma
Chondroid chordoma is a variant of chordoma that contains areas of
neoplastic hyaline cartilage admixed with conventional chordoma45

A

B

C

Figure 18-10. Chordoma. The tumor cells are embedded in an extracellular myxoid
matrix (A). A cellular area of chordoma demonstrates cells without significant atypia.
The nuclei are round to oval-shaped and contain scattered small nucleoli (B). The
lobulated architecture of chordoma is created by fibrous septa traversing through the
tumor (C).
281

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
described 19% of 73 base of skull chordomas in children and adolescents as highly cellular tumors with a solid growth pattern and no
myxoid matrix or lobular architecture.35 Within this group, 11% were
designated as cellular chordoma and 8% were classified as poorly differentiated chordoma. The latter group was characterized by poorly
differentiated epithelioid cells in a fibrous stroma. Both groups were
positive for cytokeratin, EMA, and S-100 protein. The poorly differentiated variant was associated with a worse prognosis when compared
to the other groups. Another study by Coffin and colleagues also found
atypical histologic features, including round cell and spindle cell
tumors, in their series of pediatric chordomas.46

Figure 18-11. Chordoma. Abundant myxoid matrix and nuclear compression by intracytoplasmic vacuoles create a microcystic low-power pattern.

(Fig. 18-16). The chondroid areas may be present as small scattered
foci or occasionally represent the dominant pattern. The vast majority
of chondroid chordomas are located in the base of the skull. Although
the existence of this entity has been the subject of much debate,
research by Vujovic and coworkers has shown that chondroid chordomas show expression of brachyury in both the chondoid and chondroid regions, providing support to the legitimacy of this diagnosis.40
One article has suggested that when it is desirable to separate conventional chordoma from chondroid chordoma, use of EMA, carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), and HMB-45 are valuable; conventional lesions
are positive in more than 50% of all cases, whereas chondroid variants
are negative for all three.44
Pediatric Chordoma
Chordomas in children and adolescents account for less than 5% of all
chordomas, and most of them occur in the skull base. Approximately
50% of reported cases are classic (conventional) chordoma, and 11%
to 23% are chondroid chordoma.35 Interestingly, a subset of tumors in
this age group display unusual histologic features. Hoch and colleagues

A

Dedifferentiated Chordoma
Dedifferentiated chordoma makes up approximately 5% of all chordomas. The age range and anatomic distribution are similar to conventional chordoma. They can arise de novo, in a chordoma that has
been irradiated, or in the setting of recurrent tumor. Radiologically,
conventional chordomas are hyperintense on T2-weighted MRI,
whereas dedifferentiated chordoma may contain areas of low-signal
intensity in a predominately hyperintense conventional component.
Dedifferentiation has been described at all levels of the axial skeleton,
but most occur in the sacrum.47–49 On gross examination, the dedifferentiated component is fleshy in appearance, with or without
hemorrhage/necrosis in contrast to the myxoid presentation of the
conventional areas. Microscopically, the tumor contains variable
amounts of conventional chordoma and a high-grade sarcoma such as
osteosarcoma, pleomorphic undifferentiated sarcoma, fibrosarcoma,
or rhabdomyosarcoma. Immunoreactivity for keratins, S-100 protein,
and brachyury is seen in the classic chordoma component but absent
in the high-grade sarcoma component (Fig. 18-17). The prognosis is
worse compared to conventional chordoma.
Extra-axial Chordoma
Rare examples of neoplasms with morphologic and immunohistochemical features identical to axial chordoma have been recognized at
extra-axial sites, where they occur as either an intraosseous or soft
tissue tumor.50,51 The diagnosis requires positive immunostaining for
brachyury to distinguish it from histologic mimics such as metastatic
carcinoma and parachordoma/myoepithelioma.50,52,53 Although prognostication is difficult due to limited experience with this entity, these

B

Figure 18-12. Chordoma. Voluminous eosinophilic cytoplasm imparts a resemblance to hepatocellular carcinoma (A). Clustering of tumor cells with prominent cytoplasmic clearing
in a hemorrhagic background mimics renal cell carcinoma (B).

prominent nuclear atypia.

chordoma.

Figure 18-14. Chordoma. Brachyury expression in chordoma characterized by strong
nuclear staining.
