admixed with conventional chordoma.

neoplasms appear to exhibit similar behavior to their midline counterparts, including slow but locally aggressive growth.

chondroid chordoma from chondrosarcoma may be difficult, particularly with limited biopsy tissue. However, the discovery of brachyury
expression in chordoma has made this distinction much easier (Table
18-3). In addition to lacking expression of brachyury, chondrosarcomas are also negative for cytokeratins. Rarely, primary mixed tumor/
myoepithelioma and extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma of bone
may occur.53–55 Although mixed tumor/myoepithelioma may exhibit a
relatively similar immunoprofile to chordoma, including reactivity
for cytokeratins, EMA, and S-100 protein, these tumors are negative
for brachyury. Moreover, a subset of myoepitheliomas harbor EWSR1
gene rearrangement by fluorescence in situ hybridization. Extraskeletal
myxoid chondrosarcoma lacks reactivity for cytokeratins and EMA
as well as brachyury, and it is characterized by NR4A3 gene
rearrangements.

Differential Diagnosis
As described previously, the distinction between chordoma and benign
notochordal tumor is critical. Key features of chordoma not found in
benign notochordal tumors include a lobular architecture, moderate
to occasionally marked cytologic atypia, myxoid stroma, syncytial cell
cords, and a destructive growth pattern (see Table 18-1). Other nonnotochordal lesions to consider in the differential diagnosis of chordoma include metastatic carcinoma, chondrosarcoma, mixed tumor/
myoepithelioma, and extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma (Table
18-2). Metastatic carcinoma is much more common than any primary
bone neoplasm. Although metastatic adenocarcinoma, particularly
one with mucinous features, may resemble chordoma on morphologic
examination, it will lack S-100 protein and, most importantly, brachyury reactivity. Clinical information such as a prior history of malignancy or multiple lesions on imaging may also be helpful. Distinguishing

Genetics
Chordomas that have been studied by cytogenetic analysis show neardiploid or moderately hypodiploid karyotypes with several numerical

Treatment and Prognosis
Complete surgical resection with a wide margin is the treatment of
choice and the most important predictor of survival and local recurrence.38,57 Unfortunately, total en bloc resection is oftentimes not possible, particularly for those located in the skull base. Therefore, the use
of radiotherapy, either intensity-modulated radiation therapy or proton
beam therapy (when available), as an adjuvant or, at times, primary
treatment, is often recommended. In general, chordomas tend to be
insensitive to conventional chemotherapies.57

Brachyury

Chordoma is a locally aggressive neoplasm with a high likelihood
of local recurrence. According to the SEER database from 1973 to 2003,
including 414 chordomas located in the vertebral column and sacrum,
the median survival was 50 months.33 Those patients with isolated
disease had a median survival of 96 months, whereas patients with
metastatic disease had a much shorter survival (24 months). Studies of
sacral chordomas have shown 5-year and 10-year survival rates of 45%

Notochordal and Related Lesions
to 77% and 28% to 52%, respectively.38 At initial presentation, 5% of
chordomas are metastatic, and in advanced disease, as many as 65%
are metastatic.57 Sites of metastatic disease include lung, bone, skin,
brain and liver. The most common cause of death is locally advanced
disease with compression of vital structures.

Summary

299

Osteoarthritis
Definition and Synonyms
Bone morphogenetic protein (BMP): a member of a superfamily of
proteins that promotes the formation of bone and the skeleton
Collagen IX: a fibrous protein that is a heterodimeric molecule
expressed mainly in cartilage
Collagen X: the fibrous protein constituent of hypertrophic and
mineralizing cartilage
Endochondral ossification: the process of bone development from
cartilage seen during skeletal development of long bones and in
reparative processes such as fracture and other forms of injury
Linkage analysis: a study to establish linkage between genes
Osteoarthritis: a degenerative joint disease
Pannus; the vascular synovial tissue covering an articular surface
Synovium: the thin layer of connective tissue lining joints and
tendons

•
•
•
•
•
•
•
•

Brief Historical Overview
Osteoarthritis (OA) is the leading cause of musculoskeletal disability
in the Western world and is the most common form of arthritis. This
disease entity was originally defined by Sir Archibald E. Garrod in
the 1890s. Based on anatomic features, Nicholas and Richardson in
1909 divided chronic osteoarthritis into two major categories:

inflammatory types and noninflammatory or degenerative types. In
common usage, however, OA is usually the term reserved for noninflammatory, degenerative conditions, and the inflammatory types are
described as arthritis with a clinically descriptive prefix, such as rheumatoid arthritis. Using either nomenclature, two pathologic groups
emerge: (1) disorders with primary changes in synovium and perichondrium and (2) disorders with primary degeneration of articular
cartilage.1
Incidence and Demographics
In the United States and in most other Western countries, OA is a very
common joint disorder.2 The number of people affected with symptomatic OA in the United States has grown from 21 million to nearly
27 million due to the growing aging population and factors such as
obesity.3 In adults 30 years of age or older, symptomatic disease in the
knee and hip occurs in about 6% and 3%, respectively.4 OA typically
affects the hand, foot, or knee of women older than 50 years of age and
the hip of men older than 50 years of age.5-9 Both racial and ethnic
differences have been described in the prevalence and pattern of
OA.5-15
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
OA is the outcome of a range of disorders that result in structural and
functional failure of synovial joints. The clinical presentation of OA
can be any one of the following:

• Presentation limited to a single large joint (unilateral or bilateral),
usually the knee or hip
• A generalized process involving small joints such as the distal and
•
•

proximal interphalangeal joints of hands, first carpometacarpal
joint, knees, hips, and metatarsophalangeal joints
OA secondary to neurologic deficits such as Charcot joints (peripheral neuropathies due to diabetes, pernicious anemia, and spinal
cord degeneration as seen in tabes dorsalis and syringomyelia)
A rarer form of erosive arthritis radiologically mimicking inflammatory arthritis and affecting the distal and proximal interphalangeal joints and occasionally large joints1

The characteristic symptoms of OA include joint pain that is typically related to activity, stiffness of short duration, reduced function,
289

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

Figure 19-1. Osteoarthritis. Anteroposterior (A) and lateral (B) views of the knee showing severe medial compartmental narrowing and mild subchondral sclerosis. There is tricompartmental osteophytic lipping (arrows). Intra-articular loose bodies are readily seen. A subchondral cyst in the inferior aspect of patella can be seen (B, arrowhead). (Courtesy
of Dr. Cornelia Wenokor, University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

and joint instability (buckling or giving away). Physical examination
can reveal tenderness over the joint line; crepitus; restricted range of
movement; pain on passive joint motion; instability; bony enlargement
of the distal and proximal interphalangeal joints (Heberden and
Bouchard nodes); and deformities such as bowed legs, altered gait,
muscle atrophy, or weakness and joint effusion.16
Radiologic Features
Conventional radiography is the most commonly used modality in the
diagnosis of osteoarthritis. The most characteristic imaging finding is
joint space narrowing. Other features visualized on conventional
imaging include marginal osteophytes, increased bone density (subchondral sclerosis on both sides), and subchondral cysts or geodes
(Figs. 19-1A and B and 19-2). The presence of asymmetric narrowing
of joint space, especially in large joints with the presence of osteophytes, is considered fairly specific for OA.17,18 Computed tomography
(CT) is helpful in the assessment of facet joint OA of the spine and
detection of intra-articular loose bodies.17 Magnetic resonance imaging
(MRI) is seldom used in routine assessment or initial diagnosis of OA.
However, MRI is well capable of detecting early changes and is uniquely
able to depict all compartments of the joint, including the articular
cartilage, menisci, intra-articular ligaments, synovium, capsular structures, bone contours, and bone marrow.19 This has aided in understanding the natural history of the disease and in guiding therapeutic
interventions. Imaging modalities such as ultrasound play a role in
assessing the synovitis associated with OA.17 One must keep in mind
that the radiologic changes do not always reflect clinical symptoms and
signs and progression of disease in these patients.15,20
Gross Pathology
Gross changes in an arthritic joint reflect alterations in the shape, loss
of integrity of cartilage matrix or support structures, and alterations in
the mechanical properties of the joint tissue. Alterations in shape in
OA are often due to the formation of osteophytes at the joint periphery
290

Figure 19-2. Osteoarthritis. Specimen radiograph of femoral head with subchondral
sclerosis and osteophytes (arrows). (Courtesy of Dr. Edward McCarthy, Johns Hopkins
University, and Dr. Seena Aisner, University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

and sometimes under the articular surface. Macroscopic changes in the
cartilage surface are seen as fibrillation, erosion (ulceration), and
cracking. Roughened, clefted areas in fibrillated cartilage replace the
normal smooth cartilage surface. Progressive degenerative changes
lead to erosion and denudation, thus exposing the underlying dense
bone, a process called eburnation (Figs. 19-3 and 19-4). Cracking is
seen as deep clefting into the cartilage. Softening of the cartilage is
called chondromalacia, and it may precede fibrillation. Osteophytes,
bony or cartilaginous outgrowths, can be seen at the periphery of
the joint. Subchondral bone is densely sclerotic due to increased osteoblastic activity. Loss of overlying articular cartilage allows synovial
fluid to accumulate through bony defects within the marrow spaces,

Degenerative Joint Disease

19

Figure 19-5. Normal cartilage showing flat chondrocytes lying parallel to the surface
in the superficial zone. Grouping of chondrocytes is seen beneath the superficial layer.

Figure 19-3. Osteoarthritis. Gross photograph of a knee joint showing erosion and
cracking of articular surface with denudation and exposure of underlying bone. (Courtesy of Dr. Edward McCarthy, Johns Hopkins University, and Dr. Seena Aisner, University
of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

Figure 19-6. Basophilic line known as the “tide mark” (arrow).

Figure 19-4. Osteoarthritis. Gross photograph of a femoral head showing erosion and
cracking of articular surface with denudation and exposure of underlying bone. (Courtesy of Dr. Edward McCarthy, Johns Hopkins University, and Dr. Seena Aisner, University
of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

thus forming subchondral cysts or geodes. In addition, bone and cartilage fragments can separate and become incorporated into synovial
fluid and proliferate as loose bodies. Synovium surrounding damaged
joints tends to proliferate, to become hyperplastic, and to extend into
the joint, thus forming a pannus.

Histopathology
Articular cartilage is an avascular tissue devoid of nerves and lymphatics and is composed of chondrocytes enmeshed in a heavily hydrated
extracellular matrix made up of proteoglycans and collagen. Normal
articular cartilage has four layers or zones: superficial, intermediate,
deep, and calcified. In the superficial layer the chondrocytes are flat
and lie parallel to the surface (Fig. 19-5). In the intermediate and deep
zones the chondrocytes form groups, and in the calcified layer they are
nonviable with calcification of the matrix. Above the calcified cartilage
there is a basophilic line known as the “tide mark” (Fig. 19-6). Microscopic examination of OA reveals many clefts in the cartilage at the
articular surface, the majority of which are vertically oriented (Fig.
19-7). The chondrocytes located deeper show proliferation and formation of cell nests or clones. The “tide mark” undergoes duplication and
shows marked irregularity (Fig. 19-8). There is endochondral ossification with osteoblastic proliferation, and increased vascularity and
immature woven bone formation is seen at the bone-cartilage interface
(Fig. 19-9A). This leads to sclerosis of bone at the subchondral zone.
Underneath the articular surface, subchondral cysts may be present
showing sparsely cellular, myxoid, and loose connective tissue (see Fig.
19-9B). Osteophytes develop at the margins of the joint by endochondral ossification (Fig. 19-10). Synovial membranes show proliferation
and hyperplasia and mild chronic inflammation (Fig. 19-11A). Pannus
291

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 19-7. Osteoarthritis. Clefts in cartilage showing a vertically oriented fibrillated
appearance. Nesting of chondrocytes is seen underneath the clefts.

A

Figure 19-8. Osteoarthritis. Osteoarthritic cartilage with duplication of the “tide
mark” (arrow).

B

Figure 19-9. Osteoarthritis. A, Endochondral ossification with osteoblastic proliferation, new bone formation, and increased vascularity at the bone-cartilage interface. B, The
bone cartilage interface with bony sclerosis and subchondral cyst formation, containing loose and myxoid connective tissue.

292

Degenerative Joint Disease
formation is seen in the form of synovial growth into the joint (see Fig.
19-11). Osteochondral loose bodies can be seen around the joint due
to separated fragments of bone and cartilage from the damaged joint,
with incorporation into the synovial membrane and proliferation by
endochondral ossification (Fig. 19-12). Rarely, bone and cartilage
detritus can be seen within the proliferative synovium (Fig. 19-13).

The salient histologic features are as follows:

• Fibrillation of articular cartilage with vertical or horizontal
clefting
• Duplication of the “tide mark”
• Endochondral ossification with new bone formation at the bone•

Subchondral cysts
Osteophytes at the periphery

Differential Diagnosis
Primary OA should be distinguished from secondary OA and inflammatory arthritis. The prototypic inflammatory arthritis is rheumatoid
arthritis.
The other causes of secondary osteoarthritis include the following:

• Skeletal dysplasias
• Congenital dislocation of the hip
• Slipped capital femoral epiphysis
• Avascular necrosis, including Legg–Calvé–Perthes disease
• Infection
• Metabolic disorders (gout, calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate

and bone as a result of endochondral ossification.

Gaucher disease)
Endocrine disorders (diabetes mellitus, acromegaly, hyperparathyroidism, hypothyroidism)
Trauma (e.g., fractures)
Paget disease

in late osteoarthritis.

Secondary Osteoarthritis
Rheumatoid Arthritis
Rheumatoid arthritis (RA), a chronic inflammatory arthritis, predominantly affects the synovial membrane, with associated joint swelling
and pain and subsequent joint deformities. RA most commonly affects
the hands and feet and tends to be polyarticular, symmetrical, and
bilateral.21,22 Acutely affected joints are swollen and tender, and aspirated synovial fluid shows an increased white cell count (range, 2000
to 75,000/mm3), about 50% of which will be neutrophils.1,23 Ultrasound
and MRI are superior to CT and conventional radiography for detection of early changes such as synovial hyperemia, joint effusion, and
294

Figure 19-14. Conventional radiograph of a right wrist with periarticular osteopenia
at the carpus and metacarpophalangeal joints. There is marked radiocarpal joint space
narrowing (arrows). Small erosions are present at the radiolunate articulation and fourth
metacarpal base (arrowhead). (Courtesy of Dr. Cornelia Wenokor, University of Medicine
and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

Figure 19-15. Axial fat-suppressed magnetic resonance image of the wrist with
gadolinium showing extensive synovial pannus at the carpus and extensor tendon
compartments III to IV (white and black arrows). A large erosion in the triquetrum is
seen due to the ingrowth of pannus (arrowhead). (Courtesy of Dr. Cornelia Wenokor,
University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

synovitis. In late stages, joint space narrowing, subchondral cysts, subchondral sclerosis, and loose bodies are seen, as in OA. Figures 19-14
and 19-15 highlight joint space narrowing, periarticular erosions, and
synovial proliferation with pannus formation. Osteophytes are not a
prominent component of RA. Late sequelae include massive bone erosions, scar formation, fibrosis, subluxation, and fibrous and bony
ankylosis.24

Degenerative Joint Disease
and endocrine disorders, have been implicated in the etiology of SCFE.
Avascular necrosis is also a known complication. The physis shows
abnormal cartilage clustering and disarray in the proliferative and
hypertrophic zones.23,26-28

19

Avascular Necrosis

Figure 19-16. Rheumatoid arthritis. Photomicrograph of the synovium showing papillary hyperplasia, vascular proliferation, and lymphocytic infiltration.

Pathologic findings in RA result from nonsuppurative chronic
inflammation of the synovium and its sequelae in cartilage and bone.
The pertinent findings are listed below:

• Synovial proliferation with papillary hyperplasia (Fig. 19-16)
• Vascular proliferation
• Nodular lymphocytic proliferation with germinal centers and
plasma cell cuffing (Fig. 19-17A to C)
• Presence of giant cells (see Fig. 19-17D)
• Fibrinous exudates with neutrophils and formation of “rice bodies”
composed of fibrin and debris (Fig. 19-18).
• Extension of hypertrophied synovium into the joint forming pannus
(Fig. 19-19)
• Cartilage degradation leading to chondrolysis
• Subchondral bone formation with chronic inflammation and lymphoid follicles
• Osteopenia
• Subchondral cysts
• Detritus
Skeletal Dysplasia
The skeletal dysplasias constitute a group of precocious OA recognized
as aggressive and most often inherited in a mendelian fashion. These
include spondylo-epiphyseal dysplasia, Stickler and Marshall syndromes, and multiple epiphyseal dysplasias, among others. Patients
present with early-onset OA, which is often rapidly progressive and
includes articular and extra-articular involvement that is not typical of
classic OA.25

Slipped Capital Femoral Epiphysis
Slipped capital femoral epiphysis (SCFE) is a disease of adolescent
boys, more often of African-American ethnicity. It results from
gradual displacement of the femoral neck from the capital femoral
epiphysis, leading to retroversion of the head and subsequently osteoarthritis. Many biomechanical factors, including instability, obesity,

A significant cause of secondary OA is avascular necrosis (AVN)
involving hip, knee, and other major joints.29,30 Approximately 10,000
to 20,000 new patients are affected by AVN of the femoral head every
year, often presenting in the third or fourth decades.31 Clinical onset is
usually sudden or the presence of local pain for several months;
however, the duration of symptoms is usually shorter than that of
primary OA or RA.23 Nontraumatic causes include alcoholism, sickle
cell disease, corticosteroids, hyperbaric events, storage disorders,
SCFE, coagulopathies, autoimmune diseases, and Legg–Calvé–Perthes
disease.32
Radiologic features differ from those of OA by preservation of joint
space and subchondral fracture with increased bone density and
change in contour. Radiologic staging systems exist to assess the extent
of damage based on conventional imaging, radionuclide scanning, and
MRI findings. A characteristic radiolucent “crescent sign” (Fig. 19-20)
has been described at later stages to demonstrate the linear fracture
beneath the end plate.33,34
Gross examination of the articular surface of early AVN reveals an
intact articular cartilage and a characteristic bright yellow opaque subchondral zone, representing bone necrosis (Fig. 19-21). A hyperemic
zone may be present at the margin.
The following microscopic findings characterize the disease (Figs.
19-22 and 19-23):

• A subchondral yellow opacity consisting of necrotic bone and bone
•
•
•

marrow with replacement of marrow elements by granular eosinophilic material and ghosts of adipocytes (see Fig. 19-22)
Cystic changes within marrow lipid with calcification (see Fig.
19-23)
Infarcted bone with empty osteocytic lacunae (see Figs. 19-22 and
19-23)
Increased osteoblastic activity and vascular and fibroblastic proliferation at the edge of the infarct, representing the hyperemic zone
on gross examination
In the late stages, the following events occur:

• Buckling or fragmentation of cartilage above the necrotic zone due
to fracture
• Presence of bony and cartilaginous detritus
• Cartilage erosion and bony eburnation around the collapsed
infarcted segment
• Osteophytes may be present but not as pronounced as in primary
OA

Neuropathic Joint (Charcot Joint)
Charcot joint represents a spectrum of disorders caused by primary
neurologic deficits ranging from central sensory and motor disorders
to peripheral neuropathies, leading to progressive destructive changes
in the articular bone.35 These neuropathic arthropathies are most commonly associated with diabetes mellitus and other degenerative neuromuscular diseases such as syringomyelia.35-37 Radiographically, there
is marked destruction as evidenced by bone sclerosis, cyst formation,
osteophytes, and debris.38 Intra-articular osseous debris and subluxation are common (Fig. 19-24A and B). Histologically, there is synovial
hyperplasia, variable inflammation, giant cells, and large amounts of
bone and cartilage detritus.23
295

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C

D

Figure 19-17. Rheumatoid arthritis. Photomicrographs of synovium showing a nodular lymphocytic proliferation and plasma cell cuffing at 40× (A), 100× (B), and 400× (C).
D, Photomicrograph showing synovial proliferation and presence of giant cells (original magnification, 400×).

Septic Arthritis
Septic arthritis most commonly involves the hips of infants and
younger children and the knees of older children and adults.39 Contributing factors include contiguous osteomyelitis, injury, hematogenous spread, and intra-articular procedures, as well as previous joint
damage and chronic inflammation in conditions such as RA and
lupus.40 Diagnosis is usually established by aspiration of joint fluid with
culture and fluid analysis. In cases of untreated septic arthritis, the
radiologic changes mirror that of degenerative joint disease with joint
space narrowing, periarticular erosions, irreversible articular damage,
and synovial proliferation with pannus formation (Figs. 19-25 and
296

19-26). Marked acute inflammation of the synovium with destruction
of normal histology is the usual pathologic finding.

Crystal Deposition Disease
The most frequently encountered crystal deposition disorders are gout
due to sodium urate and pseudogout or chondrocalcinosis due to
calcium pyrophosphate crystals/calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate
deposition disease (CPPD).41 Gout is characterized by a granulomatous
inflammatory response with crystal aggregation in the joint and soft
tissue seen as discrete marginal erosions in the articular bone in
radiographs. These chalky white deposits are seen as brown aggregates

Degenerative Joint Disease
surrounded by a granulomatous reaction in formalin-fixed tissue preparations. CPPD is seen as radiodense linear calcifications in articular
cartilage, fibrocartilage, and joint tissues. Histologically, these are seen
as acellular purple granular deposits with positive birefringence on
polarized microscopy. These characteristic features distinguish these
disorders from primary OA. Both gout and pseudogout can result in
secondary OA.
Table 19-1 highlights the differences in pathologic features of
primary OA and selected other forms of arthritis.

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Most ancillary diagnostic tests for osteoarthritis are directed toward
the elimination of secondary causes such as RA, septic arthritis, and
crystal deposition disease. At the present time, no specific diagnostic
immunohistochemical marker is available to distinguish among these

disorders. As mentioned previously, the diagnosis of these entities is
based on clinical, radiologic, pathologic, hematologic, and microbiologic findings.
Genetics
The role of genetics in OA has been investigated through familial
aggregation studies, twin studies, linkage analysis, and candidate geneassociation studies.42-44 Classic twin studies after the adjustment of data
for other risk factors such as age, sex, and body mass index showed
298

Figure 19-23. Avascular necrosis (AVN). A photomicrograph of AVN showing calcified
debris in bone marrow and infarcted bony trabeculae without osteocytes.

that the influence of radiographic OA was higher in female monozygotic twins.45 Assessment of genetic contribution to disease progression is being investigated by multiple studies, notably the Multicenter
Osteoarthritis Study (MOST)46 and the Osteoarthritis Initiative (OAI)
(www.oai.ucsf.edu), both funded by the National Institutes of Health.
It has been shown that genes involved in bone mineral density and
bone remodeling, notably LRP5,47 OPG,48 VDR, and ESRJ, are associated with risk for OA.49 Genome-wide linkage analysis studies have
shown genetic regions in areas of chromosomes 7q34-7q36.3, llpl2llql3.4, 6p21.1-6ql5, 2q31.1-2q34, and 15q21.3-15q26.1 were most
likely to harbor OA susceptibility genes.50 The role of early candidate
genes of cartilage matrix such as COL2Al, collagen IX, and aggrecan
is not well established.43 Comparison of cDNA libraries from
OA-affected and normal cartilage and synovium allowed Valdes and
colleagues to identify single nucleotide polymorphisms in 9 out of 22
genes that were associated with OA in a population-based cohort.51
Genes involved in bone morphogenetic protein and Wnt pathways
have been replicated in the largest number of independent population
studies by linkage analysis. Two polymorphisms in one of the genes
involved in the Wnt pathway and the frizzled transmembrane receptors
(FRZB) have been associated with increased risk for clinical OA and
total hip replacement in women.52,53 Among the bone morphogenetic
protein genes implicated in OA, three genes—BMP5, BMP2, and
GDF5—appear to play a role in individual susceptibility to develop
OA.54 The ongoing genome-wide association studies will lead to genegene and gene-environment interaction studies to better detect individual genes implicated in OA and to determine how they exert their
effect.55
Treatment and Prognosis
There is no known cure for OA. Therefore, the goal of management of
OA patients is control of pain and improvement in function and
health-related quality of life and avoidance of therapeutic toxicity. Systemic treatment for pain includes nonopioid analgesics, nonsteroidal
anti-inflammatory agents, and sometimes opioid analgesics.56,57 Topical
analgesics such as capsaicin cream can be appropriate as an adjunct to

Degenerative Joint Disease

19

A

B

Figure 19-24. Charcot joint. Anteroposterior (A) and lateral (B) views of a knee joint with extensive fragmentation of the tibial plateau with intra-articular ossified debris. Posterior
and lateral tibial translation with knee subluxation is present. (Courtesy of Dr. Cornelia Wenokor, University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

Figure 19-25. Septic joint. Frog-leg lateral view of the right hip of a 19-year-old man
with significant joint space narrowing and loss of the cortical margin of the acetabulum
(arrow) and femoral head. Small erosions are seen at the acetabular roof and femoral
head. (Courtesy of Dr. Cornelia Wenokor, University of Medicine and Dentistry of New
Jersey.)

systemic therapy.58 Surgical intervention for OA includes osteotomy,
arthroscopy, arthrodesis, and arthroplasty. The most significant
advancement in surgical treatment of OA in the past century has been
total joint arthroplasty. It has been the most effective of all medical
interventions with elimination of pain with near-normal function and
is also highly cost effective.59-61 New experimental approaches to treatment include cartilage transplantation (osteochondral autografting,

Figure 19-26. Septic joint. A coronal, reformatted, contrast-enhanced computed
tomography image of the right hip bone window settings showing the erosion of the
acetabular roof and femoral head (arrows). In addition, there is a large hip joint effusion
and a rim enhancing soft issue abscess (arrowheads). (Courtesy of Dr. Cornelia Wenokor,
University of Medicine and Dentistry of New Jersey.)

osteochondral allografting, and tissue engineering to transplant committed chondrocyte progenitor cells).62,63

Summary
OA, even though readily diagnosed by conventional radiography, is a
commonly encountered pathologic diagnosis. The gross and microscopic pathologies almost always complement the radiologic features.
OA can be primary or secondary. In late stages, secondary OA shows
similar histologic features such as subchondral cysts, sclerosis, and
loose bodies. However, osteophytes are a less prominent sign in

margin, subchondral lucent area

Necrotic bone and bone marrow at subchondral opaque
zone, cystic changes, increased osteoblasts at hyperemic
zone
Late stages: cartilage erosion, bony eburnation,
nonpronounced osteophytes

Neuropathic joint

Marked destruction of joint with formation of
subchondral sclerosis, cysts, and osteophytes

Extreme joint deformity with subluxation

Synovial hyperplasia, variable inflammation, bone and
cartilage detritus, subchondral cysts

Septic arthritis

Joint space narrowing, effusion, periarticular
erosions, synovial proliferation

Synovial proliferation, abscess

Marked acute inflammation of synovium

Crystal deposition
disease

Discrete marginal erosions in articular bone

Radiodense linear calcification of articular
cartilage, fibrocartilage and other tissues

Granulomatous reaction and brown aggregates in gout
and purple granular deposits in pseudogout

secondary OA. AVN is a significant contributory factor to secondary
OA. Correlation with radiology and attention to the specific gross
appearance of the subchondral opaque zone for sampling is essential
for accurate pathologic diagnosis. Neuropathic joints often show
extreme destruction with bone sclerosis, subchondral cysts, osteophytes, and detritus. Examination of the accompanying synovium is
important in elucidating underlying etiologies such as RA, septic
arthritis, and crystal deposition disorders. Classic twin studies have
shown an increased risk for radiographic OA in monozygotic twins,
and genome-wide association studies are underway to uncover genegene and gene-environment interactions to understand the etiology
and progression of primary osteoarthritis. Some genetic studies have
implicated bone morphogenetic protein and Wnt pathways in the
pathobiology of primary OA.

20
Crystal Arthropathies
Edward F. DiCarlo, MD

The history of recognized CPPD disease, on the other hand, is
much shorter and less colorful, having been recognized in the late
1950s and more fully characterized in the decade that followed. The
clinical presentation of cases of CPPD disease, which may mimic gout,
is referred to as “pseudogout.”4
The history of diseases associated with HA (and other basic calcium
phosphate crystals) is not well defined and runs generally concurrently
with that of CPPD. It dates to the advent of the use of chemical and
ultrastructural analyses in human tissues over the past several decades.

Hydroxyapatite Deposits 310
Handling of Crystal-Containing Sections and Specimens

311

General Comment and Summary 312

Incidence and Demographics

The term crystalline arthropathy is a general term that refers to the
presence of crystalline deposits in or around joints, sometimes within
tendon sheaths and bursae as well. Essentially three crystalline substances may be found in articular and periarticular tissues. These three
are monosodium urate (MSU), the cause of gout and gouty arthritis;
calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD), a finding in many degenerate joints and the cause of pseudogout; and hydroxyapatite (HA), the
deposit in cases of other “calcific” diseases.

Radiologic Features

Historical Background
The history of gout has been colorful and often poetic, in stark contrast
to the exquisitely painful nature of the disease. Gout in one form or
another has been known for millennia, having been identified in Egyptian mummies. Although gout may affect individuals of all socioeconomic classes, it has often been recognized as a disease of the well-to-do,
mostly because of its tendency to develop in individuals of an upper
or privileged class with access to foods rich in purines such as meat,
fish, and poultry.1 Today, this is not quite the case, but it helps explain
the colorful history of this condition. Many sufferers were highly educated and often erudite individuals, such as Benjamin Franklin2 and
Thomas Sydenham, who gave an eloquent description of the exquisitely painful acute attacks of gout he personally experienced in the
17th century.3

Gouty deposits may be the most dramatic, clinically and histologically,
of the crystal-associated diseases, but CPPD, usually without clinical
symptoms, is by far the most common in surgical materials. CPPD is
present in approximately 17% of total knee specimens (ranging from
less than 10% in individuals younger than 60 years to more than 30%
in individuals older than 80 years), with nearly identical incidences for
intervertebral disk samples. HA is more common than gouty deposits
but less than CPPD deposits in surgical materials.

Deposits of MSU are not visible radiologically except where the deposits may be secondarily calcified (Fig. 20-1). Deposits of CPPD (Fig.
20-2) and HA (Fig. 20-3), both of which contain radiologically dense
calcium, are generally visible radiologically as punctate, globular, or
even massive calcific densities. Radiologically visible deposits of these
crystals are referred to as “chondrocalcinosis” because they are visible
in locations known to contain cartilage, meniscal tissues, and other soft
tissues that have a chondral or fibrochondral matrix. “Chondrocalcinosis” is equally appropriate as a descriptive term at gross examination
of these same tissues.

Crystals in Synovial Fluids/Soft Tissue Aspirates
Joint or soft tissue aspiration is a common diagnostic procedure for the
analysis of crystalline deposits. Crystals in the specimens are present
within cells and free in the fluid. In addition, they are almost always
303

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 20-1. This radiograph of the feet of a patient suffering from gout shows fullness
of the soft tissues without calcification medial to the left first metatarsophalangeal joint.
It also demonstrates erosions of the bones at the periphery of the joint line.

Figure 20-2. This radiograph is of the right knee of a patient who has degenerative
narrowing of the medial compartment and calcifications of the lateral meniscus (chondrocalcinosis) due to the deposition of calcium pyrophosphate.

accompanied by an inflammatory, usually neutrophilic, exudate, measuring up to 50,000 cells/mL.
Examination of fluids in polarized light requires clean glass slides
free of particulate debris. Good quality, “strain-free” polarizing filters,
one below the tissue section (the “polarizer”) and one above it (the
“analyzer”), should be centered in the light path. These filters, when
their planes of polarization are placed at 90 degrees relative to each
other, should produce a dark, nearly black background. The use of a
first-order red (F-OR) compensating filter is necessary to determine
the light-rotational properties of the crystals. This filter may be placed
in the optical path anywhere above the polarizer and below the analyzer, either above or below the section. The axis of this filter (lambda
axis) should be oriented at 45 degrees relative to the crossed polarizers.
Crystals then should be aligned to this axis. In most instances, it is
possible to rotate this lambda axis through 90 degrees, making it possible to view crystals oriented parallel and perpendicular to the alignment of the filter so as to avoid the need for a circular, rotating stage.
304

Figure 20-3. This radiograph of the right shoulder of an individual with “calcific
capsulitis” shows sizable globular and streaky calcific densities due to hydroxyapatite
in the soft tissues lateral to the humeral head and greater tuberosity.

Of the approximately 200 or so synovial fluid samples examined by
the author each year, MSU crystals account for approximately 7% of
the samples, CPPD crystals for approximately 3%, and HA-containing
samples, which are rare, for less than 1%.
MSU and CPPD have characteristic optical features that clearly
allow their identification in polarized light, whereas HA is not visible
in polarized light. MSU crystals have an elongated needle shape measuring up to 30 microns in length and are usually very numerous in
the unspun sample. When present in a cell, usually a neutrophil, they
frequently impart a Saturn-like appearance to the cell because the
crystals are longer than the diameter of the cell and appear to “stab”
through the cell, which actually remains intact (Fig. 20-4A). In polarized light using the F-OR filter, when the MSU crystals are aligned with
(parallel to) the axis of the compensating filter, the crystals are strongly
yellow (blue when perpendicular to the axis) and are, therefore, levorotatory or negatively birefringent (see Fig. 20-4B and C). MSU crystals
may break during collection, but their optical features are not altered.
Care should be taken to avoid mistaking individual fibrils of fibrin as
crystals of MSU. Such fibrils are usually slightly curved and are not
visible in polarized light (Fig. 20-5).
CPPD crystals have an elongated rhomboidal shape and are bulkier
than MSU crystals, ranging up to 15 microns in length, and they are
usually not very numerous, even in a spun sample. They too are accompanied by a neutrophilic exudate, so “disease” is not likely present when
neutrophils are not numerous in the sample. Crystals are contained
within cells, singly or in small numbers, and they may produce a
coarser, less elegant “Saturn cell.” Also, they are present free in the fluid.
In compensated polarized light, these crystals are weakly positively
birefringent (i.e., blue when parallel and yellow when perpendicular to
the optical axis of the F-OR filter; Fig. 20-6). Care should be taken to
ascertain that the crystals change color appropriately when rotated
through the filter planes because many contaminants in the fluid will
not be monoclinic and therefore will not be purely positively birefringent (Fig. 20-7). This is the case for dry or crystalline anticoagulants
in specimen tubes and is the reason why examination of synovial fluids
can be difficult or result in false positive assessments.
HA crystal aggregates have a globular appearance because they are
composed of randomly arranged submicroscopic crystals that are measured in nanometers. These aggregates are granular and refractile in

nonpolarized light but are not visible in polarized light. They are visible
in a hematoxylin and eosin (H&E)–stained slide as granular purple-red
amorphous debris or as clear angular structures in the granular background (Fig. 20-8).

Pathology of Crystalline Deposits in Tissues
The gross features of crystalline deposits have many similarities,
depending on the size of the deposits, and frequently they are in the
differential diagnosis of each other. The microscopic features are more
distinctive but overlap to some degree in some circumstances.

Monosodium Urate Deposits (Gout)
Gross deposits of MSU crystals have a consistent, chalky white appearance regardless of where they develop (Fig. 20-9). Such deposits are
bulky and develop in loose soft tissues such as synovium, subcutaneous
tissues, and the bone marrow fat, and although they may accumulate
306

at the surface of intra-articular structures, they are not likely to be
found deep in the articular cartilage, meniscal tissue, or other dense
connective tissues such as ligaments and tendons. Frequently, crystalline material can be readily removed from the center of these deposits,
which are usually bordered by a small amount of scar tissue. Large
deposits producing sizable soft tissue masses are referred to as tophi,
leading to the diagnosis of tophaceous gout.
In addition to the presence of the crystals, the microscopic appearance of MSU deposits is characterized by a significant mononuclear
and giant cell granulomatous reaction at the periphery of the deposits
(Figs. 20-10 and 20-12). Large deposits are composed of aggregates of,
and sometimes confluent, smaller deposits. In these large deposits, the
cellular reaction normally seen at the periphery of the individual
deposits generally becomes attenuated and replaced by fibrotic tissue.
Inflammation is usually not present to a significant degree, but when
present and neutrophilic it is usually a sign of superimposed infection.
The crystals in these deposits have the typical needle shape and

Crystal Arthropathies

20

A

B

Figure 20-8. These photomicrographs are of aspirated material, stained with H&E, from a patient suffering from calcific bursitis. A, The hydroxyapatite (HA)-containing material
appears mostly as stained granular background debris with clear or poorly stained “voids” presenting HA crystals. B, These HA crystals appear as nonstaining “voids” within a
small amount of background material and are not visible in polarized light.

Figure 20-9. This photograph of a partially dissected toe in this case of destructive
gout shows the globular white deposits of monosodium urate.

Figure 20-10. This photomicrograph of a case of tophaceous gout shows the prominent granulomatous reaction at the periphery of the lobulated deposits, which are
surrounded by fibrous scar tissue.

negative birefringence of the crystals seen in synovial fluids but are
frequently much larger. The MSU crystals are typically densely packed
in aggregates that resemble randomly oriented sheaves of wheat.
Where the crystals are dense in the section, they have a brown appearance in nonpolarized light (Fig. 20-11A) and have the typical vivid
negative optical activity in polarized light (see Fig. 20-11B and C).
When the crystals have not been preserved properly in the tissue, pink
proteinaceous material that codeposits with the crystals is usually still
present and preserves the shapes of the dissolved crystals—“ghosts” of
the crystals (see Fig. 20-12).

finely punctate appearance (Fig. 20-13A and B). When they occur in
meniscal tissue and dense structures such as ligaments and tendons,
they frequently also have a characteristic streaky appearance, following
the extended linear structure of the collagen fibers in these dense tissues
(see Fig. 20-13A and C). Their presence in these types of tissue is
appropriately referred to as “chondrocalcinosis.” Occasionally, massive
deposits of calcium pyrophosphate may mimic the gross appearance
of gouty tophi, and these are referred to as “tophaceous CPPD.”
The microscopic appearance of CPPD deposits varies according to
the tissue in which they develop, although the crystals themselves are
still rhomboidal. In dense tissues such as cartilage and meniscus, the
deposits have a blue-purple, granular, puddlelike appearance, displacing the stroma of the tissue without a cellular reaction (Fig. 20-14A).
In less dense or fibrillated tissues, such as degenerate meniscus and
intervertebral disk tissue, the deposits are more diffuse but still do not
have a cellular reaction (see Fig. 20-14B). Deposits of CPPD crystals

Calcium Pyrophosphate Deposits
Gross deposits of CPPD crystals also have a consistent, chalky white
appearance regardless of where they develop, but the shape of these
deposits, when small, is dependent on the tissue in which they develop.
When deposits occur within articular cartilage, they have a delicate,

B, This photomicrograph of fibrillated meniscal tissue shows the less common diffuse granular appearance of calcium pyrophosphate that may also
deposit in less dense tissues such as fibrillated meniscus and intervertebral disk tissue. Note also the lack of a peripheral cellular reaction.
309

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
in synovium and other loose connective tissues are associated with a
mononuclear and giant cell reaction similar to that seen with the
smaller deposits of MSU crystals (Fig. 20-15). It appears that the presence of this cellular reaction is dependent on the vascularity of the
tissue and whether macrophages can be recruited into the region of
the deposit. When the crystals have not been preserved properly in the
tissue, the “puddles” have a pale color, but “ghosts” of the crystals may
still be seen (Fig. 20-16).

Hydroxyapatite Deposits
Gross deposits of hydroxyapatite and related constructs also have a
chalky white appearance and may be bulky, punctate, or streaky, and,

Figure 20-15. This photomicrograph of a small deposit of calcium pyrophosphate
within synovial tissue shows the typical cellular reaction to such a deposit in synovial
tissue.

A

in addition, may be hemorrhagic. They are frequently in the fibrous
and fibrochondral connective tissues of joint capsules as well as in
disrupted ligaments and tendons and have a punctate appearance
when small (Fig. 20-17). Such deposits may also be referred to radiologically as “chondrocalcinosis” and clinically as calcific capsulitis or
calcific tendinitis. Large deposits may be truly massive, and frequently
the center of these deposits has a thick, creamy liquid-chalky consistency. The largest deposits of these crystals are seen in tumoral
calcinosis.
The microscopic appearance of the deposits of HA crystals has
the most variation of the crystal diseases and depends on the size and
age of the deposits, on their site of development, and to some extent

Figure 20-16. This photomicrograph from the synovium containing deposits of
calcium pyrophosphate shows a small deposit without crystals but with the preserved
“ghosts” of such crystals in a deposit that also demonstrates a peripheral cellular
reaction.

B

Figure 20-17. These photographs are of a bursa that was expanded by heavy deposits of hydroxyapatite (HA)-containing calcific material. A, The gross specimen shows the typical
dense deposits of chalky, in this case, yellow-white material as streaks and lobular aggregates within fibrous tissue. B, This radiograph of the specimen in A shows the dense
radiopacity of HA deposits, which, despite similarities in gross appearances, clearly distinguished this kind of deposit from a gouty deposit, which is radiolucent (compare Figs. 20-1,
20-3, and 20-9).

310

Crystal Arthropathies
Figure 20-18. This photomicrograph of microscopic deposits of hydroxyapatite from a
shoulder capsule shows typical lamellated “calcospherites” within the dense tissue without
a cellular reaction.

on the manner in which the deposits develop. The most common
deposits encountered appear as small granular aggregates within capsular and tendinous tissues without a cellular reaction (Fig. 20-18).
Many of these deposits are composed of crystalline structures that
are lamellated and cuboidal or spherical and are referred to as “calcospherites.” When present in looser tissues or synovium, however, there
is usually an associated mononuclear and giant cell reaction (Fig.
20-19). HA deposits are refractile in tissue sections but are not visible
in polarized light. The material is not susceptible to dissolution in
nonbuffered formalin or in routine stains, but it is removed during
decalcification.

Handling of Crystal-Containing Sections
and Specimens
When deposits are small, both MSU and CPPD crystals may not be
visible in paraffin sections even though the deposit has characteristic
features otherwise. This is often attributed to the use of formalin as a
fixative—thus the historical practice of using alcohol as a fixative and
avoiding the “aqueous” environment of formalin. It should be noted
that MSU and CPPD crystals deposit in the body in an aqueous environment. In the body, however, the tissues are at the slightly basic pH
of 7.4. Even with commercially prepared solutions, formalin may not
be adequately buffered and may be sufficiently acidic to dissolve at least
some of the crystals in the tissue. Therefore, the use of correctly buffered formalin should not reduce the amount of the crystals in the
tissue.
Staining with H&E is another processing technique that reduces the
amount of crystal visible in tissue sections. Hematoxylin solutions are
variably acidic, with pH as low as 2.5. This fact, in combination with
the time the section is exposed to the stain, the use of variably acidic
clarifying solutions, and the small amount of crystalline material in a
5 micron–thick section, results in loss of much, if not all, of the material from the section. This problem can easily be overcome simply by
not using hematoxylin and leaving the section unstained but coverslipped (personal communication: Peter G. Bullough, MD, and
Kenneth P.H. Pritzker, MD). Staining only in eosin, which is alcoholbased, may not be completely satisfactory because this may also be
somewhat acidic, with pH as low as 4.5 or so. Simply avoiding the use
of conventional stains in selected sections will eliminate this problem
(Fig. 20-20).

20

Whenever a specimen contains grossly apparent chalky white
deposits, a useful procedure is to take a clean glass slide and touch
it to the exposed, incised deposit to make a smear; crushing crystals
will not alter their optical activity. A slide thus prepared can be gently
fixed and dehydrated through alcohols, defatted, and cover-slipped
as per frozen section routine without using any stains. The crystals
will then be readily interpretable if they are present and may become
a permanent part of the tissue record. Such a smear is a perfect
known control for verifying the arrangement of the polarizing
apparatus.
Because some of these crystal deposits are either heavily calcified
or present in tissues that also contain bone, it may be necessary
to decalcify the tissue before histologic embedding and sectioning.
In this case, making a touch preparation from the fresh tissue, or
obtaining a small piece of tissue such as the articular cartilage for
processing without decalcification, will help in identifying the
crystals.

Figure 20-19. This photomicrograph of microscopic deposits of hydroxyapatite also
shows the granular deposits and “calcospherites” of variable size and shape within
vascular synovial tissue with a typical cellular reaction.

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B

C

Figure 20-20. These photomicrographs of a focus of calcium pyrophosphate in
degenerate meniscal tissue illustrate the effect of staining with H&E on a small deposit.
A, The deposit in stained tissue has a somewhat washed-out appearance with only
a small amount of residual crystalline material remaining in the upper region of the
image. B, The same deposit viewed in polarized light shows only the residual crystals
present in the darkly staining region of A (polarized light). C, The same deposit identified in a parallel section cut from the same block shows the original amount of crystalline material in the deposit when viewed in polarized light without prior staining
(unstained).

General Comment and Summary

Suggested Readings

Deposits of crystals in the tissues are encountered fairly often in
routine pathologic examination of skeletal and connective tissue
samples. Each of the three main crystalline deposits, MSU, CPPD, and
HA, has sufficiently unique features that each should be easily distinguished from the others despite their similarities. The overlap in the
clinical presentations for which a biopsy or aspirate may be considered,
in particular between gout and pseudogout, is easily overcome by the
ease of distinguishing between the highly characteristic microscopic
features of the crystals. In most clinical settings it should not be difficult to identify the crystal deposits in pathologic specimens and to
make the associated diagnosis. The difficulty may, in fact, be in finding
the crystals themselves, especially in synovial fluid aspirates, or overcoming the effects of the processing of the tissue samples. On the
whole, however, the crystals and their associated diseases constitute a
manageable collection of pathologic entities.
