Synovial Sarcoma 325

Synovial-based lesions encompass both reactive and neoplastic processes. Although any joint may be affected, the most common site of
disease is the knee. Pathologic changes in the small joints of the fingers
are typically due to rheumatoid disease or localized tenosynovial giant
cell tumor, whereas changes in the ankles and wrists may be due to
intra-articular diffuse tenosynovial giant cell tumor. Malignant lesions
of the joints are uncommon but well described.

BENIGN LESIONS
Rheumatoid Disease
Brief Historical Overview
Rheumatoid disease is a chronic, systemic, autoimmune disease that
primarily affects the joints (rheumatoid arthritis [RA]) and periarticular tissues but that is also associated with extra-articular manifestations. RA was initially recognized as a distinct entity by Dr. LandréBeauvais, who differentiated it from common gout in his doctoral
dissertation in 1800.1

Although the etiology of rheumatoid disease is not completely
understood, both environmental and hereditary factors are clearly
involved.2–5 Familial clustering and concordance in monozygotic twins
brought early recognition to the importance of a genetic component,6,7
and a correlation between human leukocyte antigen (HLA) type
and the development of RA was identified in the 1970s.8 Although
other candidate gene polymorphisms have been identified, particularly
using genome-wide association studies, the strongest link is with
HLA-DRB1.9–11
Incidence and Demographics
RA has an incidence of approximately 38 cases per 100,000 and a
prevalence of approximately 10.7 cases per 1000 people in North
America.12 There is a strong female predominance, with a female-tomale incidence ratio of approximately 2 : 1. RA affects patients of all
ages but is most common in the fourth to the sixth decades of life.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
RA is a polyarticular, often symmetric process that is most common
in the small joints of the hands, feet, and cervical spine; however, larger
joints such as the knee and shoulder may be affected. The joints are
typically warm, swollen, and painful in large part due to effusions that
have a high protein content and a milky appearance. The proportion
of neutrophils is significantly lower than that seen in septic arthritis.
Inflammatory destruction of the articular cartilage may result in
joint fusion or, if the tendons, ligaments, and joint capsule are affected,
joint instability and subluxation. In contrast to degenerative osteoarthritis, RA appears to have a circadian rhythm, with pain and stiffness
generally greater in the morning. Because rheumatic disease is a systemic disease, extraskeletal manifestations are common and include
inflammation of the vessels, pleura, and pericardium as well as rheumatoid nodules in the soft tissue.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
In the early stages of the disease, imaging studies may be inconclusive
and show only localized soft tissue swelling, marginal erosions, and
periarticular osteopenia. With progression of the disease, however,
destruction of the joints with associated bone resorption can be easily
visualized on conventional radiographs (Fig. 21-1). Magnetic
313

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

Figure 21-1. The conventional radiograph of a patient with long-standing rheumatoid
arthritis shows symmetric destruction of the metacarpophalangeal joints and the small
bones of the wrist.

Figure 21-2. Rheumatoid arthritis is characterized by nonsuppurative chronic inflammation with hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the synovial lining cells. Redundant folds
of hyperplastic synovial lining cells can form papillary structures similar to those seen
in pigmented villonodular synovitis.

resonance imaging (MRI) may be useful in confirming extraskeletal
disease.
In the event of arthroplasty, the resected joint is characterized by
hyperplastic synovium that has a papillary architecture and that may
have a reddish tinge due to previous hemorrhage. In more advanced
cases, the synovium may extend over the articular cartilage, which can
display significant erosive changes. Joint fusion is a less common
finding.
Histopathology
The synovium in RA is characterized by chronic, nonsuppurative, proliferative synovitis. Hypertrophy and hyperplasia of the synovial lining
cells results in villous formation similar to that seen in pigmented villonodular synovitis (PVNS) (Fig. 21-2). Lymphocytic aggregates are
common and, in the subintimal region, a mixed inflammatory infiltrate
composed of plasma cells, lymphocytes, and mast cells predominates
(Fig. 21-3). Multinucleated giant cells can also be seen either in this
area or deeper in the synovium, and fragments of eroded bone may be
incorporated into the synovium.
The synovium may eventually extend over the articular cartilage,
forming a pannus. This inflamed synovium may destroy the underlying
cartilage by actively degrading the tissue as well as by preventing adequate nutritional support.
The subchondral bone also shows pathologic findings, including
extensive chronic inflammation, which may attack the overlying articular cartilage, along with lymphoid aggregates and occasional foci of
necrosis.
Differential Diagnosis
Degenerative arthritis can usually be excluded by both clinical and histologic features. Although chronic inflammation may be seen in degenerative arthritis, the abundant plasma cells and pannus formation seen
in RA are absent. Secondary degenerative changes are common in the
setting of RA, and it is easy to overlook RA in this context.
Acute RA may have a significant component of neutrophils trapped
within the fibrinous exudates. An infectious etiology can be excluded
by microbial culture and clinicoradiologic correlation.
314

Figure 21-3. In this example of rheumatoid arthritis, lymphoid aggregates cluster in
the subintimal region, which also displays a mixed inflammatory infiltrate, including
plasma cells, lymphocytes, and mast cells. A fibrinous exudate can be appreciated at
the left edge of the image.

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
A serum test for rheumatoid factor, an antibody against the Fc portion
of immunoglobulin G, is positive in approximately 80% of patients with
RA. However, this test is also positive in other connective tissue diseases
such as Sjögren syndrome and systemic lupus erythematosis.13 Antibodies to cyclic citrullinated peptide are more specific for RA.14 Culture of
the synovial fluid can help exclude inflammatory arthritides.
Genetics
RA is a non-neoplastic condition that, nonetheless, has a strong hereditary component.11,15 Genome-wide association studies have identified
more than 30 RA susceptibility loci; however, these are believed to
account for less than 50% of the total genetic heritability.9 HLA-DRB1
and PTPN22 have been implicated in severe RA characterized by autoantibodies to cyclic citrullinated peptide.16

Joint-Associated Lesions
Treatment and Prognosis
There is no cure for RA, and treatment focuses on managing symptoms
and slowing disease progression. Typically, drugs that reduce pain and
inflammation (e.g., aspirin, corticosteroids) are combined with diseasemodifying anti-rheumatic drugs, medications that moderate disease
progression and reduce joint destruction (e.g., methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine). Surgery may be necessary in cases of severe joint
destruction.
For an additional discussion of rheumatoid disease, see Chapter 19.

Synovial Hemangioma
Brief Historical Overview
Synovial hemangioma was initially described in 1856 by the French
physician Eugène Bouchut in his treatise on erectile tumors of the
knee.17 The first significant clinicopathologic study of the entity was
published by the surgeons George Bennet and Milton Cobey in 1939;
they presented 5 of their own cases and reviewed 24 cases from
the literature.18 They were the first to suggest categorizing synovial
hemangiomas as diffuse versus localized lesions. In 1993, a large
series of 20 cases retrieved from the files of the Armed Forces Institute
of Pathology over a 25-year period clarified clinicopathologic parameters and the differential diagnosis of synovial hemangioma. In
addition, this study also argued against a biological relationship
with PVNS, now better termed “diffuse tenosynovial giant cell
tumor.”19
Localization and Clinical Presentation
Synovial hemangioma is a rare, monoarticular lesion that generally
arises in the knee joints of children and adolescents.19 The second most
common site is the elbow, with few cases described in the fingers. There
is a slight male predominance.
The typical clinical presentation is relatively nonspecific, and delay
in diagnosis is common. A long history of a painful swelling of the
knee with or without an obvious mass, recurrent joint effusions, and

A

hemarthrosis are common presenting symptoms.20 Atrophy of the
thigh muscles has also been described.

21

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Synovial hemangiomas may be either localized or diffuse and are typically restricted to the synovial space. Conventional radiographs are
often nondiagnostic, although phleboliths may suggest the diagnosis
(Fig. 21-4A).19 MRI evaluation shows enhancement in both T1- and
T2-weighted images and may be pathognomonic for localized hemangiomas as a lobulated intra-articular mass with these signal characteristics (see Fig. 21-4B). This modality is also useful in delineating the
tumor extent for surgical planning.21
Grossly, localized synovial hemangiomas typically have a pedunculated appearance, whereas diffuse lesions can display villous hypertrophy and abundant hemosiderin deposition.
Histopathology
Histologic patterns include cavernous, capillary, and mixed hemangiomas. Although earlier work suggested that diffuse synovial hemangiomas are typically of the cavernous type and localized synovial
hemangiomas are more often of the capillary type,22 the most recent
large series did not support this.19 The authors did not specifically
address hemangioma histology according to lesion extent. However,
70% of the lesions were localized, and only 25% of the cases were
considered capillary hemangiomas.
Most synovial hemangiomas display cavernous histology consisting
of thin-walled, dilated vessels identical to those seen in their soft tissue
counterpart (Fig. 21-5). In contrast, capillary synovial hemangiomas
are composed of small, capillary-sized vessels arranged in a lobular
architecture. Hemangiomas showing a mixed pattern of thick- and
thin-walled vessels have been called “arteriovenous” hemangiomas and
are the least common.19
The overlying synovium shows reactive changes, including villous
hyperplasia and, in the setting of frequent bleeds into the joint
space, abundant hemosiderin deposition. Hemosiderin can be found

B

Figure 21-4. Synovial hemangioma. A T2-weighted magnetic resonance image of synovial hemangioma shows a multilobulated mass in the infrapatellar bursa with high signal
intensity (A). Low signal intensity voids represent phleboliths that can also be identified on the plain radiograph (B).
315

Practical Orthopedic Pathology

A

B
Figure 21-5. High-power (A) and low-power (B) views of cavernous synovial hemangioma.

in both superficial and deep synovium and may have a patchy distribution.19 Lymphoid follicles are rare and myxoid stromal change is
uncommon.
Differential Diagnosis
Clinically, a diagnosis of lipoma arborescens, nonspecific synovitis, or
intra-articular tenosynovial giant cell tumor may be suggested. The
histologic differential diagnosis shows some overlap and includes nonspecific synovitis, soft tissue hemangioma/angiomatosis, organizing
hemorrhage, and PVNS.
Although lipoma arborescens may be considered clinically because
it, like synovial hemangioma, is bright on T1 and T2 imaging, these
two entities are easily distinguished histologically. Nonspecific synovitis is both a clinical and histologic consideration; an associated reactive
vascular proliferation and/or increased vessel prominence secondary
to myxoid change in the surrounding stroma may resemble synovial
hemangioma.19 Perivascular myxoid change is uncommon in true
synovial hemangiomas and is a useful point of distinction.
Hemangiomas of soft tissue extending into the joint may mimic a
synovial hemangioma. Joint involvement can also be rarely seen in
hemangiomatosis; this possibility should be ruled out by clinical and
radiologic evaluation due to the high rate of local recurrence of this
entity.23 Organizing hemorrhage, particularly when sited in a papillary
frond of synovium, can resemble synovial hemangioma but lacks an
associated vascular network.
PVNS is perhaps the most challenging and most critical entity to
exclude because it may mimic a diffuse synovial hemangioma radiologically, grossly, and, in cases with abundant hemosiderin and clefting,
microscopically. However, synovial hemangiomas lack the characteristic monocytes and multinucleated giant cells seen in PVNS. Immunohistochemical stains for endothelial antigens (e.g., CD31, CD34) will
be positive in synovial hemangiomas but negative in the lining cells of
the slitlike spaces seen in PVNS.
Treatment and Prognosis
Synovial hemangiomas are benign lesions. However, when they are
symptomatic, complete local excision is warranted. Pedunculated
lesions can usually be treated arthroscopically, but more diffuse lesions
may require arthrotomy.19,24
316

Lipoma Arborescens
Brief Historical Overview
The term “lipoma arborescens” was first used to describe this condition
in 1838 by Johannes Müller.25 At the time, these lesions were considered by many to be secondary to tuberculosis; however, in the late 19th
century, Schuchardt was able to demonstrate that the hyperplastic
synovium did not have microscopic features of tuberculosis and was
probably reactive in nature.26 This theory gained credence with Albert
Hoffa’s description of adipose tissue in the pathologic knee joint26 and,
although the exact etiology of lipoma arborescens is unclear, most
current authors support a reactive origin.27,28 Consequently, terms such
as “villous lipomatous proliferation of the synovial membrane,”29
“synovial lipomatosis,”30 and “synovial lipomatosis arborescens”31 that
reflect the non-neoplastic etiology of this lesion have been proposed;
however, they have not been widely adopted.
Incidence and Demographics
Lipoma arborescens is a rare lesion that is most common in adult males
in the fifth to seventh decades. However, pediatric cases have also been
described.32,33
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Lipoma arborescens usually presents as a monoarticular disease affecting the knee joint, particularly the suprapatellar pouch. Cases have also
been described in the hip,34 ankle,32,35 shoulder,36 elbow,37 and wrist
joints. In addition, cases involving the synovial sheaths surrounding
tendons and bursae have been described.27,38 Typically, patients present
with a gradual increase in swelling around the joint with an associated
effusion. Multifocal lipoma arborescens is uncommonly described.30,39,40
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Lipoma arborescens can often be diagnosed based on imaging studies.
Although plain radiograph studies are nonspecific, high signal intensity in both T1- and T2-weighted images with decreased signal intensity on fat-suppressed sequences is a hallmark of this entity (Fig.
21-6).41 Ultrasound analysis shows a hyperechoic mass with fronds of
tissue that wave in real time during joint manipulation.42
Grossly, the lesion is composed of papillary fronds of synovium that
have the glistening yellow appearance of adipose tissue (Fig. 21-7).

Joint-Associated Lesions

21

Figure 21-8. In lipoma arborescens, unlike normal synovium, in which the adipose
tissue is a minor component, the synovium is composed almost entirely of fat that fills
the subsynovial space.

Figure 21-6. Fat-saturated T2-weighted magnetic resonance image of lipoma arborescens shows dark fronds of fatty tissue separated by high signal–intensity synovial
fluid.

arborescens, intra-articular synovial lipoma is a more localized process
and presents as a single encapsulated mass.43–45
PVNS can be recognized and separated radiographically by low
signal intensity in both T1- and T2-weighted images due to the presence of hemosiderin. Furthermore, on microscopic examination, the
absence of the multinucleated giant cells and monocytes typically seen
in PVNS aids in differentiating these two entities. Other diagnostic
considerations, including diffuse synovial hemangioma, synovial
osteochondromatosis, and RA can be easily excluded by routine
microscopy.
Genetics
Lipoma arborescens appears to be a reactive, non-neoplastic process.
Although chromosomal aberrations are well described in true lipomas
of soft tissue, there are no reported cases in the English language literature of karyotypic evaluation for lipoma arborescens.
Treatment and Prognosis
Symptomatic lipoma arborescens can be treated medically, using intraarticular injection of steroids or radioactive compounds such as
yttrium 90.46 If symptoms recur or fail to resolve, complete excision is
recommended.46,47

Figure 21-7. Grossly, this lesion is composed of papillary fronds of synovium that
have the glistening yellow appearance of adipose tissue. (Courtesy of Dr. Michael J.
Klein.)

Histopathology
Variable amounts of fat are an integral part of the normal joint and
generally admixed with fibroblasts, histiocytes, and inflammatory cells.
In lipoma arborescens, however, proliferating fat extends to the synovial lining and results in grossly apparent papillary folds (Fig. 21-8).
Differential Diagnosis
The clinicoradiologic diagnosis at the time of presentation may include
intra-articular synovial lipoma, PVNS, synovial hemangioma, synovial
osteochondromatosis, and RA.
True synovial lipoma is extremely rare, with only a few cases
described in the literature, primarily as case reports. In contrast to the
diffuse fatty infiltration of subsynovial tissue seen in lipoma

Giant Cell Tumor of Tendon Sheath
(Localized Nodular Tenosynovitis)
Brief Historical Overview
Tenosynovial giant cell tumor (TGCT) is generally subclassified
according to growth pattern as either localized or diffuse, with the
latter category subdivided as intra-articular and extra-articular. Localized TGCT arising in the fingers was described in 1852 by the French
surgeon Charles Marie Édouard Chassaignac, who considered it to be
a sarcoma.48 In 1865, a similar tumor was described in the knee by
Gustav Simon, who also thought it to be a malignant process.49 Since
then, it has been recognized that this is a benign although neoplastic
process.
These subclassifications are primarily of clinical importance. Overexpression of colony-stimulating factor-1 (CSF1) appears to be the
causative factor in both diffuse and localized lesions, and it is possible
that further research will demonstrate anatomic variants of the same
pathologic process.50,51
317

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Incidence and Demographics
Localized TGCT is the most common subset of these lesions. The
tumor has a female predominance and affects patients in the fourth to
sixth decades.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
On T1-weighted images, MRI signal intensities can be isointense to
skeletal muscle or between fat and skeletal muscle (see later discussion;
see Fig. 21-15). On T2-weighted images, the latter is more common.52
Grossly, localized TGCT is a lobulated, firm mass that is well circumscribed. Unlike extra-articular, diffuse TGCT, the lesions tend to be
small in size.

318

focally adopt a more spindled appearance. Myxoid change is uncommonly seen.
Differential Diagnosis
The characteristic admixture of monocytes, multinucleated giant cells,
siderophages, and foamy macrophages generally suggests the diagnosis. Fibroma of tendon sheath presents a similar clinical appearance;
however, the overall reactive appearance and absence of multinucleated giant cells aid in recognition. Occasionally, giant cell tumor of
bone enters into the differential diagnosis. Separation can be challenging when viewed solely by histologic criteria and when there is extrinsic invasion into bone. However, utilizing a combination of radiologic
images and demographic information, this becomes the rare exception
rather than the rule.53

Localization and Clinical Presentation
Localized TGCT typically presents as a discrete, lobulated nodule
arising from the tendon sheath of the fingers or, less commonly, the
toes. These lesions are usually cured by simple excision, although
recurrence can occur in 10% to 20% of cases.

Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Immunohistochemistry is not usually required for the diagnosis of
TGCT. Both the monocytes and multinucleated cells typically express

Histopathology
Localized TGCT is well circumscribed and lacks an infiltrative border.
At low magnification, the tumor has a nodular appearance and modulates from areas of greater to lesser cellularity with variable stromal
hyalinization that may show clefting (Fig. 21-9). The majority of the
lesion is composed of rounded to polygonal eosinophilic cells that fuse
to form a smaller population of multinucleated giant cells (Fig. 21-10).
These multinucleated cells generally resemble osteoclasts but may
mimic Touton giant cells. The number of nuclei is variable and is of no
clinical or biologic significance. The multinucleated cells are distributed unevenly throughout the tumor and may be rare, leading to diagnostic difficulty.
The monocytes and multinucleated giant cells are admixed with a
variable population of xanthoma cells and siderophages (Fig. 21-11).
Both localized and diffuse TGCT display a wide range of histologic
appearances, depending on the relative composition of these cells and
the degree of hyalinization and cellularity; although none of these is
generally classified as a histologic “variant,” it is important to appreciate
the protean morphologic possibilities. Stromal hyalinization is generally at least focally present and may be prominent to the point of
mimicking osteoid (Fig. 21-12). In some tumors, the monocytes may

Figure 21-10. The majority of this tenosynovial giant cell tumor is composed of
rounded to polygonal eosinophilic cells that fuse to form a smaller population of
multinucleated giant cells, which may mimic Touton giant cells.

Figure 21-9. Localized tenosynovial giant cell tumor is typically well circumscribed
and demonstrates a nodular architecture with variable cellularity.

Figure 21-11. In this tenosynovial giant cell tumor, a variable population of siderophages and foamy macrophages is admixed with the monocytes and multinucleated cells.

Joint-Associated Lesions

21

Figure 21-12. In this tenosynovial giant cell tumor, dense stromal hyalinization mimics
osteoid deposition.

Figure 21-14. Immunohistochemical stains for desmin are positive in dendritic cells
in approximately 30% of cases of tenosynovial giant cell tumor, as shown here.

chromosomes 5 and 7, aneuploidy, and a t(1;2)(p13;q37) translocation
that fuses CSF1 with COL6 A3.50,55
CSF1 is an inflammatory mediator that binds to the tyrosine kinase
receptor, CSF1R. Both localized and diffuse TGCT are characterized
by increased elaboration of CSF1, an inflammatory mediator associated with inflammatory arthritis.50 The mechanism in the majority of
cases is a COL6 A3-CSF1 fusion protein due to a t(1;2)(p13;q37).51
Interestingly, although increased CSF1 can be recognized by in situ
hybridization and immunohistochemistry, not all tumors have the
translocation, suggesting that other mechanisms may be involved in
these cases.
Treatment and Prognosis
Localized TGCT is best treated with marginal excision.56 In the event
of recurrence, which occurs in 8% to 22% of cases, reexcision is
appropriate.

Diffuse-Type Giant Cell Tumor of Tendon Sheath
(Diffuse Tenosynovial Giant Cell Tumor)

Figure 21-13. Both the monocytes and the multinucleated giant cells are typically
positive for CD68 in tenosynovial giant cell tumor.

CD68 (Fig. 21-13). Antibodies raised against CSF1 have also been
examined in TGCT. It has been suggested that, based on staining patterns, TGCT can be differentiated from reactive synovitides; TGCT
displays diffuse staining throughout the tumor in a punctuate, perinuclear pattern, whereas the latter is characterized by synovial lininglocalized linear staining.51
A potential pitfall is the identification of a population of desminpositive dendritic cells in approximately 45% of cases, which may lead
to an erroneous diagnosis of rhabdomyosarcoma (Fig. 21-14).54
Genetics
Clonal chromosomal abnormalities have been identified in both
localized and diffuse TGCT. These findings include trisomies of

Brief Historical Overview
The first case of diffuse TGCT was described in 1909 by Ernst Moser
who, like Chassaignac, considered it to be a malignant process.57
Charles N. Dowd was the first to question whether this truly represented a sarcoma and raised the possibility that it represented a villous
form of arthritis.58 In the following years, various terms were used,
including xanthoma, xanthogranuloma, giant cell tumor, xanthomatous giant cell tumor and myeloplaxoma of the tendon sheath.59
In 1941, Jaffe and colleagues suggested that these disparate lesions
actually represented a single lesion, which they termed pigmented villonodular synovitis, bursitis, and tenosynovitis, and they suggested
that this represented “an inflammatory rather than a neoplastic reaction.”59 However, recent molecular data support a neoplastic process,
which, nonetheless, is considered to be benign in most instances.
Incidence and Demographics
Diffuse TGCT is classified as either intra- or extra-articular. Intraarticular diffuse TGCT is more common than extra-articular diffuse
TGCT, which, in many cases, may represent the soft tissue extension
from an initially intra-articular tumor. Extra-articular diffuse TGCT
shows a slight female predominance and has a mean onset of
319

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
approximately 41 years.60 However, cases in adolescents and children
have also been described.60
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Intra-articular diffuse TGCT (PVNS) generally arises in and around
the large joints of the extremities, specifically, the knee, ankle, and
wrists. The presenting symptoms are nonspecific and include joint pain
and effusion.
Extra-articular diffuse TGCT also affects the large joints and predominantly arises in the lower extremities. Despite this predilection
for the lower extremities, the wrist is the most common single site,
followed by the knee.60 Unlike intra-articular TGCT, extra-articular
TGCT is typically painless and presents as slow, periarticular
swelling.60
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Intra-articular TGCT may appear normal or show periarticular soft
tissue swelling on conventional radiographs. Bone erosion is most
common in joints with a constrained capsule, such as hip and ankle;
it is less common in the knee joint. MRI is the best modality to appreciate tumor extent and, in intra-articular TGCT, highlights its hypertrophic, frondlike synovium.
On MRI, diffuse extra-articular TGCT mimics a soft tissue sarcoma
or fibromatosis and is hypointense on T1-weighted images with hyperintense areas consistent with hemorrhage.61,62 T2-weighted imaging
can have a more variable appearance and may be hypo-, iso- or hyperintense when compared to skeletal muscle (Fig. 21-15).62
The gross appearance of diffuse TGCT varies depending on whether
the tumor is intra- or extra-articular. Intra-articular TGCT typically
has a villous appearance with significant hemosiderin staining,
accounting for the clinical term “pigmented villonodular synovitis”
(Fig. 21-16). In contrast, extra-articular TGCT consists of yellow to
white nodules, which may show cystic degeneration.60

Histopathology
The grossly apparent villous architecture seen in intra-articular diffuse
TGCT can be appreciated microscopically (Fig. 21-17). The lesions
typically have an infiltrative border, at least focally, and the giant cell
population may be minimal. Due to its pedunculated architecture,
PVNS may present as an entirely infarcted tumor in which ghosted
mononuclear and giant cells may be the only clues to diagnosis (Fig.
21-18). In these cases, clinical correlation is critical.
Lesions of extra-articular diffuse TGCT are infiltrative, although
they may appear circumscribed grossly. Like localized TGCT, the
lesions have a nodular architecture and variable cellularity, which is
best appreciated at lower power. Clefting is seen in most cases,60 and
pseudocystic and pseudoglandular proliferations are more common
than in localized TGCT. The number of multinucleated giant cells is
variable, and regional intratumoral variation is common. Other characteristic features include thick-walled capillaries, a mixed inflammatory infiltrate with a lymphocyte predominance, and sheets of foamy

Figure 21-16. Intra-articular tenosynovial giant cell tumor typically has a villous
appearance with significant hemosiderin staining, accounting for the clinical term
“pigmented villonodular synovitis.” (Courtesy of Dr. Michael J. Klein.)

Figure 21-15. Fat-saturated, T2-weighted magnetic resonance imaging of an intraarticular diffuse tenosynovial giant cell tumor is dark on both T1- and T2-weighted
images. In this T2-weighted image, a complex mass limited to the joint space is clearly
visible in contrast to the bright synovial fluid.
320

Figure 21-17. In intra-articular diffuse tenosynovial giant cell tumor, the grossly
apparent villous architecture can also be appreciated microscopically.

Joint-Associated Lesions

21

Figure 21-18. Due to its pedunculated architecture, pigmented villonodular synovitis
frequently infarcts, leaving only ghosts of monocytes and multinucleated cells admixed
with residual hemosiderin.

macrophages. Necrosis, brisk mitotic activity, and rare abnormal
mitotic figures have been described in clinically indolent lesions.60
Differential Diagnosis and Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
The villous appearance and hemosiderin deposition seen in hemosiderotic synovitis can mimic PVNS. Recently, it has been suggested that
immunohistochemical staining for CSF1 can help differentiate reactive
synovitis from PVNS. In contrast to the diffuse staining of sheets of
cells seen in the latter, linear staining that highlights the synovial lining
is more characteristic of reactive synovitides.51
Extra-articular diffuse TGCT may present a diagnostic challenge,
particularly when the multinucleated giant cell population is minimal
and an association with a large joint is obscure. When pseudoglandular
spaces are prominent, the tumor may appear biphasic, raising the possibility of synovial sarcoma.
Giant cell–poor TGCT may mimic a round cell sarcoma due to the
high cellularity and brisk mitotic activity; however, the monocytes are
relatively bland and uniform in appearance. Identification of giant cells
and foamy histiocytes is helpful. Desmin-positive dendritic cells also
support the diagnosis.54
Histologic Variants
Pseudoalveolar change is more common in diffuse TGCT compared
to localized TGCT. This finding may overwhelm the usual nodular
architecture and characteristic histologic findings (Fig. 21-19). Combined with the paucity of multinucleated giant cells seen in diffuse
TGCT, this may cause diagnostic difficulty. Focal areas of typical TGCT
can usually be appreciated. Rarely, synovial sarcoma enters the differential diagnosis; immunohistochemical studies and careful microscopic examination will exclude this possibility.
Genetics
For much of the modern era, diffuse TGCT was considered an
inflammatory, non-neoplastic condition.59 Chromosomal analysis
of both localized and diffuse TGCT has confirmed the neoplastic
nature of these tumors and suggests that they represent the same
pathologic process, which, nonetheless, has multiple clinical and histologic features depending on anatomic constraints (see previous
section).50,51

Figure 21-19. A pseudoalveolar appearance can overwhelm the usual architectural
features.

Treatment and Prognosis
Unlike localized TGCT, diffuse TGCT follows a much more aggressive
course, and frequent recurrences are common.56 These lesions are typically symptomatic and require multiple surgical interventions throughout the patient’s lifespan. Unresectable tumors or those with frequent,
aggressive recurrences may be treated with CSF1R inhibitors.

Synovial Chondromatosis
Brief Historical Overview
The term “synovial chondromatosis” (SC) was first used by Jaffe in 1958
to describe multiple nodules of metaplastic cartilage arising in the
synovium.63 Primary SC (as opposed to that seen secondary to osteoarthritis, RA, and so on) is extremely rare and the pathogenesis is
unclear. Although considered by many to represent a reactive, nonneoplastic process, some cases have been shown to harbor clonal chromosomal abnormalities and it is likely, therefore, that at least a
subpopulation of primary SC are true neoplasms.
Localization and Clinical Presentation
Primary SC arises in the synovium of joints (intra-articular SC) and
the tenosynovium of tendons and bursae (extra-articular SC). The
most common site is the knee (55% to 65% of cases), where it may be
bilateral in up to 10% of cases,64 followed by the hip.65 Cases in the
elbow, shoulder, ankle, and temporomandibular joint have also been
described. Extra-articular SC is most prevalent in the tendon sheaths
of the hands and feet.66 Secondary SC is most often due to degenerative
joint disease and therefore affects joints subject to this process, most
commonly the hip and knee.67
Symptoms in primary SC include gradually increasing pain and
stiffness as well as swelling around the affected joint. Limitation of
motion is a common complaint. In secondary SC, many of the symptoms are directly referable to the underlying cause.
Incidence and Demographics
As noted earlier, primary SC is an uncommon lesion.66 Intra-articular
cases predominantly affect males (male-to-female ratio, 1.8 : 1) in the
third to fifth decades of life.68 Secondary SC is more common and
usually arises in the older patient population that is subject to degenerative joint disease.
321

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Figure 21-20. Lateral radiograph (A) and sagittal CT reformation (B) of
synovial chondromatosis show numerous intra-articular osteochondral
bodies distending the posterior joint capsule. Note extrinsic pressure erosion
at the bony attachment of the capsule (arrow). (Courtesy of Dr. Robert J.
Lopez.)

A

B

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Diagnostic findings of primary and secondary SC can usually be easily
appreciated on conventional radiographs as multiple calcified loose
bodies within the joint (Fig. 21-20). Early lesions may lack sufficient
calcification for radiographic identification and MRI can be a useful
adjunct technique. Calcification becomes more prominent in longstanding lesions and shows the typical flocculent, stippled pattern of
calcified cartilage. Ossification can also be seen in patients with a long
clinical history.68
The underlying bone is eroded in 20% to 50% of cases and can be
visualized depending on the imaging modality.69 Intra-articular osteochondral fragments can be seen in both primary and secondary SC.
Murphey and associates have reported a multiple-ring pattern of calcification in secondary SC that is not seen in primary SC.65 Imaging in
secondary SC also often reveals the underlying cause such as osteoarthritis or RA.

Figure 21-21. Grossly, synovial chondromatosis is characterized by variably sized
cartilaginous/calcified nodules that arise in the synovium or are loose in the joint space.
(Courtesy of Dr. Michael J. Klein.)
322

Grossly, SC is characterized by variably sized cartilaginous/
calcified nodules that arise in the synovium or that are loose in the
joint space (Fig. 21-21). The nodules range in size from 0.1 to several
centimeters, with fusion of several nodules accounting for some of the
larger specimens. In addition, there may be diffuse thickening of the
synovium.
Histopathology
Microscopically, both primary and secondary SC are composed of
nodules of hyaline cartilage (Fig. 21-22). Long-standing nodules may
display calcification and ossification. In primary SC, the chondrocytes
are organized in clusters within an abundant chondroid matrix and
may show moderate cytologic atypia and binucleation. The cytologic
atypia is comparable to that seen in enchondromas of the short tubular

Figure 21-22. Synovial chondromatosis consists of nodules of hyaline cartilage distributed in the synovium of joints and bursae.

Joint-Associated Lesions
a more limited approach, consisting of arthroscopic excision of
loose bodies and limited synovectomy of the affected synovium, is
pursued.76,77 Recurrences are treated by reexcision.

21

MALIGNANT LESIONS
Malignant Tenosynovial Giant Cell Tumor
Brief Historical Overview
Malignant TGCT is extremely rare; the largest series to date includes
only seven cases.78 The entity is defined as either concurrent histologically benign and malignant TGCT or as malignant TGCT diagnosed
in a patient previously diagnosed with a benign TGCT.
Incidence and Demographics
Malignant TGCT arises primarily in the lower limbs. Patients tend to
be older than those with benign TGCT (mean, 50.2 years).78

Figure 21-23. The chondrocytes in synovial chondromatosis show minimal cytologic
atypia. This example of secondary synovial chondromatosis is characteristically
paucicellular.

bones or in chondromas of soft tissue and should not be overinterpreted as malignant. Mitotic figures are practically nonexistent, and
their presence should suggest the possibility of chondrosarcomatous
transformation.68 Myxoid change can also be seen.
Secondary SC is often associated with degenerative changes of the
articular cartilage and bone. Cellularity is lower than in primary SC
(Fig. 21-23), and calcification may have, as noted above, the appearance of multiple rings, a finding not reported in primary SC.65
Differential Diagnosis
Extra-articular SC may be misdiagnosed as chondroma of soft parts,
which also primarily affects the hands and feet.66 The latter, however,
typically presents as a solitary mass in younger patients. Due to its
extraordinary rarity, synovial chondrosarcoma is not often a significant
diagnostic consideration; however, the degree of cytologic atypia and
cellularity seen in SC may suggest this unlikely possibility. In such
cases, it is important to remember that these features are entirely consonant with benignancy in this context.
Genetics
Eight cases of SC with cytogenetic abnormalities have been reported
in the literature.70–73 Of these eight cases, five showed losses in chromosome 6. Rearrangements of 1p22 and 1p13 and an extra copy of chromosome 5 were also identified in multiple cases.
The role of these chromosome 6 abnormalities in the pathogenesis
of SC has not been established. One hypothesis implicates Rab23,which
is a negative regulator of the Sonic Hedgehog pathway that localizes to
6p11. Sonic Hedgehog signaling is required for maintenance of FGF9,
the primary ligand for fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 (FGFR3),
which is expressed by a subpopulation of proliferating chondrocytes
in synovial chondromatosis.74,75 A second theory suggests that the relevant genes may encode collagen type X at 6q22~q21 and collagen IX
at 6q12~q14, which are expressed by hypertrophic and proliferating
chondrocytes, respectively.70
Treatment and Prognosis
The traditional approach to SC was open arthrotomy with removal of
all loose bodies followed by partial or total synovectomy. Currently,

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Tumors have a heterogeneous appearance on MRI. Extra-articular soft
tissue extension is a common feature of malignant TGCT that can be
appreciated using this modality.78,79 Tumors appear heterogeneous and
may enhance following gadolinium injection.78 As in benign TSGT,
hemosiderin deposition is often seen.
Histopathology
The majority of cases retain the overall histologic features of a benign
TGCT, including a nodular architecture, rounded monocytes, and
multinucleated cells, but display increased cytologic atypia, cellular
enlargement, bizarre giant cells, multifocal geographic necrosis, and a
more invasive interface with adjacent soft tissue (Fig. 21-24).78,79 The
sarcomatous component does not typically display any particular line
of differentiation (e.g., skeletal muscle, smooth muscle). It is best classified simply as a high-grade pleomorphic undifferentiated sarcoma
that may have a myxoid component or display heterologous differentiation. This constellation of features has been referred to as giant cell
tumor–like sarcomatous change, which may include sheets of epithelioid cells similar to the monocytes seen in benign TGCT.78 Other
patterns described include fibrosarcomatous, malignant fibrous
histiocytoma–like, and myxosarcomatous.
Differential Diagnosis
In cases where there is no previous history of benign TGCT or where
this component is minimal, pleomorphic rhabdomyosarcoma, pleomorphic leiomyosarcoma, pleomorphic undifferentiated sarcoma, and
fibrosarcoma should be excluded. The desmin-positive dendritic cells
in benign TGCT may rarely adopt a sarcomatous appearance while
retaining desmin expression and are a potential pitfall78; absence of
myogenin and MyoD1 expression excludes rhabdomyoblastic differentiation. Pleomorphic leiomyosarcoma may be likewise excluded when
the lesional cells are negative for desmin and smooth muscle actin.
Recognizing fibrosarcoma and pleomorphic undifferentiated sarcoma
may be more difficult; although it has not been demonstrated that
CSF1 is expressed in malignant TGCT, this may ultimately be shown
to be a useful diagnostic tool. Differentiating malignant TGCT from
fibrosarcoma and pleomorphic undifferentiated sarcoma may have
little clinical relevance. The critical matter is to recognize that a sarcomatous component is present and that the tumor is not a benign TGCT.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies
Immunohistochemical stains for desmin, smooth muscle actin, myogenin, and MyoD1 may be helpful in excluding pleomorphic rhabdomyosarcoma and pleomorphic leiomyosarcoma.

Figure 21-24. At low power (A), this tumor from the periacetabular region of a 64-year-old man is consistent with a tenosynovial giant cell tumor; however, higher power

Genetics
Evaluation of CSF1 expression by immunohistochemistry and molecular analysis for the 1p13 translocation has not yet been published for
malignant TGCT.
Treatment and Prognosis
Malignant TGCT are aggressive tumors; 50% of patients develop
metastases and approximately 33% of patients die from their disease.78
Of interest, the most common metastatic site is the regional lymph
nodes, followed by the lung and spine. Treatment is based on wide or
radical surgical resection, sometimes supplemented by adjuvant chemotherapy or radiation.78

Synovial Chondrosarcoma
Brief Historical Overview
It is not clear whether synovial chondrosarcoma arises from preexisting synovial chondromatosis or whether it represents a de novo lesion.
The majority of lesions appear to arise as secondary lesions, and an
associated component of SC is present in most lesions. The relative risk
of malignant transformation of SC has been estimated to be 5% to
10%.68,69 However, this number may be inflated due to selection bias.
324

True primary synovial chondrosarcomas have also been described but
are much less common.80,81
Incidence and Demographics
Synovial chondrosarcoma is extremely rare. Fewer than 30 cases are
described in the literature.80,81
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
Secondary synovial chondrosarcomas and SC overlap significantly in
their anatomic distribution, with both primarily affecting the knee
joint.
Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
Synovial chondrosarcoma cannot be reliably distinguished from SC
based on imaging studies. Although there is significant overlap in the
gross appearance of these two entities, Bertoni and coworkers noted
that there was a tendency for synovial chondrosarcoma to have a “glistening” or “mucoid” appearance.81
Histopathology
Histologic features that aid in making a diagnosis of synovial chondrosarcoma include prominent myxoid change in the chondroid matrix

Joint-Associated Lesions

21

A

C

and loss of the typical clustering pattern seen in SC (Fig. 21-25). Cytologic atypia is less useful as a criterion because of the variable appearance possible in SC. However, the presence of sheets of cells or cell
spindling is suggestive of malignancy.
Differential Diagnosis
Synovial chondrosarcoma chondromatosis must be distinguished from
SC. Unfortunately, neither radiologic nor clinical parameters are particularly helpful in this context, although a rapid increase in size is
suggestive of malignancy.81 The histologic differences can be subtle;
they are similar to those used to distinguish enchondroma from welldifferentiated intramedullary chondrosarcoma. As in that setting, loss
of nested architecture, increased cellularity, myxoid stroma, and cell
spindling are useful.
Ancillary Studies
No ancillary studies are currently in use for this entity.
Genetics
No genetic abnormalities have been described for this entity.
Treatment and Prognosis
Optimal treatment for synovial chondrosarcoma is based on wide or
radical resection for local control because disease-free and overall survival correlate with completeness of resection.81 Local recurrence is

B

Figure 21-25. This low-power view (A) shows a bland cartilaginous neoplasm that
arose in the ankle of a 47-year-old man, 6 years after a resection diagnosed as synovial
chondromatosis. Other areas (B and C) show marked cytologic atypia, cellular crowding, and loss of architectural organization. (Courtesy of Dr. Carrie Y. Inwards.)

common, perhaps as the result of previous surgical interventions with
consequent seeding of multiple compartments. In the largest series to
date (ten patients), more than 50% of the patients developed pulmonary metastases, and 45% of them died.81

Synovial Sarcoma
Brief Historical Overview
The tumor now known as synovial sarcoma was originally termed
“synovioma” by Smith in 1927 and later designated as synovial sarcoma
by Knox in 1936.82,83 However, it has been long recognized that synovial
sarcoma neither displays synovial differentiation nor arises from
synovium; instead, it is one of the chromosomal translocation–
associated sarcomas. Intra-articular synovial sarcoma is exceedingly
rare, with 20 cases currently described in the literature.84–87
Incidence and Demographics
Intra-articular synovial sarcoma generally affects young adults in the
second to fourth decades.86,88 There is a male predominance.
Localization and Clinical Manifestations
The knee is the most commonly affected joint.86–88 Intra-articular synovial sarcoma may present as long-standing pain or decreased range of
motion. The clinical impression is often of a benign reactive condition
such as SC or synovitis or PVNS.
325

Practical Orthopedic Pathology
Figure 21-26. Intra-articular synovial sarcoma frequently shows
significant calcification as can be seen in this conventional radiograph (A) and computed tomography scan (B) of a 20-year-old
woman with an 18-month history of progressive hip pain. The
radiologic diagnosis was synovial chondromatosis. (Courtesy of Dr.
Jeanne Meis.)

A

B

Radiologic Features and Gross Pathology
MRI of intra-articular synovial sarcoma is nonspecific. On T2-weighted
images, the tumor may appear as a multilocular cystic mass with heterogeneous enhancement. Intra-articular synovial sarcomas are often
heavily calcified (Fig. 21-26).
Grossly, the tumor may appear circumscribed or infiltrative. On cut
surface, cysts and/or calcifications may be seen.
Histopathology
Synovial sarcomas are classified as monophasic, biphasic, and poorly
differentiated. Monophasic synovial sarcoma is composed of monomorphic spindled cells with minimal cytoplasm arranged in fascicles.
Biphasic synovial sarcoma also has a spindle cell population, but in
addition, it has a glandular component with intraluminal eosinophilic
material that can calcify (Fig. 21-27 A and B). Poorly differentiated
synovial sarcoma may be either monophasic or biphasic and displays
a round cell morphology with frequent mitotic figures. Of the published cases of intra-articular synovial sarcoma, biphasic morphology
is twice as common as monophasic morphology.87
Differential Diagnosis
Biphasic synovial sarcoma is rarely a diagnostic challenge. Monophasic
synovial sarcoma must be differentiated from other spindle cell sarcomas, none of which is common in the joint space. However, malignant
peripheral nerve sheath tumor (MPNST) and fibrosarcoma can arise
in the periarticular tissues, and an immunohistochemical panel is
useful in distinguishing these entities.
Both MPNST and synovial sarcoma can express cytokeratin, epithelial membrane antigen (EMA), and S-100 protein. The cells in
MPNST tend to have a more “buckled” or “wavy” morphology and the
nuclei appear slightly pointed at the tips in contrast to the rounded
appearance of the nuclei in synovial sarcoma. These are somewhat
“soft” criteria and, if synovial sarcoma is strongly suspected, molecular
analysis for the t(X;18) chromosomal translocation (see later discussion) is essential for definitive diagnosis.
326

Fibrosarcoma has an essentially null phenotype and should be
excluded if the tumor is positive for cytokeratins or EMA. CD34 may
be seen in superficial adult fibrosarcomas but is practically never seen
in synovial sarcoma.89 Once more, chromosomal analysis may be necessary for definitive classification.
Primitive round cell tumors enter the differential diagnosis in
poorly differentiated synovial sarcoma. These, too, are uncommon
within the joint space but may affect periarticular structures. Rhabdomyosarcoma can be recognized most definitively using an immunohistochemical panel that includes desmin, myogenin, and MyoD1.
Extraskeletal Ewing sarcoma should also be excluded; both Ewing
sarcoma and synovial sarcoma can be positive for CD99, cytokeratins,
and EMA.90 Therefore, chromosomal analysis for the chromosomal
translocations seen in these two tumors may be necessary.
Ancillary Diagnostic Studies and Genetics
Diagnosis of biphasic synovial sarcoma is rarely challenging because
of the characteristic appearance. Prior to recognition of the conserved
t(X;18), diagnosis of monophasic synovial sarcoma required exclusion
of other spindle cell sarcomas such as MPNST and fibrosarcoma.
Expression of epithelial markers including cytokeratin AE1/AE3 (see
Fig. 21-27C) and EMA, at least focally in the spindle cells, supports the
diagnosis. As many as 30% of synovial sarcomas may express S-100
protein.91,92
Development of molecular diagnostic techniques using reverse
transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) and fluorescence in
situ hybridization (FISH) to confirm the presence of the X:18 translocation has facilitated the diagnosis in monophasic and poorly differentiated synovial sarcoma and in cases where minimal tissue is available
for evaluation. In most synovial sarcomas, the X:18 translocation
results in a fused gene incorporating the 5′ end of the SYT gene and
the 3′ end of either the SSX1 or SSX2 gene.93–96 Rarely, SSX4 may be
the fusion partner.97
FISH analysis using a break-apart probe to the SYT gene is optimal
for identifying all three potential fusions; however, RT-PCR is used

Joint-Associated Lesions

21

A

B

C

Figure 21-27. An excision specimen from the patient in Figure 21-26 showing predominantly epithelial cells with areas of coarse calcifications (A). Higher magnification reveals
sheets of epithelial cells with calcified eosinophilic secretions (B). An immunohistochemical stain for cytokeratin AE1/AE3 highlights the glandular morphology (C). (Courtesy of Dr.
Jeanne Meis.)

in many laboratories because of the lower cost and the improved
turnaround time. Ideally, both techniques should be used in difficult
cases.
Treatment and Prognosis
Synovial sarcomas of soft tissue are known to be aggressive tumors that
frequently metastasize to the lungs. Unfortunately, treatment and
follow-up information for intra-articular cases is limited. Friedman
and colleagues reported that two patients died 4 months and 2 years
after diagnosis, respectively, and one patient had pulmonary metastasis
18 months after amputation. Four patients were alive and well 1 to 9
years following diagnosis.87
